text: F.C. Netanya, founded in 2012 by Eli Cohen, competes in Liga Gimel Sharon and operates youth teams in regional divisions.

*The Fury of the Aquabats!* is the second studio album by American band The Aquabats, released on October 28, 1997. Blending ska, punk, and surf rock with comedic and pop culture-inspired themes, the album marked a commercial breakthrough during the 1990s ska revival, peaking at number 172 on the *Billboard* 200. It introduced Jimmy the Robot and featured drummer The Baron von Tito, later known as Travis Barker of Blink-182. The lead single, "Super Rad!", gained airplay and MTV rotation, while the album received positive reviews for its energetic and eclectic style.

Brahmachari (transl. Bachelor) is a 1992 Indian Tamil-language comedy film directed by Muktha Srinivasan. The film stars Nizhalgal Ravi and Gautami. The film, produced by S. Ravi and R. Govind, had musical score by Deva and was released on 15 January 1992.
The Brass era Reliance automobile was manufactured by the Reliance Automobile Manufacturing Company in Detroit, Michigan from 1904 to 1907.

Carfilzomib, sold under the brand name Kyprolis, is a proteasome inhibitor used in the treatment of relapsed and refractory multiple myeloma. Administered intravenously, it works by irreversibly inhibiting the chymotrypsin-like activity of the 20S proteasome, leading to the accumulation of polyubiquitinated proteins, which induces cell cycle arrest and apoptosis. Carfilzomib was developed from the natural product epoxomicin and advanced through clinical trials by Proteolix, later acquired by Onyx Pharmaceuticals. It received FDA approval in 2012 based on Phase II trials demonstrating efficacy in heavily pretreated patients.

Clinical trials, including the ASPIRE trial, have shown improved progression-free survival and overall response rates when combined with lenalidomide and dexamethasone. Common side effects include hematologic toxicity, gastrointestinal disturbances, and cardiovascular events. Carfilzomib is considered a significant advancement in multiple myeloma therapy, particularly for patients resistant to prior treatments.

Cape Newenham LRRS Airport (IATA: EHM, ICAO: PAEH, FAA LID: EHM) is a military airstrip located one nautical mile (1.2 miles; 1.9 km) southeast of Cape Newenham, in the Dillingham Census Area of the U.S. state of Alaska. It is not open for public use.
Robert Henry Doolan was a man living in Cincinnati, Ohio. During World War II, he was a navigator on a B-17 Flying Fortress before being captured by the Germans.

A Beautifully Foolish Endeavor is a science fiction novel by American author Hank Green that was published on July 7, 2020. Green's second novel, also starring main character April May, it is the sequel to An Absolutely Remarkable Thing and the final installment in the Carl Saga.

Youngblood is the third studio album by Australian pop rock band 5 Seconds of Summer. The album was initially set to be released on 22 June 2018, but was later brought forward to 15 June. Four singles were released in support of the album: "Want You Back", "Youngblood", "Valentine" and "Lie to Me". The album was released to positive reviews, with critics praising the lyrical maturity and the sound change from their previous work. In support of the album, the band embarked on the Meet You There Tour.

As well as appearing on a multitude of weekly and 2018 and 2019 year-end charts over several countries and receiving multiple certifications, the album's title track, "Youngblood", impacted the ARIA decade-end chart (2010-2019), which ranks the most popular songs of the decade, at number thirty-seven. In the ARIA Australian decade-end chart (2010-2019), ranking the most popular songs of the decade by Australian artists, "Youngblood" ranked at number four. "Youngblood" sold over five million adjusted copies and is the most streamed Australian song of all time on Apple Music. According to ARIA certifications,"Youngblood" is the eleventh best-selling single in Australian history. Youngblood was the most-streamed Australian album of 2018. In February 2020, the band were honored in the APRA AMCOS' The 1,000,000,000 List, for "Youngblood" having been streamed over 1 billion times.
Wilkinson Heights is a census-designated place (CDP) in Orangeburg County, South Carolina, United States. The population was 3,068 at the 2000 census.

The Heian-jingu Shrine (平安神宮, Heian-jingū) is a Shinto shrine located in Sakyō-ku, Kyoto, Japan. The Shrine is ranked as a Beppyō Jinja (別表神社) (the top rank for shrines) by the Association of Shinto Shrines. It is listed as an important cultural property of Japan.

The women's 1500 metres at the 2016 Summer Olympics took place at the Olympic Stadium from August 12 to 16. Faith Kipyegon of Kenya won gold with a time of 4:08.92, defeating world record holder Genzebe Dibaba, who took silver, while Jennifer Simpson of the United States claimed bronze. The race featured a tactical final, with Kipyegon outpacing Dibaba in the final 200 meters.
Ludvík Podéšť, pseudonym Ludvík Binovský (19 December 1921 in Dubňany – 27 February 1968 in Prague), was a Czech composer, conductor, music journalist and editor.

Stuyvesant Street is a historic street in Manhattan, New York City, originally part of Petrus Stuyvesant's 17th-century farm. It ran from Bowery Road (now Fourth Avenue) to the Stuyvesant manor house, which burned down in 1778. The street, known for its quaint charm, is part of the St. Mark's Historic District and features landmarks like the Hamilton Fish House. Stuyvesant Street is notable for being one of the few streets to deviate from Manhattan's 1811 grid plan. Portions of the street have been repurposed, including Abe Lebewohl Park, named after a local deli owner.

Homewood Mountain Resort is a ski area located on the west shore of Lake Tahoe in California, near the unincorporated village of Homewood. Situated on Ellis Peak within the Tahoe National Forest, the resort features a vertical drop of 1,650 feet, a skiable area of 1,260 acres, and 60 trails catering to all skill levels. Known for its stunning lake views and excellent snow conditions, Homewood receives an average of 400 inches of snowfall annually and enjoys about 300 sunny days per year. In summer, the resort offers hiking and mountain biking trails, along with limited lodge and café services.

"Take Me to the Pilot" is a song written by English musician Elton John and songwriter Bernie Taupin, and performed by John. It was originally released on John's eponymous second album in 1970.

It was recorded at Trident Studios in London in January 1970 and released in the United States in October 1970 as the A-side of a single, with "Your Song" as the B-side. Both songs received airplay, but "Your Song" was preferred by disc jockeys, becoming the singer's first hit (on both sides of the Atlantic) and rendering "Take Me To The Pilot" as the B-side.

The country duo Brothers Osborne recorded a remake of the song for the 2018 tribute album Restoration: Reimagining the Songs of Elton John and Bernie Taupin.

This is a list of current, amended, spent and repealed Acts of the Parliament of the United Kingdom relating to its former membership and current relationship to the European Communities and the European Union from 1972 onwards.

Some of the Acts (particularly the European Communities Act 1972 and the European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018) represent some of the most important constitutional legislation formerly or currently on the statute book.

The Vidarbha movement includes political activities organised by various individuals, organizations and political parties, for creation of a separate state of Vidarbha, within the republic of India, with Nagpur as the capital. The proposed state corresponds to the eastern 11 districts of the state of Maharashtra. It makes up for 31% of area and 21% of population of the present state of Maharashtra. The area is covered by thick tropical forests and is surplus in electricity, minerals, rice and cotton.

Abd al-Malik ibn Umar ibn Marwan (c. 718–c. 778) was an Umayyad noble and general who played a pivotal role in establishing the Umayyad Emirate in al-Andalus (Islamic Spain). A grandson of Caliph Marwan I, he fled to al-Andalus following the Abbasid overthrow of the Umayyad Caliphate in 750. Abd al-Malik became a trusted advisor and general under Emir Abd al-Rahman I, leading key military campaigns to consolidate Umayyad control over rebellious factions. His victories, including the suppression of a major revolt in 774, secured western al-Andalus for the emirate. His descendants held prominent positions in the region for generations.

Secours populaire français (SPF) is a French non-profit organization founded in 1945, dedicated to combating poverty, discrimination, and child abuse in France and globally. It provides emergency aid, supports equal access to essential services, and collaborates with local and international partners on development projects. Originating from the Secours Rouge International in 1926, SPF evolved through significant historical events, including World War II, and has become a leading charitable organization in France. It campaigns for social justice, organizes events for disadvantaged children, and responds to global crises, such as the 2015 Nepal earthquake.

Edith Gertrude Clements (1874–1971), also known as Edith S. Clements and Edith Schwartz Clements, was an American botanist and pioneer of botanical ecology who was the first woman to be awarded a Ph.D. by the University of Nebraska. She was married to botanist Frederic Clements, with whom she collaborated throughout her professional life. Together they founded the Alpine Laboratory, a research station at Pikes Peak, Colorado. Clements was also a botanical artist who illustrated her own books as well as joint publications with Frederic.

Both Clementses were involved with the study of phytogeography, especially those factors determining the ecology of vegetation in particular regions, and they would be praised as "the most illustrious husband-wife team since the Curies." It is impossible to entirely disentangle the work of each Clementses as they worked together during their noteworthy years.
John's River or St. John's River is a small river that snakes its way through Waterford city before joining the River Suir at Adelphi Quay, Ireland.

Marco Pinotti (born February 25, 1976) is a retired Italian professional road cyclist and time trial specialist. Known as "L'Ingegnere" (The Engineer) for his analytical approach, he achieved notable success in the Giro d'Italia, winning two individual time trial stages and two team time trial stages. Pinotti also held the race leader's pink jersey during the 2007 Giro. Over his career, he won six Italian National Time Trial Championships and excelled in stage races and one-day events. After retiring in 2013, he transitioned to a role in sports science.

Matt Seers (born 28 June 1974) is an Australian former professional rugby league footballer who played in the 1990s and 2000s. He played for the North Sydney Bears, Wests Tigers and Wakefield Trinity Wildcats, as a fullback, centre or five-eighth.

Daniel Berthiaume (born January 26, 1966) is a Canadian former professional ice hockey goaltender who played 215 NHL games for teams including the Winnipeg Jets, Minnesota North Stars, and Los Angeles Kings. Nicknamed "The Bandit," he also had a lengthy minor league career, played professional roller hockey, and later transitioned to coaching. Berthiaume currently resides in Hardy, Virginia, where he operates a fishing charter business and coaches youth hockey.

Armenia Marriott Hotel Yerevan (Lang-hy), is a hotel at the central Kentron District of Yerevan, Armenia. It was opened in 1958 as Armenia Hotel, a state-owned enterprise during the Soviet period. After the collapse of the Soviet Union, the hotel was privatized in 1998 and following a major renovation; it was reopened as the Armenia Marriott Hotel Yerevan in 1999.

The hotel is located on 1 Amiryan Street, overlooking the Republic Square. The National Gallery and the government building are located in front of the hotel.

As of 2016, with 259 guestrooms, Armenia Marriott is the 2nd-largest hotel in Armenia. As of 2020, the hotel has 144 rooms in the inventory with the Ararat wing being temporarily closed.

Fuxi or Fu Hsi (伏羲 ~ 伏犧 ~ 伏戲) is a culture hero in Chinese legend and mythology, credited along with his sister and wife Nüwa with creating humanity and the invention of music, hunting, fishing, domestication, and cooking as well as the Cangjie system of writing Chinese characters around 2,000 BC. Fuxi was counted as the first of the Three Sovereigns at the beginning of the Chinese dynastic period.
Feliksas Vaitkus (1907–1956), also known as Felix Waitkus, was an American-born Lithuanian pilot and the sixth pilot to fly solo across the Atlantic.

Cleveland Board of Education v. LaFleur, 414 U.S. 632 (1974), found that overly restrictive maternity leave regulations in public schools violate the Due Process Clause of the Fifth Amendment and the Fourteenth Amendment.

FC Stal Kamianske was a Ukrainian football club founded in 1926 and dissolved in 2018. Based in Kamianske, the club underwent several name changes and periods of inactivity before becoming FC Stal Kamianske in 2016. The team competed in various leagues, including the Ukrainian Premier League, where it played from 2015 to 2018 after taking the place of the dissolved FC Metalurh Donetsk. Following relegation in 2018, the club re-registered as PFC Feniks Bucha but withdrew from competition due to financial difficulties. Its colors were silver, blue, and white.

Kenny Francis (born December 1, 1969) is a former NASCAR crew chief and engineer from Jacksonville, Florida. Active in the Monster Energy NASCAR Cup Series from 2003 to 2018, he worked with teams such as Evernham Motorsports and Hendrick Motorsports, achieving 17 wins, 21 poles, and a best championship finish of fourth in 2012 with Kasey Kahne. Francis began his career as a driver and transitioned to engineering and crew chief roles, contributing to multiple race wins and a 1999 Winston Cup Series championship.

The Camp Beauregard Memorial is a historic site near Water Valley, Kentucky, commemorating Confederate soldiers who died at Camp Beauregard during the Civil War. Established in 1909 by the United Daughters of the Confederacy, it features two monuments and was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1997.

"Remember a Day" is a song by the English rock band Pink Floyd, written and sung by keyboardist Richard Wright. It was recorded in 1967 at De Lane Lea Studios in London and appears on their second studio album, *A Saucerful of Secrets* (1968). The track features contributions from Syd Barrett on guitar and producer Norman Smith on drums. Although planned as a single in the UK, it was only released as a single in the United States. In 2008, David Gilmour performed the song live as a tribute to Wright following his death.

"The Crocodile" is the fourth episode of the second season of the American ABC fantasy/drama television series Once Upon a Time, and the show's 26th episode overall, which aired on October 21, 2012.

It was co-written by David H. Goodman and Robert Hull, while being directed by David Solomon.

In this episode, Mr. Gold, David, and Ruby go searching for a missing Belle, while flashbacks show Rumpelstiltskin's past with Captain Hook.

Albert Henri Bourderon (26 November 1858 – 2 April 1930) was a French cooper, trade unionist, socialist, and pacifist. A prominent figure in the French labor movement, he co-founded the coopers' union and played a key role in the General Confederation of Labour (CGT). During World War I, Bourderon was an outspoken pacifist, participating in the Zimmerwald Conference and co-founding the Committee for the Resumption of International Relations. After the war, he shifted toward reformism and remained active in socialist and labor causes until his death.

The Prospect Hill neighborhood in North Omaha, Nebraska is one of the oldest neighborhoods in the city. In addition to being home to some of the city's oldest structures, the neighborhood is also the site of the Prospect Hill Cemetery which is located between North 31st and 33rd Streets and Parker and Grant Streets. The neighborhood's boundaries are North 30th Street from Hamilton Street to Lake and up to Creighton Boulevard; then over to Blondo and up to North 38th Street to Hamilton. The Omaha Belt Line ran near the northwest corner of the neighborhood. The neighborhood is also home to the historic Franklin Elementary School.

Meckenbach is an Ortsgemeinde – a municipality belonging to a Verbandsgemeinde, a kind of collective municipality – in the Bad Kreuznach district in Rhineland-Palatinate, Germany. It belongs to the Verbandsgemeinde Kirner Land, whose seat is in the town of Kirn.

Fredrik Backman (born 2 June 1981) is a Swedish columnist, blogger, and novelist, best known for his debut novel *A Man Called Ove* (2012), which was adapted into a film in 2015. His works, including *Beartown* and *My Grandmother Asked Me to Tell You She's Sorry*, have been translated into multiple languages and achieved international success. Backman has also written nonfiction, such as *Things My Son Needs to Know about the World*. He lives in Sweden with his wife, Neda Shafti Backman, and their two children.
Leif Carlsen (4 December 1943 – 14 September 2018) was a Danish footballer who played as a forward for Boldklubben Frem, Hvidovre IF, and Sweden's Landskrona BoIS.

Barbara Keiler (born April 7, 1953 in New York City, United States), known more widely by her pseudonym Judith Arnold, is a best-selling American author of crime fiction and over eighty-five romance novels. She has been writing since 1983, and has also been published under the pen names Ariel Berk and Thea Frederick.

A zograscope is an optical device, first developed in the early 18th century, that enhances the depth perception and realism of images, creating an immersive viewing experience. Often described as an early form of "virtual reality," the zograscope magnifies images using a biconvex or plano-convex lens, allowing viewers to perceive objects in greater depth. This effect is achieved by minimizing depth cues that indicate flatness, such as blocking the edges of the image and collimating light to prevent accommodation. Additionally, binocular stereopsis, chromostereopsis, and color contrast further enhance the perception of depth.

Zograscopes were particularly popular in France, where they allowed presbyopic individuals to view images clearly without glasses, though their enduring appeal was largely due to the realistic appearance of the depicted scenes. Early prints often used hand-tinted colors to exploit depth cues, with saturated colors in the foreground and pale washes in the background.

Modern variations of the zograscope can be constructed using simple materials, such as a fresnel lens and a frame, to enhance the depth of images displayed on computer monitors. The device remains a fascinating example of early optical technology and its ability to create lifelike visual experiences.

A phantom border (Lang-de) is an informal delineation following the approximate course of an abolished political border, associated with demographic differences on each side as a continuing legacy of historical division, despite official geopolitical union. Not all former political borders are today phantom borders. Factors that may increase the likelihood of a political border becoming a phantom border upon dissolution, include: short time elapsed since the border's dissolution, long existence of the former border, imporousity of the former border, and divergent characteristics of the political entity formerly governing one side of the border.

Phantom borders have many different implications: in Ukraine they are associated with conflict, while in countries such as Romania they play an important part in relations with neighboring countries.

Boris Nikolaevich Chichkov (Борис Николаевич Чичков), born 1955 in Novokuznetsk is a German-Russian Physicist whose research focus is on the development of novel laser technologies and their applications in material processing, photonics, and biomedicine.

Hopanoids are a diverse subclass of triterpenoids with the same hydrocarbon skeleton as the compound hopane. This group of pentacyclic molecules therefore refers to simple hopenes, hopanols and hopanes, but also to extensively functionalized derivatives such as bacteriohopanepolyols (BHPs) and hopanoids covalently attached to lipid A.

The first known hopanoid, hydroxyhopanone, was isolated by two chemists at The National Gallery, London working on the chemistry of dammar gum, a natural resin used as a varnish for paintings. While hopanoids are often assumed to be made only in bacteria, their name actually comes from the abundance of hopanoid compounds in the resin of plants from the genus Hopea. In turn, this genus is named after John Hope, the first Regius Keeper of the Royal Botanic Garden, Edinburgh.

Since their initial discovery in an angiosperm, hopanoids have been found in plasma membranes of bacteria, lichens, bryophytes, ferns, tropical trees and fungi. Hopanoids have stable polycyclic structures that are well-preserved in petroleum reservoirs, rocks and sediment, allowing the diagenetic products of these molecules to be interpreted as biomarkers for the presence of specific microbes and potentially for chemical or physical conditions at the time of deposition. Hopanoids have not been detected in archaea.

Alnus glutinosa, the common alder, black alder, European alder, European black alder, or just alder, is a species of tree in the family Betulaceae, native to most of Europe, southwest Asia and northern Africa. It thrives in wet locations where its association with the bacterium Frankia alni enables it to grow in poor quality soils. It is a medium-sized, short-lived tree growing to a height of up to 30 metres (98 feet). It has short-stalked rounded leaves and separate male and female flowers in the form of catkins. The small, rounded fruits are cone-like and the seeds are dispersed by wind and water.

The common alder provides food and shelter for wildlife, with a number of insects, lichens and fungi being completely dependent on the tree. It is a pioneer species, colonising vacant land and forming mixed forests as other trees appear in its wake. Eventually common alder dies out of woodlands because the seedlings need more light than is available on the forest floor. Its more usual habitat is forest edges, swamps and riverside corridors. The timber has been used in underwater foundations and for manufacture of paper and fibreboard, for smoking foods, for joinery, turnery and carving. Products of the tree have been used in ethnobotany, providing folk remedies for various ailments, and research has shown that extracts of the seeds are active against pathogenic bacteria.

Akai Katana (赤い刀, Red Katana) is a bullet hell shooter video game developed by Cave that was released on August 20, 2010 in arcades. A video game console port, Akai Katana Shin (赤い刀 真), was released on the Xbox 360 on May 26, 2011 in Japan. Rising Star Games released the game in North America and Europe on May 15, 2012.

It is the fourth horizontal shoot 'em up game from the company, the prior three being Progear, Deathsmiles, and Deathsmiles II. The game's soundtrack was composed by Ryu Umemoto. City Connection is scheduled to re-release Akai Katana Shin for Nintendo Switch, PlayStation 4, and Xbox One on December 15, 2022.

The Chicago Ballers were a basketball team based in Chicago, Illinois. The team competed in the Junior Basketball Association (JBA), a league created for high school and junior college players as an alternative to the National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA).
F.C. Netanya (Lang-he) is an Israeli football club based in Netanya. The club currently plays in Liga Gimel Sharon division.

AFSCME Council 31 is the Illinois chapter of the American Federation of State, County and Municipal Employees (AFSCME), a major public sector labor union in the United States. Established in 1942, it has played a significant role in advocating for collective bargaining rights, particularly through milestones like the Illinois Public Labor Relations Act of 1983. The council has been involved in political organizing, protests, and legal disputes, including standoffs with Illinois governors over labor contracts and layoffs.

Boomplay or Boomplay Music is a media streaming and download service developed by Transsnet Music Limited. The service was first launched in Nigeria in 2015 by TECNO Mobile, Transsion Holdings. Boomplay has a freemium and subscription based service; basic features are free with advertisements or limitations, while additional features, such as download for offline play and ad-free listening are offered via paid subscriptions. The service is currently available for Web via www.boomplay.com, Android and iOS use. As of August 3, 2018, Boomplay Music recorded 10 million installations off the Google Play and App store. It currently has over 70 million monthly users (MAU) with a catalogue of over 85 million songs.

El Camino High School is a public high school located in Oceanside, California, established in 1975. Part of the Oceanside Unified School District, the school serves nearly 3,000 students with a faculty of 144 and a student-to-teacher ratio of 26:1 as of the 2018–19 academic year. Its mascot is the Wildcat, and its colors are brown and gold. The school competes in the CIF San Diego Section's Avocado West League and maintains a rivalry with Oceanside High School.

The school has garnered media attention, notably in 1986 when a request to ban books on sorcery and satanism was rejected, and in 2008 for a controversial anti-drunk driving program involving a staged fake car accident to raise awareness among students.
Bing Yeh (Chinese: 葉炳輝; pinyin: Yè Bǐnghuī; Wade–Giles: Yeh Ping-hui; born c. 1950) is a Taiwanese-American electrical engineer and business executive.
Remnantz is a historic house in Marlow, Buckinghamshire, built around 1720, formerly housing the Royal Military Academy (1802–1811).

Justin Meldal-Johnsen (born March 26, 1970) is an American musician, record producer, songwriter and musical director. He is best known for his work with artists such as Beck, Paramore, Nine Inch Nails, M83, Air, and St. Vincent.

Meldal-Johnsen served as the touring bassist, guitarist, and keyboardist for Nine Inch Nails between 2008 and 2009, and was Beck's bassist and musical director from 1996 until mid-2016. In 2021, he began work as a bassist, keyboardist and musical director for St. Vincent.

Other artists Meldal-Johnsen has worked with include Garbage, The Mars Volta, Frank Ocean, Dixie Chicks, Tori Amos, Dido, Charlotte Gainsbourg, Goldfrapp, Blood Orange, Pete Yorn, Turin Brakes, Marianne Faithfull and Ladytron. As a producer, Meldal-Johnsen has worked with M83, Paramore, Metric, Tegan and Sara, Deafheaven, Poppy, Jimmy Eat World, The Naked and Famous, School of Seven Bells, Neon Trees, Young the Giant, Ken Andrews, Division Day and more.

Eclecticism is a conceptual approach that does not hold rigidly to a single paradigm or set of assumptions, but instead draws upon multiple theories, styles, or ideas to gain complementary insights into a subject, or applies different theories in particular cases. However, this is often without conventions or rules dictating how or which theories were combined.

It can sometimes seem inelegant or lacking in simplicity, and eclectics are sometimes criticized for lack of consistency in their thinking. It is, however, common in many fields of study. For example, most psychologists accept certain aspects of behaviorism, but do not attempt to use the theory to explain all aspects of human behavior.

Eclecticism in ethics, philosophy, politics and religion is also known as syncretism.

Susan Sonnenschein is an American developmental psychologist and professor at the University of Maryland, Baltimore County (UMBC). She earned her BA in psychology from New York University, an M.S. in educational psychology from Pennsylvania State University, and a Ph.D. in developmental psychology from Stony Brook University. A certified Level II School Psychologist, Sonnenschein has been a faculty member at UMBC since 1979, teaching courses in child development, cognitive development, and child assessment. She has mentored numerous students and served on over 50 dissertation and master's committees. In 2008, she received UMBC's Donald Creighton Award for Outstanding Faculty Member.

In New York State, Regents Examinations are statewide standardized examinations in core high school subjects. Students are required to pass these exams to earn a Regents Diploma. To graduate, students are required to have earned appropriate credits in a number of specific subjects by passing year-long or half-year courses, after which they must pass at least five Regents examinations in some of the subject areas. For higher-achieving students, a Regents with Advanced designation and an Honors designation are also offered. Students with disabilities or enrolled in an English as a Second Language program are able to earn a local diploma.

The Regents Examinations are developed and administered by the New York State Education Department (NYSED) under the authority of the Board of Regents of the University of the State of New York. Regents exams are prepared by a conference of selected New York teachers of each test's specific discipline who assemble a test map that highlights the skills and knowledge required from the specific discipline's learning standards. The conferences meet and design the tests three years before the tests' issuance, which includes time for field testing and evaluating testing questions.

Edith Gertrude Clements (née Schwartz; 1874–1971) was an American botanist, ecological pioneer, and botanical illustrator. She was the first woman to earn a Ph.D. in botany from the University of Nebraska, where she specialized in phytogeography. Alongside her husband, Frederic Clements, she conducted groundbreaking research in plant ecology, supported by the Carnegie Institution. The couple developed the Alpine Laboratory at Pikes Peak, Colorado, training many ecologists and publishing extensively on plant succession and adaptation. Edith illustrated numerous scientific works, including *Rocky Mountain Flowers* (1914), and translated their research into foreign languages.

During the Dust Bowl, the Clementses promoted conservation efforts across the Great Plains. Edith's memoir, *Adventures in Ecology* (1960), detailed her multifaceted contributions to their expeditions. Her legacy includes archival collections at the University of Wyoming and the University of Nebraska. She was nominated for the Nebraska Hall of Fame in 2012.

USCGC *Robert Yered* (WPC-1104) is a Sentinel-class cutter of the United States Coast Guard, commissioned on February 15, 2013. Built by Bollinger Shipyards, it is designed for coastal security, search and rescue, and interdiction missions. Named after Silver Star recipient Robert J. Yered, the cutter has participated in multiple rescue operations and migrant interdictions.
The Merchant Street Historic District in Honolulu, Hawaii, was the city's earliest commercial center.

Airblue Limited (stylized as airblue) is a private Pakistani airline with its head office on the 12th floor of the Islamabad Stock Exchange (ISE) Towers in Islamabad, Pakistan. Airblue operates scheduled domestic and international flights, the latter to Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates.

Stuyvesant Street is one of the oldest streets in the New York City borough of Manhattan. It runs diagonally from 9th Street at Third Avenue to 10th Street near Second Avenue, all within the East Village, Manhattan, neighborhood. The majority of the street is included in the St. Mark's Historic District.

Although the street runs diagonally in relation to the Manhattan street grid, geographically it is one of the few true east–west streets in Manhattan, since most of the grid runs southeast–northwest at a 28.9 degree offset. It is a one-way street, running eastbound.
Roberto Juan Poljak (17 September 1932, Buenos Aires; – 30 May 2019, Baltimore County) was an Argentine biophysicist and immunologist.

Abha Dawesar (born 1 January 1974) is an Indian-born novelist writing in English. Her novels include Babyji, Family Values, That Summer in Paris, and Miniplanner. Her 2005 novel Babyji won the Lambda Literary Award for Lesbian Fiction and the Stonewall Book Award.

Scrabble variants are alternative versions of the classic word game Scrabble, featuring modified rules, gameplay mechanics, or formats. These include fast-paced games like Anagrab and Speed Scrabble, mathematical adaptations like Equate and Numble, and unique formats such as Clabbers, Duplicate Scrabble, and Super Scrabble. Variants like Bananagrams and UpWords have also gained commercial popularity.

The siege of Ypres occurred between 8 June and 8 August 1383 as part of Despenser's Crusade and the Revolt of Ghent (1379–1385). It was conducted by English forces and forces from the Flemish city of Ghent. The siege was a failure.

The 2022 Argentine Torneo Federal A, was the tenth season of the Torneo Federal A, the regionalised third tier of the Argentine football league system. The tournament is reserved for teams indirectly affiliated to the Asociación del Fútbol Argentino (AFA), while teams affiliated to AFA have to play the Primera B Metropolitana, which is the other third tier competition. The competition was contested by 29 of the 31 teams that took part in the 2021 season and four teams promoted from Torneo Regional Federal Amateur. Two teams will be promoted to Primera Nacional and four teams were relegated to Torneo Regional Federal Amateur. The season began on 27 march and ended in 13 November 2022.

An air pump is a device used to move air, often by creating pressure or a vacuum, for various applications. The concept dates back to 1649 when Otto von Guericke invented the spool vacuum air pump, which utilized leather washers to minimize leaks. In 1658, Robert Hooke constructed the first effective air pump in England for Robert Boyle's scientific experiments. Later, in 1705, Francis Hauksbee developed a double-barrelled air pump for creating vacuums in scientific research.

Air pumps have significantly improved human life, playing a vital role in HVAC systems, food preservation, data center operations, and ensuring indoor air quality in homes, healthcare facilities, and offices. 

There are several types of air pumps, including air compressors, bellows, bicycle pumps, diaphragm pumps, engine-driven tire pumps, reciprocating pumps, and rotary pumps. Each operates on different principles, such as positive displacement or compression, to move air. For example, diaphragm pumps use a flexible chamber to expel and refill air, while rotary pumps use meshing gears to pressurize air. These pumps are widely used in various industries and everyday applications, from inflating tires to powering tools and maintaining air circulation.

Zeitenspiegel Reportagen is a co-operative of writers and photographers based in Weinstadt, near Stuttgart, Germany. The agency works for all the major German magazines such as Stern, Focus, GEO, and Die Zeit, as well as for international newspapers and magazines.

The agency’s signature style is reportage.

There is that split second in which a gesture, a disarming look, the play of colours in an iris coalesce into a moment that creates a full-length story.

In 2008, Zeitenspiegel members received awards, including the Ernst Schneider Award of the German Chamber of Industry and Commerce and a first place in the European Parliament Prize for Journalism. In addition to classic photo reportages, the Zeitenspiegel group also takes on short assignments. Zeitenspiegel Reportagen is active in the field of advertising (Daimler, Pfizer) and corporate publishing through their subsidiary agentur.zs.

Lala Hsu (traditional Chinese: 徐佳瑩; simplified Chinese: 徐佳莹; pinyin: Xú Jiāyíng; Pe̍h-ōe-jī: Chhî Ka-êng; born 20 December 1984) is a Taiwanese singer-songwriter. Hsu was born in Hualien County, Taiwan, and grew up in Taichung.

Hsu was a contestant in the third season of CTV's One Million Star in 2008. She won the contest with a self-composed and written song on 15 August of that year. She released her self-titled debut album on 29 May 2009. She has since published five albums to date; Lala Hsu in 2009, Limits in 2010, Ideal Life in 2012, Missing in 2014 and The Inner Me in 2017.

In 2010, Hsu won the Best New Artist award at the 21st Golden Melody Awards for her eponymously named album, Lala Hsu, becoming the first One Million Star alumni to win a Golden Melody Award. In 2015, she was nominated in six categories at the 26th Golden Melody Awards for the album Missing.

In 2016, Hsu was a participant of the fourth season of I Am a Singer which gained her wider attention in mainland China. A significant rise in popularity lead to numerous invitations to perform the theme songs of film and television works.

In 2018, Hsu was named Best Mandarin Female Singer at the 29th Golden Melody Awards.
Hanamsagar, also spelled as Hanumasagara is a village in the Kushtagi taluk of Koppal district in Karnataka state, India.

Colt Keliikoa Lyerla (born November 13, 1992) is a former American arena football wide receiver and convicted felon. He played tight end at the University of Oregon for the Ducks but left in October 2013 due to personal reasons. He signed with the Green Bay Packers after going undrafted during the 2014 NFL Draft. He was a member of the Arizona Rattlers and Portland Steel of the Arena Football League. He also played for AS Carcassonne in the Elite One Championship rugby league.
The *Komsomolets Ukrainy* was a Soviet Kashin-class destroyer, active from 1962 to 1991.

Robert Prosky (born Robert Joseph Porzuczek, December 13, 1930 – December 8, 2008) was an American actor. He became a well-known supporting actor in the 1980s with his roles in Thief (1981), Christine (1983), The Natural (1984), and Broadcast News (1987).

Prosky's other notable movies include Gremlins 2: The New Batch (1990), Hoffa (1992), Mrs. Doubtfire (1993), Last Action Hero (1993), Miracle on 34th Street (1994), Dead Man Walking (1995), and Mad City (1997). His most notable television role was of Sgt. Stan Jablonski on the TV police drama Hill Street Blues.

Climax is a village in Kalamazoo County in the U.S. state of Michigan. The population was 767 at the 2010 census. The village is located within Climax Township on the northern boundary with Charleston Township, and is roughly 12 miles (19 km) west of Battle Creek and 16 miles (26 km) east of Kalamazoo. Climax shares a school district with the neighboring town of Scotts.

Abubakar Umar Tutare is a Nigerian politician who was elected Senator for the Taraba Central Senatorial District of Taraba State, Nigeria in the April 2011 general elections, running on the People's Democratic Party (PDP) platform.

Hiroko Anzai (安西 ひろこ, Anzai Hiroko) (born February 9, 1979, in Kanagawa, Japan) is a Japanese actress, model and gravure idol, who has appeared in various magazines and photo albums.

Her first photo album was released in 1996 and titled Mystic. Fuji TV selected Hiroko as its Visual Queen of 1998. She has body measurements of 89-62-90 cm, and stands 1.57 m tall.

In Michigan folklore, the Michigan Dogman was allegedly witnessed in 1887 in Wexford County, Michigan. The creature is described as a seven-foot tall, blue-eyed, or amber-eyed bipedal canine-like animal with the torso of a man and a fearsome howl that sounds like a human scream. According to legends, the Michigan Dogman appears in a ten-year cycle that falls on years ending in 7. Sightings have been reported in several locations throughout Michigan, primarily in the northwestern quadrant of the Lower Peninsula. In 1987, the legend of the Michigan Dogman gained popularity when disc jockey Steve Cook at WTCM-FM recorded a song about the creature and its reported sightings.

Lewis James Emanuel (born 4 October 1983) is an English former professional footballer who played as a left-back and left-winger. He began his career at Bradford City, later playing for Luton Town and Brentford. Emanuel was part of Luton's 2009 Football League Trophy-winning team. His career ended following injuries, and in 2013, he was sentenced to eight years in prison for armed robbery.
Oakbank School is a coeducational secondary school located in the hamlet of Ryeish Green (near Spencers Wood) in Berkshire, England.

The Dickinson, North Dakota micropolitan area, as defined by the United States Census Bureau, had a population of 23,524 as of the 2000 census. Predominantly White, the area had a median household income of $32,597 and a per capita income of $16,058, with 1.02% identifying as Hispanic or Latino.

Homaro "Omar" Cantu Jr. (September 23, 1976 – April 14, 2015) was an American chef and inventor known for his use of molecular gastronomy. As a child, Cantu was fascinated with science and engineering. While working in a fast food restaurant, he discovered the similarities between science and cooking and decided to become a chef. In 1999, he was hired by his idol, Chicago chef Charlie Trotter. In 2003, Cantu became the first chef of Moto, which he later purchased.

Through Moto, Cantu explored his unusual ideas about cooking including edible menus, carbonated fruit, and food cooked with a laser. Initially seen as a novelty only, Moto eventually earned critical praise and, in 2012, a Michelin star. Cantu's second restaurant, iNG, and his coffee house, Berrista, focused on the use of "miracle berries" to make sour food taste sweet. He was working on opening a brewery called Crooked Fork at the time of his suicide in 2015.

In addition to being a chef, Cantu was a media personality, appearing regularly on TV shows, and an inventor. In 2010, he produced and co-hosted a show called Future Food. Through his media appearances, he advocated for an end to world hunger and thought his edible paper creation and the miracle berry could play a significant role in that goal. Cantu volunteered his time and money to a variety of charities and patented several food gadgets.

Nestor Labastilla Principe, also called "Ka Wadi" (March 15, 1945 - May 7, 1973) was a Filipino activist, writer, and martial arts instructor best known for his student activism at the Lyceum of the Philippines, his community mobilization work among the poor communities of Manila's port ara, and for his martyrdom on May 7, 1973, when he was killed and beheaded by elements of the Philippine Constabulary during Ferdinand Marcos' Martial Law regime.

On November 30, 2018, Principe's name was engraved on the Wall of Remembrance of the Bantayog ng mga Bayani, which honors the martyrs and heroes that fought against the Martial Law regime of Ferdinand Marcos.
Lezli Baskerville (born January 22, 1956) is an American lawyer and is the president and CEO of the National Association for Equal Opportunity in Higher Education.

The Troy Slapshots were a professional ice hockey team based in Troy, New York, that competed in the Atlantic Coast Hockey League during the 1986–87 season. Originally founded as the New York Slapshots in 1985, the team faced significant challenges, including financial instability, low attendance, and a lack of proper facilities. After relocating to Troy and playing only four home games at the Houston Field House, the team folded in November 1986 due to insufficient funding and poor management.

Melchior Inchofer or Imhofer, in Hungarian: Inchofer Menyhért (c. 1584 – 28 September 1648) was an Austrian-Hungarian Jesuit. He played an important part in the trial of Galileo, by his arguments, later published in his Tractatus Syllepticus, that Galileo was an advocate of the Copernican system. His role in the Galileo affair is being reassessed in the light of fresh documentary evidence.
116 is a natural number, notable in mathematics for its properties as a noncototient, its factorial prime status, and its connections to combinatorics and Costas arrays.

Ebenezer Norman (born June 10, 1981) is a Liberian humanitarian activist and founder of New Dimension of Hope (NDHope), an organization focused on constructing and managing schools in Liberia to meet international academic standards. Inspired by the aftermath of the Second Liberian Civil War, Norman built his first school in Troyah Town, Liberia, despite challenges such as the Ebola outbreak and property disputes. His efforts have garnered international support, including from Nobel Peace Prize Laureate Leymah Gbowee. Norman, a Global Goodwill Ambassador and recipient of the LOOP Award, continues to expand educational opportunities in Liberia while residing in Denver, Colorado.

Heian Shrine (平安神宮, Heian-jingū) is a Shinto shrine in Kyoto, Japan, established in 1895 to commemorate the 1100th anniversary of Heian-kyō, the former capital of Japan. Dedicated to Emperor Kanmu and later Emperor Kōmei, the shrine features a partial reproduction of the Heian Palace at 5/8 scale, designed by Itō Chūta. Its architecture reflects the Heian Period, including one of Japan's largest torii gates. The shrine's expansive garden, created by Ogawa Jihei VII, includes ponds fed by the Lake Biwa Canal and supports rare species. Heian Shrine hosts major festivals, including the Jidai Matsuri, and serves as a venue for traditional weddings and cultural events.

The fourteenth season of *Dancing with the Stars*, an American reality television series, premiered on ABC on March 19, 2012, and concluded on May 22, 2012. Hosted by Tom Bergeron and Brooke Burke Charvet, with judges Carrie Ann Inaba, Len Goodman, and Bruno Tonioli, the season featured twelve celebrity and professional dancer pairs competing for the Mirrorball Trophy. The Harold Wheeler orchestra and singers provided live music throughout the season.

NFL player Donald Driver and his professional partner Peta Murgatroyd were crowned the winners, marking Murgatroyd's first win on the show. The season introduced two new professional dancers, Henry Byalikov and Emma Slater, to the dance troupe, joining returning members Sharna Burgess, Kiki Nyemchek, Oksana Dmytrenko, and Sasha Farber.

The season included themed weeks such as Rock Week, Motown Week, and Trio Week, where couples performed various dance styles. Weekly scores and performances were judged on a 30-point scale, with the highest and lowest scores highlighted throughout the competition.

USS Romulus (ARL-22) was laid down as a United States Navy LST-542-class tank landing ship but converted to one of 39 Achelous-class repair ships that were used for repairing landing craft during World War II. Named for Romulus (along with Remus, one of the legendary twin sons of Mars and the Vestal Rhea Silvia), she was the only US Naval vessel to bear the name.
Front Royal is the only incorporated town in Warren County, Virginia, United States. The population was 15,011 at the 2020 census. It is the county seat of Warren County.
Tõnis Kõiv (born 29 October 1970, in Paide) is an Estonian politician and lawyer who represented the Estonian Reform Party in the Riigikogu from 2005 to 2015.

The Sumunkur Ainu (Katakana: スムンクㇽ, literally "west in people", i.e. "Western people") is the name of the Ainu subgroup living along the southern coast of Hokkaido, traditionally from Iburi to Hidaka. The subgroup is known to have fought with the Menasunkur Ainu to the east in the 17th century. In Japanese texts, they were also called the Haekuru Ainu (Ainu ‹See RfD› hay-kur) and the Sarunkuru Ainu (Ainu ‹See RfD› sar-un-kur, "plains-on-people", "people on the plains").

The New South Wales State Heritage Register, also known as NSW State Heritage Register, is a heritage list of places in the state of New South Wales, Australia, that are protected by New South Wales legislation, generally covered by the Heritage Act 1977 and its 2010 amendments. The register is administered by the Heritage Council of NSW via Heritage NSW, a division of the Government of New South Wales Department of Planning and Environment.

The register was created in 1999 and includes items protected by heritage schedules that relate to the State, and to regional and to local environmental plans. As a result, the register contains over 20,000 statutory-listed items in either public or private ownership of historical, cultural, and architectural value. Of those items listed, approximately 1,785 items are listed as significant items for the whole of New South Wales; with the remaining items of local or regional heritage value. The items include buildings, objects, monuments, Aboriginal places, gardens, bridges, landscapes, archaeological sites, shipwrecks, relics, bridges, streets, industrial structures and conservation precincts.

Typically, an item will first attract local listing, then regional or State listing. If the item is of significance to the nation, the State will advocate for listing on the Australian National Heritage List or the Commonwealth Heritage List. Finally, if the item is of global significance, the Australian Government will advocate for the item to be listed on the UNESCO World Heritage List.

Van Johnson (August 25, 1916 – December 12, 2008) was an American actor known for his boyish charm and all-American image, which made him a popular star during Hollywood's Golden Age. Born in Newport, Rhode Island, he began his career in theater before transitioning to film, where he gained recognition at Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer (MGM). Johnson's breakout role came in *A Guy Named Joe* (1943), and he became a leading man in musicals, comedies, and war dramas, including *Thirty Seconds Over Tokyo* (1944) and *The Caine Mutiny* (1954). 

Despite a serious car accident early in his career, Johnson's injuries exempted him from World War II service, allowing him to rise to stardom during a time when many actors were enlisted. He later appeared in television and stage productions, including *The Music Man* and *La Cage aux Folles*. Johnson died in 2008 at age 92, leaving behind a legacy as a beloved Hollywood icon.

Harloe, born Jessica Ashley Karpov on July 10, 1992, in Queens, New York, is an American singer, songwriter, and music producer known for her work in contemporary R&B, soul, and pop. She began her career as Jessica Ashley, releasing the EP *Prelude* in 2012, before rebranding as Harloe in 2016. She has collaborated with artists like Robin Schulz and co-wrote the hit "POP/STARS" for Riot Games. Her debut EP, *Rivers Run Dry*, was released in 2020.

The 1965 Pakistani presidential election was held on January 2, 1965, under the Basic Democracy System, where an electoral college of 80,000 Basic Democrats voted to elect the president. The election saw incumbent President Ayub Khan of the Convention Muslim League (CML) face Fatima Jinnah, sister of Pakistan's founder Muhammad Ali Jinnah, along with two independent candidates. Ayub Khan, who had come to power through a military coup in 1958 and later consolidated his rule with the 1962 Constitution, leveraged his economic reforms and political influence to secure victory. Despite losing the popular vote, Ayub won 62.43% of the electoral vote, aided by faithless electors and strong rural support.

Fatima Jinnah, though popular in urban centers like Karachi and Dhaka, faced significant challenges, including limited campaign resources, indirect elections, and opposition from some religious clerics. The election, marred by allegations of rigging, sparked protests but also marked a turning point for women's political participation in Pakistan, inspiring greater involvement in politics.

CubETH is a 1U CubeSat technology demonstration mission led by ETH Zurich, with a planned duration of 18 months. The mission aims to demonstrate the use of low-cost GNSS receivers for satellite positioning in space, with onboard computation of orbit and attitude. Developed in collaboration with Swiss academic and industrial partners, CubETH was scheduled for launch in 2017.
The Drammen Museum of Art and Cultural History, established in 1996, is a Norwegian museum showcasing art, cultural history, and historic architecture in Drammen.
Dottle is the remaining plug of unburnt tobacco and ashes left in the bottom of a tobacco pipe when it has been smoked.

Common ownership refers to the shared ownership of resources, property, or assets by a group or society. It has been practiced historically in various forms, including by early Christian communities and religious groups like the Hutterites and Bruderhof, where property is owned collectively to support members. In capitalist economies, common ownership is found in non-profit organizations, cooperatives, and public bodies. 

In Marxist theory, common ownership is a key feature of a classless, communist society, distinguished from earlier communal systems by its basis in technological and social advancements. The concept also plays a role in antitrust economics, where overlapping ownership of competing firms by large investors can reduce competition. Additionally, contract theory explores the implications of common ownership on investment incentives, with debates on its efficiency depending on the context of human or physical capital investments.

Christian College is a K-12 independent school located over six campuses in Geelong, Victoria, Australia.

Three campuses are located in suburban Geelong: the Senior Campus at Waurn Ponds (Year 10–12), the Middle School in Highton (Year 5–9), and the Junior School in Belmont (Prep – Year 4). The Bellarine Campus is located in Drysdale (Prep- Year 9), and the Surf Coast campus is located in the coastal town of Torquay, which opened in 2018. There are also two other specialist campuses: "Back Creek" in Scotsburn (near Ballarat) is a dairy farm attended by Year 9 students, including Back Creek Cafe located in Meredith. The specialised Arts centre, Villa Paloma, is located off campus in Waurn Ponds a short distance from the Senior School, where VCE Arts students have their classes. Christian College also offers preschool kindergarten programs at the Junior School campus in Belmont (Williams House Kindergarten), and at the Surf Coast campus in Jan Juc (Butterfield House Kindergarten).

Christian College students are from a number of different areas in Geelong, these areas include Torquay, Drysdale, Anglesea, Ocean Grove, Queenscliff and Winchelsea as well as a majority from Geelong itself. Notable people to have gone to Christian College Geelong include AFL players Gary Ablett, Jr., Nathan Ablett, Matt Maguire, Taylor Adams, Connor Idun, AFLW player Renee Garing, netballer Tegan Philip (nee Caldwell), and Olympian Jakara Anthony.
Alfred Edward Bailey (14 March 1871 – 1 August 1950) was a professional cricketer who played first-class cricket for Somerset County Cricket Club from 1900 to 1911 as slow left-arm spin bowler.

Season fourteen of Dancing with the Stars premiered on March 19, 2012, on the ABC network.

Starting the week of April 16, and for the next two weeks after that, the bottom two couples competed in a dance duel. Both couples performed at the same time, at which point the judges decided who would be eliminated that week.

Green Bay Packers wide receiver Donald Driver and Peta Murgatroyd won the competition over British classical singer Katherine Jenkins and Mark Ballas, who finished second, and Cuban-American Telenovela star William Levy and Cheryl Burke, who finished third.

This season introduced "Ballroom Battles" where Cheryl Burke, Mark Ballas, Louis van Amstel, Lacey Schwimmer, Karina Smirnoff, and Tony Dovolani trained a pair of amateur dancers to compete against other amateur couples. The teams of Burke, Dovolani, and van Amstel competed in the final stage by performing a jive. Burke's team were named the champions by the judges with Dovolani's and van Amstel's teams taking second and third places respectively.

Quintus Pompeius Falco (c. 70 – after 140 AD) was a Roman senator and general of the early 2nd century AD. He was governor of several provinces, most notably Roman Britain, where he hosted a visit to the province by the Emperor Hadrian in the last year. Falco achieved the rank of suffect consul for the nundinium of September to December 108 with Marcus Titius Lustricus Bruttianus as his colleague.

Operation Northern Falcon (Lang-uk) is a joint Ukrainian–Danish operation in Greenland for the transportation of fuel from the United States Air Force's Thule Air Base to the Danish Station Nord by the military transport aircraft Ilyushin Il-76MD of the Ukrainian Air Force. It has been conducted annually since 2009.

Doğuş Balbay (born January 21, 1989) is a Turkish professional basketball player who plays as a point guard for Anadolu Efes in the Turkish Basketball Super League (BSL) and the EuroLeague. A two-time EuroLeague champion, Balbay is known for his defensive prowess and leadership on the court.

The 1900 House is a historical reenactment reality television series made by Wall to Wall/Channel 4 in 1999. The program features a modern family attempting to live in the way of the late Victorians for three months in a modified house. It was first broadcast on Channel 4 in the United Kingdom and PBS in America (with American commentary).

The series was accompanied by the book 1900 House: Featuring Extracts from the Personal Diaries of Joyce and Paul Bowler and Their Family by Mark McCrum and Matthew Sturgis. It won a Peabody Award in 2000 for being "an often humorous, always perceptive, series about the realities of life in 1900 that reveals themes of perseverance, human adaptation and family dynamics."

The Reinhardswald is a vast, sparsely populated forest in northern Hesse, Germany, bordered by the Weser, Fulda, and Diemel rivers. Covering over 200 km², it is the largest contiguous forest in Hesse, known for its beech and oak trees, wood pastures, and historical significance as a former imperial forest. Notable attractions include Sababurg Castle, the "Sleeping Beauty" castle, and the ancient Sababurg forest. Recent plans for wind turbine development have sparked environmental and tourism-related controversies.

Paterson station is a commuter rail station in downtown Paterson, New Jersey, served by NJ Transit’s Main Line. Opened in 1832, it originally served the Erie Railroad and later its successors. The station features a high-level island platform and provides connections to Hoboken and Port Jervis. Historically, it was a hub for long-distance passenger and freight services, including routes to Chicago, but now exclusively serves commuter traffic. The station underwent significant changes, including elevation in the 1920s and realignments following the Erie-Lackawanna merger in 1960. It remains a key transit point in Paterson.

Gorky Park, also known as Gorodskoy Garden, is a historic urban park located in Rostov-on-Don, Russia. Established in 1813 on the site of a former town dump, the park spans 12.2 hectares (30 acres) and features landscaped gardens, walking paths, fountains, sculptures, and recreational facilities. Initially formed by combining several smaller gardens, the park was developed with contributions from private organizations and underwent significant improvements over the years, including the addition of exotic plants, a stone grotto, and a reservoir with swans.

Notable landmarks include the first Municipal Theatre, a rotunda, and the park's iconic entrance designed by architect Nikolai Doroshenko. Today, it remains a popular cultural and recreational destination.
Dolní Bečva is a municipality in the Zlín Region of the Czech Republic, first mentioned in 1597. It lies in the Moravian-Silesian Beskids.
Freud's inner circle or Secret Committee consisted of Sigmund Freud's most trusted psychoanalysts.
St Andrew's Church, Buckland Monachorum is a Grade I listed parish church in the Church of England Diocese of Exeter in Buckland Monachorum, Devon.

*Police Academy* is a 1984 American comedy film directed by Hugh Wilson and written by Neal Israel, Pat Proft, and Wilson. The film follows a group of misfit recruits who join a police academy after the mayor mandates the acceptance of all applicants, leading to comedic chaos. Starring Steve Guttenberg, Kim Cattrall, Bubba Smith, and George Gaynes, the film blends slapstick humor with a satirical take on law enforcement training. Despite mixed critical reviews, *Police Academy* was a commercial success, grossing $149.8 million worldwide against a $4.5 million budget, making it the sixth highest-grossing American film of 1984. Its success spawned a franchise, including six sequels, a television series, and an animated series.

¡Que viva la lucha! is a 2007 film directed and produced by Gustavo Vazquez about Lucha libre in Tijuana, Mexico, considered to be a form of extreme lucha libre. The film follows Extreme Tiger, an up-and-coming professional luchador from Tijuana, on a journey that places him in a mask vs. hair match against Joe Líder. In addition, he follows other new talent, Angel Negro Jr. and Pancho Cachondo. In the process, the film also interviews other luchadores, promoters, commissioners, families, and fans who discuss the cultural significance of lucha libre as sport, ritual, and spectacle. The film includes music by Carne Cruda, Marziano, White Pine, and Nortec Collective. ¡Que viva la lucha! premiered at the 30th annual Mill Valley Film Festival on Saturday, Oct. 13, 2007, and has won two awards; at the Latino Film Festival and at the San Francisco International Film Festival.
Software was a German electronic duo active from 1984 to 1999, originally composed of Peter Mergener [de] and Michael Weisser [de]. The duo formerly used the name Mergener & Weisser.
Geometrics are an English five piece indie rock band from Hertfordshire, England.

John Mortimer Boudreaux Jr. (December 10, 1936 – January 14, 2017) was an American drummer and influential figure in New Orleans rhythm and blues. Known for his innovative drumming style, he contributed to iconic recordings such as "Mardi Gras Mambo," "Mother-in-Law," and "I Like It Like That." Boudreaux worked with prominent artists like Professor Longhair, Allen Toussaint, and Dr. John, and was a key member of AFO Records, advocating for Black musicians' rights. He later relocated to California, where he continued session work with artists like Sam Cooke and Sonny and Cher.

Caledonite is a rare sulfate mineral with the chemical formula Cu₂Pb₅(OH)₆CO₃(SO₄)₃, known for its blue to green color and vitreous luster. It forms in oxidized copper and lead deposits, often associated with minerals like linarite, malachite, and cerussite. Primarily a collector's mineral, caledonite is prized for its well-formed crystals, which are found in limited localities such as the Mammoth-St. Anthony Mine in Arizona and select sites in California and Chile. Its identification relies on properties like density, streak, and crystal habit.

Augustus Jones (c. 1757 – November 16, 1836) was an American-born Upper Canadian farmer, land speculator, magistrate, militia captain and surveyor. Jones trained as a surveyor in New York City, and fled as a United Empire Loyalist to Upper Canada. In Upper Canada, he worked as a crown surveyor in the Nassau District, where he quickly rose to the position of Deputy Surveyor General, the highest position in a district of Upper Canada. He occupied that position from 1789 informally, and 1791 formally, until his retirement in 1799. During that time he laid down many of the township boundaries in the Niagara Peninsula and on the north shore of Lake Ontario. He led various teams that cut many of the first sideroads and concession roads into these areas, facilitating their settlement by European and American immigrants. Jones also surveyed the routes for Dundas Street and Yonge Street, and supervised their construction. After his retirement, Jones farmed first in Saltfleet Township, later moving to Brantford and finally an estate outside Paris named Cold Springs, where he died in 1836.

The England Boxing National Amateur Championships previously known as the ABA Championships is the premier boxing tournament hosted annually by England Boxing. The Championships are 'open' class: any boxer who is registered with a club registered with England Boxing (aged over 17 years by the 1 October) can enter.

Jessica Ashley Karpov (born July 10, 1992), better known as Harloe (stylized in all caps), is an American singer and songwriter, based in Los Angeles. In addition to her career as a recording artist, Karpov co-wrote and co-produced four songs on Kelly Clarkson's 2017 album Meaning of Life, including the singles "I Don't Think About You"and"Heat". She has also written for Britney Spears, Charli XCX, Celine Dion, Zayn, K/DA, Sabrina Claudio, and Olivia Holt.

Up the Elephant and Round the Castle is a British television sitcom, which aired from 1983 to 1985, and was produced by Thames Television for the ITV network. Starring comedian Jim Davidson, who played the role of Jim London, the show spawned a sequel, Home James!, which was also made by Thames. Home James ran from 1987 to 1990.

Nancy Duff Campbell is an American attorney, law professor, and women's rights advocate. She co-founded the National Women's Law Center in 1981, focusing on gender equality and economic justice. Campbell has participated in landmark Supreme Court cases and policy initiatives, including tax reforms and child support enforcement, significantly advancing legal protections for women and families.

György Szondy was a Hungarian soldier and the captain of Drégely Castle. He was a respected soldier, even by his Turkish foes, whose recognition can be seen by his burial by Hadim Ali Pasha with full military honours.

The John Wescott Three-Decker, also known as the Wescott-Mulcahy Three-Decker is a historic triple decker house in Worcester, Massachusetts. It is a well-preserved example of a Queen Anne triple-decker, built about 1892. The building was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1990.

*The Gateway* (originally titled *Curtains*) is a 2015 horror film that follows Danni, a former hospice nurse turned whale conservation activist, who discovers a mysterious portal in her shower. Her investigation leads to deadly consequences, including encounters with monsters and a sinister figure known as the Pale Man. The film received mixed reviews, with critics praising its originality and charm but noting its limited appeal and unconventional narrative.

Ronaldo Soares Giovanelli (born November 20, 1967) is a Brazilian former professional footballer who played as a goalkeeper, primarily for Corinthians, where he became the club's third most capped player with 602 appearances. Known for his physicality and decisive performances, he was instrumental in Corinthians' 1990 Campeonato Brasileiro title and earned the Bola de Prata award twice. After leaving Corinthians in 1998, he played for several other clubs before retiring in 2005. Beyond football, Ronaldo is the lead singer of the band Ronaldo e os Impedidos and a radio host for Kiss FM.

The E3 Tug Project is an initiative focused on developing tugboats that are efficient in operations, environmentally friendly, and economically viable. Spearheaded by Smit International and Damen Shipyards Gorinchem, the project is divided into three stages. The first stage involves studying the existing Smit Elbe tug to gather data. The second stage focuses on designing a new propulsion system to enhance efficiency and reduce emissions. The final stage envisions a future tugboat design incorporating advanced technologies, such as LNG power, that are not yet widely feasible.

Allison Jones (born July 13, 1955) is an American casting director known for her work in television and film. Active since 1982, she has cast acclaimed TV shows such as *Freaks and Geeks* (for which she won an Emmy), *The Office*, *Parks and Recreation*, and *Brooklyn Nine-Nine*. Her film credits include *The 40-Year-Old Virgin*, *Superbad*, *Bridesmaids*, and *Lady Bird*. A graduate of Pomona College, Jones is celebrated for shaping modern comedy through her casting choices.

"Just Got Started Lovin' You" is a song by American country music artist James Otto, released on July 3, 2007, as the lead single from his album *Sunset Man*. Co-written by Otto, Jim Femino, and D. Vincent Williams, the song was inspired by Otto's desire to create a "sexy" and romantic track, drawing comparisons to Conway Twitty. Produced by John Rich and Otto, the song received positive reviews for its smooth, romantic style. A music video directed by Ryan Smith accompanied the release.

The crested servaline genet (*Genetta cristata*) is a small carnivorous mammal in the family Viverridae, native to southern Nigeria and western Cameroon. It inhabits primary and secondary forests, as well as bush–mango plantations, at elevations up to 1,000 meters. Characterized by its khaki-colored fur, dark spots, and a nuchal crest, it is classified as Vulnerable by the IUCN due to habitat loss from agriculture, oil production, and hunting.

The Wrocław Global Forum (WGF) is an annual international conference held in Wrocław, Poland, since 2010. Organized with support from the Atlantic Council and other institutions, it focuses on transatlantic dialogue, global challenges, and security issues. The event features high-profile speakers, the Atlantic Council's Freedom Awards, and is supported by governments and corporate sponsors.

The Fairmont Century Plaza, originally opened in 1966 as the Century Plaza Hotel, is a luxury hotel located at 2025 Avenue of the Stars in Los Angeles, California. Designed by architect Minoru Yamasaki, the 19-story, arc-shaped building was the centerpiece of the Century City development, conceived as "a city within a city." Over the decades, the hotel has hosted numerous high-profile events, including presidential visits, state dinners, and award ceremonies. After a $2.5 billion renovation, the hotel reopened in 2021 with 400 guest rooms and 63 condominiums, alongside two new residential towers. It remains a prominent cultural and historical landmark in Los Angeles.

The Faculty of Informatics and Statistics at the University of Economics in Prague, established in 1991, is a public institution located in Prague, Czech Republic. Led by Dean prof. Ing. Jakub Fischer, Ph.D., the faculty has 2,490 students and offers Bachelor, Master, and doctoral programs in fields such as Informatics, Econometrics, and Statistics. It collaborates with institutions like the Czech Technical University, the Academy of Sciences, the Czech Statistical Office, and international universities.
"Christians, awake, salute the happy morn" is an English Christmas hymn on a text by John Byrom. It is usually sung to the tune "Yorkshire" by John Wainright.

Kaberamaido is a town in Eastern Uganda, located in the Teso sub-region and serving as the administrative center of Kaberamaido District. Situated at an elevation of 1,080 meters (3,540 feet), it lies approximately 163 kilometers northwest of Mbale and 64 kilometers west of Soroti. As of 2011, the town's population was estimated at 3,400.

Substance Church is a non-denominational, evangelical megachurch founded in 2004 by Pastor Peter Haas in partnership with the Association of Related Churches. Known for its youthful congregation, with over 70% under 30, the church employs contemporary worship methods, including turntables and EDM. With permanent facilities in Spring Lake Park and downtown Minneapolis, it emphasizes church planting, creative media, and progressive methodologies.

Paul Lawrence Smith (June 24, 1936 – April 25, 2012) was an American-Israeli actor known for his imposing stature and roles in films such as *Midnight Express* (1978), *Popeye* (1980), and *Dune* (1984). He also appeared in television series like *CHiPS* and *Hawaii Five-O*. Smith began his career with a role in *Exodus* and later gained recognition for his legal victory over the unauthorized use of his name. He passed away in Ra'anana, Israel, at age 75.

"Her Strut" is a 1980 hard rock song by Bob Seger & The Silver Bullet Band from the album *Against the Wind*. Known for its fast-paced rhythm, the song sparked controversy for its lyrics, which some critics viewed as objectifying women. Seger, however, stated the song was inspired by Jane Fonda's activism and intended to celebrate strong, independent women. Despite mixed reviews, "Her Strut" has appeared on compilation albums like *Greatest Hits 2* and *Ultimate Hits: Rock and Roll Never Forgets*, as well as in video games such as *Grand Theft Auto IV* and *Guitar Hero World Tour*.

Anthony Waddington (9 November 1924 – 21 January 1994) was an English football manager at both Crewe Alexandra and Stoke City.

Waddington had a seven-year playing career with Crewe Alexandra before becoming a coach at Stoke City. He progressed to assistant manager to Frank Taylor and took his position in June 1960. He set about staving off the threat of relegation before bringing back club legend Stanley Matthews in an effort to rekindle the club's supporter base. It worked well and he had enough money to bring in a number of established veterans as Stoke took the Football League Second Division title in 1962–63 and reached the 1964 Football League Cup Final, losing out to Leicester City.

More fine signings followed as Stoke enjoyed great success at the beginning of the 1970s reaching two FA Cup semi-finals, playing in the UEFA Cup twice and winning their first major trophy, the Football League Cup in 1972. Stoke then nearly won the First Division in 1974–75 but after the Butler Street Stand roof fell off in a strong storm at the Victoria Ground Stoke had to sell their best players to cover the repair costs and a despondent Waddington quit in March 1977 with Stoke heading for relegation. He later had a two-year spell at Crewe Alexandra before becoming associate director of Stoke in 1991 until his death in 1994.

The Merchant Street Historic District in Honolulu, Hawaii, is a seven-acre area showcasing architectural styles from the 1850s to the 1930s, including Italianate, Richardsonian Romanesque, and Mission Revival. Added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1973, it features notable buildings such as the Kamehameha V Post Office, Bishop Bank, and Judd Building, reflecting the city's commercial development.

Rui Pedro Gonçalves Pinto (born 20 October 1988) is a Portuguese activist, whistleblower, creator of the Football Leaks website, and responsible for Malta Files and Luanda Leaks revelations.

Pinto created the Football Leaks website in September 2015, with the intention of revealing the financial picture of association football, including the tax avoidance schemes used by top players and the controversial third-party ownership models used by clubs. He is being accused of having used illegal methods to gather the data he leaked, by accessing into private servers and exfiltrating hundreds of internal documents and communications. But these allegations have not yet been proven at trial.

Pinto was arrested in Budapest, Hungary, in January 2019 at the request of the Portuguese authorities on suspicion of attempted qualified extortion, violation of secrecy and illegally accessing information. He was released in August 2020.

Hugh Anthony Allison (born 1986), better known by his stage name Mr. Muthafuckin'eXquire (often censored as Mr. MFN eXquire or simply eXquire), is an American rapper. Born and raised in Brooklyn, he debuted with the release of his first mixtape, The Big Fat Kill, in 2008. Allison would follow-up with what is considered to be his breakout project, the mixtape Lost in Translation, released by Mishka NYC in 2011. He signed his first major-label record deal with Universal Republic Records, in 2012, which he subsequently left. In 2017, with the release of Allison's fourth EP Brainiac, he launched Chocolate Rabbit, his own record label imprint. His self-titled debut studio album was independently released in 2019.

The Gonzaga Bulldogs are the athletic teams representing Gonzaga University in Spokane, Washington. Competing in the NCAA's West Coast Conference (WCC), the Bulldogs field 18 varsity teams. Known for their powerhouse men's basketball program, Gonzaga has made 22 consecutive NCAA tournament appearances and achieved national prominence. The university's mascot is Spike the Bulldog, with "Zags" serving as an unofficial nickname. Historically, Gonzaga also had notable football and boxing programs, with football ending in 1941 and boxing in 1952.

Carl Froch vs. Glen Johnson was a championship fight for the WBC Super Middleweight championship. The winner of the bout went on to the Finals of the Super Six World Boxing Classic and face WBA Champion Andre Ward, after his decision win over Arthur Abraham in May 2011. It was held on June 4, 2011, at Boardwalk Hall, in Atlantic City, New Jersey, United States, and televised on Showtime.

Red Obsession is a 2013 Australian documentary film which collects interviews with winemakers and wine lovers across the world. The film is narrated by Russell Crowe. Red Obsession was co-directed and co-written by David Roach and Warwick Ross.

Mélite, or The False Letters, is a comedy in five acts by Pierre Corneille. Written in 1625, it is Corneille's first play and debuted on stage in December 1629 in Berthaud’s Jeu de paume court, and was performed by the acting troupe of Montdory. Mélite represents Corneille’s creation of a new genre, the comedy of manners, which was a departure from the coarse or buffoonish farce in vogue at the time.
Alexandra Pringle, Hon. FRSL (born 1952/53), is a British publisher. She was a founding director of Virago Press and the editor-in-chief of Bloomsbury Publishing for 18 years as of March 2018.

Margaret Ransone Murray (November 16, 1901 – July 13, 1986) was an American scientist known primarily for her work on methods to establish cultures of neuronal cells. Her in vitro studies in cellular neurobiology shed light on both nerve- muscle relationships and axon myelination

Isometric projection is a method of visually representing three-dimensional objects in two dimensions, where the scale along each axis is equal, and the angles between axes are 120°. First formalized in the 19th century, it has been widely used in engineering, architecture, and art. Unlike perspective projection, isometric projection maintains consistent object sizes regardless of depth, which is useful for technical drawings but can create visual distortions. It has also been employed in video games and pixel art to create a three-dimensional effect, though often using variations like dimetric projection.
E11Even Miami is a renowned nightclub and entertainment venue, opened in 2014, known for hosting celebrities and accepting cryptocurrency.

1,3,2,4-Dithiadiphosphetane 2,4-disulfides are a class of organophosphorus compounds characterized by a P2S2 ring structure. These compounds, including the well-known Lawesson's reagent, are synthesized through reactions involving P4S10, such as electrophilic aromatic substitution with arenes or reactions with thiols or aryl ethers.
