They are widely used in organic synthesis for applications such as thionation, dehydration, and the formation of heterocyclic compounds like thiophenes. 

Their reactivity is influenced by the dithiophosphine ylides, which undergo nucleophilic or electrophilic attacks, enabling the synthesis of insecticides, herbicides, and other functional materials. Variants with higher solubility or unique reactivity, such as Woollins' reagent, have also been developed. These compounds are notable for their diverse chemical transformations and applications in both academic and industrial chemistry.

Nathan's Famous is an American fast food company founded in 1916 by Nathan and Ida Handwerker in Coney Island, Brooklyn, New York. Known for its "kosher-style" all-beef hot dogs, the brand grew from a single stand to an international chain with locations across the United States and abroad. The company is also famous for its annual Fourth of July Hot Dog Eating Contest, held at its original location.

Raquel Torres Cerdán, also Raquel Torres (born 19 November 1948) is a Mexican anthropologist and restauranteur, who has worked to record, preserve and showcase the cuisines of the indigenous peoples of Veracruz, through her restaurants and food writing.

A debug port is a chip-level diagnostic interface (akin to a computer port) included in an integrated circuit to aid design, fabrication, development, bootstrapping, configuration, debugging, and post-sale in-system programming. In general terms, a debug port is not necessary for end-use function and is often hidden or disabled in finished products.

When constituted in a high availability or safety critical system, a debug port may continue to operate as a system monitor for integrity validation in the finished product. In low-cost or high-volume electronics, debug ports are sometimes left enabled to avoid costs of design changes, and can be used by modders and hackers to gain control of the device and/or access to extra functionality.

Due to their high complexity, debug ports are nearly universal in microprocessors (CPUs) and microcontrollers (MCUs). These are discussed below.
Sofya Andreyevna Arzhakovskaya (Lang-ru; born 12 August 1987), known professionally as Sofya Skya (Lang-ru), is a Russian actress, ballet dancer and acting coach.

The Czech units of measurement were traditional systems used in Bohemia, Moravia, Silesia, and Prague before the adoption of the metric system. These included units for length, such as the *stopa* and *loket*, and for area and capacity, like the *měřice* and *korec*.

The Reinhardswald (German pronunciation) is a range of hills up to 472.2 m above sea level (NN) and covering an area of over 200 km2 in the Weser Uplands in the district of Kassel, Hesse (Germany). Of this, 183 km2 are part of the unincorporated area known as Gutsbezirk Reinhardswald.

The area is known for its myths and legends, such as Grimms' Fairy Tales, and the "Sleeping Beauty" castle of Sababurg is internationally known.

The Sportpalast speech (Lang-de) or Total War speech was a speech delivered by German Propaganda Minister Joseph Goebbels at the Berlin Sportpalast to a large, carefully selected audience on 18 February 1943, as the tide of World War II was turning against Nazi Germany and its Axis allies. The speech is particularly notable as Goebbels almost mentions the Holocaust, when he begins saying "Ausrotten" (using the German word for extermination), but quickly changes it to Ausschaltung (i.e. exclusion). This was the same word Heinrich Himmler used on 18 December 1941, when he recorded the outcome of his discussion with Adolf Hitler on the Final Solution, wherein he wrote "als Partisanen auszurotten" ("exterminate them as partisans").

It is considered the most famous of Joseph Goebbels's speeches. The speech was the first public admission by the Nazi leadership that Germany faced serious dangers. Goebbels called for a total war (German: totalen Krieg) to secure victory over the Allies, and exhorted the German people to continue the war even though it would be long and difficult because—as he asserted—both Germany's survival and the survival of a non-Bolshevist Europe were at stake.
The Hong Kong Film Award for Best Action Choreography is an annual Hong Kong industry award presented to a choreographer or a group of choreographers for the best achievement in action choreography.

Iberia is the flag carrier airline of Spain, founded in 1927. It played a key role in connecting Spain with Europe and Latin America, evolving through nationalization, privatization, and a 2010 merger with British Airways to form the International Airlines Group.

Borough Green & Wrotham railway station is located in Borough Green in Kent, England. It is 29 miles 46 chains (47.6 km) down the line from London Victoria. Train services are provided by Southeastern.

MAZON: A Jewish Response to Hunger is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization founded in 1985 and headquartered in Los Angeles, California. Inspired by the Ethiopian famine of 1985 and Jewish traditions of feeding the poor, it was established by Leonard Fein to address hunger through a combination of grantmaking, education, advocacy, and strategic initiatives. The organization encourages donations from celebrations like bar and bat mitzvahs and weddings to support its mission. Led by President and CEO Abby J. Leibman since 2011, MAZON works to provide immediate access to nutritious food while advocating for long-term solutions to end hunger. Rabbi Joel Pitkowsky serves as the current board chair.

Vilnius Girls' Gymnasium was a secondary education institution for girls in Vilnius while it was part of the Russian Empire. It was established in 1860 with the funding from the Office of the Institutions of Empress Maria and was viewed as a tool of Russification by the officials. Therefore, it was not popular and there were only 58 students in 1862. Officials considered reorganizing it into a three-year school. However, gradually the number of students increased and reached 831 in 1912. Most of the students were daughters of the nobility. The school was evacuated to Russia during World War I.
The FC Ingolstadt 04 II is a German association football club from the city of Ingolstadt, Bavaria. It is the reserve team of FC Ingolstadt.
Personal and Vehicular Basic Loads and The Morrow Project Role Playing Expansion is a 1983 role-playing game supplement published by Timeline for The Morrow Project.

The Multi Fibre Arrangement (MFA) governed the world trade in textiles and garments from 1974 through 1994, imposing quotas on the amount developing countries could export to developed countries. Its successor, the Agreement on Textiles and Clothing (ATC), expired on 1 January 2005.
Desiderio Macías Silva (March 31, 1922-February 1995) was a Mexican poet and medical doctor born in Asientos, Aguascalientes. He served as President of the Autonomous University of Aguascalientes.

In mathematics, the Tor functors are the derived functors of the tensor product of modules over a ring. Along with the Ext functor, Tor is one of the central concepts of homological algebra, in which ideas from algebraic topology are used to construct invariants of algebraic structures. The homology of groups, Lie algebras, and associative algebras can all be defined in terms of Tor. The name comes from a relation between the first Tor group Tor1 and the torsion subgroup of an abelian group.

In the special case of abelian groups, Tor was introduced by Eduard Čech (1935) and named by Samuel Eilenberg around 1950. It was first applied to the Künneth theorem and universal coefficient theorem in topology. For modules over any ring, Tor was defined by Henri Cartan and Eilenberg in their 1956 book Homological Algebra.

Immune Therapy Holdings AB or ITH is a Swedish biotechnology R&D holding company headquartered at the Karolinska Institutet and Karolinska University Hospital in Stockholm.

ITH's research is primarily focused on its Tailored Leukapheresis (TLA) treatment for immune mediated inflammatory diseases (IMIDs).

Mild androgen insensitivity syndrome (MAIS) is a condition that results in a mild impairment of the cell's ability to respond to androgens. The degree of impairment is sufficient to impair spermatogenesis and / or the development of secondary sexual characteristics at puberty in males, but does not affect genital differentiation or development. Female genital and sexual development is not significantly affected by the insensitivity to androgens; as such, MAIS is only diagnosed in males. The clinical phenotype associated with MAIS is a normal male habitus with mild spermatogenic defect and / or reduced secondary terminal hair.

MAIS is one of three types of androgen insensitivity syndrome, which is divided into three categories that are differentiated by the degree of genital masculinization: complete androgen insensitivity syndrome (CAIS) is indicated when the external genitalia is phenotypically female, mild androgen insensitivity syndrome (MAIS) is indicated when the external genitalia is phenotypically male, and partial androgen insensitivity syndrome (PAIS) is indicated when the external genitalia is partially, but not fully masculinized.

Androgen insensitivity syndrome is the largest single entity that leads to 46,XY undermasculinization.

Boomplay is a music streaming service launched in August 2015 in Lagos, Nigeria, focusing on African and urban music content. Available on web, Android, iOS, and KaiOS, it has over 70 million monthly active users. The platform introduced premium subscriptions in 2016, offering ad-free listening and offline downloads. Boomplay has partnered with major music labels, including Universal Music Group, Warner Music Group, and Sony Music Group, to distribute extensive catalogs of global and local artists. It has expanded operations across several African countries, including Kenya, Ghana, and Tanzania. In 2021, Boomplay's streaming data was integrated into Billboard charts, further solidifying its influence in the music industry.

*Age of War* is the debut studio album by the Pakistani heavy metal band Black Hour, released on October 25, 2011, through Yourlabel Records in the United States and Afterwind Records in Pakistan. Recorded between 2009 and 2011 at 11/8 Studios in Islamabad, the album features a blend of heavy metal and progressive rock. It gained critical acclaim and sold over 10,000 copies worldwide, establishing the band’s presence in both local and international music scenes. The album includes the single "Crucifix."
Nashipur is a village in Murshidabad district, West Bengal, India, known for the Nashipur Rajbari, Nashipur Akhara, and historical landmarks.

Phussa Buddha is one of the 28 Buddhas venerated in Theravāda, Mahāyāna, and Vajrayāna traditions. Born in Kāsika to King Jayasena and Queen Sirimā, he renounced his royal life after the birth of his son to practice asceticism. He attained enlightenment under the Maha Bodhi tree after six months of practice. Phussa is noted for predicting the future Buddhahood of Gotama Buddha. He lived for 90,000 years and attained parinibbāna at Senārāma monastery.

Lady Mary Whitley (née Lady Mary Ilona Margaret Cambridge; 24 September 1924 – 13 December 1999) was a British aristocrat and a second cousin of Queen Elizabeth II. A great-great-great-granddaughter of King George III, she was closely connected to the royal family, serving as a bridesmaid at several royal weddings, including those of Princess Elizabeth (later Queen Elizabeth II) and the Duke of Kent. Educated at home, she spent her early years near Hyde Park and often played with her royal cousins. During World War II, she volunteered as a nurse in London. In 1951, she married Peter Whitley, with whom she had two children. Lady Mary regularly attended major royal events throughout her life but did not undertake royal duties.

Wang Dan (王旦; 957 or January 958 – October 6, 1017) was a Chinese statesman and scholar who served as a grand councilor during the Song dynasty under Emperor Zhenzong from 1006 to 1017. Born during the Later Zhou dynasty, he passed the imperial examination in 980 and held various administrative and scholarly positions before rising to prominence in the central government. As grand councilor, Wang Dan played a significant role in the development of the civil service examination system and was known for recommending and promoting talented officials, several of whom later became grand councilors themselves. He was posthumously honored with the title Wenzheng (文正).

Myrica cerifera is a small evergreen tree or large shrub native to North and Central America and the Caribbean. Its common names include southern wax myrtle, southern bayberry, candleberry, bayberry tree, and tallow shrub. It sees uses both in the garden and for candlemaking, as well as a medicinal plant.

*Dragon Ball Xenoverse* is an action role-playing fighting game developed by Dimps and published by Bandai Namco Games. Released in February 2015 for multiple platforms, it features 3D battles, character customization, and an original story involving time travel to fix the Dragon Ball universe's timeline. The game received mixed reviews but achieved significant commercial success, shipping over 3 million copies by 2016. A sequel, *Dragon Ball Xenoverse 2*, was released in 2016.
*In Search of Sunrise* is a trance music compilation series launched in 1999, originally mixed by Tiësto. Later installments featured Richard Durand and Markus Schulz as curators.
Roberto Poljak (1932–2019) was an Argentine biophysicist known for determining the first 3D structures of antibodies and antigen-antibody complexes.

Oreophrynella quelchii, commonly known as the Roraima black frog or Roraima bush toad, is a species of toad in the family Bufonidae. This species is restricted to the transboundary summit of Mount Roraima in Venezuela, Guyana and Brazil, and to the nearby Wei-Assipu-tepui on the Brazil–Guyana border. It has been recorded at elevations of 2,300–2,800 m (7,500–9,200 ft) above sea level.

The assassination of Deryan Aktert occurred on October 10, 2016, in Dicle, Diyarbakır Province, Turkey. Aktert, the chairman of Dicle district, was fatally shot by members of the People's Defence Forces, the armed wing of the Kurdistan Workers' Party (PKK).

USS *Romulus* (ARL-22) was a United States Navy landing craft repair ship commissioned in 1944. Originally designated LST-962, she served in the Pacific during World War II, providing repair services in Guam, Saipan, Japan, and Okinawa. Recommissioned for the Korean War, she supported UN forces as a repair facility. Decommissioned in 1960, she was transferred to the Philippine Navy, serving as BRP *Aklan* and later *Kamagong* until 1989.
Infra is the 2010 album by neo-classical composer Max Richter, released on July 19, 2010 on FatCat Records. The album was reissued on April 25, 2014 on Deutsche Grammophon.

Muhammad Said al-Attar (Lang-ar) (26 November 1927 – 20 November 2005) was the acting Prime Minister of Yemen for five months in 1994. During his career, he held various ministerial positions in Yemen and represented his country at the United Nations. From 1974 to 1985, Muhammad Said Al-Attar was Executive Secretary of the United Nations Economic Commission for Western Asia (currently ESCWA).

"Christians, awake, salute the happy morn" is an English Christmas hymn written around 1750, adapted from a poem by John Byrom. It recounts the Nativity story from Luke 2 and is traditionally sung to the tune "Yorkshire," composed by John Wainwright. First published in 1766, the hymn gained popularity in England and America, appearing in major hymnals by the 19th century.

Kettle War infobox

The Kettle War (Lang-nl) was a military confrontation between the troops of the Holy Roman Empire and the Republic of the Seven Netherlands on 8 October 1784. It was named the Kettle War because the only shot fired hit a soup kettle.

Prakash Vir Shastri (30 December 1923 – 23 November 1977) was an Indian politician, scholar, and orator. Serving as an Independent member of both the Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha from 1958 to 1977, he was also a prominent advocate for Hindi as India's national language and a devotee of the Arya Samaj movement. Shastri proposed the Religious Protection Bill in 1960 to safeguard religious minorities. He was Vice-Chancellor of Gurukul Vrindavan and earned recognition for his speeches, later compiled posthumously. Shastri died in a train accident in 1977.
Liu Junwei (born 1 May 1976) is a Chinese football manager and former defender, currently managing Suzhou Dongwu.

The Irving–Williams series refers to the relative stabilities of complexes formed by transition metals. In 1953 Harry Irving and Robert Williams observed that the stability of complexes formed by divalent first-row transition metal ions generally increase across the period to a maximum stability at copper: Mn(II) < Fe(II) < Co(II) < Ni(II) < Cu(II) > Zn(II).

Specifically, the Irving–Williams series refers to the exchange of aqua (H2O) ligands for any other ligand (L) within a metal complex. In other words, the Irving–Williams series is almost exclusively independent of the nature of the incoming ligand, L.

The main application of the series is to empirically suggest an order of stability within first row transition metal complexes (where the transition metal is in oxidation state II).

Another application of the Irving–Williams series is to use it as a correlation "ruler" in comparing the first stability constant for replacement of water in the aqueous ion by a ligand.

Lectionary 34, designated as Codex Monacensis 329, is a 9th-century Greek manuscript of the New Testament, containing lessons from the Gospels. Elegantly written in uncial script, it is housed in the Bavarian State Library.

Terranova is a German electronic music group formed in Berlin, known for their eclectic style blending electronic, hip-hop, and rock influences. The group was founded by Fetisch and Shapemod in the mid-1990s, with their debut release, *Fiasko*, in 1996. They gained recognition with their 1998 DJ mix album *DJ-Kicks* and their debut LP *Close the Door* (1999), featuring collaborations with artists like Cath Coffey and Tricky. Over the years, Terranova released several albums, including *Hitchhiking Nonstop with no Particular Destination* (2002) and *Hotel Amour* (2012), while collaborating with labels like !K7 Records, Ministry of Sound, and Kompakt.
Hakea rostrata is a shrub in the family Proteaceae, native to Victoria, Australia, found in heathlands and heathy woodlands.

Jim Radakovich is an American painter and sculptor associated with the East Village art movement of the 1980s. Known for his Neo-Expressionist and Neo-Surrealist works, his art explores urban dramas, psychological ambiguity, and modern life through vibrant paintings and totemic sculptures. Radakovich gained prominence with solo and group exhibitions in New York and internationally, and his work is part of notable collections. He continues to exhibit in contemporary art shows.

Mesa/Boogie (also known as Mesa Engineering) is an American company in Petaluma, California, that manufactures amplifiers and other accessories for guitars and basses. It has been in operation since 1969.

Mesa was started by Randall Smith as a small repair shop which modified Fender amplifiers, particularly the diminutive Fender Princeton. Smith's modifications gave the small amps much more input gain, making them much louder as well as creating a high-gain, distorted guitar tone. Prominent early customers included Carlos Santana, and Ron Wood and Keith Richards of The Rolling Stones. Exposure from these top players helped to establish Mesa/Boogie's position on the market, and it is frequently referred to as the first manufacturer of boutique amplifiers.

Hope Hibbard (December 18, 1893 – May 12, 1988) was an American zoologist, professor, and advocate for women in science. She earned degrees in zoology from the University of Missouri and Bryn Mawr College, later completing a doctorate of science at the Sorbonne. Hibbard began her academic career at Elmira College before spending 34 years at Oberlin College, where she became the first woman to hold the title of Adelia A. Field Johnston Professor of Zoology and served as department chair. Her research spanned cytology, histology, and marine biology, and she was a trustee at the Woods Hole Marine Biological Laboratory.

A vocal advocate for women in science, Hibbard encouraged women to balance professional and personal lives. She was active in organizations like the AAUW and inspired future generations through her writings and leadership. Oberlin College established the Hope Hibbard Memorial Award in her honor.

Jefferson Joseph DeBlanc (February 15, 1921 – November 22, 2007) was an American World War II Marine Corps fighter pilot and flying ace, credited with shooting down nine Japanese aircraft during two tours of duty in the Pacific at Guadalcanal and Okinawa. On January 31, 1943, despite mechanical problems with his aircraft, he pressed forward with a mission and shot down five Japanese aircraft in minutes before being shot down himself. DeBlanc parachuted to safety and was rescued by native islanders, traded for a sack of rice, and picked up by a Navy seaplane. He received the Medal of Honor for his actions that day. He served in the Marine Corps Reserve until 1972. In that time, he also taught school and completed graduate and doctoral degrees in math, physics and education.

Ranpak is a public company specializing in recyclable packaging and packaging machinery, founded in 1972 by Raymond Q. Armington and George R. Johnson in Painesville, Ohio. The company developed innovative technology to convert kraft paper into cushioning material for shipping. Headquartered in Concord, Ohio, Ranpak serves markets across the Americas, Europe, Asia, and Australasia. It has undergone multiple ownership changes, with Omar Asali leading as CEO since 2019. Recognized for its innovation, Ranpak has been lauded for its sustainable packaging solutions, including being named a top innovator by *Fast Company* in 2021.

Comproportionation is a chemical reaction where two species of the same element in different oxidation states react to form a single species in an intermediate oxidation state, often analyzed using Frost diagrams.

Anthony Di Pietro (born 15 February 1969) is a businessman from Melbourne, Australia. Di Pietro is most known for his current and longest standing chairmanship of Melbourne Victory FC. Alongside sport, Di Pietro is also the Chief Executive Officer and a board member of industry leading fruit & vegetable producers and marketers, Premier Fresh Australia.
James Edgar Atwell (3 June 1946 – 12 December 2020) was an English priest who was Dean of Winchester.

Victory Day over Nazism in World War II (Lang-uk) or Victory Day (Lang-uk) is a national holiday and a day off in Ukraine. It was first celebrated on 9 May 2015 and follows the Day of Remembrance and Reconciliation on May 8 (which pays tribute to the victims of World War II and was also first celebrated in 2015). The holiday replaced the Soviet Union/Russian Federation "Victory Day", which was celebrated in the post-Soviet Union states, including Ukraine, until 2014 inclusive.

William Szathmary (October 5, 1924 – June 15, 2017), known as Bill Dana, was an American comedian, actor, and screenwriter. He often appeared on television shows such as The Ed Sullivan Show, frequently in the guise of a heavily accented Bolivian character named José Jiménez. Dana often portrayed the Jiménez character as an astronaut.

Johann Friedrich Christian Hessel (27 April 1796 – 3 June 1872) was a German physician (MD, University of Würzburg, 1817) and professor of mineralogy (PhD, University of Heidelberg, 1821) at the University of Marburg.

In psychologist Hans Eysenck's P–E–N (psychoticism, extraversion and neuroticism) model of personality, psychoticism is a trait which is typified by aggressiveness and interpersonal hostility. In 2010, a paper titled "The nature of the relationship between personality traits and political attitudes" claimed to find a strong positive correlation between conservatism and psychoticism. This error was repeated in subsequent papers by the same authors; however, around 2015, the authors acknowledged the correlation is actually negative rather than positive, and began issuing corrections.
KNIN-FM (92.9 MHz), branded as "92.9 NIN," is a Top 40 (CHR) radio station serving the Wichita Falls, Texas area. Established in 1975, it is owned by Townsquare Media and operates with 100,000 watts.

Hope Hibbard (December 18, 1893- May 12, 1988) was an American biologist, cytologist, zoologist, and professor of zoology. Born in Altoona, Pennsylvania, she conducted research in the fields of histology and marine biology, utilizing organisms such as silkworms, limpets, earthworms, and frogs. Hibbard dedicated most of her life to education as a professor at multiple institutions, including Bryn Mawr College, Elmira College, and Oberlin College. She received accolades for her research and academic merits, such as the Sarah Berliner Research Fellowship and the Adelia A. Field Johnston Professor of Zoology. Hibbard is not only remembered for her intellectual endeavors, but for her support of women in the scientific sphere and her involvement in associations that forwarded female roles in science and research, such as the American Association of University Women (AAUW).

Entropy estimation refers to the process of approximating the entropy of a probability distribution, which quantifies the uncertainty or randomness in a dataset. Various methods are used for entropy estimation, each with its strengths and limitations. The histogram estimator approximates entropy using histograms of observations but can produce biased results. Sample-spacing methods estimate entropy based on the spacing between ordered data points, though they struggle with multidimensional data. Nearest-neighbor approaches use distances between data points to estimate entropy, while Bayesian estimators, such as the NSB estimator, incorporate prior distributions for improved accuracy in under-sampled scenarios.

Moses Rischin (1925-2020) was an American historian, author, lecturer, editor, and emeritus professor of history at San Francisco State University. He coined the phrase new Mormon history in a 1969 article of the same name.

Rischin is considered an authority on American ethnic and immigration history and a pioneer in the field of American Jewish history. Historian Selma Berrol, however, has challenged the minimal treatment Rischin has given to the tensions between earlier German Jews and later Russian Jews in America.
Cinéaste is an American quarterly film magazine that was established in 1967.

Wang Dan (c. 957 – 6 October 1017), courtesy name Ziming, was a major politician in the Song dynasty, serving as the grand councilor from 1006 until shortly before his death in 1017. Well trusted by Emperor Zhenzong, Wang Dan was given plenipotentiary authority over some matters after 1008.

Wang Dan is generally remembered as a faithful and virtuous official, mainly because he kept a low profile, recruited talented men to the bureaucracy while blocking his notoriously unscrupulous colleague Wang Qinruo from advancing for most of his tenure. But Wang Dan also flattered and encouraged Emperor Zhenzong's excessive and ridiculous Taoist pursuits, for which he expressed regret on his deathbed.

The Apostles'Fast, also called the Fast of the Holy Apostles, the Fast of Peter and Paul, or sometimes St. Peter's Fast, is a fast observed by Eastern Orthodox, Oriental Orthodox, Eastern Catholic, and Reformed Orthodox Christians. In the Byzantine tradition, the Fast begins on the second Monday after Pentecost (the day after All Saints' Sunday), whereas in the Coptic and old Syriac traditions, the Fast begins on the first Monday after Pentecost. It continues until the Feast of Saints Peter and Paul on June 29. Traditionally, its duration varies from eight to forty-two days because of the moveable nature of Pascha. However, in Eastern Orthodox Churches that follow the Revised Julian calendar, the fast can be as long as 29 days, or may not occur at all in some years.

The history of Koreans in Houston traces back to the 1970s, when the community was small and lacked cohesion. Over the decades, the population grew significantly, with estimates reaching 30,000 by the 2000s. Spring Branch became a hub for Korean businesses and institutions, including the Korean Community Center of Houston. The community hosts cultural events like the Korean Festival and maintains organizations, schools, and churches to support its members.
Paul D. Graves (November 23, 1907 – September 7, 1972) was an American lawyer and politician from New York.

Anna Maria Rückerschöld (5 February 1725 – 25 May 1805), born Rücker, was a Swedish author who wrote several popular books on housekeeping and cooking in the late 18th and early 19th century. She was an advocate of women's right to a good education in household matters and propagated this view in public debate through an anonymous letter in 1770. Along with Cajsa Warg and other female cookbook authors, she was an influential figure in culinary matters in early modern Sweden.

The Additional Civil Lord of the Admiralty or formally the Office of the Additional Civil Lord of the Admiralty sometimes called the Department of the Additional Civil Lord of the Admiralty was a member of the Board of Admiralty first from 1882 to 1885 and then again from 1912 to 1919 who was mainly responsible for administration of contracts for matériel for the Fleet, supervision of the contracts and purchase department and general organisation of dockyards within the Admiralty.
Iberia Líneas Aéreas de España, S.A. (Iberia Airlines of Spain in English), usually shortened to Iberia, is the largest airline of Spain, based in Madrid.

A number of locally-specific units of measurement were used in the Czech lands to measure length, area, capacity and so on. In 1876, the metric system was made compulsory; however, local measurements and old Viennese measurements were still in use.

Sir Robert James Buckland KBE KC (born 22 September 1968) is a British politician who served as Secretary of State for Wales from July to October 2022. He previously served as Secretary of State for Justice and Lord Chancellor from 2019 to 2021. A member of the Conservative Party, he has been Member of Parliament (MP) for South Swindon since 2010.

Buckland was Solicitor General for England and Wales from 2014 to 2019 and Minister of State for Prisons from May to July 2019. He was appointed Secretary of State for Justice and Lord Chancellor by Boris Johnson in July 2019, serving until the cabinet reshuffle in September 2021. In July 2022 he was appointed Secretary of State for Wales, a position he held until October of the same year.
General Applied Science Laboratory (GASL), founded in 1956 by Antonio Ferri, specializes in advanced propulsion systems and hypersonic research.

*Kalavari Samsaram* is a 1982 Indian Telugu-language drama film directed by K. S. Rami Reddy and produced by Donepudi Brahmaiah. Starring Krishna and Sridevi, the film follows Vishnu, a man who takes on his family's financial burdens after his father's death, striving to protect their honor.

*Circumstance* (Persian: شرایط) is a 2011 drama film directed by Maryam Keshavarz, exploring themes of youth, rebellion, and forbidden love in contemporary Iran. Shot in Lebanon, the film follows two teenage girls navigating societal restrictions and personal desires. It received critical acclaim, winning the Audience Award at the Sundance Film Festival and other accolades, but was banned in Iran for its portrayal of Iranian society.

Islesboro is a town in Waldo County, Maine, located on Islesboro Island in Penobscot Bay. Incorporated in 1789, it was originally a shipping hub before becoming a resort community with luxurious summer homes. The town covers 68.88 square miles, with 14.29 square miles of land. As of 2020, its population was 583. Islesboro Central School serves students from kindergarten through 12th grade, including tuition-paying mainland students.

The American Federation of Aviculture (AFA) is a nonprofit educational organization founded in 1974 to promote the advancement of aviculture, including the care, breeding, and conservation of exotic birds. Headquartered in Austin, Texas, the AFA was established in response to government actions to euthanize exotic parrots during an outbreak of Exotic Newcastle Disease, advocating for bird owners and clubs. The organization supports aviculture through educational programs, publications like the *AFA Watchbird*, and a certification program on avian care and conservation. It also hosts annual conventions featuring expert speakers and provides grants for avian research and conservation projects. Additionally, the AFA monitors legislation affecting bird ownership and breeding, working to protect the interests of bird enthusiasts and professionals worldwide.

Odd Reidar Humlegård (born 9 May 1961, in Porsgrunn) is a Norwegian lawyer, civil servant and the current National Police Commissioner. Humlegård previously served as head of the National Criminal Investigation Service, and was appointed as director to the National Police Directorate in August 2012 after the controversial resignation of Øystein Mæland.

The **Amritsar–Jamnagar Expressway** is a 1,256.95 km (781.03 mi) long expressway in India, connecting Amritsar in Punjab to Jamnagar in Gujarat. Maintained by the National Highways Authority of India (NHAI), it passes through Punjab, Haryana, Rajasthan, and Gujarat, linking major cities such as Amritsar, Bathinda, Bikaner, Jodhpur, and Jamnagar. The expressway is part of the Bharatmala Phase-I initiative and is funded by the National Investment and Infrastructure Fund (NIIF). It became operational in September 2023.

The expressway is notable for connecting three major oil refineries in Bathinda, Barmer, and Jamnagar, with nearly half of its length (637 km) passing through Rajasthan. The project, valued at approximately ₹80,000 crores, includes both greenfield and brownfield sections, divided into 30 construction packages. Additional upgrades to existing highways will enhance connectivity to ports and cities in Gujarat.

The Manitoba Centre for Health Policy (MCHP), established in 1991 by Drs. Noralou and Leslie Roos, is an academic research institute based at the University of Manitoba in Winnipeg, Canada. It specializes in population health and health care services research, maintaining the Manitoba Population Research Data Repository, a comprehensive de-identified database on health, social, and economic data. MCHP collaborates with government and academic partners, focusing on health determinants, Indigenous well-being, and service provision, while ensuring strict privacy and security protocols.

Section 101 of the *Constitution Act, 1867* grants the federal Parliament of Canada the authority to establish courts "for the better administration of the laws of Canada." This provision, found in Part VII of the Act, has been instrumental in shaping Canada's judicial system. It empowered Parliament to create the Supreme Court of Canada in 1875 as a general court of appeal, with jurisdiction over federal, provincial, civil, and criminal law. Initially, the Supreme Court's decisions could be appealed to the Judicial Committee of the Privy Council in Britain, but this practice ended in 1949.

Section 101 also enabled the creation of federal courts, including the Exchequer Court (1875), which evolved into the Federal Court and Federal Court of Appeal in 1971, and specialized courts like the Tax Court of Canada. While federal courts can adjudicate constitutional issues, the provincial superior courts retain core constitutional jurisdiction under section 96 of the Act.

Common ownership refers to holding the assets of an organization, enterprise or community indivisibly rather than in the names of the individual members or groups of members as common property.

Forms of common ownership exist in every economic system. Common ownership of the means of production is a central goal of communist political movements as it is seen as a necessary democratic mechanism for the creation and continued function of a communist society. Advocates make a distinction between collective ownership and common property as the former refers to property owned jointly by agreement of a set of colleagues, such as producer cooperatives, whereas the latter refers to assets that are completely open for access, such as a public park freely available to everyone.

Braeden Matthew Lemasters (born January 27, 1996) is an American actor, musician, and singer. He is best known for his role as Albert Tranelli in the TNT comedy-drama series Men of a Certain Age. He has made guest appearances in several television series such as, Criminal Minds, ER, House, Grey's Anatomy and Amazon Prime Video's The Romanoffs. He is also the lead guitarist and singer for the American alternative rock band Wallows.

In films, Lemasters has appeared in the thriller film The Stepfather (2009), the romantic comedy film Easy A (2010), the family comedy film A Christmas Story 2 (2012) and the horror film Totem (2017).
Phlebiella is a genus of crust fungi in the order Polyporales.

The 1960 Louisiana gubernatorial election marked a significant turning point in the state's political history. Democrat Jimmie Davis defeated Republican Francis Grevemberg in the general election, which was notable for being the first contested gubernatorial general election in Louisiana since Reconstruction. The campaign was dominated by issues of segregation and race, with Davis adopting segregationist rhetoric to secure victory. This election also signaled the decline of the Long political dynasty, as no candidate backed by Huey or Earl Long advanced to the runoff.

A *Zirkel* is a symbol used by German-speaking student societies (*Studentenverbindungen*), consisting of intertwined letters and often an exclamation mark, signifying the society's name and active status.

Angela Jackson is an American basketball coach and former player from Detroit, Michigan. She played college basketball at Odessa College and Old Dominion University, graduating in 1991 with a degree in Health and Physical Education. Jackson began her coaching career as an assistant at Wayne State University and later served as head coach at Saginaw Valley State and Chicago State University. During her tenure at Chicago State (2003–2018), her team won a conference championship and reached the WBI Final Four in 2010–2011.

Jackson Township is one of the eighteen townships of Richland County, Ohio, United States, covering an area of 24.4 square miles. As of the 2000 census, it had a population of 3,861. Part of the city of Shelby lies within its boundaries. Governed by a three-member board of trustees and an elected fiscal officer, it is one of thirty-seven Jackson Townships in Ohio.

John Armand Lafore Jr. (May 25, 1905 – January 24, 1993) was an American politician who was a Republican member of the U.S. House of Representatives from Pennsylvania. He was also president of the American Kennel Club.

John Graham (born 1954–1957) is a Canadian activist and former member of the American Indian Movement (AIM). He participated in various Indigenous rights protests, including the Native People's Caravan and the Beothuk Patrol. Graham was convicted in 2010 of felony murder for his role in the 1975 killing of AIM activist Anna Mae Aquash, a case that has drawn significant controversy and legal scrutiny. He is currently serving a life sentence in the South Dakota State Penitentiary. Graham has maintained his innocence, and his conviction has been the subject of multiple appeals and ongoing debate among activists and legal experts.

*The Forging of a Rebel* is a semi-autobiographical trilogy by Spanish writer Arturo Barea, originally published in English between 1941 and 1946. The work, comprising *The Forge*, *The Track*, and *The Clash*, chronicles Barea's life from his childhood in Madrid to his experiences in the Rif War and the Spanish Civil War, culminating in his exile to the United Kingdom. The trilogy explores themes of class struggle, political upheaval, and personal transformation, employing a realist style to depict early 20th-century Spanish society. Praised by critics such as George Orwell and Gabriel García Márquez, it was later translated into Spanish and adapted into a 1990 television miniseries by RTVE.

Dan Carpenter (born November 25, 1985) is a former American football placekicker who played nine seasons in the National Football League (NFL). After a standout college career at the University of Montana, where he earned multiple All-Big Sky honors, Carpenter went undrafted in 2008 but signed with the Miami Dolphins, where he earned Pro Bowl honors in 2009. He later played for the Buffalo Bills, Arizona Cardinals, and New York Jets, achieving notable success with the Bills, including multiple game-winning field goals and AFC Special Teams Player of the Week awards. Carpenter retired with 236 career field goals made.

The eCRM or electronic customer relationship management coined by Oscar Gomes encompasses all standard CRM functions with the use of the net environment i.e., intranet, extranet and internet. Electronic CRM concerns all forms of managing relationships with customers through the use of information technology (IT).

eCRM processes include data collection, data aggregation, and customer interaction. Compared to traditional CRM, the integrated information for eCRM intraorganizational collaboration can be more efficient to communicate with customers.

*Slaughter Studios* is a 2002 American horror-comedy film directed by Brian Katkin and written by Dan Acre, John Huckert, and Damian Akhavi. The film follows a group of film students who break into an abandoned movie studio, infamous for a tragic on-set death decades earlier, to shoot a low-budget horror film before the building is demolished. As the students race against time to complete their project, they are stalked by a mysterious killer, leading to a series of gruesome murders.

The film blends slasher tropes with dark humor, featuring an ensemble cast that includes Nicolas Read, Amy Shelton-White, Tara Killian, and Darren Keefe. The story culminates in a twist revealing the killer's identity and a supernatural connection to the studio's haunted past. An alternate ending, which was ultimately unused, would have framed the events as part of a "film within a film."

*Slaughter Studios* was distributed by New Concorde and released on December 31, 2002. It runs for 86 minutes and is noted for its self-referential humor and homage to classic horror films. The movie explores themes of ambition, exploitation, and the blurred lines between reality and fiction in the filmmaking process.
Karl Hermann Heinrich Benda (also Carl Benda), (baptised 2 May 1748 – 15 March 1836) was a German violinist and composer of Bohemian origin.

Arnold V, Count of Loon (died August 22, 1327), was a medieval nobleman who played a significant role in regional conflicts, including the Battle of Worringen (1288) and the War of Limburg Succession. He governed the County of Loon and inherited the County of Chiny in 1299. Arnold participated in the Peace of Fexhe (1316), which formalized power-sharing in the Principality of Liège. Toward the end of his reign, he gradually relinquished his territories, selling Chiny to his son Louis in 1313 and Loon in 1323. Arnold married Marguerite of Vianden in 1280 and was buried at Averbode Abbey.

The 2009 FIA GT3 European Championship season was the fourth season of the FIA GT3 European Championship. The season began on 2 May at Silverstone and ended on 14 November 2009 at Zolder. The season featured six double-header rounds, with each race lasting for a duration of 60 minutes. Most of the events were support races to the 2009 FIA GT Championship season, the 2009 GT4 European Cup and the 2009 Lamborghini Super Trofeo.

Phoenix Racing pairing Christopher Haase and Christopher Mies claimed the overall title, with a fourth place in the final race of the season at Zolder. In the Teams Championship, Hexis Racing were champions ahead of Matech Racing and Team Rosberg.

Tulane University Law School is the law school of Tulane University. It is located on Tulane's Uptown campus in New Orleans, Louisiana. Established in 1847, it is the 12th oldest law school in the United States.

In addition to the usual common law and federal subjects, Tulane offers electives in the civil law, giving students the opportunity to pursue comparative education of the world's two major legal systems (Louisiana is the only state to have a civil law system, rather than common law). Students are permitted to survey a broad range of subject areas or to concentrate in one or more.

Tulane Law School's environmental law and sports law programs are considered among the strongest nationwide, and its maritime law program is among the most well-regarded in the world. For more than 20 years, the school has hosted the Tulane Corporate Law Institute, a preeminent mergers and acquisitions (M&A) and corporate law forum.

The Faculty of Informatics and Statistics (FIS VŠE), also known as the School of Informatics and Statistics, is the fourth of six faculties at Prague University of Economics and Business. The faculty was established in 1991, following the dissolution of the Faculty of Direction. Its academic focus is informatics, statistics, econometrics and other mathematical methods applied to business practice. The faculty has eight departments and several research laboratories, and hosts around 2,500 students across its programs.

Lloyd Quinan (born 1957) is a Scottish actor, television presenter, director, and former politician. He served as a Member of the Scottish Parliament (MSP) for the West of Scotland region from 1999 to 2003 as a member of the Scottish National Party (SNP). Before his political career, Quinan worked extensively in theatre and television, including as a presenter for STV. An active campaigner, he was involved in anti-nuclear protests and supported Scottish independence.

Dick Guindon (December 2, 1935 – February 27, 2022) was an American cartoonist best known for his self-titled cartoon series, *Guindon*. His work appeared in publications like *The Nation*, *Playboy*, and *The Realist*.

The crested servaline genet (Genetta cristata), also known as the crested genet, is a genet species endemic to Nigeria and Cameroon. As the population has declined due to loss of habitat, it is listed as Vulnerable on the IUCN Red List. It was first recorded in the Mamfe Division in Cameroon and initially considered a subspecies of the servaline genet (Genetta servalina). But now it is regarded as a distinct species.

Barry Ono (1876–1941) was a British variety theatre performer, collector of penny dreadfuls and benefactor of the British Library. His collection of songbooks was bequeathed to the British Library in 1941.

Ports North, officially known as the Far North Queensland Ports Corporation Limited, is a statutory corporation responsible for managing the ports in Far North Queensland, Australia. Headquartered in Cairns, Queensland, it oversees key facilities including the Cairns Seaport, Cairns Marlin Marina, and the Cityport urban renewal project. The Cairns Seaport, located on Trinity Inlet, is a vital hub for cargo and passenger vessels, contributing significantly to the region's tourism economy as one of Australia's busiest cruise ship destinations. It handles over 1.16 million tonnes of cargo annually, supporting exports like sugar and molasses and imports such as petroleum and fertiliser. The Cairns Marlin Marina, near the entrance to Trinity Inlet, features 264 berths and serves private yachts, catamarans, and commercial tourism operators offering Great Barrier Reef tours. The Cityport project focuses on urban redevelopment near the seaport, enhancing the southern end of the Cairns central business district.
Linda Gottlieb is an American television writer and film producer.

HMS *Ardent* was a Royal Navy torpedo boat destroyer launched in 1894 and built by Thornycroft in Chiswick. Designed as part of the Ardent-class, she served primarily in the Mediterranean, performing duties such as training stokers and participating in fleet exercises. *Ardent* achieved notable speeds during trials, exceeding 27 knots. After a collision in 1906 and subsequent service, she was placed in reserve in 1911 and sold for breaking later that year.

The SNCF Class BB 1800, originally designated as Z 5000, was a series of early electric locomotives introduced in 1901 for France's first electrified rail services. Featuring a wooden body with steel framing, they operated on 600 V DC third rail and were later modified with updated motors and electrical equipment. Renumbered over time, they served in passenger and shunting roles until their withdrawal in 1956.
Laura Rajnović Evetović (Serbian Cyrillic: Лаура Рајновић Еветовић; born 1 June 1975) is a politician and administrator in Serbia. She served in the Assembly of Vojvodina from 2012 to 2020.

Gayopakhyanam (Telugu: గయోపాఖ్యానం) is a Telugu play written by Chilakamarti Lakshmi Narasimham in 1890. It is also called Prachanda Yadavam (The story of fierce Yadava King - Sri Krishna). This play is the forerunner in presenting mythical themes in the Hindu epics. The plot is based on war between Nara-Narayana, the incarnation of Arjuna and Sri Krishna induced by Gaya, a Gandharva King.

Lu Rongjing (born August 1933) is a Chinese politician who held several prominent positions in Anhui province. He served as the Communist Party Secretary of Anhui, the province's top political position, from 1988 to 1996, and as Governor of Anhui from 1988 to 1989. He later became Chairman of the Anhui Provincial Committee of the Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference from 1996 to 2000. A member of the Chinese Communist Party since 1954, Lu held various leadership roles throughout his career, including in industrial and economic administration, before rising to provincial leadership.
Abdul Qadeer Baloch (Lang-ur), better known as Mama Qadeer (Lang-ur) is a human rights activist from the Balochistan province of Pakistan.

Ultraman Powered is a fighting game developed by Tose and published by Bandai for the 3DO Interactive Multiplayer. The player takes on the role of the extraterrestrial superhero Ultraman, tasked with protecting the Earth from destructive aliens and monsters. Gameplay primarily consists of one-on-one battles where the player must deplete an adversary's health meter using both basic and special fighting techniques. The game also contains 3D rail shooter sections and a two-player versus option.

The game is based on the 1993 television series Ultraman: The Ultimate Hero, known in Japan as Ultraman Powered. The game was created using digitized backgrounds and character sprites from this series and uses clips from it as full-motion video (FMV) cutscenes. Ultraman Powered was released exclusively in Japan on March 20, 1994 as a launch title for the 3DO in that region. Global critical reception for the game has been mixed with many reviewers complimenting its graphics but panning its slow gameplay and controls.

Paterson is a New Jersey Transit commuter railway station located on an elevated viaduct above Market Street in downtown Paterson, New Jersey. The railway through the station is double tracked, for north and south traffic on the NJT Main Line.

The White Obelisk is a large stone monolith found at the ancient Assyrian settlement of Nineveh, northern Iraq. Excavated by the British archaeologist Hormuzd Rassam in 1853, it is one of only two intact obelisks to survive from the Assyrian empire, the other being the Black Obelisk of Shalmaneser III. Both are now preserved in the British Museum. The White Obelisk dates to the beginning of the Neo-Assyrian Empire and has been variously ascribed to the reigns of Ashurnasirpal I (c. 1040 BC), Tiglath-Pileser II (c. 950 BC) or Ashurnasirpal II (c. 870 BC).

"Love Song" is a single by Japanese rock band Luna Sea, released on November 8, 2000. Written primarily by Sugizo, the song features an acoustic foundation with a band arrangement and guitar solo. Released during the *Lunacy* recording period but excluded from the album, its release coincided with the band's announcement of their disbandment. The single includes two B-sides, "Into the Sun" and "Until the Day I Die," and its music video was directed by Shūichi Tan.

John Bullock was a 15th-century Scottish cleric who served as the Bishop of Ross from 1418 until his death between 1439 and 1440. A canon lawyer and former vicar, he was appointed bishop by Pope Benedict XIII during the Western Schism. His episcopate is largely obscure, though he played a role in a 1439 contract involving the guardianship of King James II of Scotland.

Joint Assistant for Development and Execution (JADE) is a U.S. military system used for planning the deployment of military forces in crisis situations.

The U.S. military developed this automated planning software system in order to expedite the creation of the detailed planning needed to deploy military forces for a military operation. JADE uses Artificial Intelligence (AI) technology combining user input, a knowledge base of stored plans, and suggestions by the system to provide the ability to develop large-scale and complex plans in minimal time.

"Maximum Carnage" is a 14-part comic book crossover published in Marvel Comics'Spider-Man family of titles in 1993. It featured Spider-Man, Venom, and a host of other superheroes teaming up to face Venom's murderous offspring Carnage and his team of supervillains.

Madden NFL 25 is an American football sports video game based on the National Football League and published by EA Sports. It was released for PlayStation 3 and Xbox 360 in August 2013. Instead of numbering it Madden NFL 14 with the year like in previous versions, the "25" in the title refers to the 25th anniversary of the Madden NFL series. The eighth-generation console versions of Madden NFL 25 are the very first games to run on EA Sports's Ignite game engine. However, the seventh generation versions still run on EA's previous game engine, Impact. The seventh-generation versions featured former Detroit Lions running back Barry Sanders on the cover, while the eighth-generation versions featured Minnesota Vikings running back Adrian Peterson, and were released as launch titles for the PlayStation 4 and Xbox One in November 2013.

Robert Henry Doolan (1917–2022) was an American World War II veteran and former prisoner of war. A navigator in the U.S. Army Air Corps, he flew 12 combat missions before being shot down on his 13th mission in 1943. After evading capture for 21 days with the help of the Dutch resistance, he was apprehended by the Gestapo and spent two years in the Stalag Luft III prison camp, famously depicted in *The Great Escape*. Liberated in 1945, Doolan returned to the United States, where he pursued a career in civil engineering. He lived to the age of 105.

Tariq Fancy (born 1978) is a Canadian entrepreneur and former Chief Investment Officer (CIO) of sustainable investing at BlackRock. He is best known as the founder of The Rumie Initiative, a nonprofit organization focused on providing free digital education to underserved communities. Fancy has gained significant attention for his outspoken criticism of environmental, social, and governance (ESG) investing practices in the financial industry. 

In a widely discussed op-ed and subsequent articles, Fancy accused the financial services industry of "greenwashing" by promoting sustainable investing as a public relations strategy rather than a meaningful solution to climate change. He argued that ESG investing lacks evidence of social impact and serves as a distraction from addressing critical environmental issues. Fancy expanded on these claims in a three-part series published on Medium, where he detailed his experiences and interactions within the sustainable investment sector during his tenure at BlackRock. His critiques have sparked debate about the effectiveness and integrity of ESG practices in the financial world.

Susan Sonnenschein is a Professor of Psychology at the University of Maryland, Baltimore County (UMBC), where she directs the Children and Families, Schooling and Development Lab. She has served as Graduate Program Director of the Applied Developmental Psychology doctoral program since 2006. Sonnenschein's research examines ways to promote the academic success of children. Her research explores the influence of both home and school, but with a focus on the associations between parental beliefs, practices, and children's academic development. She has edited and contributed to several books and is the author of over 70 peer-reviewed articles focusing on educational contexts of development for diverse populations.

The Universal Time-Sharing System (UTS) was an operating system developed by Xerox Data Systems for the Sigma series of computers, first released in 1966. Written in assembly language, it featured a command-line interface and supported time-sharing and batch processing. UTS was succeeded by CP-V in 1973, which expanded its capabilities to include real-time and transaction processing. CP-V and its derivatives, such as CP-6, were used on Xerox and later Honeywell systems, with CP-6 being developed to transition users to Honeywell equipment after Xerox exited the computer business in 1975. CP-6 systems remained in use until 2005 and are commemorated at the Computer History Museum.
Liu Junwei (Chinese: 刘俊威; born 1 May 1976 in Shenyang) is a Chinese football manager and former football player.

Paleontology in New Hampshire refers to paleontological research occurring within or conducted by people from the U.S. state of New Hampshire. Fossils are very rare in New Hampshire because so much of the state's geology is highly metamorphic. The state's complicated geologic history has made dating its rocks and the fossils they contain "a difficult task." The state's Devonian rocks are especially metamorphosed, yet its Mississippian rocks formed too recently to have been subject to the same metamorphism. Nevertheless, despite the geologic complications some fossils have been discovered in the state.

During the early Paleozoic, New Hampshire was covered by a warm, shallow sea that would come to be inhabited by creatures like brachiopods, bryozoans, corals, crinoids, and trilobites. Geologic forces raised the local elevation during the Devonian, and local sediments were eroded away from the state rather than deposited, leading to a gap in the local rock record. Erosion continued through the Mesozoic and the early to mid Cenozoic, leaving few rocks of that age, and no fossils. By the Ice Age, the state was being worked over by glaciers and had similar wildlife to the modern Canadian Arctic.

New Hampshire fossils had attracted scientific attention by the late 1800s when C. H. Hitchcock reported local invertebrate remains preserved near Littleton. Other notable finds include fossils preserved in metamorphic rock that are still recognizable despite the extreme geologic forces exerted on these rocks.

The Jackson Plan or Raffles Town Plan, an urban plan of 1822 titled "Plan of the Town of Singapore", is a proposed scheme for Singapore drawn up to maintain some order in the urban development of the fledgling but thriving colony founded just three years earlier. It was named after Lieutenant Philip Jackson, the colony's engineer and land surveyor tasked to oversee its physical development in accordance with the vision of Stamford Raffles for Singapore, hence it is also commonly called Raffles Town Plan. Raffles gave his instructions in November 1822, the plan was then drawn up in late 1822 or early 1823 and published in 1828. It is the earliest extant plan for the town of Singapore, but not an actual street map of Singapore as it existed in 1822 or 1827 since the plan is an idealised scheme of how Singapore may be organised that was not fully realised. Nevertheless, it served as a guide for the development of Singapore in its early days, and the effect of the general layout of the plan is still observable to this day.

The plan is currently on display in the Singapore History Gallery at the National Museum of Singapore.

Society for the Rise of Kurdistan (Lang-ku) also known as the Society for the Advancement of Kurdistan (SAK), was secretly established in Constantinople on 6 November 1917 and officially announced organization formed on the 17 December 1918. It was headquartered in Istanbul, with the aim of creating an independent Kurdish state in eastern Turkey. The Society based its statements for an independent or autonomous Kurdistan on the Treaty of Sèvres and the Fourteen Points stipulated by Woodrow Wilson. The society formed many local dependencies in the eastern provinces of Turkey.

Cary Castle was a motte-and-bailey castle in Castle Cary, Somerset, England, built in the 11th or 12th century. It played a role during The Anarchy, changing hands multiple times before being abandoned by 1468. Excavations in 1890 revealed the foundations of a stone keep and baileys, though only earthworks remain today.

Melukote, also known as Yadavagiri, is a historic town and pilgrimage center in the Mandya district of Karnataka, India. Situated 51 km (32 miles) from Mysore, it is renowned for its religious, cultural, and architectural significance. The town is home to the Cheluva-Narayana Swamy Temple, a prominent Vaishnavite shrine associated with the 12th-century saint Ramanujacharya, who resided here for 14 years, establishing it as a center of Srivaishnavism. The temple houses the Vairamudi crown, central to the annual Vairamudi Brahmotsava festival, which attracts thousands of devotees.

Melukote also features the Yoga-Narasimha Swamy Temple atop a hill, the Kalyani pond, and several other shrines. The town is known for its contributions to Sanskrit scholarship, with institutions like the Academy of Sanskrit Research and the Sri Veda Vedantha Bodhini Sanskrit College. Additionally, Melukote hosts the Melkote Temple Wildlife Sanctuary, preserving local flora and fauna, including wolves and blackbucks.
The Downs is a 412-acre public open space in Bristol, England, comprising Durdham Down and Clifton Down. Managed jointly since 1861, it is used for leisure, sports, events, and sightseeing.

The Armenia Marriott Hotel Yerevan is a luxury hotel located in Yerevan, Armenia, overlooking Republic Square. Originally opened in 1958 as the Hotel Armenia, it was the largest hotel in the Armenian SSR and was operated by the Soviet Intourist agency. Designed by architects Mark Grigorian and Eduard Sarapyan, the building was constructed with pink Armenian tufa and basalt stone. Following Armenia's economic crisis in the 1990s, the hotel was acquired by AK Development in 1998 and underwent a $40 million renovation before reopening in 1999 under the Marriott Hotels & Resorts brand.

The hotel features 144 rooms, 23 suites, four restaurants, 10 conference halls, a fitness center, and an outdoor pool.

Tobin David Stokes (born 1966) is a Canadian composer and theatre creator, notable for his work in opera, theatre, choral music and television. His works have been performed by New York City Opera, Long Beach Opera, City Opera of Vancouver, the Moscow Symphony Orchestra, and the Victoria Symphony. He also composes for television, including scores for documentaries by the BBC, ABC, and PBS.

Manitoba Centre for Health Policy (MCHP) is a public, not-for-profit research unit in the Department of Community Health Sciences within the Rady Faculty of Health Sciences at the University of Manitoba (Winnipeg, Manitoba, Canada). MCHP provides and uses population-based administrative data to inform health policy, social policy and health and social service delivery provincially, nationally and internationally. MCHP also creates population-based research on health and the social determinants of health.

Amritsar–Jamnagar Expressway (NH-754) is an under-construction 1,257 km long, 4/6-lane wide expressway in the north-western part of India. The expressway will reduce the distance and time travel between Amritsar and Jamnagar from earlier 1,430 km to 1,316 km (including Kapurthala-Amritsar section) and the time travel from 26 hours to only 13 hours. It is a part of the Bharatmala and Amritsar–Jamnagar Economic Corridor (EC-3). It will pass through four states of Punjab, Haryana, Rajasthan and Gujarat.

The expressway is strategically important, as it will connect 3 big oil refineries of HMEL Bathinda, HPCL Barmer and RIL Jamnagar. It will also connect Guru Nanak Dev Thermal Plant (Bathinda) and Suratgarh Super Thermal Power Plant (Sri Ganganagar).

The expressway will meet the Ludhiana-Bathinda-Ajmer Expressway of the Pathankot–Ajmer Economic Corridor at Bathinda. The construction work on the expressway started in Haryana and Rajasthan in 2019, and it is expected to be completed by September 2023.

St. Mary's High School, located in Belagavi, Karnataka, India, is a coeducational institution established in 1851. Originally founded for the children of British soldiers, it later served Anglo-Indian and Indian students. The school offers education from LKG to grade 10 and emphasizes academics, extracurricular activities, and sports. It features a house system, student organizations, and scholarships, fostering holistic development.
The Troy Slapshots were a professional ice hockey team based in Troy, New York. They were a member of the Atlantic Coast Hockey League in the 1985–86 season.

L.A. Law: The Movie is a 2002 American made-for-television drama film based on the 1986–1994 television series L.A. Law which reunited most of the original cast, although not all prominent cast members returned, including Blair Underwood (Jonathan Rollins), Jimmy Smits (Victor Sifuentes), Amanda Donohoe (C.J. Lamb) and John Spencer (Tommy Mullaney). The film aired on NBC on May 12, 2002.

The film's initial working title was L.A. Law: Return To Justice.

Helen Tufts Bailie (January 9, 1874 – May 1962) was a social reformer and activist. Tufts is known as outing the Daughters of the Revolution for having a blacklist about individuals and organizations, in 1928. This controversy led Tufts to be banned from the organization and to become an advocate for women's, labor, and social rights.

A diatreme is a volcanic pipe formed by a gaseous explosion, often associated with maar-diatreme volcanoes, which are among the most common types of volcanoes on continents and islands. Diatremes form when igneous intrusions interact with groundwater, a specific geological setting that is not universally present. Notable examples include the Blackfoot and Cross diatremes in British Columbia, Canada.

Diatremes hold economic importance due to their potential association with kimberlite magmas, which originate in the Earth's upper mantle. Kimberlite diatremes can transport diamonds, formed at depths of 150–200 kilometers, to the surface as xenoliths, making them valuable for diamond mining. This connection to diamond deposits has made diatremes significant in the study of mineral resources.

The Turgeon River is a 216.6 km (134.6 mi) long tributary of the Harricana River, flowing through Quebec and Ontario, Canada. Originating from Lake Turgeon in Eeyou Istchee Baie-James, it traverses forests, historical settlements, and interprovincial boundaries. Historically inhabited by Algonquins, the region saw settlement during the 1930s economic crisis. The river is notable for its covered bridges and its name, officially designated in 1968, honors Adélard Turgeon, a former Minister of Lands and Forests.

The Tihvinskaya water system was one of the waterways connecting the Volga River with the Baltic Sea, and specifically the Mologa River with Syas River. In terms of the current administrative division of Russia, the waterway belongs to Vologda and Leningrad Oblasts.

It was first conceived by Peter the Great, but construction started only in 1802. The Tihvinskaya water system was built for the passage of middle-sized ships. This Tikhvin system functioned until the middle of the 20th century, when shipping along the Svir River and the construction of railways had increased competition which led to its closure.

Martyrdom in Judaism is one of the main examples of Jews doing a kiddush Hashem, a Hebrew term which means "sanctification of [the] name". An example of this is public self-sacrifice in accordance with Jewish practice and identity, with the possibility of being killed for no other reason than being Jewish. There are specific conditions in Jewish law that deal with the details of self-sacrifice, be it willing or unwilling.

The opposite or converse of kiddush Hashem is chillul Hashem ("Desecration [of] God's Name" in Hebrew) and Jews are obligated to avoid it according to Halakha (Jewish religious law). There are instances, such as when they are faced with forced conversion to another religion, when Jews should choose martyrdom and sacrifice their lives rather than commit a chillul Hashem which desecrates the honor of God. Martyrdom in Judaism is thus driven by both the desire to Sanctify God's Name concurrently and the wish to avoid the Desecration of God's Name.

In Hebrew a martyr is known as a kaddosh which means "[a] holy [one]", and martyrs are known as kedoshim meaning "holy [ones]". Thus the six million Jews who were murdered in the Holocaust are known as the Kedoshim.

Jewish history is replete with many episodes in which Jews who lived in different times and places chose to become individual and mass martyrs.
Joachim Schmettau (born February 5, 1937) is a German sculptor known for public artworks.
Eric Records is a music label founded in 1968 by Bill Buster in Philadelphia, specializing in re-releasing original versions of popular songs, later transitioning to compact discs in 1996.

HIV is recognized as a health concern in Pakistan with the number of cases growing. Moderately high drug use and lack of acceptance that non-marital sex is common in the society have allowed the HIV epidemic to take hold in Pakistan, mainly among injecting drug users (IDU), male, female and transvestite sex workers (MSW, FSW and TSW) as well as the repatriated migrant workers. HIV infection can lead to AIDS that may become a major health issue.

The National AIDS Programme's latest figures show that by the mid-2000s, the number of HIV cases had increased to approximately 0,102 million. This number was estimated little over 4,000, as the HIV cases reported since 1986. The UN and government estimated the number of HIV/AIDS cases around 97,000 ranging from a lowest estimate of 46,000 to a highest estimate of 210,000.

More realistic estimates that are based on actual surveillance figures, however, suggest that this number may be closer to 40,000–45,000. The overall prevalence of HIV infection in adults aged 15 to 49 is 0.1%. (0.05% if one accepts the lower estimates). Officials say that the majority of cases go unreported due to social taboos about sex and victims’ fears of discrimination. The other reason for all the cases not being reported is that HIV is a disease either not systematically tested for or reported in the routine surveillance system.

"Motiv8" is a song by J. Cole from his 2018 album *KOD*. A hip hop track, it samples Crime Mob's "Knuck If You Buck" and Junior M.A.F.I.A.'s "Get Money." The song explores themes of financial motivation and received critical praise for its production and lyrical content. It was featured in NBA Playoffs promotions and debuted at number 15 on the US Billboard Hot 100.

Design studies can refer to any design-oriented studies but more formally is an academic discipline or field of study that pursues, through both theoretical and practical modes of inquiry, a critical understanding of design practice and its effects in society.

Kodiak is the second level leadership development course for Venturers in the Boy Scouts of America's Venturing program.

Introduction to Leadership Skills for Crews is recommended but not required. Kodiak is the second course in the overall Venturing youth leadership development program called Nature of Leadership. When it was first being developed, Kodiak was being offered as Nature of Leadership Treks.

This training is one of several programs available within the youth leadership training program. Participants must complete unit-level training, National Youth Leadership Training or a special bridge course that has not yet been defined. This training is an element of the overall leadership training program.

David Lodge (born January 28, 1935) is a British author, literary critic, and playwright, known for his satirical novels, particularly those exploring academia and Catholicism. Born in Brockley, London, Lodge studied at University College London and later earned a PhD from the University of Birmingham, where he became a professor of English literature. His works often draw on his personal experiences, including his Catholic upbringing, military service, and academic career. Notable novels include *Changing Places*, *Small World*, and *Nice Work*, which form his acclaimed "Campus Trilogy." Lodge has received numerous accolades, including the Hawthornden Prize and a CBE for services to literature. He continues to write and resides in Birmingham.

Immune Therapy Holdings is a privately held Swedish biotechnology company founded in 2005 and headquartered in Stockholm. Specializing in immunotherapy, the company developed Tailored Leukapheresis (TLA), an innovative apheresis treatment targeting pro-inflammatory cells in immune-mediated inflammatory diseases (IMIDs). TLA has demonstrated efficacy in clinical trials and received multiple awards, including the Athena Prize and Universal Biotech Innovation Prize.

Maria Clara Eimmart (1676–1707) was a German astronomer and artist known for her detailed astronomical illustrations, including lunar phases and solar eclipses. Trained by her father, Georg Christoph Eimmart, she contributed significantly to early astronomical observations and depictions.

Mộng-Lan is a Vietnamese-born American writer, visual artist, musician, dancer, and educator. Former Stegner Fellow at Stanford University, Fulbright Scholar, she has published seven books of poetry & artwork, three chapbooks, has won numerous prizes such as the Juniper Prize and the Pushcart Prize. Poems have been included in international and national anthologies such as Best American Poetry Anthology and several Norton anthologies. Her books include: Song of the Cicadas (Juniper Prize Winner); Why is the Edge Always Windy?; Tango, Tangoing: poems & art; One Thousand Minds Brimming, 2016; and Dusk Aflame: poems & art, 2018. Her latest music album releases include Arrabal de Tango: Tango por Siempre, voice & guitar, 2020; Perfumas de Amor, de Argentina y Viet Nam, (Tango por Siempre) (voice and guitar), 2018; New Orleans of My Heart, jazz piano, 2019; Dreaming Orchid: Poetry & Jazz Piano, 2016. www.monglan.com

*Youngblood* is the third studio album by Australian pop band 5 Seconds of Summer, released on June 15, 2018, through Capitol Records. The album marked a significant stylistic shift for the band, embracing a polished pop sound with influences from 1980s music, moving away from their earlier pop-punk roots. Produced by a team including Andrew Watt, Louis Bell, and Mike Elizondo, the album was recorded primarily in Los Angeles and features singles such as "Want You Back," "Youngblood," "Valentine," and "Lie to Me." The title track, "Youngblood," achieved global success, becoming the most-streamed Australian song of all time and earning multiple platinum certifications worldwide.

The album received generally positive reviews, with critics praising its mature sound and strong songwriting, though some noted the loss of the band's earlier edge. *Youngblood* debuted at number one in Australia and the United States, making 5 Seconds of Summer the first Australian act to achieve three consecutive number-one albums in the U.S. The album was supported by the 5SOS III promotional tour and the Meet You There Tour, both of which received critical acclaim. *Youngblood* was a commercial success, earning accolades such as the ARIA Award for Best Group and recognition as one of the best albums of 2018 by *Alternative Press*.

The New South Wales State Heritage Register is an official list of heritage sites in New South Wales, Australia, encompassing both natural and cultural heritage. Established on 2 April 1999, the register is maintained by Heritage NSW and the Heritage Council of New South Wales, a statutory body appointed by the state government. The Heritage Council, which includes representatives from the community, government, conservation professionals, and organizations like the National Trust of Australia, advises the Minister for Environment and Heritage and recommends places and objects for inclusion on the register.

The register operates under the provisions of the Heritage Act 1977 and its 2010 amendments, which require government agencies to compile and manage a register of heritage assets under their care. Aboriginal heritage is also protected under the National Parks and Wildlife Act 1974. Items nominated for the State Heritage Register are assessed against specific criteria to determine their significance, with eligibility requiring that they meet at least one of these criteria. The register aims to ensure the preservation and protection of sites and objects deemed significant to the people of New South Wales. Elizabeth Farm was the first item inscribed on the register.

*Dick Tracy vs. Crime, Inc.* is a 1941 Republic Pictures film serial directed by William Witney and John English. Starring Ralph Byrd as the titular detective, the serial follows Dick Tracy as he battles a villain known as The Ghost, who possesses the ability to become invisible. Praised for its action and suspense, it is considered one of the best Dick Tracy serials. It was re-released in 1952 and later on DVD.

Jerry Collins (4 November 1980 – 5 June 2015) was a Samoan – New Zealand rugby union player. He played for New Zealand, for whom he was capped 48 times, as well as for the Wellington Hurricanes in New Zealand, Toulon and Narbonne in the Rugby Pro D2, Ospreys in the UK, and Yamaha Júbilo in Japan. He played as a flanker and number eight, and was considered to be one of the hardest tacklers in rugby history, along with Brian Lima.

Collins and his partner, Alana Madill, were killed in a car accident in southern France in June 2015.

"Anything But Conservative" (ABC) refers to a series of strategic voting campaigns in Canada aimed at preventing the election of Conservative Party candidates. The term gained prominence during the 2008 federal election, when Newfoundland and Labrador Premier Danny Williams launched the ABC campaign in response to what he viewed as a broken promise by Prime Minister Stephen Harper regarding equalization payments. The campaign successfully shut out federal Conservative candidates in Newfoundland and Labrador, though it had limited impact nationally.
