The ABC strategy has since been associated with grassroots efforts to encourage voters to support the most viable non-Conservative candidate in their ridings. Similar campaigns emerged in subsequent elections, including the 2015 federal election, where strategic voting contributed to a Liberal majority government. The movement has been supported by unions and advocacy groups, though it has also faced criticism for its impact on smaller progressive parties like the NDP.

The Taguig Integrated Terminal Exchange (TITX), (IPA-tl) originally conceptualized as the South Integrated Transport System (ITS-South), is a proposed intermodal transport terminal in Arca South, Taguig. It is primarily a bus terminal for buses going to and from southern Luzon, Visayas, and Mindanao but connected to the FTI station and the under-construction Metro Manila Subway. Originally scheduled for completion in 2020, as of July 2022, construction has yet to begin as pre-construction activities were temporarily suspended due to the ongoing COVID-19 pandemic.

John Bullock O.S.A. (d. 1439 × 1440) was an Augustinian canon and prelate active in the 15th century Kingdom of Scotland. While earning a university degree between 1409 and 1417, Bullock gained several benefices in Scotland, and claimed the headship of St Andrews Cathedral Priory before becoming Bishop of Ross in 1418. He held the latter position until his death, which occurred in either 1439 or 1440.

Save Our Waterways Now (SOWN) is an Australian environmental organization focused on habitat restoration and waterway management in the Enoggera catchment area of Brisbane, Queensland. Established in 1994 by Brisbane City Council Alderman Brian Hallinan as a project of Men of the Trees, SOWN brought together environmental activists and local residents to restore native vegetation and improve waterways. The organization received funding from the National Heritage Trust, enabling it to expand its efforts.

By the late 1990s, SOWN had become a significant environmental group, with numerous restoration sites and hundreds of active volunteers contributing thousands of hours to planting native trees and grasses. In 2007, SOWN merged with the Brisbane Rainforest Action and Information Network (BRAIN) and launched its flagship project, Ithaca Intact, in 2008 to restore Ithaca Creek.

Cavaliere Modesto Marini (born in Lake Garda, Italy) is an Italian chef, restaurateur and entrepreneur. He is the Founder of The Marini's Group which includes Marini's on 57 (Malaysia's highest rooftop bar, lounge and contemporary Italian restaurant), Marble 8 (Kuala Lumpur's premier steakhouse specializing in producing its own dry-aged steak); and M Marini Caffè, Malaysia's first dedicated champagne and caviar cafe.

Cavaliere Modesto Marini was awarded the prestigious Ordine Della Stella D’Italia (Knight of the Order of the Star of Italy) award in October 2016, which carries the title “Cavaliere” and is one of the highest civilian honors of the Italian government.

It recognizes Italians living abroad or foreigners who have been instrumental in the preservation and promotion of national prestige abroad, who have made important contributions to relations between their country and Italy. It is awarded in many areas, including philanthropic activities, scientific and technological research, commercial enterprises and the promotion of Italian food and wine.

Ebenezer Norman is a Liberian philanthropist, humanitarian, public speaker, and founder of the education non-profit A New Dimension of Hope (NDHope). He is involved in humanitarian efforts in Liberia and throughout West Africa, notably for efforts to build schools in attenuated or war-torn communities after the Second Liberian Civil War in 2003 and the Ebola virus epidemic in Liberia in 2015.

Howard M. Radzely (born 1970) is an American attorney who served as Acting United States Secretary of Labor in 2009 under President Barack Obama. He previously held roles as Deputy Secretary and Solicitor of Labor during the George W. Bush administration. A graduate of the University of Pennsylvania and Harvard Law School, Radzely clerked for prominent judges before entering private practice and later joining the Department of Labor. He is currently a Senior Vice President and Assistant General Counsel at The Boeing Company.

Xbase++ is a programming language and development platform designed as a successor to Clipper, supporting its syntax and features. It includes tools like a visual development environment, an integrated debugger, and a resource compiler. Xbase++ supports Replaceable Database Drivers (RDD) for various database formats, including DBF and SQL servers. It emerged after Computer Associates abandoned Clipper, leading to the development of alternative solutions.

Heinz Rein (pseudonym: Reinhard Andermann) was an influential German novelist writing before and after the Second World War. He became a major figure in the "rubble literature" period, and his famous novel Berlin Finale, published in 1947, was one of the first bestsellers of the German rebuilding period.
St. Dominic's Abbey is a 13th-century Dominican monastery in Cashel, County Tipperary, Ireland.

Greg Standridge (May 6, 1967 – November 16, 2017) was an American politician who served as a Republican member of the Arkansas Senate from the 16th district from 2015 until his death in 2017. A graduate of Arkansas Tech University, he won a special election to succeed Michael Lamoureux and passed away from cancer at age 50.

Dragan Bender (born 17 November 1997) is a Croatian professional basketball player for Monbus Obradoiro of the Spanish Liga ACB. He stands 7 ft 0 in (2.13 m) and plays the power forward and center positions. He was selected by the Phoenix Suns with the fourth overall pick in the 2016 NBA draft. Bender represents the Croatian national team, with experience in the FIBA Europe junior tournaments. Before playing in Israel, he competed with multiple teams in Croatia and in Nikola Vujčić's academy.

*This Is Orson Welles* is a 1992 book by filmmaker Peter Bogdanovich and actor-director Orson Welles, published by HarperCollins. The book is a compilation of interviews between the two, recorded over several years in the United States, Mexico, and Europe. It offers insights into Welles's life, career, and creative process, as well as his perspectives on filmmaking and the industry. The interviews are loosely organized, reflecting the conversational nature of their discussions, and were extensively revised by Welles before his death in 1985.

The project originated in 1968 when Welles suggested to Bogdanovich, a rising filmmaker and admirer of Welles's work, that they collaborate on a book. Work on the book was intermittent, with delays caused by Welles's other commitments, including an offer to write his memoirs. After Welles's death, Bogdanovich resumed work on the project with the assistance of film critic Jonathan Rosenbaum, who edited the extensive transcripts into the final published version.

The book also includes a detailed chronology of Welles's career and serves as a significant resource for understanding his artistic legacy. It was released in print and audiobook formats and remains a key text for fans and scholars of Welles and cinema history.

"Tsunagu" is a 2017 single by Japanese boy band Arashi, released on June 28, 2017, under the J Storm label. It served as the theme song for the film *Shinobi no Kuni*, starring Arashi member Satoshi Ohno. The single was released in two editions, featuring various B-sides and bonus content. "Tsunagu" achieved commercial success, debuting at number one on the Oricon and Billboard Japan charts and earning platinum certification from the RIAJ.

The Apostles' Fast is a period of fasting and prayer observed in various Christian traditions, particularly in the Eastern Orthodox, Oriental Orthodox, and Eastern Catholic Churches. It commemorates the preparation of the Apostles for their missionary work following the Ascension of Jesus Christ and the Feast of Pentecost. Rooted in Sacred Tradition and scriptural references, the fast is seen as a time of thanksgiving for the Apostles' endurance and dedication.

The fast begins on the Monday after the Sunday of All Saints and ends on June 29, the Feast of Saints Peter and Paul. Its duration varies depending on the date of Pascha (Easter). The fasting rules are less strict than those of Great Lent, with variations across traditions. Oriental Churches, such as the Coptic, Syriac, and Maronite Churches, observe modified versions of the fast, with differing durations and practices.

Richard Gordon Guindon (December 2, 1935 – February 27, 2022) was an American cartoonist best known for his gag panel Guindon. Guindon's cartoons have appeared in the Minneapolis Tribune, The Realist, and the Detroit Free Press.
Oliver Bäte is a German business executive and CEO of Allianz, having previously held roles as COO and CFO. He joined Allianz's board in 2008.

Samuel Lord (1803–1889) was a British-American entrepreneur and co-founder of the iconic American department store Lord & Taylor. Born in Saddleworth, Yorkshire, he emigrated to the United States in 1826, establishing a dry goods store in Manhattan that evolved into the renowned retail chain. After retiring in 1862, he returned to England, where he pursued horticulture until his death in 1889.

"Lottery" (लॉटरी) is a 1933 Hindi short story by Indian writer Premchand. First published in *Zamana*, it follows a school teacher and his friend Vikram as they pool funds to buy a lottery ticket, leading to family conflicts and moral dilemmas. The story, known for its dramatic irony and moral message, has been included in Indian textbooks, compilations, and theatrical adaptations.
Luise Koch (11 October 1860 – 14 March 1934) was a German politician, suffragist, and educator who advocated for women's voting rights.

The FIVB Senior Continental Rankings is a ranking system for men's and women's national teams in volleyball. Divided to five continental confederations, the teams of the member nations of FIVB, volleyball's world governing body, are ranked based on their game results with the most successful teams being ranked highest. A points system is used, with points being awarded based on the results of all FIVB-recognised full international matches. The rankings are used in international competitions to define the seeded teams and arrange them in pools. Specific procedures for seeding and pooling are established by the FIVB in each competition's formula, but the method usually employed is the serpentine system.

Anima Yell! (アニマエール!, Anima Ēru!) is a Japanese four-panel manga series by Tsukasa Unohana. It was serialized in Houbunsha's seinen manga magazine Manga Time Kirara Carat from its April 2016 to October 2020 issues and has been collected in five tankōbon volumes. An anime television series adaptation by Doga Kobo aired from October 7 to December 23, 2018.

Lau is a populated area, a socken (not to be confused with parish), on the Swedish island of Gotland. It comprises the same area as the administrative Lau District, established on 1 January 2016. Originally an island, it is now part of the main Gotland island due to the isostasy. It is mostly known for the good water from the spring Lau Käldu.

CCM (Canada Cycle & Motor Co. Ltd.) was a Canadian bicycle manufacturer founded in 1899 through the merger of four major Canadian bicycle companies. Based in Weston, Ontario, and later Montreal, CCM became a dominant force in Canadian bicycle production, accounting for 85% of the market at its peak. The company also diversified into motor vehicles, establishing the Russell Motor Car Company in 1904. During World War II, CCM produced bicycles for the Canadian military. Despite its success, financial struggles led to bankruptcy in 1983, after which its assets were acquired by Procycle Group Inc. Today, CCM-branded bicycles are sold in Canada, and the company is remembered for its iconic models like the Flyte, Mustang Marauder, and Targa.

The 2010 South Carolina gubernatorial election was held on November 2, 2010, to elect the governor of South Carolina. Republican nominee Nikki Haley defeated Democratic nominee Vincent Sheheen to succeed outgoing Republican Governor Mark Sanford. Haley's victory made her the first female governor of South Carolina. The campaign featured endorsements from prominent organizations, including the National Rifle Association for Haley and the South Carolina Chamber of Commerce for Sheheen. Several debates were held, including a historic bipartisan primary debate aired on SCETV. Haley's win continued Republican control of the governorship in South Carolina.

The Irish Centre for High-End Computing (ICHEC) is the national high-performance computing centre in Ireland. It was established in 2005 and provides supercomputing resources, support, training and related services. ICHEC is involved in education and training, including providing courses for researchers.

The Forging of a Rebel is an autobiographical trilogy of books written by the Spanish author, broadcaster and journalist Arturo Barea. They were first published during the author's exile in the United Kingdom after the Spanish Civil War.

I Feel Pretty is a 2018 American comedy film written and directed by Abby Kohn and Marc Silverstein. The film stars Amy Schumer, Michelle Williams, Emily Ratajkowski, Rory Scovel, Aidy Bryant, Busy Philipps, Tom Hopper, Naomi Campbell, and Lauren Hutton. The plot involves an insecure woman who, after suffering a head injury, gains extreme self-confidence in her appearance.

The film was released in the United States on April 20, 2018, by STX Entertainment, and grossed over $94 million worldwide. It received mixed reviews from critics, with some saying it did not fully commit to its premise or tone, although the performances of both Schumer and Williams were praised.

"Tsunagu" is the 52nd single by Japanese boy band Arashi. It was released on June 28, 2017 under their record label J Storm. "Tsunagu" is the theme song for the film Shinobi no Kuni starring Arashi member Satoshi Ohno. The single sold over 380,000 copies in its first week and topped the weekly Oricon Singles Chart. The single was certified Platinum by the Recording Industry Association of Japan (RIAJ).
Lieutenant-General Sir Richard Maurice Hilton Vickers KCB CVO OBE (born 21 August 1928) is a former British Army officer who served as Director-General of Army Training from 1982 until 1983.

The French Covered Court Championships was a defunct indoor tennis tournament held from 1895 to 1971, primarily at the Tennis Club de Paris. Played on wood courts, it hosted 69 editions and was a predecessor to the Paris Masters.

Old Street is a 1-mile (1.6 km) street in inner north-east Central London that runs west to east from Goswell Road in Clerkenwell, in the London Borough of Islington, via St Luke's and Old Street Roundabout, to the crossroads where it meets Shoreditch High Street (south), Kingsland Road (north) and Hackney Road (east) in Shoreditch in the London Borough of Hackney.

It has London Underground station Old Street on the Northern line which has other platforms for National Rail's Northern City Line.

Its west half is inside London's Congestion Charging Zone; a short part of its eastern half is the inner ring marking its limit.
Satannish is a fictional character appearing in American comic books published by Marvel Comics.

Speshow is the fourth Mandarin studio album of Taiwanese Mandopop artist Show Lo (Chinese: 羅志祥). It was released on 17 November 2006 by Avex Taiwan. The name Speshow is a pun of 'Special' and his name 'Show', as he hoped it will be special for his fans. This is the last studio album of Show's, released by Avex Taiwan before his move to EMI Music Taiwan.

It was available on pre-order and four editions were released, which includes Speshow (Happiness Hunter MV Celebration Edition) (Speshow 幸福獵人影音慶功版), Speshow (Corner with Love Special Edition) (Speshow 愛*轉角 冠軍特別版) and Speshow (King's Game Live Gold Edition) (Speshow 國王遊戲LIVE黃金版) with 60 minutes of highlight live footage from Show Lo BenQ Speshow Concert at Taipei NanGang101.

Victory Day over Nazism in World War II is a Ukrainian national holiday observed annually on May 9. Established in 2015 as part of Ukraine's decommunization efforts, it replaced the Soviet-era Victory Day and emphasizes remembrance over celebration, akin to the United Kingdom's Remembrance Day. The holiday prohibits the use of Soviet and Nazi symbols and avoids the term "Great Patriotic War," favoring "World War II." Its official symbol is the red poppy, and its motto is "1939–1945. We remember. We prevail."

American Zombie is a 2007 American mockumentary horror film directed by Grace Lee, written by Rebecca Sonnenshine and Lee, and starring Lee and John Solomon as documentary filmmakers who investigate a fictional subculture of real-life zombies living in Los Angeles.

Yawalapiti (Jaulapiti) is an Arawakan language of Brazil. The Agavotaguerra (Agavotoqueng) reportedly spoke the same language. Speakers of the language live in a village along the edge of the river Tuatuari, a tributary of the Kuluene River, located in the southern part of the Xingu Indigenous Park (Upper Xingu), in the state of Mato Grosso.

Climax is a village in Kalamazoo County, Michigan, United States. Incorporated in 1899, it has a total area of 1.05 square miles and a population of 712 as of the 2020 census. Settled in the early 19th century, it was named in 1834 by the Eldred family. Climax is notable for being the first community in Michigan to receive free rural mail service in 1896.

James Fitton (11 February 1899 – 2 May 1982) was a British painter, illustrator, and designer. Known for his social awareness, he worked across various mediums, including posters, cartoons, and cinema art. Fitton studied at the Manchester School of Art and the Central School of Arts and Crafts, exhibiting at the Royal Academy and becoming a member of the London Group. His work is held in major collections, including Tate and the National Portrait Gallery.

68th Siege Battery was a unit of Britain's Royal Garrison Artillery (RGA) formed in Wales during World War I. It served on the Western Front, including the Battles of the Somme and Passchendaele, defended against the German Spring Offensive and took part in the crushing victories of the Allied Hundred Days Offensive in 1918.
Middletown Valley, also historically known as Catoctin Valley, is a valley in western Frederick County in the state of Maryland.

Lee Franklin Hanmer (October 24, 1871 – April 27, 1961) was an American educator and recreation advocate. He served as a physical training supervisor in New York City schools, worked with the Playground Association of America and the Russell Sage Foundation, and directed recreation for servicemen during World War I. Hanmer received the Silver Buffalo Award in 1926.
GE Power (formerly known as GE Energy) is an American energy technology company, owned by General Electric.

*Bathothauma lyromma* is a species of glass squid in the family Cranchiidae, notable for its large paralarvae with stalked eyes and a wide oceanic distribution in tropical and subtropical regions. It undergoes ontogenetic vertical migration, maturing at depths exceeding 1,500 meters. Currently the sole species in its genus, it may represent a species complex.

Margaret Bernadine Hall (1863–1910) was a British painter known for her works in social realism, including *Fantine* and *Les Abandonées*. Born in Liverpool, she studied art in Paris and exhibited internationally, including at the Paris Salon. Hall traveled extensively before returning to England, where she died in Hampstead Heath. Few of her paintings survive, with *Fantine* housed at the Walker Art Gallery.
Harry Ruilton Watson (August 31, 1921 – June 8, 2001) was an American child actor, a U.S. Coast Guard combat photographer in World War II, and a pioneer in television journalism.

Meckenbach is a municipality in the Bad Kreuznach district of Rhineland-Palatinate, Germany. Located in the North Palatine Uplands, it has a population of 364 (as of December 2022). The village has historical roots dating back to Roman times, with evidence of copper smelting and early settlement. Meckenbach features a Romanesque Evangelical church, expanded in the 18th century, and a Stumm organ from 1836. Historically, it was part of the Waldgravial Amt of Kyrburg and later under French and Prussian administration. The municipality is governed by an eight-member council and Mayor Michael Schlarb.

Robert Neelly Bellah (February 23, 1927 – July 30, 2013) was an American sociologist and the Elliott Professor of Sociology at the University of California, Berkeley. He was internationally known for his work related to the sociology of religion.

Charles Thomas "Chuck" Hunt III (March 2, 1954 – December 21, 2021) was an American dairy farmer, businessman in the construction industry, and physical education teacher. He served as the First Gentleman of Massachusetts from 2001 to 2003 during the tenure of his wife, acting Governor Jane Swift. He was the first man to hold the position of state First Gentleman, cementing "a place in Massachusetts history", as the Boston Globe later wrote.

The major prion protein (PrP) is a glycoprotein encoded by the *PRNP* gene, located on chromosome 20 in humans. It is highly conserved across mammals and exists in two main isoforms: the normal cellular form (PrP<sup>C</sup>) and the misfolded, disease-associated form (PrP<sup>Sc</sup>). PrP<sup>C</sup> is primarily found on the surface of neurons and other cells, where it is anchored to the membrane via a glycosylphosphatidylinositol (GPI) anchor. While its precise function remains unclear, PrP<sup>C</sup> is thought to play roles in copper ion transport, cell signaling, synaptic function, and memory formation.

The misfolded PrP<sup>Sc</sup> isoform is associated with transmissible spongiform encephalopathies (TSEs), a group of fatal neurodegenerative diseases, including Creutzfeldt–Jakob disease and kuru. PrP<sup>Sc</sup> aggregates in neural tissue, leading to neurodegeneration. Polymorphisms in *PRNP* influence susceptibility to prion diseases and other conditions, such as Alzheimer's disease, where PrP interacts with amyloid-beta oligomers. Research continues to explore PrP's physiological roles, its involvement in disease, and its potential as a therapeutic target.

*1939 Battle of Westerplatte* is a 2013 Polish-Lithuanian war film directed by Paweł Chochlew, depicting the Battle of Westerplatte during the Nazi invasion of Poland. The film sparked controversy for its perceived revisionist portrayal of the battle, with some critics labeling it "anti-Polish" and offensive to the honor of Polish soldiers. Despite this, others praised it as a tribute to the defenders' heroism. The film's title and marketing underwent changes to address criticism.

Caserma Ederle is a United States Army base located in Vicenza, Italy, serving as a key installation for U.S. military operations in Europe. Established in 1955 as part of the U.S. Army Southern European Task Force (SETAF), the base has played a significant role in NATO operations and Cold War defense strategies, including the management of nuclear weapons stockpiles and regional tactical operations. Over the years, it has housed various infantry and support units, including elements of the 173rd Airborne Brigade Combat Team and the United States Army Africa headquarters.

The base provides extensive amenities for military personnel and their families, including housing, schools, and recreational facilities. Recent expansions include the construction of Caserma Del Din, a LEED-certified annex, to accommodate additional troops. Caserma Ederle remains a vital hub for U.S. military presence in southern Europe.

Lars Eric Mattsson is a Finnish musician, composer, and record producer from Åland, known for his contributions to heavy metal, hard rock, and neoclassical metal. Active since the late 1970s, he has released numerous solo albums and collaborated with bands like Condition Red, Mattsson, Book of Reflections, and Vision.

His Majesty's Hired armed cutter Sandwich served the Royal Navy from 23 May 1798 until the French frigate Créole captured her on 14 June 1799. She then served in the French Navy until the Royal Navy recaptured her on 15 October 1803. The Navy purchased her in 1804 and she served for some months in 1805 as HMS Sandwich before she was sold in Jamaica. During this period she captured three small French privateers in two days.

Hansdiha Junction railway station is a railway station on the Dumka–Bhagalpur line of Sahibganj loop under Malda railway division of the Eastern Railway Zone. The station is situated beside Hansdiha-Ramgarh Road, Kasba, Hansdiha in Dumka district in the Indian state of Jharkhand.

Song Bird is a studio album by American country music artist Margo Smith. It was released in July 1976 via Warner Bros. Records and was produced by Norro Wilson. Songbird was the third studio recording of Smith's music career. The album contained a total of ten tracks, including the single release, "Save Your Kisses for Me." The album would reach chart positions and the single would become a major hit.

The 2nd Infantry Brigade was a formation of the New Zealand Military Forces that served during the First World War and later as part of the Territorial Force. Formed in 1916, it was part of the New Zealand Division and fought on the Western Front in major battles such as Messines and Passchendaele. Disbanded in 1919, it was re-established in 1924 as a Territorial Force unit, serving until its final disbandment in 1964.

Melukote in Pandavapura taluk of Mandya district, Karnataka, in southern India, is one of the sacred places in Karnataka.It is built on rocky hills, known as Yadugiri, Yaadavagiri and Yadushailadeepa, overlooking the Cauvery valley. Melukote is about 51 km (32 miles) from Mysore and 133 km (83 mi) from Bangalore.

Melukote is the location of the Cheluvanarayana Swamy Temple, with a collection of crowns and jewels which are brought to the temple for the annual celebration. On the top of the hill is the temple of Yoganarasimha. Many more shrines and ponds are located in the town. Melukote is home to the Academy of Sanskrit Research, which has collected thousands of Vedic and Sanskrit manuscripts.

Early in the 12th century, the famous Srivaishnava saint Sri Ramanujacharya, who hailed from Tamil Nadu, stayed at Melukote for about 12 years. It has thus become a prominent centre of the Srivaishnava sect.

The Mississippi Wing of Civil Air Patrol (CAP) is the highest echelon of Civil Air Patrol in the state of Mississippi. Mississippi Wing headquarters are located in Jackson, Mississippi. The Mississippi Wing consists of over 500 cadet and adult members at over 16 locations across the state of Mississippi.

W. & J. Sloane was a prominent American retail company specializing in rugs, furniture, and home furnishings, founded in 1843 by William Sloane in New York City. Renowned for introducing oriental carpets to the U.S. and setting national decorating trends, it served elite clients, including the White House. After decades of success, the company declined following its 1955 acquisition, filing for bankruptcy in 1985.

The Mizoram Peace Accord, signed on June 30, 1986, in New Delhi, India, marked the end of two decades of insurgency led by the Mizo National Front (MNF) against the Indian government. The conflict stemmed from grievances over the integration of the Mizo people into Assam, neglect during the 1959 famine, and demands for independence or a unified "Greater Mizoram." The MNF, under Laldenga, declared independence in 1966, leading to widespread violence and military intervention.

Negotiations for peace, facilitated by church leaders and social organizations, began in the 1970s and culminated in the accord. The agreement required the MNF to renounce violence and adhere to Indian laws. In return, Mizoram was granted statehood on February 20, 1987, with Laldenga becoming its first Chief Minister. The accord is celebrated annually as "Remna Ni" (Peace Day) and is considered a model for resolving insurgencies in India.

SMS *Frankfurt* was a German *Wiesbaden*-class light cruiser built for the Imperial German Navy during World War I. Laid down in 1913 and commissioned in August 1915, the ship was armed with eight 15 cm guns, four torpedo tubes, and could carry 120 mines. She served in several major naval engagements, including the Bombardment of Yarmouth and Lowestoft and the Battle of Jutland, where she acted as the flagship of II Scouting Group. *Frankfurt* also participated in Operation Albion and the Second Battle of Heligoland Bight in 1917. 

After Germany's defeat, *Frankfurt* was interned at Scapa Flow, where she was scuttled by her crew in June 1919. However, British forces salvaged the ship, and she was later transferred to the United States as a war prize. Commissioned into the U.S. Navy in 1920, *Frankfurt* was studied for her design features before being used as a target ship in bombing tests led by General Billy Mitchell. On July 18, 1921, she was sunk off Cape Henry, Virginia, during these tests, which demonstrated the effectiveness of aerial bombing against warships.

Carroll Ashmore Campbell Jr. (July 24, 1940 – December 7, 2005) was an American politician and member of the Republican Party who served as the 112th governor of South Carolina from 1987 to 1995. Known for his leadership during Hurricane Hugo and his role in bringing BMW's first U.S. manufacturing plant to South Carolina, he also chaired the National Governors Association. After leaving office, Campbell worked as a lobbyist and was diagnosed with Alzheimer's disease in 2001.

The Hồ dynasty (1400–1407), officially known as Đại Ngu, was a short-lived imperial dynasty in Vietnam founded by Hồ Quý Ly. It succeeded the Trần dynasty after Hồ Quý Ly's coup d'état and marked a period of significant political and social reform. Hồ Quý Ly implemented land reforms, introduced paper currency, promoted the Chữ Nôm script for the Vietnamese language, and restructured the education system to include mathematics and agriculture. He also renamed the country Đại Ngu, claiming descent from the ancient Chinese Emperor Shun.

Despite initial military successes, including territorial gains from Champa, the dynasty faced internal unrest and external threats. In 1407, the Ming dynasty invaded, capturing Hồ Quý Ly and his family, leading to the dynasty's collapse and the Fourth Era of Northern Domination.

Daniel Roy Carpenter (born November 25, 1985) is a former American football placekicker. He was signed by the Miami Dolphins as an undrafted free agent in 2008 after playing college football for the University of Montana. He has also played for the Buffalo Bills.

*Cleveland Board of Education v. LaFleur*, 414 U.S. 632 (1974), was a landmark United States Supreme Court case that ruled overly restrictive maternity leave policies in public schools unconstitutional under the Due Process Clauses of the Fifth and Fourteenth Amendments. The Court found such policies arbitrary and discriminatory, marking a significant step in protecting the rights of female teachers and advancing workplace equality. This decision also influenced the development of family leave laws, such as the Family and Medical Leave Act of 1993, and addressed longstanding biases against women in the teaching profession.

Fish fingers (British English) or fish sticks (American English) are a processed food made using a whitefish, such as cod, hake, haddock, shark or pollock, which has been battered or breaded. They are commonly available in the frozen food section of supermarkets. They can be baked in an oven, grilled, shallow fried, or deep-fried.
Tome VI is an album by composer and bandleader Gil Mellé and the Jazz Electronauts recorded in 1968 and released on the Verve label.

In molecular biology, this protein domain belongs to the terpene synthase family (TPS). Its role is to synthesize terpenes, which are part of primary metabolism, such as sterols and carotene, and also part of the secondary metabolism. This entry will focus on the C terminal domain of the TPS protein.
Rodney Bullock Jr. (born 3 June 1994) is an American professional basketball player for Prishtina of the Kosovo Basketball Superleague. He competed in college basketball for Providence.

*Gayopakhyanam* (గయోపాఖ్యానం) is a Telugu play written by Chilakamarti Lakshmi Narasimham in 1890 at the age of 22. Based on a Hindu epic, it narrates the story of Gaya, a Gandharva king, and his conflict with Sri Krishna, which is ultimately resolved by Lord Shiva. The play was highly popular, selling over 100,000 copies after its publication in 1909, and was later adapted into films, including *Sri Krishnarjuna Yudham* (1962/1963).

The Associated Equipment Company (AEC) was a British automotive manufacturer founded in 1912 and based in Southall, England. Specializing in lorries, buses, and motorcoaches, AEC gained prominence for its reliable vehicles, including contributions to military efforts during both World Wars. Acquired by Leyland Motors in 1962, AEC ceased production in 1979 following the closure of its Southall plant by British Leyland.

Canajoharie Creek is a 34-mile-long (55 km) tributary of the Mohawk River located in Central New York, flowing through Otsego and Montgomery counties. Originating north of Cherry Valley, the creek features notable waterfalls, including Judds Falls and Canajoharie Falls, and passes through the Canajoharie Gorge before emptying into the Mohawk River in the village of Canajoharie. The creek's name, derived from the Mohawk language, means "the pot that washes itself," referencing a distinctive pothole formation known as the "boiling pot." Historically, the creek was significant to early European settlers and the Mohawk people, and it remains a site for fishing and geological interest.

Clidastes is an extinct genus of marine lizard belonging to the mosasaur family. It is classified as part of the Mosasaurinae subfamily, alongside genera like Mosasaurus and Prognathodon. Clidastes is known from deposits ranging in age from the Coniacian to the early Campanian in the United States.

Clidastes means "locked vertebrae", which originates from the Greek noun κλειδί, or kleid meaning key (akin to Latin claudere meaning to shut). This refers to how the vertebral processes allow the proximal heads of the vertebrae to interlock for stability and strength during swimming.

It was one of the earliest hydropedal mosasaurs, representing one of the first properly marine predatory forms alongside other early hydropedal genera like Tylosaurus and Platecarpus. It was likely an agile swimmer that preyed upon cephalopods, fish and other small vertebrates in shallow water. Isotopic analysis on teeth specimens has suggested that this genus and Platecarpus may have entered freshwater occasionally, just like modern sea snakes.

Rhyssa persuasoria, also known as the sabre wasp, is a species belonging to the family Ichneumonidae subfamily Rhyssinae. Members of this subfamily, including those of Rhyssa and the allied Megarhyssa, are also known collectively as giant ichneumonid wasps or giant ichneumons.

The 2014 Mountain Province bus accident occurred on February 7, 2014, in Sitio Paggang, Talubin, Bontoc, Mountain Province, Philippines, when a GV Florida Transport bus fell 100 to 120 meters off a mountain road. The accident resulted in 14 deaths, including prominent figures such as comedian Tado, and 32 injuries. Mechanical issues were suspected as the cause.

Pierre Marinovitch (1 August 1898 – 2 October 1919) was a French World War I flying ace and the youngest ace of the war, credited with 21 aerial victories. Born in Paris to Serbian and Polish parents, he joined the French military in 1916 and later served in Escadrille Spa.94. Marinovitch was awarded the Légion d'honneur and Croix de Guerre for his service. He died in a flying accident in Brussels in 1919.

W. & J. Sloane was a luxury furniture and rug store in New York City that catered to the prominent, including the White House and the Breakers, and wealthy, including the Rockefeller, Whitney, and Vanderbilt families.

After a long period of prosperity and prominence, the firm went through a short period of decline and was forced to file for bankruptcy in 1985.

Henry Stuart Russell (16 March 1818 – 5 March 1889) was an English-born explorer, politician, historian and pastoralist, best known for establishing the Cecil Plains Station around the Condamine River area of Australia.

Ultimate Canada is the national governing body for the sport of ultimate in Canada, established in 1993 and affiliated with the World Flying Disc Federation. The organization oversees the development and promotion of ultimate across the country, supporting leagues, tournaments, and player development at all levels. Canada has a rich history in ultimate, with the sport introduced in the early 1970s by Ken Westerfield, who helped establish the Toronto Ultimate Club (TUC), the country's first ultimate league. The TUC, founded in 1980, remains one of the largest and oldest leagues in Canada, with over 3,300 active members.

Ultimate Canada also organizes the Canadian Ultimate Championships, first held in 1987, and has seen Canadian teams achieve international success, including top rankings in the World Flying Disc Federation's Ultimate World Rankings. The sport has grown significantly, with major leagues and associations in cities such as Vancouver, Ottawa, Montreal, Calgary, and London. In 2013, Canada introduced its first semi-professional ultimate team, the Toronto Rush, which won the American Ultimate Disc League (AUDL) Championship in its inaugural season.

Ultimate Canada also established the Canadian Ultimate Hall of Fame in 2011 to honor pioneers and contributors to the sport. With a focus on inclusivity and growth, the organization continues to expand ultimate's reach across the country.

Omsk hemorrhagic fever is a viral hemorrhagic fever caused by a Flavivirus.

It was found in Siberia and was named for an outbreak in the city of Omsk. First records of the new virus appeared around 1940–1943.

The *George Washington* bust at the Indiana Statehouse is a 1932 plaster copy of Jean Antoine Houdon's original 1785 sculpture, created from life at Mount Vernon. Commissioned for the George Washington Bicentennial Celebration, it features Washington in a toga-like drape. The bust is owned by the Indiana Historical Bureau and is displayed on the Statehouse's fourth floor.

Ali Alipour Ghara (born 11 November 1995) is an Iranian professional footballer who plays as a forward for Portuguese club Gil Vicente. He began his career with Nassaji Mazandaran and later played for clubs including Persepolis, where he became the youngest scorer in the Tehran derby and was the Persian Gulf Pro League top scorer in 2017–18. Alipour has also represented the Iran national team, debuting in 2018.

Jim Radakovich is an American sculptor and painter living and working in New York City. He was a key figure in the East Village art scene in New York from 1982 to 1987 often showing together both Neo-Surrealist paintings and totem-like sculpture. He frequently exhibited with other artists who emerged at the time, including Kiki Smith, David Wojnarowicz, George Condo, Rick Prol, Peter Schuyff, Mark Kostabi and Marilyn Minter.

"The Crocodile" is the fourth episode of the second season of the American fantasy drama series *Once Upon a Time*. Directed by David Solomon and written by David H. Goodman and Robert Hull, it originally aired on October 21, 2012. The episode explores the backstory of Captain Hook (Killian Jones) and his rivalry with Rumplestiltskin, while also focusing on Belle's struggle to assert her independence in Storybrooke. It received critical acclaim for its character development, action, and Colin O'Donoghue's performance as Captain Hook.

Allison Jones (born 1955) is an American casting director who is credited for helping bring together realistic ensemble casts for such popular television shows as The Fresh Prince of Bel-Air (1990–1996), Freaks and Geeks (1999–2000), Curb Your Enthusiasm (2000–), Undeclared (2001–2002), Arrested Development (2003–2006), the American version of The Office (2005–2013), United States of Tara (2009–2011), Parks and Recreation (2009–2015), Brooklyn Nine-Nine (2013–2021), The Good Place (2016–2020), and What We Do in the Shadows (2019–).

Veronika Ivanovskaia (born February 1, 1995) is a German professional pool player of Russian descent. A former junior European champion, she has won multiple titles, including the 2018 European Pool Championship in eight-ball. Ivanovskaia also competes internationally in World and Euro Tour events.

"Charles" is a short story by Shirley Jackson, first published in Mademoiselle in July 1948. It was later included in her 1949 collection, The Lottery and Other Stories, and her 1953 novel, Life Among the Savages.

The Tres Amigas SuperStation (lit. 'Three Friends SuperStation') is a planned project to unite North America’s two major power grids (the Eastern Interconnection and the Western Interconnection) and one of its eight minor grids (the Texas Interconnection), with the goals of enabling faster adoption of renewable energy and increasing the reliability of the U.S. grid. The project will use superconducting wires from Massachusetts-based American Superconductor Corp. for electrical distribution and to interconvert alternating current (AC) and direct current (DC) power.

Proposed by Tres Amigas, LLC in 2009, the project is in the process of finalizing funding. Permits and construction contracts are in place. CEO Phillip G. Harris is the former CEO of PJM Interconnection, an East Coast regional transmission organization (RTO).
John William Goode Jr. (March 10, 1923 – February 5, 1994) was a lawyer in San Antonio who was a figure in the 1950s and 1960s rebirth of the Republican Party in Texas.

The Michigan Dogman is a cryptid from Michigan folklore, first reported in 1887 in Wexford County, where two lumberjacks described seeing a creature with a man's body and a dog's head. Subsequent sightings have been reported across Michigan, including notable accounts in 1937, the 1950s, and 1967. The legend gained widespread attention in 1987 when disc jockey Steve Cook recorded "The Legend," a song inspired by North American myths, which led to numerous listener reports of similar creatures. The Dogman has since been featured in media, including the TV series *MonsterQuest* and the 2011 film *Dogman*. It remains a popular subject in cryptid lore.

The spiders of New Zealand include a diverse range of species, with notable examples being the fishing spiders of the genus *Dolomedes*. These spiders have adapted to hunt on the surface of water and are represented by four species in New Zealand. Three of these species are widespread across the two main islands. *Dolomedes aquaticus* inhabits open, unforested riverbanks and shelters under rocks near rivers, while *Dolomedes dondalei* is found along forested riverbanks. The most common species, *Dolomedes minor*, also known as the nursery web spider, is not restricted to rivers and constructs white nursery webs on shrubs, though it retains its fishing abilities. The largest fishing spider, *Dolomedes schauinslandi*, is found exclusively on South East and Mangere islands in the Chatham Islands. These spiders exemplify the unique adaptations and ecological niches occupied by New Zealand's arachnid fauna.

An air pump is a pump for pushing air. Examples include a bicycle pump, pumps that are used to aerate an aquarium or a pond via an airstone; a gas compressor used to power a pneumatic tool, air horn or pipe organ; a bellows used to encourage a fire; a vacuum cleaner and a vacuum pump. All air pumps contain a part that moves (vane, piston, impeller, diaphragm etc.) which drives the flow of air. When the air gets moved, an area of low pressure gets created which fills up with more air.

Pumps and compressors use very similar mechanisms, and basically perform the same action, but in different fluid regimes. At some point there is a crossover point in terminology, but here are some stereotypes:

• Compressors operate on compressible fluids, typically gases. Pumps operate on fluids, typically liquids, approximated as in-compressible.

• Compressors are intended to develop a very high pressure rise against a closed system; pumps are designed to develop relatively little pressure against a free-flowing system with minimal back-pressure.

• Pumps are often used in continuous-flow operation, while many lower-end compressors must have intermittent duty cycles.

• Compressors usually have a feedback sensor to shut off when they reach a desired pressure; pumps have a fixed design and operate freely across their performance curve as conditions change

Helene Hewitt OBE is a British climate scientist who is a research fellow at the Met Office. Her research considers climate and ocean models. Hewitt serves on the CLIVAR Ocean Model Development Panel. She was awarded an Order of the British Empire in the 2022 Birthday Honours.

The Open Siddur Project (Lang-he, IPA: pʁojeqt hassidduʁ hapatuaħ) is an open-source, web-to-print publishing and digital humanities project intent on sharing the semantic data of Jewish liturgy and liturgy-related work with free-culture compatible copyright licenses and Public Domain dedications. The project collaborates with other efforts in open-source Judaism in sharing content and code, advocates among related user-generated content projects to adopt Open Content licensing, and solicits copyright owners of related liturgical materials to share their work under free-culture compatible terms.
Lodestar is the seventh studio album by the English folk musician, Shirley Collins. The album is Collins's first in 38 years, making it one of the longest gaps between studio albums.

Lectionary 34, designated by siglum l 34 (in the Gregory-Aland numbering). It is a Greek manuscript of the New Testament, on parchment leaves. Palaeographically it has been assigned to the 9th century. The manuscript is lacunose.

The Commissioner of the Delhi Police is the head of the Delhi Police, established in 1978 following the recommendations of the Khosla Commission. The inaugural holder was J.N. Chaturvedi, and the current commissioner is Sanjay Arora.
116 (one hundred [and] sixteen) is the natural number following 115 and preceding 117.

*Akai Katana* is a bullet hell shoot 'em up video game developed by Cave, first released in arcades in Japan on August 20, 2010. Set in a parallel world resembling Japan's Taishō period, the game follows a group of rebels wielding powerful Blood Swords and advanced fighter planes as they rise against a tyrannical empire. The game was later ported to the Xbox 360 in 2011 and globally released for modern platforms, including the Nintendo Switch, PlayStation 4, and Xbox One, in December 2022. *Akai Katana* received critical acclaim for its challenging gameplay, 2D graphics, and Ryu Umemoto's hard rock soundtrack, earning praise as a standout entry in the bullet hell genre.

"The Things That I Used to Do" is a blues standard written by Guitar Slim. He recorded it in New Orleans, where the young Ray Charles arranged and produced the session. Specialty Records released the song as a single in 1953 and it became a bestseller the following year. Specialty founder Art Rupe believed that the appeal would be limited to the Southern U.S. rural audience. However, urban rhythm and blues radio stations in the North began airing the song and built it into a national hit. As a result, Guitar Slim became in great demand as a performer and played at venues such as the Apollo Theater in New York City.

The single was one of the biggest hits in Specialty's history and stayed on the Billboard's Rhythm and Blues Records charts for 42 weeks. The song remained at number one for six weeks and was the best-selling R&B record of the year, selling more than a million copies.
Q.S. Serafijn (born February 19, 1960) is a Dutch conceptual artist and author, who is working as sculptor, photographer, and installation artist.

The Church of Nossa Senhora do Rosário (Church of Our Lady of the Rosary) is a Roman Catholic church located in Topo, São Jorge, in the Azores, Portugal. Originally built in the 16th century, it was reconstructed after the 1757 "Mandado de Deus" earthquake under architect José de Avelar de Melo. The church features a Roman architectural style, a prominent bell tower, and a richly decorated interior with Baroque influences, including gilded retables, painted panels, and silver-plated lamps.

Pieter Woortman (born 23 March 1700 – 14 April 1780) was a slave trader and an administrator of the Dutch West India Company. He was one of the longest-serving Director-General of the Dutch Gold Coast, in office between 1767 and 1769 (ad interim) and from 1769 until his death in 1780.

Gene Winstead (born 1951) is an American businessman and politician who served as the 12th mayor of Bloomington, Minnesota, from 2000 to 2020. A Republican, he was the city's longest-serving mayor, overseeing significant developments such as the expansion of the Mall of America, infrastructure improvements, and the introduction of organized garbage collection. Before his mayoral tenure, Winstead held various roles in local government, including positions on the Planning Commission and Housing and Redevelopment Authority. Known for his pragmatic approach, he prioritized economic growth, affordable housing, and transportation projects. Winstead retired from public office in 2020 after 35 years of public service.
Gerhard vom Rath (1830–1888) was a German geologist and mineralogist known for his contributions to mineralogy and crystallography.

The cast of *ER*, a medical drama that aired for 15 seasons from 1994 to 2009, featured a large ensemble of actors over its run. The original main cast included Anthony Edwards, George Clooney, Sherry Stringfield, Noah Wyle, Eriq La Salle, and Julianna Margulies. Over the years, the show introduced numerous new characters, including Gloria Reuben, Alex Kingston, Maura Tierney, and Goran Visnjic, among others. Many cast members departed throughout the series, with some returning for guest appearances, including in the series finale. The show also featured notable guest stars, several of whom received Emmy recognition for their performances.

Margaret Ransone Murray (November 16, 1901 – July 13, 1986) was an American biologist and pioneer in tissue culture techniques, particularly in neurobiology. She was a professor at Columbia University and made significant contributions to understanding tumorigenesis, myelination, and synaptogenesis. A founding member of the Tissue Culture Association and the American Society for Cell Biology, Murray was widely recognized for her mentorship and research, receiving numerous awards throughout her career.
Ya'akov Ekhoiz (Lang-he; born 29 May 1957) is an Israeli former footballer.

Denshaw is a small village in the civil parish of Saddleworth, within the Metropolitan Borough of Oldham, Greater Manchester, England. Historically part of the West Riding of Yorkshire, it is situated in the South Pennines, approximately 11 miles northeast of Manchester. The village, with a population of around 500, is known for its rural character, traditional stone buildings, and historical significance, including evidence of Stone Age and Bronze Age activity. The name "Denshaw" is derived from Old Norse, indicating a settlement during the Danelaw period.

Denshaw did not experience significant industrialization during the Industrial Revolution, though it was home to a calico printing factory and a reservoir supporting local mills. The village is notable for landmarks such as Christ Church, a Grade II listed building, and its war memorial. Denshaw hosts an annual Whit Friday brass band contest and has a vibrant community centered around its village hall and primary school. It is accessible via local bus services and nearby motorways.

William J. Maddox Jr. (May 22, 1921 – January 5, 2001) was a highly decorated United States Army major general and aviator who served from 1943 to 1976. A veteran of World War II, the Korean War, and the Vietnam War, he held numerous command positions, including leadership of the United States Army Aviation School and the 164th Aviation Group. Known as the "Grey Ghost," Maddox earned numerous awards, including the Army Distinguished Service Medal, four Silver Stars, and eight Distinguished Flying Crosses. After retiring, he advised King Hussein of Jordan and held leadership roles in aviation organizations in the Middle East and Asia.

Bustard Island is located in the Regional Municipality of Wood Buffalo, Alberta, Canada, within the Athabasca Plains sub-region of the Canadian Shield. The island features storm beaches, sandy shores, rock points, two lagoons, and supports large colonies of California Gulls.

Fort Frederick was a fort in Albany, New York from 1676–1789. Sitting atop State Street Hill (Capitol Hill) it replaced the earlier decaying Fort Orange along the Hudson River. The fort was named for Frederick Louis, Prince of Wales, son of King George II. The fort was referred to as Fort Albany in the 1936 novel Drums Along the Mohawk. Several historical markers have been placed west of the location of the fort.

Joseph Tate is an American attorney who represented government official Lewis Libby in the CIA leak grand jury investigation. Tate is a partner with the law firm Dechert LLP in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania. He specializes in defending clients against charges of complex white collar crime, in particular, antitrust and other regulatory issues. Previously, Tate worked in the Antitrust Division of the United States Department of Justice.

In polymer chemistry, condensation polymers are any kind of polymers whose process of polymerization involves a condensation reaction (i.e. a small molecule, such as water or methanol, is produced as a byproduct). Condensation polymers are formed by polycondensation, when the polymer is formed by condensation reactions between species of all degrees of polymerization, or by condensative chain polymerization, when the polymer is formed by sequential addition of monomers to an active site in a chain reaction. The main alternative forms of polymerization are chain polymerization and polyaddition, both of which give addition polymers.

Polycondensation A polymerization in which the growth of polymer chains proceeds by condensation reactions between molecules of all degrees of polymerization. Notes: 1. The growth steps are expressed by: Px+Py→Px+y+L {x}∈{1,2,.∞};{y}∈{1,2,.∞}

where Px and Py denote chains of degrees of polymerization x and y, respectively, and L a low-molar-mass by-product. 2. The earlier term 'polycondensation' was synonymous with 'condensation polymerization'. The current definitions of polycondensation and condensative chain polymerization were both embraced by the earlier term 'polycondensation'.

Condensation polymerization is a form of step-growth polymerization. Linear polymers are produced from bifunctional monomers, i.e. compounds with two reactive end-groups. Common condensation polymers include polyamides, polyacetals, and proteins.

*Mélite* is a comedy play by Pierre Corneille, first performed in 1629. The plot revolves around misunderstandings and deception among lovers, sparked by forged letters. Éraste, in love with Mélite, becomes jealous when she falls for his friend Tircis. His scheme to disrupt their relationship leads to chaos, including heartbreak, fainting, and madness. Ultimately, the misunderstandings are resolved, with Mélite and Tircis marrying, and Éraste finding redemption by marrying Cloris, Tircis' sister.

Mount Kineo is a prominent mountain in Piscataquis County, Maine, with an elevation of 1,789 feet (545 m) and a rich geological and cultural history. Known for its large rhyolite formation, used by Indigenous peoples for crafting tools and weapons, the mountain has been a significant site for centuries. Accessible only by water, it is part of a state park featuring hiking trails and the historic Mount Kineo Golf Course, established in 1893.

The Hôtel de la Marine is a historic neoclassical building located on the Place de la Concorde in Paris, France. Designed by Ange-Jacques Gabriel and completed in 1774, it originally served as the Garde-Meuble de la Couronne, housing royal furniture, art, and decorative objects. During the French Revolution, it became a pivotal site, with its weapons used in the storming of the Bastille. From 1789 to 2015, it served as the headquarters of the French Navy, undergoing significant modifications over the centuries.

The building is renowned for its grand facade, inspired by the Louvre Colonnade, and its richly decorated interiors, including the apartments of the Intendant and the opulent salons used for naval and state functions. Following extensive restoration completed in 2021, the Hôtel de la Marine reopened as a public monument under the Center of National Monuments. It now houses the Al Thani Collection, showcasing art and artifacts from around the world, and serves as a prominent cultural and historical landmark in Paris.
General Applied Science Laboratory (GASL) is an American aerospace company, known as a pioneer of hypersonic propulsion.

Crymogæa (from Greek Κρυογαια, "ice-land") is a book in Latin written by the Icelandic scholar Arngrímur Jónsson and published in Hamburg in 1609 and again in 1610. It was the first historical description of Iceland since Íslendingabók and the first comprehensive book about the history of Iceland.

FC Stal Kamianske (Lang-uk) was a professional Ukrainian football club based in Kamianske, Dnipropetrovsk Oblast, Ukraine. After being relegated to the Ukrainian First League after the 2017–18 Ukrainian Premier League season, the club re-registered to Bucha and changed its name to PFC Feniks Bucha in June 2018. The club didn't play any game under the new name and was dissolved on 9 July 2018.

The club was sponsored by the Dnieper Metallurgical Combine (DMK) which is a member of the Industrial Union of Donbas (ISD).

Borscht Corporation is a Miami-based film collective and production company known for its innovative and experimental approach to filmmaking. Their work has been showcased at prestigious venues and festivals worldwide, including the Museum of Modern Art, Sundance, SXSW, and Venice Film Festival. Borscht Corp. has received numerous accolades, including grants from the Knight Foundation and Creative Capital, and their films have won awards at Sundance, AFI Fest, and SXSW. They also host the Borscht Film Festival, celebrated as one of the "coolest" and "weirdest" film festivals globally.

The Fairmont Century Plaza is a landmark 19-story luxury hotel in Los Angeles. Located in Century City, the hotel forms a sweeping crescent design fronting the Avenue of the Stars, adjacent to the twin Century Plaza Towers and the 2000 Avenue of the Stars complex. At the time of its opening in 1966, the Century Plaza Hotel was the highest building in Century City, with views extending all the way to the Pacific Ocean. It was also the first hotel to have color televisions in all of its rooms. The hotel closed for renovations in 2016, and reopened on September 27, 2021. It is now operated by Fairmont Hotels and Resorts.

Jhajhar is a village in the Jhunjhunu district of Rajasthan, India, known for its historical significance as part of the Shekhawat clan's territory. It celebrates traditional festivals like Gangaur, Teej, and Dussehra with vibrant cultural practices.

KushCo Holdings is an American company that sells packaging, containers, and other ancillary products for the cannabis industry. It is based in Garden Grove, California and is publicly traded on the New York OTCQB marketplace under the stock ticker symbol, KSHB.

Sir Stephen Tallents (1884–1958) was a British civil servant and public relations pioneer. He served in roles including British Commissioner for the Baltic Provinces, secretary of the Empire Marketing Board, and the BBC's first Controller of Public Relations.

Oscar Braynon (born February 1, 1977) is an American politician and former Democratic member of the Florida Legislature. He served in the Florida House of Representatives from 2008 to 2011 and the Florida Senate from 2011 to 2020, representing districts in Miami-Dade and Broward counties. Braynon was the Minority Leader of the Florida Senate from 2016 to 2018. Before his legislative career, he served on the Miami Gardens City Council, including as Vice-Mayor.

Le Sueur County is a county in the U.S. state of Minnesota, founded on March 5, 1853, and named after French explorer Pierre-Charles Le Sueur. Its county seat is Le Center, and its largest city is Le Sueur. The county spans 474 square miles, with the Minnesota River forming its western border. Known for its rich soil and rolling hills, the area is part of Minnesota's savanna region. As of the 2020 census, the population was 28,674. Historically, the county has been predominantly Republican in national elections.

The Mercedes-Benz W140 is a full-size luxury car produced by Daimler-Benz from 1991 to 1998, with model years spanning 1992 to 1999. Available as a sedan in standard (W140) and long-wheelbase (V140) versions, as well as a coupé (C140), it succeeded the W126 and was later replaced by the W220 S-Class and C215 CL-Class. Designed by Olivier Boulay under the direction of Bruno Sacco, the W140 introduced advanced safety and comfort features, including double-pane windows and self-closing doors. It also marked the debut of Mercedes-Benz's first passenger V12 engine.

Despite its engineering excellence, the W140 faced criticism for its high cost and competition from Japanese luxury brands, leading to slower sales. Many enthusiasts regard it as the "last true S-Class" due to its robust design. The W140 gained notoriety as the car involved in the 1997 crash that killed Princess Diana.

*Callulops fojaensis* is a species of frog in the family Microhylidae, known only from the Foja Mountains in Indonesia. Described in 2012, it inhabits wet, mossy mid-montane forests at around 1,600 meters above sea level. The species is characterized by its robust, pear-shaped body, pale brown coloration, and distinctive advertisement call of loud, harsh barking notes. It is considered rare, with only two male specimens documented to date. The International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) has classified its conservation status as Data Deficient.

*NFL Blitz* is a series of arcade-style American football video games developed by Midway Games and later EA Sports. First released in 1997, the series is known for its fast-paced, over-the-top gameplay that deviates from traditional NFL rules, featuring smaller teams, exaggerated tackles, and a focus on aggressive, theatrical play. Early entries allowed for late hits, excessive celebrations, and wrestling-style moves, though these elements were toned down in later releases due to NFL restrictions. The series also introduced unique mechanics, such as 30-yard first downs and high-scoring, fast-paced matches.

After losing the NFL license in 2005, Midway released *Blitz: The League*, which featured fictional teams, mature themes, and a story mode, earning the franchise its first "Mature" ESRB rating. Following Midway's bankruptcy in 2009, EA Sports acquired the rights and released a reboot in 2012, returning to the arcade-style roots.

The series also included a spinoff, *Kurt Warner's Arena Football Unleashed* (2000), based on the Arena Football League. *NFL Blitz* received positive critical reception, particularly for its unique gameplay and departure from traditional football simulations.

*Crymogæa* is a historical work by Arngrímur Jónsson that chronicles Iceland's history, culture, and language. Divided into three parts, it covers Iceland's medieval golden age and subsequent decline. Published in the 17th century, it inspired Icelandic nationalism, revived interest in sagas, and introduced Norse mythology to Europe.
Boulos Fahmy Eskandar (b. 1 January 1957) is a Egyptian lawyer and the current President of the Supreme Constitutional Court of Egypt.

Azerbaijan–France relations refer to the diplomatic, economic, cultural, and educational ties between Azerbaijan and France. Diplomatic relations were established in 1992, with embassies in Baku and Paris. The two nations collaborate in areas such as trade, energy, and education, including the establishment of the French-Azerbaijani University. High-level visits and cultural exchanges have strengthened bilateral ties, despite occasional political tensions.

The Acheloos Painter was an ancient Greek vase painter active in Athens around 525–500 BCE, associated with the black-figure style and the Leagros Group. He is named after his "name vase," a black-figure amphora depicting Heracles battling the river god Acheloos, portrayed as a horned centaur. Known for his comic and detailed depictions, his works often juxtaposed heroic mythology with humorous or exaggerated elements. His notable pieces include scenes of mythological battles and everyday life.

Laura Rajnović Evetović is a Serbian economist, politician, and chief of Subotica's communal police. She served in the Subotica city assembly and the Assembly of Vojvodina as a member of the Serbian Progressive Party.

David Loud (born November 28, 1961, in Cincinnati, Ohio) is an American music supervisor, music director, conductor, vocal and dance arranger, pianist and actor. He is best known for his collaborations with and interpretations of the music of both Kander and Ebb and Stephen Sondheim.

A publicist is a person whose job is to generate and manage publicity for a company, a brand, or public figure – especially a celebrity – or for a work such as a book, film, or album. Publicists are public relations specialists who have the role to maintain and represent the images of individuals, rather than representing an entire corporation or business. Publicists are also hired by public figures who want to maintain or protect their image. Publicists brand their clients by getting magazine, TV, newspaper, and website coverage. Most top-level publicists work in private practice, handling multiple clients.

The term publicist was coined by the legal scholar Francis Lieber to describe the public-like role of internationalists during the late nineteenth century. Publicists are sometimes called flacks which traces back to Gene Flack, who was a well-known movie publicist in the 1930s.

The *Codex Boturini*, also known as the *Tira de la Peregrinación de los Mexica*, is an early 16th-century Aztec manuscript that chronicles the migration of the Mexica people from Aztlán to the Basin of Mexico, culminating just before the founding of Tenochtitlan. Created on amate paper and folded accordion-style, the codex is notable for its unfinished state, with red and black ink used to depict date glyphs, footprints, and key events. It is attributed to a single tlacuilo (scribe) familiar with Aztec writing systems. Acquired by Lorenzo Boturini Benaduci in the 18th century, the codex is now housed in Mexico's National Museum of Anthropology. It shares similarities with other Aztec codices, such as the Aubin and Mexicanus Codices.

Charles Jackson "Bud" Wildman (born June 3, 1946) is a Canadian former politician and educator who served as a Member of Provincial Parliament (MPP) in Ontario from 1975 to 1999, representing the Algoma riding for the New Democratic Party (NDP). He held several cabinet positions in Bob Rae's government, including Minister of Natural Resources, Minister responsible for Native Affairs, and Minister of Environment and Energy, where he advanced environmental policies and Indigenous relations. Wildman also served as interim leader of the Ontario NDP in 1996. After retiring from politics, he became a consultant and community leader, serving as Chair of the Board of Governors of Algoma University and receiving an honorary doctorate for his contributions.

St Andrew's Church, Buckland Monachorum, is a Grade I listed Church of England parish church in Devon, England, dedicated to Saint Andrew. Originally medieval, it was extensively rebuilt in the late 15th century and restored in 1868–69. The church is notable for its Drake aisle monuments, including works by John Bacon Senior and Junior, and Richard Westmacott. It houses an organ installed in 1849, later expanded, and a peal of eight bells, five of which date to 1723.

Pinecone Burke Provincial Park is a 380 km² protected area located in Metro Vancouver, British Columbia, Canada. Established in 1995, the park spans from Burke Mountain in Coquitlam to the western shore of Pitt Lake in Maple Ridge, with its northern boundary connecting to Garibaldi Provincial Park. Known for its diverse ecosystems, the park features old-growth forests, alpine lakes, wetlands, and a variety of wildlife, including black bears, grizzly bears, mountain goats, and numerous bird and fish species. It also supports sensitive species like the blue heron and tailed frog.

The park has historical significance, with remnants of early logging and mining activities, and is situated on the traditional territories of the Katzie, Kwikwetlem, and Stó:lō First Nations. Conservation efforts by groups like Friends of Burke Mountain led to its protection, and a management plan is being developed in collaboration with BC Parks, the Katzie First Nation, and the City of Coquitlam.

Visitors enjoy hiking, camping, canoeing, and winter activities like snowshoeing, with access to trails, peaks, and lakes. The park is part of the Lower Mainland Nature Legacy, emphasizing ecological preservation and sustainable recreation.
Howard Minion Radzely (born 1970) is an American lawyer who served as the United States Deputy Secretary of Labor from December 19, 2007 through February 2, 2009.

Helmut Zilk (1927–2008) was an Austrian politician and journalist who served as Mayor of Vienna from 1984 to 1994. His career was marked by a letter bomb attack and allegations of collaboration with the Czechoslovak secret police.

Joseph Tate is an American attorney who graduated from Villanova University in 1963 and its School of Law in 1966. He served as Lewis Libby's attorney during the CIA leak grand jury investigation, publicly denying Libby's involvement as journalist Judith Miller's confidential source. Tate maintained that Libby had provided a written waiver, and Miller was eventually released from jail after a phone call facilitated by their attorneys.
Boussens is a municipality in the district of Gros-de-Vaud in the canton of Vaud in Switzerland.

The Boston Fruit Company (1885-1899) was a fruit production and import business based in the port of Boston, Massachusetts. Andrew W. Preston and nine others established the firm to ship bananas and other fruit from the West Indies to north-eastern America. At the time, the banana was "considered a rare and delicious treat" in the United States. The major challenge for all banana importers was to get the highly perishable fruit to the American market before it spoiled." Ship captain Lorenzo Dow Baker served as president of the company and manager of the tropical division. By 1895"the corporation own[ed] nearly 40,000 acres, included in 35 plantations, and deep-water frontage [in Jamaica] in the harbors of Port Antonio and Port Morant. They owned their own lines of steamships, which they operated between those ports and Boston, Philadelphia and Baltimore. Besides carrying their own fruits, they carried some outside freight, and afford passenger accommodations for many tourists visiting the West-India Islands."

San Michele is a historic church in Cameri, Italy, originally documented in 1100 and rebuilt between 1583 and 1591. Featuring a Latin Cross layout, it houses 17th-century altars, 19th-century frescoes, and relics of Santa Gregoria. The facade, restored in 2002, includes statues of Saints Secondo, Roch, and the Archangel Michael, along with the town's original coat of arms. The current 42-meter belltower, built in 1827, replaced an earlier structure.

Soyuz TMA-07M was a 2012–2013 crewed spaceflight to the International Space Station (ISS) operated by Roscosmos. Launched on December 19, 2012, aboard a Soyuz-FG rocket from the Baikonur Cosmodrome, it carried Roman Romanenko, Chris Hadfield, and Thomas Marshburn. The spacecraft docked with the ISS for 143 days before safely returning to Earth on May 14, 2013.
James Fitton R.A. (11 February 1899 – 2 May 1982) was an English painter, lithographer and theatre set designer, and a founder member of the left-wing Artists' International Association.

El Valle de Arroyo Seco is a census-designated place (CDP) in Santa Fe County, New Mexico, United States. Covering an area of 5.13 square miles, it had a population of 1,431 as of the 2020 census. The community is predominantly Hispanic or Latino, with 69.97% of residents identifying as such in the 2000 census. The area is served by Pojoaque Valley Public Schools and Española Public Schools, with respective high schools being Pojoaque Valley High School and Española Valley High School.
Todd Scott is the owner and publisher of National Business Review, alongside his wife, Jackie Scott.

Robert Prosky (born Robert Józef Porzuczek; December 13, 1930 – December 8, 2008) was an American actor known for his extensive career in film, television, and theater. A Philadelphia native of Polish descent, he appeared in over 100 stage productions, including notable roles in *Death of a Salesman* and *Glengarry Glen Ross*. Prosky also had prominent roles in films such as *Christine*, *The Natural*, and *Dead Man Walking*, and was a regular on television shows like *Hill Street Blues* and *Veronica's Closet*. He received multiple award nominations and continued performing until his death.
Hakea rostrata, common name beaked hakea, is a flowering plant in the family Proteaceae, native to South Australia and Victoria.
Cheick Oumar Dabo (born January 12, 1981) is a retired Malian footballer who played as a striker.

Radio Dabanga (Arabic: راديو دبنقا) is a shortwave radio station, TV and online news magazine, that serves Sudan with daily reports on the latest political, economic and social information, including health issues and social programs such as Lost and Found. The shortwave radio has been broadcasting since 1 December 2008. Current broadcasts last for a total of one hour each day.

While Radio Dabanga only broadcast on shortwave radio for the first few years, the station now has several channels. The editors have innovated and experimented in the sphere of new media, often at the request of their listeners. These include Satellite TV, Facebook, WhatsApp, and SoundCloud. Depending on the medium, Radio Dabanga produces news in Sudanese Arabic and/or English.

Shortwave radio remains the mainstay of the radio station; one quarter of all Sudanese people are illiterate, a number that is relatively higher in Sudan’s remote areas and camps for displaced people. Many people here have no lifeline to the outside world other than telephone and radio, so it is Radio Dabanga’s mission to provide this.

In mathematics, the Tor functor is a homological algebra tool that measures the failure of flatness of modules over a ring. For a ring \( R \), the Tor groups, denoted \( \text{Tor}^R_i(A, B) \), are defined as the left derived functors of the tensor product \( A \otimes_R B \), where \( A \) and \( B \) are \( R \)-modules. These groups are computed using projective resolutions of \( A \) or \( B \), and their homology is taken at each degree \( i \). The construction is independent of the choice of projective resolution, as shown by Cartan and Eilenberg.

For commutative rings, the Tor groups are \( R \)-modules, while for non-commutative rings, they are generally abelian groups. The Tor functor is a bifunctor, meaning it is functorial in both module variables, and it has applications in algebraic topology and algebraic geometry.

An acoustic torpedo is a torpedo that aims itself by listening for characteristic sounds of its target or by searching for it using sonar (acoustic homing). Acoustic torpedoes are usually designed for medium-range use, and often fired from a submarine.

The first passive acoustic torpedoes were developed nearly simultaneously by the United States Navy and the Germans during World War II. The Germans developed the G7e/T4 Falke, which was first deployed by the submarines U-603, U-758 and U-221 in March 1943. Few of these torpedoes were actually used and quickly phased out of service in favor of the T4's successor, the G7es T5 Zaunkönig torpedo in August 1943. The T5 first saw widespread use in September 1943 against North Atlantic escort vessels and merchant ships in convoys.

On the Allied side, the US Navy developed the Mark 24 mine, which was actually an aircraft launched, anti-submarine passive acoustic homing torpedo. The first production Mk. 24s were delivered to the U.S. Navy in March 1943, and it scored its first verified combat kills in May 1943. About 204 torpedoes were launched against submarine targets, with 37 Axis submarines being sunk and a further 18 damaged.

Since its introduction, the acoustic torpedo has proven to be an effective weapon against surface ships as well as serving as an anti-submarine weapon. Today, acoustic torpedoes are mostly used against submarines.

The Corps Franc de la Montagne Noire (Free Corps of the Black Mountain) or C.F.M.N, was a fighting unit of the French resistance, during World War II. It was based in the mountainous area of the southwestern end of the Massif Central known as Montagne Noire (black mountain). Its mission was to prevent German troops from going to reinforce the landing fronts in Normandy and Provence.

Kizzuwatna (or Kizzuwadna; in Ancient Egyptian Kode or Qode), is the name of an ancient Anatolian kingdom in the 2nd millennium BC. It was situated in the highlands of southeastern Anatolia, near the Gulf of İskenderun, in modern-day Turkey. It encircled the Taurus Mountains and the Ceyhan River. The centre of the kingdom was the city of Kummanni, in the highlands. In a later era, the same region was known as Cilicia.

Jefferson Joseph DeBlanc (February 15, 1921 – November 22, 2007) was a United States Marine Corps fighter pilot and Medal of Honor recipient for his actions during World War II. Born in Lockport, Louisiana, he enlisted in the U.S. Navy Reserve in 1941 before joining the Marine Corps in 1942. DeBlanc became an "ace in a day" on January 31, 1943, during a mission over the Solomon Islands, where he shot down five Japanese aircraft despite critical fuel issues and was later rescued after bailing out near enemy territory.

After the war, DeBlanc pursued a career in education, earning multiple degrees and teaching math and physics. He retired as a colonel from the Marine Corps Reserve in 1972. DeBlanc passed away in 2007 and was the last surviving Medal of Honor recipient from Louisiana for World War II.
Wahyt Sähetmyradowiç Orazsähedow (born 26 January 1992) is a Turkmenistani football forward who plays for Turkmenistan.
