In 1934, the Rosalie received a facelift, introducing the "NH" or "B-series" models with updated styling. Despite the launch of its revolutionary successor, the Traction Avant, the Rosalie remained in production until 1938, with over 95,000 units built. The Rosalie is remembered for its competitive pricing, diverse body styles, and role in Citroën's transition to more innovative automotive designs.

Alexandra Pringle Hon. FRSL (born 1952 or 1953) is a British publisher and editor-in-chief of Bloomsbury Publishing since 1999. She previously worked at Virago, Hamish Hamilton, and as a literary agent.

The LMS Hughes Crab was a class of 2-6-0 steam locomotives designed by George Hughes for the London, Midland and Scottish Railway (LMS). Introduced in the 1920s, the design featured advanced components for its time, including long travel valves and a new boiler. Despite modifications by Henry Fowler, the locomotives performed well, particularly in Scotland, where they were favored for heavy mineral work. Nicknamed "Crabs" due to their appearance or motion, the class was later succeeded by the LMS Stanier Mogul. Withdrawn by 1967, three examples have been preserved.

The Gateway, also known as Curtains, is a 2015 horror film that was directed by Jaron Henrie-McCrea. The film had its world premiere on August 31, 2015 at Film4 FrightFest and stars Danni Smith and Tim Lueke as two activists that investigate a series of disappearing shower curtains in Smith's apartment.
"Motiv8" (pronounced "motivate") is a song by American rapper J. Cole, released on April 20, 2018 from his fifth studio album, KOD.

Paul Alden Brinegar Jr. (December 19, 1917 – March 27, 1995) was an American character actor best known for his roles in Westerns. He appeared in over 100 Western films and television shows between 1946 and 1994, often portraying grizzled cowboy sidekicks. Brinegar is most remembered for his role as George Washington Wishbone, the cook on the CBS series *Rawhide* (1959–1966), and as Jelly Hoskins on *Lancer* (1968–1970). He also appeared in films such as *High Plains Drifter* (1973) and *Maverick* (1994). Brinegar began acting after serving in the U.S. Navy during World War II and pursued a long career in both film and television. He died of emphysema in Los Angeles at the age of 77.

CCM is a Canadian bicycle manufacturer based in Montreal, Quebec.

"CCM" was originally an initialism for Canada Cycle & Motor Co. Ltd. However, the company would eventually split into two separate entities both maintaining the CCM trademark, one maintaining bicycle manufacturing and the other, CCM (The Hockey Company), producing hockey equipment.
Events in the year 1980 in Japan. It corresponds to Shōwa 55 (昭和55年) in the Japanese calendar.
Art Quimby (July 1, 1933 – December 6, 2010) was an American college basketball player for UConn, known for his record-breaking rebounding achievements.

Greenwood Village is a home rule municipality located in Arapahoe County, Colorado, United States. Incorporated on September 19, 1950, it is part of the Denver-Aurora-Lakewood Metropolitan Area and includes portions of the Denver Technological Center (DTC). The city had a population of 15,691 as of the 2020 Census. Originally developed in the 1860s as Greenwood Ranch, it transitioned from a gold rush settlement to a farming community by the early 1900s. Greenwood Village is known for its affluent population, high median household income, and notable attractions such as the Fiddler's Green Amphitheatre, an 18,000-seat outdoor venue. It is served by the Cherry Creek School District and hosts several major employers within the DTC.

*Acacia parramattensis*, commonly known as the Parramatta wattle or Sydney green wattle, is a fast-growing shrub or tree native to central New South Wales, Australia. It typically grows 2 to 15 meters tall and features bipinnate leaves, yellow spherical flowers blooming from November to February, and flat, grey-black seed pods. Described in 1962 by botanist Mary Tindale, its name references Parramatta, near Sydney. Found in dry sclerophyll forests and woodlands, it thrives on various soil types and elevations up to 900 meters. Ecologically, it supports wildlife, including birds, caterpillars, and beetles, and regenerates after fire. It holds cultural significance in Dharawal stories and is used in revegetation projects.

Kemal Deen is a Sri Lankan professor of surgery at the University of Kelaniya, known for his contributions to medical education and research. Educated at Trinity College, Kandy, and the University of Peradeniya, he later trained at the University of Birmingham and the Mayo Clinic. Deen played a key role in introducing liver transplantation to Sri Lanka and has published extensively in peer-reviewed journals. He is a Fellow of the Royal College of Surgeons and holds visiting professorships at several international institutions, including the Tata Memorial Cancer Centre in India.

Gareth James Delany (born 28 April 1997) is an Irish cricketer. He made his international debut for the Ireland cricket team in July 2019. In January 2020, he was one of nineteen players to be awarded a central contract from Cricket Ireland, the first year in which all contracts were awarded on a full-time basis.

The Slutsk uprising (November 27 – December 31, 1920) was an armed resistance by Belarusian forces against Soviet rule during the aftermath of the Soviet-Polish War and the Russian Civil War. Centered in Slutsk, Belarus, the uprising was led by local militias advocating for Belarusian independence. Despite initial successes, the poorly equipped Belarusian forces were ultimately defeated by the Red Army, forcing them to retreat into Polish territory.

Jasaw Chan Kʼawiil I (died 734) was an ajaw of the Maya city of Tikal, reigning from 682 to 734. Known for his military victory over Calakmul in 695, his reign marked Tikal's resurgence after a prolonged decline. He was buried in Tikal Temple I, a prominent stepped pyramid, while Tikal Temple II served as the tomb for his wife, Lady Lahan Unen Moʼ. His son, Yikʼin Chan Kʼawiil, succeeded him.

Joachim Herrmann (born 21 September 1956) is a German politician and member of the Christian Social Union (CSU). He has served as the Bavarian State Minister of the Interior since 2007 and as Additional Deputy Minister President of Bavaria since 2008. Herrmann has held various leadership roles within the CSU, including faction leader in the Landtag of Bavaria (2003–2007) and Deputy General Secretary (1997–1998). He has been a member of the Landtag since 1994, representing Erlangen-Stadt. Herrmann has participated in federal coalition negotiations and served as a delegate to multiple Federal Conventions. During his tenure, he has overseen responses to significant incidents of violence in Bavaria and has taken strong positions on issues such as EU-Turkey relations. Born in Munich, Herrmann is a lawyer, married, and has three children.

Howard John Ehmke (April 24, 1894 – March 17, 1959) was an American baseball pitcher. He played professional baseball for 16 years from 1914 to 1930, including 15 seasons in Major League Baseball for the Buffalo Blues (1915), Detroit Tigers (1916–1917, 1919–1922), Boston Red Sox (1923–1926), and Philadelphia Athletics (1926–1930).

Ehmke compiled a career win–loss record of 166–166 with a 3.75 earned run average (ERA). His greatest success was with the Red Sox, including a no-hitter and his only 20-win season in 1923. Ehmke still holds the American League record for fewest hits allowed (one) in two consecutive starts. Ehmke also ranks sixteenth all-time in hitting batters. He hit 137 batters in his career and led the American League in the category seven times, including a career-high 23 in 1922. Ehmke is best known for being the surprise starter who won Game 1 of the 1929 World Series for the Athletics at the age of 35.

After retiring from baseball, he started his own company that began making tarpaulins to cover baseball diamonds during rain.

Dingli (Lang-mt) is a village in the Northern Region of Malta, with a population of 3,608 as of March 2014. It is 13 kilometres (8 miles) from the capital Valletta and two kilometers (1.2 miles) from the nearest town, Rabat. The village lies on a plateau some 250 metres above sea level, which is one of the highest points of Malta. The area provides not only open sea views over the tiny, uninhabited isle of Filfla, but is also a good vantage point over Malta. From the cliffs there are also views of the nearby Buskett Gardens and Verdala Palace. The Cliffs were also the site of the mysterious death of Mike Mansholt.

Polad Israyil oglu Hashimov (January 2, 1975 – July 14, 2020) was an Azerbaijani major general and National Hero of Azerbaijan, known for his service in the Azerbaijani Armed Forces during key conflicts, including the First Nagorno-Karabakh War, the Four-Day War, and the Tovuz clashes. Born in Vandam, Gabala District, he pursued a military career, graduating from the Baku Higher Combined Arms Command School and later earning a master’s degree in military leadership. Rising through the ranks, he became a prominent military figure, respected for his leadership and dedication.

Hashimov played a significant role in the Four-Day War of 2016, where he led successful operations and was later promoted to major general. In July 2020, he commanded Azerbaijani forces during the Tovuz clashes on the Armenia-Azerbaijan border, where he was killed in action, becoming the first Azerbaijani general to die in combat. His death sparked widespread mourning and protests in Azerbaijan, highlighting his popularity and the public's frustration with the ongoing conflict.

Posthumously declared a National Hero of Azerbaijan, Hashimov's legacy is honored through books, songs, documentaries, and streets named after him. He remains a symbol of patriotism and sacrifice in Azerbaijani society.

Mysterious Girlfriend X (Japanese: 謎の彼女X, Hepburn: Nazo no Kanojo Ekkusu) is a Japanese manga written and illustrated by Riichi Ueshiba. It was originally published as a one-shot story in 2004 before becoming a serialized comic in Kodansha's Monthly Afternoon magazine in 2006. The manga is licensed in North America by Vertical. An anime adaptation by Hoods Entertainment aired in Japan from April to June 2012, with an original video animation episode released in August 2012.

16th Street Park, officially named DiDomenico – 16th Street Park, is a public park in Bayonne, New Jersey, developed as a Works Progress Administration project. It features various recreational facilities, including sports courts, fields, walking paths, and a municipal pool. The park honors former mayor G. Thomas DiDomenico.

Goodwin Park is a city park and golf course located in Hartford and Wethersfield, Connecticut. Opened to the public in 1901, it covers 227 acres (92 ha) and offers a playground, community pool and a 27-hole golf course.

In December each year, the park hosts Holiday Lights Fantasia, a Greater Hartford tradition since 1999 where illuminated structures festoon the interior road and thousands of cars stream through the park to enjoy 1 million lights.

The Fruitmarket Gallery is a contemporary art gallery in Edinburgh, Scotland.

Since its opening in 1974, the gallery has become part of the Scottish contemporary art scene. After a period of closure to undergo a significant renovation, the gallery reopened in 2021 with expanded exhibition space and facilities.

Woodford Island is a river island located in the Clarence River in New South Wales, Australia. It is the largest inland island in the Southern Hemisphere and home to approximately 300 residents. The island features a mix of floodplains and a central mountain ridge, supporting industries like sugarcane farming and cattle production. Historically significant, it was an early European settlement and a hub for timber and agriculture. Key access points include two bridges and a ferry, and notable sites include Brushgrove, Ilarwill, and the Maclean District Golf Course.

The Eurasian otter (Lutra lutra), also known as the European otter, Eurasian river otter, common otter, and Old World otter, is a semiaquatic mammal native to Eurasia. The most widely distributed member of the otter subfamily (Lutrinae) of the weasel family (Mustelidae), it is found in the waterways and coasts of Europe, many parts of Asia, and parts of northern Africa. The Eurasian otter has a diet mainly of fish, and is strongly territorial. It is endangered in some parts of its range, but is recovering in others.

Homaro Cantu (September 23, 1976 – April 14, 2015) was an American chef, inventor, entrepreneur, and advocate known for his pioneering work in molecular gastronomy and his innovative approach to food. Born in Tacoma, Washington, Cantu overcame a challenging childhood, including homelessness, to attend the Western Culinary Institute. He gained prominence as a sous chef under Charlie Trotter before opening his acclaimed Chicago restaurant, Moto, in 2004. At Moto, Cantu became renowned for his experimental dishes, such as edible menus, carbonated fruit, and laser-cooked food, earning the restaurant a Michelin star in 2012. He also opened iNG and Berrista, focusing on "flavor-tripping" with miracle berries.

Cantu was a prolific inventor, holding over 100 patents for culinary innovations, including edible paper and a polymer cooking box. He appeared on numerous television shows, including *Iron Chef America*, and co-hosted *Future Food*. A passionate advocate for combating world hunger, he envisioned using his inventions to provide sustainable food solutions. Cantu also founded the Trotter Project, a nonprofit promoting culinary education.

Despite his success, Cantu faced personal and professional challenges, including a lawsuit shortly before his death. On April 14, 2015, he died by suicide in Chicago. Remembered as a visionary and a "mad scientist" of cuisine, Cantu left a lasting legacy in the culinary world, blending science, creativity, and social impact. His life and work were chronicled in the 2016 documentary *Insatiable: The Homaro Cantu Story*.

Rangi Ruru Girls' School is an independent, single-sex girls' school in Merivale, Christchurch, New Zealand, offering intermediate and secondary education (Years 7–13) with boarding facilities. Founded in 1889 by the Gibson family, it was renamed "Rangi Ruru" in 1891, a name gifted by Māori chief Pāora Taki. The school moved to its current site in 1923 and has been governed by a Presbyterian Church-affiliated board since 1946. Known for its strong academic performance, Rangi Ruru consistently ranks among New Zealand's top schools.

Brodie Smith (born in Jacksonville, Florida) is an American professional Ultimate Disc League player, disc golfer, and trick shot performer. He began playing ultimate frisbee at Nease High School in Ponte Vedra, Florida, and later joined the University of Florida's ultimate team in 2006. During his college career, the team won two National College Championships, in 2006 and 2010. 

Smith started his professional ultimate career in 2011 with the Indianapolis Alleycats of the American Ultimate Disc League (AUDL) and later played for teams including the Chicago Wildfire, Dallas Roughnecks, and Jacksonville Cannons. His career was interrupted by injuries, including a torn ACL and meniscus, which sidelined him for two seasons.

Smith gained widespread recognition for his frisbee trick shot videos, starting with his 2011 YouTube video "Frisbee Trick Shots," which garnered millions of views. His creative and challenging trick shots have been featured on ESPN SportsCenter's Top Plays and have helped promote awareness of ultimate frisbee as a sport. Smith has performed trick shots worldwide, collaborated with YouTube group Dude Perfect, and appeared on a Japanese game show. He continues to share his content on social media platforms, inspiring fans globally.

*The City of Lost Children* (French: *La Cité des enfants perdus*) is a 1995 French science fantasy film directed by Marc Caro and Jean-Pierre Jeunet. The story follows a carnival strongman, One (Ron Perlman), and a young orphan, Miette (Judith Vittet), as they attempt to rescue One's kidnapped brother from Krank (Daniel Emilfork), a malevolent being who steals children's dreams. Known for its surreal visuals and steampunk aesthetic, the film received generally favorable reviews for its design and atmosphere, though its narrative was critiqued as confusing. Despite its critical acclaim, it was a box-office bomb, grossing $11 million against an $18 million budget.

Isometric projection is a method for visually representing three-dimensional objects in two dimensions in technical and engineering drawings. It is an axonometric projection in which the three coordinate axes appear equally foreshortened and the angle between any two of them is 120 degrees.

Kennicott Grove is an area of prairie and wooded lands that includes the home of John Kennicott (1802–1863) and his family, including his son Robert Kennicott (1835–1866). John Kennicott was an agriculturalist and a doctor. Robert Kennicott was a naturalist and an explorer, who founded the Chicago Academy of Sciences. The grove is 123 acres (0.50 km2) in size. It is located near the intersection of Milwaukee Ave. and Lake Ave. in Glenview, Illinois, in the United States. Kennicott Grove was declared a National Historic Landmark in 1976. The site is maintained by the Glenview Park District as The Grove National Historic Landmark or also The Grove, and contains a nature interpretive center, historic buildings (some original and some recreated), and nature trails.

The Moral Obligation to Be Intelligent is an essay in the collection The Moral Obligation to Be Intelligent, and Other Essays, published in 1915 by John Erskine, English professor at Columbia University.

The essay was first read before the Phi Beta Kappa Society of Amherst College, where Erskine taught before joining Columbia. Later, it was published in quarterly magazine The Hibbert Journal in 1914. During his tenure at Columbia from 1909 and 1937, Erskine formulated General Honors Course. In the early 1920s he started teaching a great books course at Columbia, which later founded the influential Great Books movement.

Religious Zionism is an ideology that combines Zionism with Orthodox Judaism, advocating for the establishment and support of a Jewish state in the Land of Israel as a fulfillment of biblical prophecy and divine will. Rooted in the teachings of Rabbi Zvi Hirsch Kalischer and later developed by Rabbi Abraham Isaac Kook, Religious Zionism views the return of Jews to their ancestral homeland as a step toward ultimate redemption and the coming of the Messiah. Rabbi Kook emphasized that Zionism, even when led by secular Jews, was part of a divine plan to restore Jewish sovereignty and enable the observance of Torah commandments specific to the Land of Israel.

The movement has faced opposition from some Haredi Jews, who view Zionism as premature and contrary to Jewish law. Religious Zionism has influenced political parties, such as the Mizrachi movement and its successors, and established educational institutions, including yeshivot and midrashot. It also promotes military service through programs like Hesder, integrating Torah study with service in the Israel Defense Forces.

The architecture of the Deccan sultanates, which flourished in southern India between the 14th and 17th centuries, is renowned for its distinctive blend of Persian, Indian, and Islamic styles. This architectural legacy is evident in the monuments of five key sultanates: Ahmadnagar, Bidar, Bijapur, Golkonda, and Gulbarga.

Ahmadnagar is home to palaces like Farah Bakhsh Bagh and tombs of nobles and saints, such as Salabat Khan and Shah Sharif. Bidar features the grand Bidar Fort, the Mahmud Gawan Madrasa, and the Bahmani and Barid Shahi tombs. Bijapur is celebrated for the Gol Gumbaz, with the second-largest pre-modern dome, and the elegant Ibrahim Rouza. Other notable structures include the Jami Masjid and several palaces.

Golkonda is famed for its impregnable fort, the Qutb Shahi tombs, and Hyderabad's iconic Char Minar. Gulbarga, the Bahmani capital, boasts the Gulbarga Fort, the Great Mosque, and the Haft Gumbad tomb complex. Collectively, these monuments reflect the artistic and engineering achievements of the Deccan sultanates.

Charles Percy Abbott (born 10 June 1939) is a former Australian rules footballer who played with Hawthorn in the Victorian Football League (VFL), a polo player, and the recipient of the Australian Sports Medal.
Eric Reed (born 1963 or 1964) is an American horse trainer. He is the trainer of Rich Strike, the winner of the 2022 Kentucky Derby.

Arnot Art Museum, opened 1913, is a municipal art museum located at 235 Lake Street in Elmira, New York. Its permanent collection includes 17th-, 18th-, and 19th-century European paintings; and 19th- and 20th-century American art. Its 21st-century collection focuses on contemporary representational art. The building is a contributing property in the Elmira Civic Historic District.

The Swiss Social Archives (Lang-de, Lang-fr) in Zurich is a historical archive, an academic library, a collection of documentation and a research facility specialising in social issues and social movements. The Swiss Confederation recognizes the archives as the country's leading research facility for social issues and social movements. The Social Archives run their own research endowment fund, the Ellen Rifkin Hill Foundation. The SSA play an important role in communicating scholarship to the broader community. They have convened several exhibitions, published essay collections on Swiss social history and conduct lecture series, presentations and information sessions. The archives work with Swiss secondary and tertiary educational institutions, archives and libraries and with similar institutions abroad. The SSA are a founding member of the International Association of Labour History Institutions (IALHI).
Kaberamaido is a town in Eastern Uganda. It is the main municipal, administrative and commercial center of Kaberamaido District and is the location of the headquarters for the district.

Anything But Conservative (Lang-fr or N'importe quoi d'autre que conservateur), also known as the ABC campaign and Vote ABC, is a political campaign which aims to defeat the Conservatives in Canadian federal elections. It encourages strategic voting.

Canadian federal elections use a first-past-the-post voting system, which allows a party with minority support among the population to win the majority of the seats in the Parliament. Given the make-up of existing political parties in the 21st century in Canada, the centre and centre-left vote tends to split between the federal NDP, Liberals and Greens, which allows for the Conservatives to win a disproportional number of seats in the parliament compared to their popular support.

It is estimated by ABC campaigners that widespread strategic voting in 2015 federal elections decreased the number of Conservative seats by over 72.
Frank S. Besson Jr. (1910–1985) was a United States Army general and logistics innovator, serving as the first commander of the Army Materiel Command.

Kachche Heere is a 1982 Bollywood Hindi action film directed by Narender Bedi, starring Feroz Khan, Reena Roy, Danny Denzongpa, Aruna Irani and Shakti Kapoor. The movie was produced by Netra Pal Singh and the music was composed by Rahul Dev Burman. The film was somewhat a sequel to the 1974 Blockbuster Khote Sikkay. The film had the same star cast and almost the exact story line. Among few changes were that Ranjeet was replaced by Shakti Kapoor. The film was released on 12 February 1982.

George Sifakis is an American political advisor who served as the Director of the Office of Public Liaison under President Donald Trump from March 6, 2017, to September 25, 2017. Prior to this role, he was a member of the George W. Bush administration and played a key role in the Trump transition team, focusing on congressional relations. Sifakis initially joined the Trump administration as Special Assistant to the President and Deputy Director of Public Liaison before being promoted to Director. He left the position in September 2017, with Johnny DeStefano succeeding him in an interim capacity.

Sifakis holds a Bachelor of Arts from Rhode Island College, a Master of Science in Political Science from Suffolk University, and a paralegal certification from Northeastern University. He also attended the Harvard Kennedy School Executive Education program and the Southern New England School of Law. A Greek Orthodox Christian, Sifakis resides in Alexandria, Virginia, with his wife, Adriana, and their three children.

Peter George Baines is an Australian geophysicist. He is an Honorary Senior Fellow at University of Melbourne and Honorary Secretary at Royal Society of Victoria.

Baines has published over 150 research articles and is the author of Topographic Effects in Stratified Flows, published in 1995 (paperback edition 1998). His major research areas include climate dynamics on the decadal time scale, volcano dynamics and Rossby wave hydraulics.

He is or has been the member of various societies such as the American Geophysical Union, Australian Meteorological and Oceanographic Society (AMOS - this new Society's first President, in 1988), Royal Society of Victoria and the Royal Meteorological Society. From 1960 to 1966, he was a member of the Royal Australian Naval Reserve, to the rank of Sub-lieutenant.

The ZBD-03 or Type 03 (industrial designation WZ506) is a Chinese airborne infantry fighting vehicle. It features a light-weight chassis and hydropneumatic suspension for airborne operations. Early prototypes received the designation ZLC-2000.
Lugazi University (LU), was a private university in Uganda. The university was founded in 2007. It closed in December 2010.

*Soul Power* is the debut studio album by American musician Curtis Harding, released on May 6, 2014, by Burger Records. Recorded at Living Room Studios in Atlanta, the album blends soul with influences from gospel, indie rock, garage, and disco. Harding's versatile vocals and genre-spanning compositions received critical praise, though some critics noted a lack of innovation.

Dingli (Maltese: Ħad-Dingli) is a village in the Northern Region of Malta, located on a plateau near the Dingli Cliffs, the highest point in Malta. Covering an area of 5.7 km², it had a population of 3,711 as of January 2019. The village is named after Sir Thomas Dingley, an English knight of the Order of St. John. Dingli has historical significance, with evidence of Phoenician, Carthaginian, and Roman settlements, including rock-cut tombs and Roman baths.

The parish, established in 1678, is dedicated to the Assumption of Our Lady, with its titular feast celebrated annually. Dingli saw significant development in the 20th century, including population growth and improved infrastructure.

WU polyomavirus (also known as WU virus, WUPyV, or Human polyomavirus 4) is a virus of the family Polyomaviridae. It was discovered in 2007 in samples of human respiratory secretions, originally from a child patient in Australia who presented with clinical signs of pneumonia and in whom other common respiratory viruses were not detected. Follow-up studies identified the presence of WU virus in respiratory secretion samples from patients in Australia and the United States, suggesting that, like other human polyomaviruses, WU virus is widely distributed.

The Schillinger System of Musical Composition, named after Joseph Schillinger (1895–1943) is a method of musical composition based on mathematical processes. It comprises theories of rhythm, harmony, melody, counterpoint, form and semantics, purporting to offer a systematic and non-genre approach to music analysis and composition; a descriptive rather than prescriptive grammar of music.

While it influenced some prominent figures, such as Lawrence Berk (founder of the Berklee College of Music) and George Gershwin (likely influencing the piece "I Got Rhythm Variations"), it began to fall out of favor in the 1960s after receiving criticisms for being over-complicated and pseudo-scientific, and was removed from the Berklee curriculum.

DXWT (92.3 FM), broadcasting as 92.3 Wild FM, is a radio station owned and operated by the UM Broadcasting Network. It serves as the flagship station of the Wild FM network. The station's studio is located at the UMBN Media Center, C. Bangoy cor. Palma Gil St., Davao City, and its transmitter is located along Broadcast Ave., Shrine Hills, Matina, Davao City.

May Song Vang (February 5, 1951 – August 5, 2013) was an American Hmong community leader and activist. She was the widow of General Vang Pao, a former member of the Royal Lao Army and prominent Hmong American leader, who died in 2011. May Song Vang became a more prominent symbol of the Hmong American community in California and the rest of the United States after the death of her husband.

Oleg Vladimirovich Losev (Lang-ru, sometimes spelled Lossev or Lossew in English) (10 May 1903 – 22 January 1942) was a Russian scientist and inventor who made significant discoveries in the field of semiconductor junctions and the light emitting diode (LED).

Although he was never able to complete a formal education and never held a research position, Losev conducted some of the earliest research into semiconductors, publishing 43 papers and receiving 16 "author's certificates" (the Soviet version of patents) for his discoveries. He observed light emission from carborundum point-contact junctions, constructing a light-emitting diode (LED), did the first research on them, proposed the first correct theory of how they worked, and used them in practical applications such as electroluminescence. He explored negative resistance in semiconductor junctions, and was first to use them practically for amplification, building the first solid-state amplifiers, electronic oscillators, and superheterodyne radio receivers, 25 years before the invention of the transistor. However his achievements were overlooked, and languished unknown for half a century before being recognized in the late 20th and early 21st century.

Contour crafting is an innovative 3D printing technology developed by Behrokh Khoshnevis for automated construction, capable of building structures such as homes in a single day with minimal material waste and environmental impact. First gaining attention in the early 2000s, the technology has been supported by organizations like Caterpillar Inc. and NASA, which explored its potential for constructing bases on the Moon and Mars using primarily local materials. In 2009, the ACASA project was launched to commercialize contour crafting, with Khoshnevis serving as CTO. By 2017, the Contour Crafting Corporation, led by Khoshnevis, partnered with Doka Ventures to advance the technology, with plans to deliver the first printers soon after. The method has been highlighted for its efficiency, sustainability, and potential to revolutionize construction both on Earth and in extraterrestrial environments.

Substance Church is a nondenominational, Evangelical Christian, megachurch in Minneapolis–Saint Paul, Minnesota, United States. In 2010, Outreach Magazine ranked the church as the 21st fastest growing congregation in the United States (8th in terms of growth percentages). Because the church has integrated ultra-contemporary worship, cell church, and cafe church methods into a singular megachurch setting, Substance is often cited as being "trend-setting".

Malice Mizer was a Japanese visual kei band active from 1992 to 2001, known for their theatrical performances, elaborate costumes, and diverse musical styles, which ranged from gothic rock and neoclassical darkwave to art rock and synthpop. Founded by guitarists Mana and Közi, the band underwent three distinct eras, each defined by a different vocalist: Tetsu, Gackt, and Klaha. Their early work featured gothic and classical influences, while the Gackt era brought a more colorful, art rock style, and the Klaha era embraced a darker, gothic aesthetic.

The band achieved significant success, particularly during the Gackt era, with their major-label debut album *Merveilles* and performances at venues like Nippon Budokan. However, internal changes, including Gackt's departure and the death of drummer Kami in 1999, marked a turning point. Malice Mizer disbanded in 2001, though members pursued solo projects, with Mana founding Moi dix Mois and a fashion label.

The band has occasionally reunited for special events, such as the Deep Sanctuary concerts, celebrating their legacy. Malice Mizer remains influential in the visual kei scene and is remembered for their innovative artistry and dramatic flair.

Chuck Hunt (March 2, 1954 – December 21, 2021) was an American educator, coach, and the first-ever First Gentleman of Massachusetts, serving from 2001 to 2003 during the governorship of his wife, Jane Swift. A native of Adams, Massachusetts, Hunt worked as a coach and construction business owner before becoming a stay-at-home father during Swift's tenure. He died in 2021 from complications of Alport syndrome, a genetic kidney disease he had lived with throughout his life.
John Graham is a Canadian former Native American activist. He is best known for being the person who shot and killed fellow American Indian Movement activist Anna Mae Aquash.

DeForest is a village in Dane County, Wisconsin, United States, with a population of 10,811 as of the 2020 census. Named after Isaac De Forest, who purchased the land in 1856, the village spans 8.57 square miles and is part of the Madison Metropolitan Area. DeForest is served by the DeForest Area School District and the DeForest Area Public Library, which reflects the area's Norwegian heritage in its design. The village is accessible via major highways, including I-39/90/94 and US-51, and is near Dane County Regional Airport.

The Coaldale Big Green was an American football team based in Coaldale, Pennsylvania, that operated from 1912 to 1933. Originally formed as Coaldale Football by the Coaldale Athletic Association and James H. Gildea, the team adopted the "Big Green" name in 1917 after introducing green jerseys. Known for its reliance on local talent, the team won the Curran Cup in 1921, 1922, and 1923. In 1924, Coaldale helped establish the Anthracite League but left after disputes over player recruitment rules. The team later joined the Eastern League of Professional Football in 1926 before returning to independent play and folding around 1933.
DeForest is a village in Dane County, Wisconsin, United States, along the Yahara River. The population was 10,811 at the 2020 census. It is part of the Madison Metropolitan Statistical Area.
Nicolas Marroc (born December 5, 1986) is a French racing driver who competed in Formula Renault and Formula Three championships.

Islam in Brazil has a history dating back to the transatlantic slave trade, when African Muslims were brought to the country as slaves. Brazil received more enslaved Muslims than any other region in the Americas, and their influence culminated in the Malê Revolt of 1835, the largest urban slave uprising in the Americas. Following this period, Islam in Brazil was shaped by immigration from the Middle East and Southeast Asia, particularly in the 20th century. Today, Brazil's Muslim population, primarily Sunni, is concentrated in São Paulo and Paraná, with over 150 mosques nationwide. Recent decades have seen a rise in conversions to Islam and growing cultural visibility.

The pluperfect, also known as the past perfect, is a grammatical construction used to describe an action that was completed before another past event. It combines past tense and perfect aspect, often indicating a sequence of events in the past. For example, in English, the pluperfect is formed with the auxiliary verb "had" and the past participle of the main verb, as in "I had eaten before she arrived." It is also used in hypothetical or conditional contexts, such as "If I had known, I would have acted differently."

The pluperfect exists in many languages, with variations in formation. Some, like Latin and Romanian, use inflection, while others, such as French, Italian, and Spanish, rely on auxiliary verbs. Its usage and forms vary widely across Germanic, Romance, Slavic, and other language families, with some languages preserving archaic or literary forms.

Li Shuai (born 18 August 1982) is a retired Chinese footballer who played as a goalkeeper. He had a 20-year career with Qingdao Jonoon, Guangzhou Evergrande, and Shanghai Shenhua, winning multiple titles, including the Chinese FA Cup in 2017 and 2019.

Faces of Ground Zero: A Photographic Tribute to America's Heroes was a traveling photo exhibition about the September 11 attacks. It was shown at several major cities in the United States, aiming to educate the public about the impact of modern urban terrorism. Faces of Ground Zero was one of the most widely seen exhibits about 9/11 and its aftermath.

The exhibit consisted of life-size photographs (9 ft × 4 ft framed images) of emergency workers, survivors, and relatives of victims of the attacks; some 272 people in all. (Some of the portraits included two or three subjects.) The aim was to capture the sense of loss, pain, and bravery of the time.

The touring exhibit was open to the public, free of charge. Made by photographer Joe McNally, in the Moby C Studio, a few blocks from the "Ground Zero" World Trade Center site in the immediate aftermath of 9/11, with "Moby C", the world's largest one-of-a-kind instant camera.

A hardcover companion photobook, Faces of Ground Zero: Portraits of the Heroes of September 11, 2001 (New York: Little, Brown & Co., 2002) was published to commemorate the project with a foreword by Rudy Giuliani and an original essay by McNally. A large percentage of the proceeds went to 9/11 charities.

Homewood Mountain Resort is a ski area located on the west shore of Lake Tahoe in the state of California, a few miles south of Tahoe City in the town of Homewood. It has 1,260 acres (510 ha) of skiable terrain and eight lifts. While it currently does not offer lodging on site, a Draft Environmental Impact Statement has been submitted to the regional planning commission to allow for the development of a resort inclusive of a hotel, condominiums, and various other additions. Since 2006, JMA Ventures, a San Francisco Bay Area developer, has owned and operated the ski area.

Lady Mary Ilona Margaret Whitley (née Cambridge; 24 September 1924 – 13 December 1999) was a relative of the British royal family. The only child of the 2nd Marquess of Cambridge, she was a second cousin of Queen Elizabeth II.

Burlington Street is a major roadway in Hamilton, Ontario, Canada, spanning 8.5 kilometers (5.3 miles) from Bay Street in the west to east of Parkdale Avenue. Originally named "Industrial Road," it was renamed for its proximity to Hamilton Harbour, historically known as Burlington Bay. The street played a pivotal role during Hamilton's industrial peak in the mid-20th century, hosting major manufacturing companies such as Stelco, Dofasco, Procter & Gamble, and International Harvester. It was also central to the city's early electrification, powered by the Decew Falls Power Generating Station in 1898.

Today, Burlington Street remains a hub of industrial and port activity, with the Hamilton Oshawa Port Authority managing significant cargo traffic. The area is home to key industries, including steel manufacturing, oilseed processing, and brewing. The street's upper level has been renamed Nikola Tesla Boulevard, reflecting its historical connection to electrical innovation.
Fiction (stylized in all caps) is an extended play by the Canadian experimental band Suuns, released October 30, 2020 via Secret City Records and Joyful Noise Recordings.

*Ghatak: Lethal* is a 1996 Indian Hindi-language action drama film directed and produced by Rajkumar Santoshi. Starring Sunny Deol, Meenakshi Seshadri, Amrish Puri, and Danny Denzongpa, the film follows Kashi (Deol), a kind-hearted wrestler who confronts a tyrannical gangster, Katya (Denzongpa), to free a terrorized colony. The story explores themes of justice, family, and resilience, culminating in Kashi liberating the oppressed community.

The film features a soundtrack composed by R. D. Burman, with one popular song by Anu Malik. Made on a budget of ₹6.25 crore, *Ghatak* was a commercial success, grossing ₹44 crore and earning blockbuster status. Initially, Kamal Haasan was cast in the lead role but later withdrew for unknown reasons.
Benjamin Hoffman (April 15, 1864 – May 20, 1922) was a Jewish-American lawyer, politician, and judge from New York.

*Pneumodesmus* is an extinct genus of millipede-like myriapods from the Late Silurian to Early Devonian period, approximately 428 to 414 million years ago. It is represented by a single species, *Pneumodesmus newmani*, named after its discoverer, Mike Newman, who found the fossil in Cowie, Scotland. The fossil is significant as it contains spiracles, indicating it was among the earliest known oxygen-breathing terrestrial animals. It is also the oldest body fossil of a myriapod, predating the first known vertebrates on land by 50 million years.

The Curse of Oak Island is a multi-season reality television series that chronicles an eclectic team of treasure hunters and their search for legendary treasure on Oak Island, off the shore of Nova Scotia, Canada. It is an American television production that premiered in Canada on the History network on January 6, 2014. The show features what is known as the Oak Island mystery, showing efforts to search for historical artifacts and treasure.

The tenth season premiered on November 15, 2022.

"Take Me to the Pilot" is a song by Elton John from his 1970 self-titled album, written by John and Bernie Taupin and produced by Gus Dudgeon. Released as the B-side to "Your Song," it features a blend of art rock and R&B. The song's cryptic lyrics have puzzled listeners, with both John and Taupin admitting they do not know their meaning, likening the writing style to abstract poetry.

A staple of John's live performances, "Take Me to the Pilot" has appeared on numerous concert recordings, including *17-11-70* and *Live in Australia*. It has been covered by artists such as José Feliciano, Ben E. King, and Rick Wakeman, and featured in the soundtrack of the 2018 series *Escape at Dannemora*.

Michael Cobbins (born August 9, 1992) is an American professional basketball player for Basket Brescia Leonessa of the Italian Lega Basket Serie A (LBA). He played college basketball for Oklahoma State.

The 2017 Ole Miss Rebels football team represented the University of Mississippi in the 2017 NCAA Division I FBS football season as a member of the Southeastern Conference (SEC). Led by first-year head coach Matt Luke, the Rebels played their home games at Vaught–Hemingway Stadium in Oxford, Mississippi, and finished the season with a 6–6 record (3–5 in SEC play). The team faced significant challenges, including an NCAA investigation that resulted in 21 charges, including 15 Level I violations, and self-imposed penalties such as a postseason ban, scholarship reductions, and recruiting restrictions.

The season also saw major coaching staff changes, including the hiring of Wesley McGriff as defensive coordinator and Jacob Peeler as wide receivers coach. The Rebels' schedule featured seven home games and five away games, including matchups against SEC opponents and their first-ever game against a Pac-12 team, the California Golden Bears.

Codex Boturini, also known as the Tira de la Peregrinación de los Mexica (Tale of the Mexica Migration), is an Aztec codex, which depicts the migration of the Azteca, later Mexica, people from Aztlán. Its date of manufacture is unknown, but likely to have occurred before or just after the Conquest of the Aztec Empire. At least two other Aztec codices have been influenced by the content and style of the Boturini Codex. This Codex has become an insignia of Mexica history and pilgrimage and is carved into a stone wall at the entrance of the National Museum of Anthropology and History in Mexico City.

The codex is currently located in the National Museum of Anthropology in Mexico City.

Sanshiro Sugata Part II (Japanese: 續姿三四郎, Hepburn: Zoku Sugata Sanshirō, a.k.a. Judo Saga II) is a 1945 Japanese action drama film written and directed by Akira Kurosawa. It is based on the novel by Tsuneo Tomita, son of Tomita Tsunejirō, the earliest disciple of judo. It was filmed in early 1945 in Japan towards the end of World War II. Unlike the original Sugata Sanshiro, the sequel is in part considered a propaganda film.

It is believed by some to be the earliest known film sequel whose title is simply the original title followed by a number, predating the likes of French Connection II (1975) by decades, although possibly mistaken as the added kanji 續 (Zoku) is not a numeral but simply means "Continuation" or "Sequel" sort of in the style of Another Thin Man or The Invisible Man Returns.

Vladimir Lenin monument in Azov (Lang-ru) is a monument dedicated the founder of USSR Vladimir Lenin which is situated in the city of Azov, Rostov Oblast, Russia. It is officially declared as an object of cultural heritage of regional importance.

Margaret JoBeth Williams (born December 6, 1948) is an American actress and television director. Her directorial debut with the 1994 short film On Hope earned her an Academy Award nomination for Best Live Action Short Film. In 2009 she began serving as president of the Screen Actors Guild Foundation; she is President Emeritus of the foundation.

Williams rose to prominence appearing in such films as Stir Crazy (1980), Poltergeist (1982), The Big Chill (1983), The Day After (1983), Teachers (1984), and Poltergeist II: The Other Side (1986). A three-time Emmy Award nominee, she was nominated for Outstanding Lead Actress in a Miniseries or a Movie for her work in the TV movie Adam (1983) and the TV miniseries Baby M (1988). Her third nomination was for her guest role in the sitcom Frasier (1994). She also starred in the TV series The Client (1995–96) and had recurring roles in the TV series Dexter (2007) and Private Practice (2009–11).

2011 MD is a bright micro-asteroid, classified as near-Earth object of the Apollo and Amor group, respectively. On 27 June 2011, at around 17:00 UTC (13:00 EDT), the object passed exceptionally close to Earth's surface at a distance of approximately 12,000 kilometers (7,500 mi), roughly the diameter of the Earth.

Vesna Györkös Žnidar (born 29 December 1977) is a Slovenian lawyer and politician who served as Minister of Interior from 2014 to 2018 in the government of Prime Minister Miro Cerar. A graduate of the University of Maribor and the London School of Economics, she worked in law and public service before entering politics. As minister, she managed Slovenia's response to the European migrant crisis, implementing stricter border controls, migration quotas, and expedited asylum procedures.

Tbilisoba is an annual festival held in Tbilisi, Georgia, celebrating the city’s history, culture, and traditions. Established in 1979 during the Soviet era under Eduard Shevardnadze, the festival was intended to honor the capital and promote new "socialist traditions." Over time, it became a celebration of Tbilisi's 1,500-year history, fostering a deeper connection to Georgian national heritage. The festival was suspended during the civil unrest of the early 1990s but was revived in 1995. Since then, it has been held annually, typically in late October, featuring various cultural and historical events.

Caledonite, whose name derives from Caledonia, the historical name of its place of discovery (Scotland), is a richly colored blue-green sulfate-carbonate mineral of lead and copper with an orthorhombic crystal structure. It is an uncommon mineral found in the oxidized zones of copper-lead deposits.
The Mali–Netherlands relations refers to the current diplomatic and military ties between the Republic of Mali and the Kingdom of the Netherlands.

Club Deportivo Corporación Arturo Fernández Vial is a football club in Chile, from the Concepción area, in the Bio-Bio Region. The team was founded on June 3, 1903, and they currently play in the second level of Chilean football, the Primera B.

Arturo Fernández Vial identifies itself since its beginning with the national corporation of railroad workers, and it was the most popular team in southern Chile. Its traditional rival is Deportes Concepción. Also, the team's "hinchada" (die-hard supporters) are known as La Furia Guerrera.

Kurihara (栗原市, Kurihara-shi) is a city located in Miyagi Prefecture, Japan. As of 30 April 2020, the city had an estimated population of 66,565, and a population density of 83 persons per km2 in 24,994 households. The total area of the city is 804.97 square kilometres (310.80 sq mi). Parts of the city are within the borders of the Kurikoma Quasi-National Park.

John Pierrakos (February 8, 1921 – February 1, 2001) was a Greek-American psychiatrist and co-founder of bioenergetic analysis, a body-oriented psychotherapy. A student of Wilhelm Reich, he collaborated with Alexander Lowen to establish the Bioenergetics Institute in 1955, introducing concepts like grounding and energy flow in the body. In 1969, Pierrakos founded The Pathwork Center with his wife, Eva Pierrakos, integrating spiritual and psychological approaches. He later established the Institute of Core Energetics in 1973, emphasizing holistic healing through physical, emotional, and spiritual integration.

James McCulloch (4 June 1953 – 27 September 1979) was a Scottish musician best known for playing lead guitar and bass, as a member of Paul McCartney's band Wings from 1974 to 1977. McCulloch was a member of the Glasgow psychedelic band One in a Million (formerly known as the Jaygars), Thunderclap Newman, and Stone the Crows.

McCulloch also made appearances on many albums, including John Entwistle's Whistle Rymes in 1972, as lead guitarist playing alongside Peter Frampton on "Apron Strings" and "I Feel Better". McCulloch also played guitar on Roger Daltrey's album One of the Boys which was released in 1977. McCulloch was a friend of the Who and a member of the band Thunderclap Newman, which was created and produced by his mentor Pete Townshend. At age 11, he was also a protégé of the Shadows' Hank Marvin. His brother is drummer Jack McCulloch.

*Force Majeure* is a 2014 satirical drama film written and directed by Ruben Östlund. The film follows a Swedish family on a ski vacation in the French Alps whose lives are upended after the father’s reaction to a controlled avalanche calls his character into question. Exploring themes of masculinity, family dynamics, and human behavior in crises, the film blends dark humor with uncomfortable drama. 

Filmed at Les Arcs ski resort in France, *Force Majeure* drew inspiration from viral videos and Östlund's background in ski filmmaking. It premiered in the Un Certain Regard section at the 2014 Cannes Film Festival, where it won the Jury Prize, and received critical acclaim, earning a 94% approval rating on Rotten Tomatoes. The film won multiple awards, including six Guldbagge Awards, and was Sweden's submission for the Best Foreign Language Film at the 87th Academy Awards.
River City Labs is an Australian coworking space and accelerator founded in 2012.

Clepsicosma is a genus of moths in the family Crambidae. As at 2022, this genus contains only one described species, Clepsicosma iridia, which is endemic to New Zealand. The species inhabits native forest in the North Island as well as the northern and western parts of the South Island down to Westland. The larval host of this species is assumed to be species of Cutty grass, possibly including Gahnia setifolia and Gahnia xanthocarpa, although the life history of this species is unknown. The adults of C. iridia are on the wing from December until May. They are nocturnal, and are attracted to light. During the day the adults rest on the underside of leaves, including those Cutty grass species that may possibly be their larval hosts.

Homoisoflavonoids (3-benzylidenechroman-4-ones) are a type of phenolic compounds occurring naturally in plants.

Chemically, they have the general structure of a 16-carbon skeleton, which consists of two phenyl rings (A and B) and heterocyclic ring (C).
Ronald J. Horvath (born 1937) has made contributions to the study of colonialism, African development, and urban geography of Sydney, Los Angeles, Detroit and Addis Ababa.

The 4th Electromagnetic Warfare Squadron is a United States Space Force unit based at Peterson Space Force Base, Colorado. Activated in 1990, it conducts offensive counterspace operations and space situational awareness, supporting global and theater campaigns.

Bill Long (28 April 1932 – 21 May 2010) was an Irish writer, broadcaster, and lecturer. Known for his radio documentaries and books, including *Bright Light, White Water* and *Change of Heart*, he documented Irish lighthouses and advocated for organ donation following his heart transplant in 1994. Born in Waterford, Long worked as a journalist for several Irish newspapers before transitioning to writing and broadcasting. He also lectured in homiletics at the National University of Ireland, Maynooth, and contributed to RTÉ Radio 1's *Sunday Miscellany*.

USCGC Robert Yered (WPC-1104) is a Sentinel-class cutter based in Miami, Florida. She was launched on November 23, 2012, and was commissioned on February 15, 2012. Debbie Wasserman Schultz, the Congressional Representative for the district containing the vessel's base, met the ship when she arrived in Miami on January 27, 2013.
Masʿūd Zazoyo or Masʿūd of Zaz (c.1430/31 – 1509/1512) was a Syriac Orthodox author, hermit, monk and prelate.
The Taylorcraft Auster was a British military liaison and observation aircraft produced by the Taylorcraft Aeroplanes (England) Limited company during the Second World War.

The 500-series format is a modular audio equipment standard widely used in professional recording studios. It originated in 1967 when Lou Lindauer and Saul Walker designed a modular mixing console for Apostolic Recording Studio in New York City. Walker's design became the foundation of the 500-series format, and the two co-founded Automated Processes, Inc. (API) in 1969. API developed iconic 500-series modules, including preamplifiers, compressors, and equalizers. 

The format gained popularity in the 1970s when engineers began repurposing modules from API consoles. API later introduced the Lunchbox, a portable chassis for 500-series modules. By 2020, the format had expanded significantly, with contributions from numerous manufacturers.

MAZON: A Jewish Response to Hunger is an American nonprofit working to end hunger among people of all faiths and backgrounds in the United States and Israel.

MAZON, which means "food" or "sustenance" in Hebrew, practices and promotes a multifaceted approach to hunger relief, recognizing the importance of responding to hungry peoples' immediate need for nutrition and sustenance while also working to advance long-term solutions. This holistic approach - executed through three interrelated strategies of education & advocacy, partnership grant-making and strategic initiatives - symbolizes its desire to embody twin Jewish ideals: tzedakah and tikkun olam.

MAZON has offices in Los Angeles, California (headquarters) and Washington, D.C.

Eugène-René Poubelle (15 April 1831 – 15 July 1907) was a French lawyer and diplomat who introduced waste containers to Paris and made their use compulsory. This introduction was so innovative at the time that Poubelle's surname became synonymous with waste bins (la poubelle) and remains the most common French word for a bin.

Leslie Quirk RBV (30 September 1914 – 20 October 2004), also known as Y Kione Jiarg (IPA-gv, "the red head") was a Manx language activist and teacher who was involved with the language's revival on the Isle of Man in the 20th century. His work recording the last native speakers of the language with the Irish Folklore Commission and the Manx Museum helped to ensure that a spoken record of the Manx language survived.

The Fairchild 9440 was a single-chip implementation of the Data General (DG) Nova minicomputer's instruction set, developed by Fairchild Semiconductor in the late 1970s. It was based on Fairchild's proprietary 3-micron bipolar Isoplanar Integrated Injection Logic (I3L) process, which allowed it to run faster and require simpler power supply requirements compared to DG's microNOVA mN601. The 9440 executed the Nova 2 instruction set in microcode, offering comparable performance to the Nova 1200, and included features like an integrated clock generator and direct memory access (DMA) support. However, it lacked certain advanced features of the mN601, such as hardware multiply and divide, which could be added via optional components.

Fairchild later introduced the 9445, an improved version using a 2-micron process. It implemented the Nova 3 instruction set, added hardware multiply and divide, expanded memory addressing, and significantly increased performance. Despite these advancements, production delays and legal disputes with Data General over licensing and competition hindered the 9440 and 9445's success. The 9445 eventually became obsolete as the minicomputer market declined. Fairchild's Nova-based processor line ended after its acquisition by National Semiconductor in 1987, though the design briefly continued as the ICD9445 under Strobe Data.

The Finse Tunnel (Lang-no) is a 10,300-metre-long (6.4 mi; 11,300 yd) railway tunnel west of the village of Finse in Eidfjord Municipality in Vestland county, Norway. The tunnel is part of the Bergen Line between the cities of Oslo and Bergen. It is the longest tunnel on the line, and the third longest railway tunnel in the kingdom. Inside the tunnel, at 1,237 metres (4,058 ft) elevation, is the highest point on the Norwegian railway network.

The tunnel was constructed to increase the regularity of the line past the Finse peak which was often blocked by snow. Planning started during the 1980s to remove this section that was regularly snowed down, and at the same time reduce the length and increase the speed of the railway. Construction started in 1990, and it was opened by King Harald V on 16 May 1993. Following the construction of the tunnel, 32 kilometres (20 mi) of railway around this area was also rebuilt to achieve higher train speeds, a shorter overall distance, and to maximize the natural protection from the weather. This was built in five stages, opening between 1995 and 1998.

The Museum of Russian Impressionism, located in Moscow, Russia, was founded by entrepreneur Boris Mints and opened on May 28, 2016. It showcases a collection of Russian Impressionist artworks, including pieces by Konstantin Korovin, Igor Grabar, and Valentin Serov, primarily from Mints' private collection. Housed in a restored late 19th-century Bolshevik mill, the museum also features contemporary works and has hosted exhibitions in Russia and abroad.

Dar Hammuda Pasha is a historic palace in Tunis, located on Sidi Ben Arous Street. Built in 1630 by Hammuda Pasha Bey, it served as a residence for Muradid and Husainid rulers before later being converted into a restaurant.
Robert C. Morgan (born 1943) is an American art critic, art historian, curator, poet, and artist.

Wild Things: Foursome is a 2010 erotic thriller film directed by Andy Hurst and starring Jillian Murray, Marnette Patterson, Ashley Parker Angel and John Schneider. It is a sequel to Wild Things: Diamonds in the Rough (2005) and the fourth and final film in the Wild Things series. The film was released on DVD on June 1, 2010.

*Aadmi* is a 1993 Hindi-language action drama film directed by Arshad Khan and starring Mithun Chakraborty, Gautami, Harish Kumar, and Ajit. The film follows Vijay Srivastav, an honest man falsely accused of murdering his family, as he battles corruption and uncovers a conspiracy involving his former boss and corrupt officials. Featuring themes of justice and revenge, the film is notable for Shakti Kapoor's rare positive role as an honest police officer.
The Council on Religion and the Homosexual was a San Francisco-based organization founded in 1964 for the purpose of joining homosexual activists and religious leaders.
The Downs are an area of public open limestone downland in Bristol, England. They consist of Durdham Down to the north and east and Clifton Down to the south, separated by Stoke Road.
The Antifascist Committee of Ukraine (AFKU) is a political organization founded in 2006, led by Heorhiy Buiko.

Culbert Levy Olson (November 7, 1876 – April 13, 1962) was an American politician and attorney who served as the 29th governor of California from 1939 to 1943, becoming the first Democrat to hold the office in 40 years. A staunch progressive and supporter of New Deal policies, Olson advocated for labor rights, social welfare programs, and government reform but faced significant opposition from a conservative legislature. A secular atheist, he was known for his criticism of religious influence in politics. Olson also supported Japanese American internment during World War II. After leaving office, he remained active in law and secularist advocacy, serving as president of the United Secularists of America.
Saint-Denis–Porte de Paris is a Paris Métro station in Saint-Denis, serving the Stade de France and nearby landmarks.

Omsk hemorrhagic fever (OHF) is a tick-borne viral disease caused by the Omsk hemorrhagic fever virus, a member of the *Flavivirus* family. Endemic to Western Siberia, it is transmitted through tick bites, contact with infected muskrats, or contaminated water. Symptoms include fever, hemorrhaging, neurological complications, and, in severe cases, encephalitis. While most patients recover, some may experience long-term effects such as hearing loss or psychological issues. The disease has a low fatality rate (0.5–3%) and no specific treatment, with care focused on symptom management. Prevention involves avoiding tick exposure and protective measures during activities like farming or hunting.

The roadometer, an early odometer, was developed in 1847 by William Clayton, Orson Pratt, and Appleton Harmon to measure distances traveled by wagon during westward migration. It used wooden gears to calculate mileage and inspired later odometer designs. Replicas and documentation have preserved its historical significance.

The Koza riot (コザ暴動, Koza bōdō) was a violent and spontaneous protest against the US military presence in Okinawa, which occurred on the night of December 20, 1970, into the morning of the following day. Roughly 5,000 Okinawans clashed with roughly 700 American MPs in an event which has been regarded as symbolic of Okinawan anger against 25 years of US military occupation. In the riot, approximately 60 Americans and 27 Okinawans were injured, 80 cars were burned, and several buildings on Kadena Air Base were destroyed or heavily damaged.

David Loud (born November 28, 1961) is an American music director, conductor, vocal arranger, pianist, and actor, known for his extensive work on Broadway. He has collaborated with renowned composers such as Kander and Ebb and Stephen Sondheim, contributing to productions like *Ragtime*, *The Visit*, *Sondheim on Sondheim*, and *The Scottsboro Boys*. Loud began his career as an actor in *Merrily We Roll Along* and later transitioned to music direction, earning acclaim for his work on *And the World Goes 'Round* and *She Loves Me*. He has also created and arranged performances for venues like Carnegie Hall and Lincoln Center and currently teaches at the Manhattan School of Music.

Boane District is a district of Maputo Province in southern Mozambique. The principal town is Boane. The district is located in the center of the province, and borders with Moamba District in the north, the city of Matola in the northeast, Matutuíne District in the south, and with Namaacha District in the west. In the east, it is bounded by the Indian Ocean. The area of the district is 815 square kilometres (315 sq mi). It has a population of 98,964 as of 2007.

The Swiss Social Archives (SSA) is a memory institution and research facility located in Zurich, Switzerland. Established in 1906 by social reformer Paul Pflüger, it was originally known as the Centre for Switzerland's Social Literature and aimed to document social issues, inspired by the Musée social in Paris. Over the years, it became a hub for intellectuals and political refugees, including Lenin and Trotsky, as well as antifascist and Cold War-era Eastern European refugees. Renamed in the 1940s, the archives were officially recognized as a research facility by the Swiss Confederation in 1974. Today, the SSA is designated as a Swiss cultural property of national significance and is managed by an independent association, employing historians, archivists, and librarians under the leadership of Christian Koller.

William W. Arnold (August 28, 1843 – March 30, 1923) was an American physician and ornithologist known for his contributions to bird protection and wildlife advocacy. Born in Connersville, Indiana, he practiced medicine in Colorado Springs from 1886 until his death, becoming a respected member of the medical community. In his later years, Arnold became an active ornithologist, joining the American Ornithologists' Union and the Cooper Ornithological Club. He and his wife, Mary Evelina Shaw, operated a bird hospital beginning around 1913, where they treated injured wild and domestic birds, often releasing them back into the wild. Arnold also wrote articles on bird care and conservation, including the story "Stubby, The Bird Evangelist," and served as an associate editor for *Colorado Sky*. A member of the Colorado Audubon Society, he frequently advocated for bird protection and educated children about wildlife. Arnold died of influenza in 1923 and is buried in Colorado Springs.

A cigarette filter is a component of a cigarette designed to reduce the inhalation of harmful substances during smoking. First patented in 1925 by Hungarian inventor Boris Aivaz, filters became widely adopted in the 1950s following health concerns linking smoking to lung diseases. By the 1960s, filtered cigarettes dominated the market, with filters primarily made from cellulose acetate, a plastic derived from cotton or wood pulp. Filters may also include additives such as menthol, sweeteners, or activated charcoal to modify flavor or reduce tar and nicotine levels.

Despite initial claims of reduced health risks, later studies found little difference in lung cancer risk between filtered and non-filtered cigarettes. Filters have also contributed significantly to environmental pollution, with cigarette butts being the most littered waste item globally. The cellulose acetate in filters degrades slowly, releasing toxins into ecosystems. Efforts to address this issue include developing biodegradable filters, imposing penalties for littering, and exploring innovative recycling methods, such as converting filter waste into energy storage materials.

The tobacco industry has also marketed "light" cigarettes with reduced tar and nicotine yields, though these have not significantly lowered health risks. Filters remain a controversial component of cigarettes due to their environmental and health impacts.

Sendle is an Australian private logistics company founded in 2014 by James Bradfield Moody, Sean Geoghegan, and Craig Davis. Headquartered in Sydney, it provides parcel and package delivery services across Australia. In 2015, it became the country's first carbon-neutral delivery service. Sendle has partnered with major e-commerce platforms like eBay and Shopify and has raised significant capital, including $35 million in 2021, to support its growth and expansion.
Léon Spilliaert (28 July 1881 – 23 November 1946) was a Belgian symbolist and expressionist painter known for his melancholic, dreamlike works and introspective self-portraits.

Rola Saad (Arabic: رولا سعد; born September 19, 1948) is a Lebanese television producer and media icon known for her innovative and successful productions in the Middle East. Her portfolio includes over 20 works spanning variety shows, reality TV, drama series, and documentaries. She is best known for directing *Star Academy*, as well as producing *Celebrity Duets* and *Splash*, pioneering reality television in the region.

In 1623 the ships Anne and Little James were the third and fourth ships financed by the London-based Merchant Adventurers to come out together in support of Plymouth Colony, as were Mayflower in 1620 and Fortune in 1621. Anne carried mostly passengers, and the much smaller Little James carried primarily cargo, albeit with a few passengers. After a stormy three-month voyage from London, Anne arrived at New Plymouth in early July 1623, with Little James a week or so later.

Between them the ships brought 90-odd new settlers along with about thirty others who were not part of the core emigrant group. Many of this emigrant contingent would serve the colony well in the coming years, while others would be judged unfit for the hardships of colony life and be sent back to England.

ER is an American drama series that aired on NBC from September 19, 1994, until April 2, 2009. The series featured a large ensemble cast that changed dramatically over its long run. The main cast was augmented by a wealth of recurring characters and award-winning guest stars.

King Arthur's Disasters is a British animated series which first aired on CITV. Co-created by Paul Parkes and Will Ashurst, the series follows and depicts attempts by King Arthur, assisted by the wizard Merlin, to woo the beautiful self-obsessed Princess Guinevere. Due to the popularity of the show, it was picked up for a second series which began transmission on CITV from 6 November 2005. Both were executive-produced by Genevieve Dexter at cake entertainment

King Arthur's Disasters was the highest-rated new CITV show during spring 2005. It regularly achieved an audience share of over 20% of children and it regularly won its time slot against CBBC. In 2006 the show was nominated for a children's BAFTA for Best Animation; however, it lost to The Amazing Adrenalini Brothers. It airs on Pop in the UK.

Sage Kotsenburg (born July 27, 1993) is an American snowboarder. He won the first-ever Olympic gold medal in men's snowboard slopestyle at the 2014 Winter Olympic Games in Sochi, Russia, and became the first gold medalist at these Olympics. Kotsenburg won a silver medal in snowboard slopestyle at the 2012 Winter X Games XVI in Aspen, Colorado, behind Mark McMorris. Kotsenburg won a bronze medal in Snowboard Big Air at the 2011 Winter X Games XV in Aspen, Colorado, behind Torstein Horgmo and Sebastien Toutant.

Dardanella was a prominent theatre company in the Dutch East Indies (modern-day Indonesia) during the 1920s and 1930s, known for its adoption of European theatrical styles, including spoken dialogue and original works, in contrast to the music-heavy bangsawan and komedi stambul traditions. Founded in 1926 by Willy A. Piedro, the troupe featured notable actors such as Devi Dja, Tan Tjeng Bok, and Andjar Asmara, who later became a key playwright. Dardanella's performances, often in Malay, were praised for their quality and cultural significance, contributing to the development of Indonesian theatre and film.

Rhizomatic learning is an educational concept inspired by the rhizome, a plant structure used by philosophers Gilles Deleuze and Félix Guattari as a metaphor for non-hierarchical, interconnected knowledge systems. It contrasts with traditional, linear models of learning, emphasizing organic, student-driven exploration. The concept has roots in John Dewey's ideas about conversational, interest-driven learning and has been further developed by feminist and critical pedagogy scholars. Rhizomatic learning has gained renewed attention in the context of online education, particularly in relation to MOOCs. However, it has faced criticism, with some questioning its practicality and its critique of formal education systems.

Theodorick Bland (December 2, 1708 – 1784), also known as Theodorick Bland, Sr. or Theodorick Bland of Cawsons, was Virginia planter who served as a member of the first Virginia Senate, as well as a militia officer and clerk of Prince George County, Virginia.

Araxá (IPA-pt) is a municipality in Western Minas Gerais state, Brazil. Its estimated population by IBGE (Brazilian Institute of Geography and Statistics) in 2020 is 107,337 inhabitants and the area of the municipality is 1,283 square kilometres (495 sq mi), with 3.45 square kilometres (1.33 sq mi) making up the urban perimeter.

Leslie Quirk (30 September 1914 – 20 October 2004) was a Manx language advocate and cultural preservationist from Peel, Isle of Man. A key figure in the Manx language revival, he learned the language from native speakers and played a vital role in bridging the gap between the last native speakers and modern learners. Quirk contributed to the Manx Folk-Life Survey and inspired future revivalists, including Brian Stowell. He received the Reih Bleeaney Vanannan award in 1997.

Ibex Peak is a 2,039-meter (6,690-foot) mountain in the North Cascades of British Columbia, Canada. It is part of the Yale Division Yale Land District and is located near its parent peak, Guanaco Peak. The peak was first ascended in 1962 by Asger Bentzen, Hamish Mutch, Glenn Woodsworth, and Bob Woodsworth, with the easiest route being a scramble via the northwest slope. 

Geologically, Ibex Peak and the North Cascades were formed through volcanic activity, tectonic uplift, and glaciation over millions of years, resulting in rugged terrain and diverse ecosystems. The area experiences a marine west coast climate, with heavy precipitation and favorable climbing conditions from July to September.
Cheick Oumar Dabo (born 12 January 1981 in Bamako) is a Malian football player.

Time's Up is a nonprofit organization founded on January 1, 2018, by prominent women in Hollywood in response to the Harvey Weinstein sexual abuse cases and the rise of the Me Too movement. Its mission is to combat workplace sexual harassment and discrimination, particularly for women in lower-wage industries and people of color. The organization operates through its advocacy arm, Time's Up Now, and its charitable arm, Time's Up Foundation, which oversees the Time's Up Legal Defense Fund. The fund, administered by the National Women's Law Center, provides legal and media support to victims of workplace sexual misconduct.

Time's Up gained widespread attention with its January 2018 open letter and high-profile protests at awards shows, including the Golden Globes. It has since launched initiatives like the 4% Challenge to promote women directors and the Time's Up Impact Lab to study workplace discrimination. However, the organization has faced criticism for its ties to Hollywood elites, internal controversies, and leadership resignations following its handling of high-profile cases, including allegations against Andrew Cuomo.
State Route 100 (SR 100) is a west–east state highway in both West Tennessee and Middle Tennessee that connects Whiteville with Nashville. It is 158.31 mi (254.8 km) long.

Longwood House is a mansion in St. Helena and the final residence of Napoleon Bonaparte, the former Emperor of the French, during his exile on the island of Saint Helena, from 10 December 1815 until his death on 5 May 1821.

Kurihara (栗原市) is a city in Miyagi Prefecture, Japan, located in the Tōhoku region. Established on April 1, 2005, through the merger of several towns and a village, it functions as a unitary administrative area. Known for its agriculture, particularly rice production, Kurihara also features natural attractions such as Mount Kurikoma, Lake Izunuma, and hot spring resorts. The city experiences a humid subtropical climate with distinct seasonal changes. Historically, the area was part of Mutsu Province and later controlled by the Date clan during the Edo period.

Qin Shengxiang (Chinese: 秦生祥; born February 1957) is an admiral of the Chinese People's Liberation Army Navy (PLAN) who was Political Commissar of the PLA Navy from September 2017 to January 2022. He formerly served as Director of the General Office of the Central Military Commission.
Samuel Lord (1803-1889) was a British-born American retail millionaire who founded Lord & Taylor. It was the oldest luxury department store in the United States.

The *Bab Ballads* is a collection of humorous poems written and illustrated by W. S. Gilbert, best known as the librettist of the Gilbert and Sullivan operas. The poems, which first appeared in the satirical magazine *Fun* between 1865 and 1871, were later compiled into several collected editions, beginning with *The "Bab" Ballads – Much Sound and Little Sense* in 1869. The title derives from Gilbert's childhood nickname, "Bab." Known for their wit, absurdity, and satirical commentary, the *Bab Ballads* were accompanied by Gilbert's own whimsical illustrations.

The poems gained popularity and influenced Gilbert's later work in comic opera. Subsequent editions, including *Songs of a Savoyard* (1890) and *The Bab Ballads, with which are included Songs of a Savoyard* (1898), integrated lyrics from his operas. The *Bab Ballads* have remained in print for decades, with modern editions expanding the canon and adaptations for recordings, music, and stage performances.

Sendle is an Australian-based company which provides courier services within the country, focusing primarily on small to medium-sized businesses and online retailers. It claims to be the first courier company in Australia to offer fully carbon neutral services. While its delivery services are worldwide - with the exception of Russia and Belarus - its operational staff are based in Australia, New Zealand, the Philippines and the United States.
Gary D. Penisten (born 1931) is an American businessman and former government official who served as Assistant Secretary of the Navy (Financial Management and Comptroller) from 1974 to 1977.

The European Data Portal was an initiative to centralize Public Sector Information from the 28 EU Member States and four EFTA countries (EU28+). It provided metadata harvested from national data portals to promote open data reuse. On April 21, 2021, it was consolidated with the EU Open Data Portal into data.europa.eu.
River City Labs is a Brisbane-based coworking space founded in 2012 by Steve Baxter.

Abdul Qayum Tutakhail (born March 28, 1940) is an Afghan neurosurgeon and retired major general in the Afghan National Army. Born in Paktia province, he earned his MD from Kabul Medical University and a PhD in neurosurgery from the Institute of Neurosurgery in Kiev. Tutakhail served as Surgeon General and specialized in combat trauma, performing over 3,000 surgeries and authoring numerous research papers. In 2010, he was reassigned as Surgeon General to address corruption and neglect in military healthcare, leading to significant improvements at Sardar Mohammad Dawood Khan hospital.
*Cinéaste* is a quarterly American film magazine, founded in 1967 by Gary Crowdus.
