Billy J. Kramer (born William Howard Ashton, August 19, 1943) is an English pop singer known for his role in the 1960s Merseybeat scene. Managed by Brian Epstein and produced by George Martin, Kramer achieved fame with hits written by Lennon and McCartney, including "Do You Want to Know a Secret," "Bad to Me," and "Little Children," the latter becoming his biggest success. Backed by the Dakotas, he enjoyed chart-topping success in the UK and the US. After his peak, Kramer pursued a solo career, later moving to the United States. He continues to perform and record, releasing new music and an autobiography in recent years.

Hyme House, located on Fitzjohn's Avenue in Hampstead, London, is a historic Arts and Crafts-style villa built in 1886. It was home to renowned portrait painter Philip de László from 1921 to 1937, where he painted notable figures, including members of the British royal family. After de László's death, the house was acquired by the Sisters of Mercy of the Holy Cross in 1938, serving as a nursing home during World War II and later as a convent and girls' school. In 1985, it became the Fitzjohn's Lodge Hotel before being converted back to residential use in 2006.

Saga (born 1975) is a Swedish singer associated with white power music and the nationalist movement. Active since 2000, she gained international recognition through tribute albums and performances across Europe and the United States. Her music was controversially cited by Anders Behring Breivik in his 2011 manifesto, which she publicly condemned, retreating from public life afterward.

George Anthony Sifakis is a former United States federal government official and government relations professional. Sifakis is currently CEO of IdeagenGlobal.com, which convenes global leaders to develop partnerships and solutions to the world's most troubling issues.

Following his role on the Presidential Transition, assisting with the confirmation process of Wilbur Ross as Secretary of Commerce, Sifakis was appointed by President Trump on January 23, 2017, to be Special Assistant to the President and Deputy Director of the Office of Public Liaison, responsible for engaging the business community, including CEOs of the Fortune 500, non-profit and public sector leaders.

On March 6, 2017, Sifakis was appointed by President Trump as Assistant to the President and Director of the Office of Public Liaison, and one of approximately 20 senior White House staff directly reporting to the White House Chief of Staff, Reince Priebus. On August 18, 2017, multiple Trump administration sources acknowledged that Sifakis would be departing the White House in September.

Nestor Principe (March 15, 1945 – May 7, 1973) was a Filipino martial artist, activist, and journalist honored as a martyr for his opposition to the Martial Law regime of Ferdinand Marcos. Born in Tagbilaran, Bohol, he grew up in Caloocan, Metro Manila, where he mastered Sikaran, a Filipino martial art, and introduced it to Malaysia as a bodyguard and instructor. Principe later traveled globally before returning to the Philippines, inspired by the First Quarter Storm, to study journalism and engage in activism. He was killed by the Philippine Constabulary in 1973, and his name was posthumously inscribed on the Bantayog ng mga Bayani Wall of Remembrance in 2018.
Tateyama Station (館山駅, Tateyama-eki) is a passenger railway station in the city of Tateyama, Chiba Prefecture, Japan, operated by the East Japan Railway Company (JR East).
*Personal and Vehicular Basic Loads and The Morrow Project Role Playing Expansion* is a role-playing game supplement providing guidelines for character skills, tasks, and item repairs.
The 62nd Ohio Infantry Regiment was a Union Army regiment during the American Civil War, active from 1861 to 1865.
Air Commodore Patrick Desmond Callaghan (16 July 1926 – 6 April 1992) was a one-star air officer in the Pakistan Air Force who is credited for his pioneering work in Flight safety in Pakistan.

Vincent (sometimes referred to as East Irwindale) is a census-designated place (CDP) in the central San Gabriel Valley, in Los Angeles County, California, United States. The city name and zipcode for addresses south of Arrow Highway is Covina and 91722, respectively, and Azusa and 91702 for addresses north of Arrow Highway. The population was 15,922 at the 2010 census, up from 15,097 at the 2000 census. Vincent is named after Vincent Avenue, which passes through the community.
Nikos Filis (Lang-el) is a Greek politician and journalist. He held the office of the Ministry of National Education and Religious Affairs of Greece from 23 September 2015 to 5 November 2016.

The long-billed curlew (*Numenius americanus*) is the largest sandpiper in North America, known for its distinctive long, downward-curving bill. It breeds in grasslands of west-central North America and feeds on invertebrates and insects. Once heavily hunted, its population has rebounded, and it is now classified as Least Concern by the IUCN. Historically abundant in areas like San Francisco and the U.S. East Coast, its numbers have significantly declined in these regions due to habitat loss and hunting.

Old Street is a historic thoroughfare in London, England, with origins dating back to at least the 13th century, and possibly earlier as part of a Roman or pre-Roman route connecting Silchester and Colchester. The street has undergone significant changes, including widening in the 19th century, but retains some 18th- and early 19th-century buildings. Located on the London Inner Ring Road, it forms part of the boundary of the congestion charge zone and is known for its vibrant nightlife, tech industry presence around "Silicon Roundabout," and notable graffiti by artists like Banksy. Old Street station, situated under the roundabout, serves as a key transport hub.

Pierre Marinovitch (Lang-sr; 1 August 1898 – 2 October 1919) was a French World War I flying ace credited with 21 confirmed and 3 probable aerial victories. He was the youngest French flying ace of the war, scoring his 5th victory at age 19 years and 169 days. He was killed in an air accident following the war.

Mijar, also known as Mijaru, is a village in the Dakshina Kannada district of Karnataka, India. Located in the Tulu Nadu region, it falls under the Mangalore taluk and is governed by a gram panchayat. The village spans an area of 23.84 square kilometers and had a population of 8,480 as of the 2011 census. The primary languages spoken in Mijar are Tulu, Kannada, Konkani, and Beary.

Situated along National Highway 169, Mijar is approximately 30 kilometers from Mangalore city and 20 kilometers from Mangalore International Airport. It is also close to other towns such as Moodbidri, Udupi, and Karkala. The village is home to several educational institutions, including the Mangalore Institute of Technology and Engineering and Alvas Institute of Engineering and Technology.

Mijar is known for its religious significance, with numerous temples such as Sri Vishnumoorthi Temple, Sri Somanatheshwara Temple, and Sri Bhoothanatheshwara Temple. The Nandini River, originating from Kanakabettu in Mijar, flows westward, encircling the Sri Kateel Durgaparameshwari Temple. Historically, women in the village engaged in rolling beedies as a source of income.

The geoduck (*Panopea generosa*) is a species of large, burrowing saltwater clam native to the west coast of North America, particularly Washington and British Columbia. Known for its long siphon and distinctive appearance, the geoduck is the largest burrowing clam in the world, with some specimens weighing over 7 kilograms (15 pounds) and living up to 140 years. Its name originates from the Lushootseed word *gwídəq*, meaning "dig deep," and it is also referred to as the elephant-trunk clam.

Highly valued in East Asian cuisine, geoduck is considered a delicacy and is often served in hot pots, sashimi, or soups. Its high market value has created a multimillion-dollar industry in the United States and Canada, though it is closely regulated to prevent overharvesting and poaching. Geoduck farming has sparked environmental debates, but studies suggest minimal long-term ecological impact. The species has also gained cultural recognition, serving as the mascot of Evergreen State College and achieving internet notoriety for its phallic appearance.

Mario Vazquez (born June 15, 1977) is an American singer known for his brief stint on *American Idol* in 2005, where he withdrew as a finalist citing personal reasons. Following his departure, he signed with J Records and released his debut album in 2006, featuring the hit single "Gallery." Vazquez has collaborated with notable artists and producers and later pursued independent music projects. His career has also been marked by controversy, including a 2007 lawsuit alleging misconduct, which was ultimately sent to arbitration.

GABA transporter 2 (GAT2; SLC6A13) also known as sodium- and chloride-dependent GABA transporter 2 is one of four GABA transporters, GAT1 (SLC6A1), GAT2 (SLC6A13), GAT3 (SLC6A11) and BGT1 (SLC6A12). Note that GAT2 is different from BGT1 despite the fact that the latter transporter is sometimes referred at as (mouse) GAT-2.

All these transporters are highly hydrophobic proteins with 12 transmembrane segments, extracellular glycosylation sites, and intracellular consensus sites for phosphorylation, and there is over 50% amino acid homology between each of them. Each binds GABA with varying affinities with BGT1 having the lowest affinity and GAT3 the highest. GAT2 (SLC6A13) is predominantly expressed in hepatocytes in the liver, but is also found in proximal tubules in the kidney as well as in the leptomeninges and in some blood vessels in the brain.
Dolní Bečva is a municipality and village in Vsetín District in the Zlín Region of the Czech Republic. It has about 1,900 inhabitants.

Kirkthorpe hydro is a hydroelectric generating plant located on the River Calder at Kirkthorpe Weir, 4 miles (6.4 km) east of the City of Wakefield in West Yorkshire, England. The plant was opened in 2017 and expects to be generating electricity for 100 years. Kirkthorpe Weir is the highest industrial weir in Yorkshire and has prevented fish passing upstream to spawn; the new hydro project has a fish pass built into it.

At a nameplate capacity of 500 kW, along with another plant at Brotherton on the River Aire, the plant is jointly the largest hydroelectric generator in Yorkshire.

The Universal Time-Sharing System (UTS) is a discontinued operating system for the XDS Sigma series of computers, succeeding Batch Processing Monitor (BPM)/Batch Time-Sharing Monitor (BTM). UTS was announced in 1966, but because of delays did not actually ship until 1971. It was designed to provide multi-programming services for online (interactive) user programs in addition to batch-mode production jobs, symbiont (spooled) I/O, and critical real-time processes. System daemons, called "ghost jobs" were used to run monitor code in user space. The final release, D00, shipped in January, 1973. It was succeeded by the CP-V operating system, which combined UTS with features of the heavily batch-oriented Xerox Operating System (XOS).

Kizzuwatna was an ancient kingdom located in southeastern Anatolia, near the Taurus Mountains, during the 2nd millennium BC. Known for its rich resources, including silver mines and fertile plains, it was home to a diverse population of Hurrians, Luwians, and Hittites. Kizzuwatna played a strategic role in the power struggles between the Hittites and the Mitanni, forming alliances and treaties with both. It later became part of the Hittite Empire, contributing troops and resources, including advanced iron weaponry, to major conflicts like the Battle of Kadesh. After the Hittite Empire's collapse, the region became part of the Neo-Hittite kingdom of Quwe.

Longwood House is a historic residence on Saint Helena, best known as the home of Napoleon Bonaparte during his exile from 1815 until his death in 1821. Originally a farm owned by the East India Company, it was converted for Napoleon's use. Following his death, the property reverted to the British Crown before being transferred to the French government in 1858. Now a museum managed by the French government, it has been restored and preserved as a significant Napoleonic site, alongside The Briars and the Valley of the Tomb.

The Schillinger System is a method of musical composition developed by Joseph Schillinger, a Russian-American composer and theorist. It is based on mathematical principles and scientific analysis, aiming to provide composers with tools to organize sound and create music free from traditional constraints. Schillinger's system emphasizes universal patterns and applies mathematical concepts to rhythm, melody, and structure, offering techniques for planning and executing large musical works. While the system was influential, it was also criticized for its complexity and dense presentation. Schillinger taught prominent musicians, including George Gershwin and Glenn Miller, and his ideas influenced jazz education and institutions like Berklee College of Music.
Ruth Cameron is an American record producer and jazz vocalist.

In topology, the Denjoy–Riesz theorem states that every compact set of totally disconnected points in the Euclidean plane can be covered by a continuous image of the unit interval, without self-intersections (a Jordan arc).

TransPeshawar, also known as Zu Peshawar, is a bus rapid transit (BRT) system serving the Greater Peshawar Region in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan. Operational since August 13, 2020, it features a 27-kilometer east–west corridor with 32 stations, connecting Chamkani to Karkhano Market. The system uses a fleet of hybrid buses, offering environmentally friendly and efficient public transportation. Developed with assistance from the Asian Development Bank, the project faced delays, cost overruns, and corruption allegations but remains the most expensive BRT system in Pakistan. It aims to improve urban mobility and reduce traffic congestion in Peshawar.

Robbie Thorpe is an Aboriginal activist from the Krautungalung people of the Gunnai Nation, known for his advocacy on Indigenous rights, including the Pay The Rent initiative, the Aboriginal Passport initiative, and campaigns addressing genocide and sovereignty. A prominent figure since the 1970s, he founded *The Koorier* publications and initiated 3CR's *Fire First* radio program. Thorpe's activism extends to legal challenges, media production, and creative projects, with his story featured in the upcoming documentary *Our Warrior: The Story of Robbie Thorpe*.

Kentucky Route 8 (KY 8) is a 114.2-mile (183.8 km) state highway in Kentucky, maintained by the Kentucky Transportation Cabinet (KYTC). The route is divided into two segments: the western segment, spanning 78.8 miles (126.8 km), runs from KY 237 near Francisville to US 62 in Maysville, while the eastern segment, 35.4 miles (56.9 km) long, extends from KY 57 near Concord to US 23 in South Portsmouth. Both segments primarily run parallel to the Ohio River.

The western segment passes through cities such as Covington, Newport, and Augusta, and has been rerouted in some areas due to landslides, including a section near Fort Thomas. The eastern segment, formerly part of KY 10, features narrow and gravel roads near its western terminus. KY 8 serves as a scenic route along the Ohio River, with portions designated for recreational use, such as the Ramblin' River Tour.

Lt. Col. Frank Moffat (1894-1978) was a Scottish rugby union player. He became an international referee and the 66th President of the Scottish Rugby Union. He had a distinguished military career with the Gordon Highlanders in the First World War.

Elfonda Mekel (born 21 May 1970), known professionally as Once Mekel, is an Indonesian pop and rock singer. He gained prominence as the lead vocalist of Dewa 19 from 2000 to 2011, contributing to the band's success with hit albums like *Bintang Lima*. After leaving the band to pursue a solo career, he released popular singles such as "Dealova" and "Symphony yang Indah." Once has won several awards, including Best Pop Soloist at the 2005 Anugerah Musik Indonesia.

The Internet in Denmark is characterized by widespread access, high speeds, and a competitive market. Denmark was an early adopter of internet technology, with the establishment of the Nordic university network NORDUnet in 1988 and the Danish Internet Exchange Point (DIX) in 1994. Broadband access began in 1996 with cable internet, followed by the introduction of ADSL in 1999. By 2012, xDSL connections were available to 98% of households, with TDC dominating the market.

Denmark has also developed extensive mobile broadband infrastructure, with near-universal 3G coverage and 4G coverage reaching 65% by 2012. Fiber-to-the-premises (FTTP) has expanded significantly, driven by investments from local power companies, with 43% of households having access by 2013. However, the rollout in Copenhagen has been limited due to TDC's acquisition of DONG Energy's fiber network.

DOCSIS 3 technology contributes significantly to next-generation access, covering 62% of households, though it is less prevalent in rural areas. WiMAX networks have also been deployed, primarily in underserved regions, though their presence has diminished in recent years.

Denmark has minimal internet censorship, with isolated cases of site blocking for copyright infringement. The competitive market includes major providers like TDC, Stofa, and FIBIA, alongside smaller players. Denmark's internet infrastructure reflects a blend of advanced technology and regulatory efforts to ensure widespread access.
Michael Spira is a British physician specializing in weight management, obesity, and general practice, currently serving as medical director of the Smart Clinics.

Hostile vehicle mitigation (HVM) refers to security measures designed to prevent or reduce the impact of vehicle-based attacks, such as vehicle ramming or vehicle-borne improvised explosive devices (VBIEDs). Common HVM solutions include crash-tested barriers, bollards, reinforced street furniture, and architectural features integrated into urban landscapes. These systems are tested to withstand specific vehicle impacts and are often implemented with protective standoff distances to mitigate blast effects. HVM is used to safeguard buildings, crowded spaces, and high-value targets, such as museums and jewelry stores, while maintaining pedestrian accessibility and urban functionality.
Eric Records is an American record company that produced singles from 1968 to 1996 mainly with reissues of successful oldies, continuing today with compact disc compilations.

The **Alliance française** is a global cultural and educational organization founded in Paris in 1883 by prominent figures including Louis Pasteur, Ferdinand de Lesseps, Jules Verne, Ernest Renan, and Armand Colin. Its mission is to promote the French language and culture worldwide. The organization operates through a network of independently run local franchises, with over 440,000 students learning French annually. The Paris branch, Alliance française Paris Île-de-France, established in 1894, serves as an international hub for French language education. The Alliance finances its activities through course fees, facility rentals, and limited French government subsidies.

Lakes Mall was an enclosed shopping mall in Lauderdale Lakes, Florida. Opened in 1972, the shopping center initially featured Jefferson Ward and Britt's discount stores, and later included the first locations for the retail chains Office Depot and Sports Authority. The complex was torn down in 1995 and redeveloped as a strip mall.

Mansfield is a borough in Tioga County, Pennsylvania, United States, settled in 1804 and incorporated in 1857. It is home to Mansfield University of Pennsylvania and is notable for hosting the first night football game under electric lights in 1892.

Ecological sanitation, commonly abbreviated as ecosan (also spelled eco-san or EcoSan), is an approach to sanitation provision which aims to safely reuse excreta in agriculture. It is an approach, rather than a technology or a device which is characterized by a desire to "close the loop", mainly for the nutrients and organic matter between sanitation and agriculture in a safe manner. One of the aims is to minimise the use of non-renewable resources. When properly designed and operated, ecosan systems provide a hygienically safe system to convert human excreta into nutrients to be returned to the soil, and water to be returned to the land. Ecosan is also called resource-oriented sanitation.

Hyundai Chungun High School (현대청운고등학교) is a private boarding school located in Ulsan, South Korea. Established in 1981 by Chung Ju-yung, the founder of Hyundai Group, the school is known for its specialized education programs aimed at fostering gifted students. It offers an "Elite Leadership Education" program, including Advanced Placement (AP) courses, and has been an official AP test center since 2007. The curriculum emphasizes foreign languages, algebra, natural sciences, and essay writing, supported by the Academic Adviser Policy.

In 2008, the school launched the Hyundai Chungun Global Leader Program (HCGLP), an international class designed to prepare students for admission to top foreign universities. The program includes English presentation courses and the Global Leader Scholarship (GLS), a three-week study program at Portland State University in the United States. Additionally, the school promotes Korean traditional music and arts through classes and clubs for Samul Nori, Daegeum, and Gayageum. Hyundai Chungun High School enrolls approximately 530 students and employs 61 faculty members.

Holy Saviour Monastery of Julfa (Lang-hy) was an Armenian Apostolic monastery, located north-west of Jugha cemetery at the end of the gorge, 3 km away from the cemetery, on that slope of a high mountain. In the southern part of the monastery, at the foot of the Magharda mountain range, the Araks River flows.
Malcolm Lockyer (1923–1976) was an English composer, conductor, and musician known for his film scores, television themes, and orchestral work.

The 2015–16 Pittsburgh Penguins season was the franchise's 49th season in the National Hockey League (NHL). Under the leadership of general manager Jim Rutherford and head coaches Mike Johnston (October–December) and Mike Sullivan (December–June), the team finished second in the Metropolitan Division and Eastern Conference with a 48–26–8 record. Captain Sidney Crosby led the team in goals (36) and points (85), while Kris Letang led in assists (51) and penalty minutes (66). Marc-André Fleury recorded 35 wins as the starting goaltender, with rookie Matt Murray emerging as a key contributor during the playoffs.

The Penguins secured their fourth Stanley Cup championship, defeating the San Jose Sharks in the Stanley Cup Final. The team wore their alternate jerseys for all home games during the playoffs. Key contributors included Crosby, who won the Conn Smythe Trophy as playoff MVP, and Murray, who posted a 2.00 goals-against average. The Penguins' success was bolstered by strong performances from their core players and mid-season adjustments under Sullivan's coaching.

The season also featured notable transactions and draft picks, as well as high attendance at PPG Paints Arena. The Penguins' championship marked a return to prominence for the franchise, setting the stage for continued success in subsequent seasons.

Ben Christo (born March 22, 1980) is an English musician, songwriter, and guitarist, best known as a member of the gothic rock band The Sisters of Mercy since 2006. He has also founded and performed with bands such as Night by Night and Diamond Black, achieving critical acclaim for his songwriting and performances. Christo has collaborated with numerous artists across genres, contributed to chart-topping albums, and toured extensively worldwide. In addition to his band work, he is a session musician, composer, and musical director, with credits in film, television, and live performances.
Geometrics is an English indie rock band formed in 2008 in Stevenage.

Wawne /ˈwɔːn/, also spelled Waghen, is a small village and civil parish in the East Riding of Yorkshire, England. The village is situated approximately 5.5 miles (8.9 km) north of Hull city centre and 4 miles (6.4 km) south-east of Beverley on the east bank of the River Hull.

The civil parish consists of the village of Wawne and the hamlet of Meaux. According to the 2011 UK census, Wawne parish had a population of 975, an increase on the 2001 UK census figure of 878.

Sofya Skya (born Sofya Andreyevna Arzhakovskaya, August 12, 1987) is a Russian actress, director, acting coach, and former ballerina. A graduate of the Vaganova Academy of Russian Ballet and The Bashkir Choreographic College, she performed as a principal dancer in notable ballets before transitioning to acting. Skya gained recognition for her roles in films such as *The Tomb* (2009), *Shadows in Paradise* (2010), and *Assassins Run* (2013). She has also appeared in international productions, including *Opus of an Angel* (2017) and *Once There Was a War* (2019). Skya won the "Mrs. World" pageant in 2006 and has participated in various charity events, including performing at the 2013 Summer Universiade opening ceremony in Kazan.

Ghatak: Lethal is an Indian 1996 Hindi-language action thriller film directed by Rajkumar Santoshi, starring Sunny Deol, Meenakshi Seshadri, and Amrish Puri, while Danny Denzongpa plays the antagonist. Marking Santoshi's third collaboration with Deol, Seshadri, and Puri after Ghayal (1990) and Damini (1993), it earned ₹440 million worldwide to become the fourth highest-grossing Bollywood film of the year, and won three awards including Best Supporting Actor for Puri at the 42nd Filmfare Awards, where it was also nominated for three other awards: Best Director for Santoshi, Best Actor for Deol, and Best Villain for Denzongpa. Ghatak is Seshadri's last film to date. The film was later remade in Telugu as Aapthudu starring Rajasekhar and Anjala Zaveri.

The April 19 University Movement (Spanish initials: MU19A) is a Nicaraguan student movement created on April 25th, 2018 in Managua, Nicaragua. The student group opposes the government of Daniel Ortega who from 1979-1990 served as the country's first President following the Nicaraguan Revolution, was re-elected in 2006, and is the nation's current President. The April 19 University Movement organized and participated in the 2018–2020 Nicaraguan protests that began April 18th, 2018 in response to Social Security (INSS) reforms. The group has also served as spokesperson in national dialogue.

This student movement is considered a terrorist group by the Nicaraguan government as mentioned by the press: “The leaders [of the April 19th University Movement] are accused of crimes including murder, robbery, terrorism, kidnapping, trafficking, possession and use of restricted weapons, fires, torture, injuries, exposing people to danger.”

George Washington (bust by Houdon) is a public artwork that is a limited edition copy of an original work by French neoclassical sculptor Jean Antoine Houdon that is on display inside the Indiana Statehouse, which is located in Indianapolis, Indiana, United States. The artwork was cast a solid piece of plaster that has been painted white.

Prostitution in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) is widespread and driven by poverty, with sex workers facing significant health and safety risks. The country has one of the highest HIV prevalence rates among sex workers in Central Africa, at 5.7% compared to 0.7% in the general population, according to a 2016 UNAIDS report. Efforts by organizations like Médecins Sans Frontières aim to promote condom use among sex workers, though reluctance among clients remains a challenge.

Child prostitution is a significant issue in the DRC, often overlooked by authorities. Non-governmental organizations (NGOs) such as Association de Solidarité Internationale (ASI) and Reiper work to address the problem. Reports have documented sexual abuse of children by soldiers during the country's wars, as well as instances of child exploitation by criminal gangs and peacekeeping forces.

The DRC is also a source, destination, and transit country for sex trafficking. Women and children are subjected to forced marriage, sexual slavery, and trafficking both within the country and abroad. Some families are deceived into sending their children to cities like Kinshasa under false promises of education, only for them to be exploited. While the 2006 sexual violence statute criminalizes sexual slavery, trafficking, and forced prostitution, enforcement remains inadequate. The U.S. Department of State ranks the DRC as a Tier 3 country for human trafficking.

The Virginia Convention of 1864 was an assembly of sixteen loyal Unionists during the American Civil War meeting under the auspices of Virginia's Restored Government. It abolished slavery in the state of Virginia, and framed the fundamental civil law that served Virginia government for six years through Appomattox, Presidential Reconstruction and Congressional Reconstruction.
Oliver Bäte (born 1 March 1965) is a German business executive who is the CEO of Allianz, a position he has held since October 2014.

Ranpak Holdings Corp. manufactures machines and paper products used in protective paper-based packing for shipping goods and merchandise for e-commerce and industry, along with automation solutions. The company is based in Concord, Ohio, and has production facilities and offices in Reno, Nevada; Raleigh, North Carolina; Kansas City, Missouri; the Netherlands; Czech Republic; Shanghai and Singapore. The paper packaging material can be used for multiple applications like wrapping, cushioning, void fill and cold chain purposes, and is sometimes known as dunnage. Ranpak counts customers globally like Amazon, IKEA and Walmart.

Amanda Jaqueline Paswall (née DaCosta; born 7 October 1989) is an American-born Portuguese former professional footballer who played as a midfielder for Washington Spirit, Chicago Red Stars and Boston Breakers in the National Women's Soccer League (NWSL), Liverpool Ladies in England's FA WSL, the Washington Freedom and MagicJack in the Women's Professional Soccer (WPS) and for the Portuguese national team.

Sylvia Fine Kaye (August 29, 1913 – October 28, 1991) was an American lyricist, composer, and producer. Many of her compositions and productions were performed by her husband, comedian Danny Kaye. Fine was a Peabody Award-winner and was nominated for two Academy Awards and two Emmys during her career. She won an Emmy award in 1976 for children's special.
Enrico Cruciani Alibrandi (9 July 1839 – 8 November 1921) was an Italian politician who served as senator and mayor of Rome.

Milner is a city in Lamar County, Georgia, United States. The population was 610 at the 2010 census, up from 522 at the 2000 census.

Milner is part of the official Atlanta 29 County Metropolitan Statistical Area - MSA.

Fairhope Plantation is a historic Gothic Revival plantation house near Uniontown, Alabama, built between 1857 and 1861 for Joseph Selden and his wife, Elizabeth Collier Minge. Designed by local builder Theophilus G. Fowler, the house features intricate Gothic Revival details, including bargeboards, hood moldings, and a cast-iron veranda. The plantation was established on land gifted by Elizabeth's family and remained in the Selden family for over a century. It was added to the Alabama Register of Landmarks and Heritage in 1991 and the National Register of Historic Places in 1992.
The WAGR M class were diesel-electric locomotives built by Walkers Limited for the Western Australian Government Railways, primarily for shunting duties.

WNED-FM (94.5 MHz) is a non-commercial radio station licensed to Buffalo, New York. WNED-FM offers a classical music radio format. It is owned by the Western New York Public Broadcasting Association (formerly the Western New York Educational TV Association), doing business as Buffalo Toronto Public Media. The organization also operates PBS network affiliate Channel 17 WNED-TV and FM 88.7 WBFO (which offers a news/talk format and programming from NPR). While WNED-FM airs no commercials, it does conduct periodic pledge drives on the air to seek donations for the station. WNED-FM has local hosts in most day-parts, including weekends.

Programming on WNED-FM is simulcast on WNJA 89.7 FM in Jamestown, New York for listeners in Southwestern New York and some parts of Pennsylvania. WNED-FM's studios and offices are at Horizons Plaza on Lower Terrace in Buffalo, and the transmitter is off Zimmerman Road in Hamburg, New York. WNED-FM and TV also maintain an office in Toronto for listeners and contributors in Canada.
The Antifascist Committee of Ukraine is an anti-fascist and pro-Russian organization, closely linked to the Communist Party of Ukraine.

Theodorick Bland (December 2, 1708 – June 1, 1784) was an American planter, politician, and member of the Virginia Senate from 1776 to 1779, representing Isle of Wight, Surry, and Prince George Counties. A member of the prominent Bland and Randolph families, he managed several plantations, including Kippax and Cawsons, and later moved to Amelia County. Bland played a role in supporting the American Revolution, including raising funds to replace gunpowder seized by British forces. He was also a militia colonel and descended from Theodorick Bland of Westover, a notable early Virginian.

Zhenskii vestnik (Russian: Women's Herald) was a Russian language monthly feminist magazine which was published in Saint Petersburg in the period 1904–1917. Its subtitle was Soiuz zhenshchin, Jus suffragii. The magazine billed itself as monthly social scientific and literary journal on equality and advancement of women.
Markku Peltoniemi (born 8 February 1948) is a Finnish former footballer and a current sporting director for Helsingin Jalkapalloklubi

The 68th Siege Battery, Royal Garrison Artillery, was a British Army unit active from 1915 to 1919 during World War I. Formed in Pembroke Dock, Wales, it served on the Western Front, participating in major battles such as the Somme, Passchendaele, and the Hundred Days Offensive. The battery was disbanded in 1919.

Stephen Langton (c. 1150 – 9 July 1228) was an English Cardinal of the Roman Catholic Church and Archbishop of Canterbury between 1207 and his death in 1228. The dispute between King John of England and Pope Innocent III over his election was a major factor in the crisis which produced Magna Carta in 1215. Cardinal Langton is also credited with having divided the Bible into the standard modern arrangement of chapters used today.

The 1979 Memorial Cup occurred May 6–13 at the Palais des Sports in Sherbrooke, Quebec, the Colisée de Trois-Rivières in Trois-Rivières, Quebec and at the Verdun Auditorium in Verdun, Quebec. It was the 61st annual Memorial Cup competition and determined the major junior ice hockey champion of the Canadian Hockey League (CHL). Participating teams were the winners of the Ontario Major Junior Hockey League, Quebec Major Junior Hockey League and Western Hockey League which were the Peterborough Petes, Trois-Rivières Draveurs and Brandon Wheat Kings. Peterborough won their first Memorial Cup, defeating Brandon in the final game.

Bill Dana (born William Szathmary; October 5, 1924 – June 15, 2017) was an American comedian, actor, and screenwriter best known for creating and portraying the character José Jiménez. A prolific comedy writer, he contributed to shows like *The Steve Allen Show* and *Get Smart*. Dana also starred in *The Bill Dana Show* (1963–1965) and wrote the iconic *All in the Family* episode "Sammy's Visit." He retired the José Jiménez character in 1970 due to changing cultural sensitivities. Dana was instrumental in establishing the American Comedy Archives at Emerson College. He passed away at age 92 in Nashville, Tennessee.

Stanstead is a village and civil parish in Suffolk, England. The name Stanstead comes from the Old English for "Stony place". Located off the B1066, it is around 4 miles (6 km) from Sudbury, and is part of Babergh district. It is about 2 kilometres (1 mi) from Glemsford, 12 miles (19 km) from Hadleigh, and 4 miles (6 km) from Lavenham.

**Compress** is a Unix-based file compression software initially released in February 1985 by Spencer Thomas. It uses the LZW algorithm to reduce file sizes, with compressed files typically given the ".Z" extension. Files can be decompressed using the accompanying `uncompress` utility, which restores the original file attributes. 

The software gained popularity but later declined due to patent restrictions on the LZW algorithm, leading to the rise of alternatives like gzip and bzip2. Despite this, compress remains available on Unix, BSD, and IBM i systems. The LZW patent expired in 2003, making compress freely usable worldwide.

Luke Michael Haines (born 7 October 1967) is an English musician, songwriter and author. He has recorded music under various names and with various bands, including The Auteurs, Baader Meinhof and Black Box Recorder.

The Zhdanov Doctrine, or Zhdanovism, was a Soviet cultural policy introduced by Andrei Zhdanov in 1946, emphasizing strict ideological conformity in the arts and promoting socialist realism. It sought to purge "bourgeois" and "cosmopolitan" influences, instead prioritizing Russian patriotism and cultural independence. The doctrine targeted prominent figures, including writers Mikhail Zoshchenko and Anna Akhmatova, and later extended to composers such as Dmitri Shostakovich and Sergei Prokofiev during the "anti-formalism campaign" of 1948. Zhdanovism also influenced Soviet-controlled Eastern Europe, enforcing socialist realism and fostering pro-Soviet intellectual movements. The policy was part of a broader effort to consolidate Soviet power and cultural dominance but was rolled back after Joseph Stalin's death, with many condemned artists rehabilitated in 1958.

Mount Kineo is a prominent geological feature located on a 1,150-acre (470 ha) peninsula that extends from the easterly shore of Moosehead Lake in the northern forest of Maine. With 700-foot (210 m) cliffs rising straight up from the water, it is the central feature of Mount Kineo State Park, a protected area of 800 acres (320 ha) managed by the Maine Department of Agriculture, Conservation and Forestry.

The Open Siddur Project is a digital humanities initiative founded by Aharon Varady in 2009, with Efraim Feinstein as lead developer. It aims to provide open-source tools and content for creating and sharing Jewish liturgy and related works, emphasizing inclusivity, pluralism, and free-culture principles. The project collects and digitizes historical and contemporary liturgical texts, offering them under open licenses. Recognized for its innovation, the project has inspired similar initiatives and serves as a model for open-source collaboration in Jewish cultural preservation and practice.

The Limestone Coast is a region in southeastern South Australia, encompassing local government areas such as Mount Gambier, Robe, and Naracoorte Lucindale. Known for its limestone geology, the area features wetlands, volcanic craters, and World Heritage-listed Naracoorte Caves. It supports agriculture, viticulture, forestry, and tourism, with significant wildlife habitats and Mediterranean climate conditions.

Hadia Tajik (born 18 July 1983) is a Pakistani-Norwegian jurist, journalist and politician from the Labour Party. She served as Minister of Labour and Social Inclusion from 2021 to 2022. She previously served as Minister of Culture from 2012 to 2013. She was 29 years of age at the time and became the youngest minister to serve in the Norwegian government. She is the first Cabinet member that is a Muslim. Tajik has served as a Member of Parliament representing Rogaland since 2017, and Oslo from 2009 to 2017. She was also the party's deputy leader from 2015 until 2022.

*UFOria* is a 1984 comedy film written and directed by John Binder, starring Fred Ward, Cindy Williams, and Harry Dean Stanton. The story follows a drifter who falls in love with a UFO-obsessed supermarket clerk while assisting a faith-healing con man. Despite production challenges and limited theatrical release, the film garnered positive reviews, with critics praising its originality and performances. It was later released on VHS in 1987 but has not been issued on DVD or Blu-ray due to music licensing costs.

*¡Que viva la lucha!* is a 2007 Mexican documentary film directed and produced by Gustavo Vazquez. The 54-minute film explores the vibrant culture of lucha libre in Tijuana, focusing on its cultural significance, the dual lives of its wrestlers, and the passionate fanbase. It follows the stories of luchadores such as Extreme Tiger, a técnico who dreams of international success, and rudos like Angel Negro Jr. and Pancho Cachondo, who embody the archetypal villains of the sport. The documentary delves into the symbolism of the wrestling mask, linking it to Mexican cultural and historical traditions, and highlights the diverse participants in lucha libre, including women, gay wrestlers, and referees. Through interviews and dynamic footage, the film captures the energy of the wrestling arena and the deep connection between the wrestlers and their fans, showcasing lucha libre as both a sport and a cultural phenomenon.

Patrick Desmond Callaghan (16 July 1926 – 6 April 1992) was an Air Commodore in the Pakistan Air Force (PAF) and a pioneer officer who served from 1941 to 1971. Initially commissioned into the Royal Indian Air Force, he opted for the PAF after the partition of India. Callaghan played key roles as a flight instructor, combat pilot during the Indo-Pakistani wars of 1965 and 1971, and Chief Inspector of Aviation Safety. After retiring, he became an internationally recognized expert in air crash investigations, assisting several countries.

Mr. Muthafuckin' eXquire, born Hugh Anthony Allison in 1986, is an American rapper from Brooklyn, New York. Emerging in 2008 with his debut mixtape *The Big Fat Kill*, he gained widespread recognition in 2011 with the release of his song "Huzzah" and its remix, "The Last Huzzah," which featured prominent rappers and was named one of the best songs of the year by *Rolling Stone*. Known for his eclectic style and bold lyricism, eXquire released several acclaimed mixtapes and EPs, including *Lost in Translation* and *Power & Passion*. After a brief stint with Universal Republic Records, he returned to independent releases, culminating in his self-titled debut studio album in 2019.

Neodiprion abietis, commonly known as the balsam fir sawfly, is a species of sawfly in the family Diprionidae. Native to North America, it primarily feeds on balsam fir and other conifers, causing significant defoliation and tree growth reduction during outbreaks. The species undergoes a complex life cycle, with larvae feeding on older foliage and exhibiting distinct physical changes across instars. Outbreaks, which can last 3–5 years, are influenced by ecological factors and can be managed using biological controls like the NeabNPV virus or insect growth regulators.

A Massachusetts general election was held on November 2, 1982 in the Commonwealth of Massachusetts.

The election included:

Democratic and Republican candidates were selected in party primaries held September 14, 1982.

*Red Obsession* is a 2013 Australian documentary film directed and written by David Roach and Warwick Ross, narrated by Russell Crowe. It explores the global wine industry, focusing on China's growing demand for Bordeaux wines. The film received critical acclaim for its cinematography and thought-provoking commentary on cultural and economic implications, earning a 100% approval rating on Rotten Tomatoes.

David Peter Ryan (born 5 January 1957) is an English former professional footballer who played as a goalkeeper. He played in the English Football League for Port Vale in 1976, on loan from Manchester United. He later played non-League football for Southport, Northwich Victoria, Hyde United and Chorley.
John Mortimer Boudreaux, Jr. (December 10, 1936, New Roads, Louisiana – January 14, 2017, Los Angeles) was an American drummer who was active in jazz, soul, and rhythm & blues idioms.

The 2013 season was Kashiwa Reysol's third consecutive campaign in the J.League Division 1 under manager Nelsinho Baptista. The team finished 10th in the league, won the J.League Cup, and reached the semifinals of the AFC Champions League. Masato Kudo was the season's top scorer with 27 goals in all competitions.

Ro-45 was an Imperial Japanese Navy Kaichū type submarine of the K6 sub-class. Completed and commissioned in January 1944, she served in the central Pacific Ocean during World War II and was sunk in April 1944 during her first combat sortie.

The Multi Fibre Arrangement (MFA) was an international trade agreement established in 1974 to regulate the global textile and clothing industry. It aimed to manage imports from developing countries to developed nations through quotas, addressing the competitive advantage of low labor costs in developing economies. While the MFA restricted exports from many developing countries, it also facilitated growth in nations like Bangladesh, which benefited from preferential treatment. The agreement was phased out under the World Trade Organization's Agreement on Textiles and Clothing, with quotas ending in 2005. This led to significant shifts in global trade, including increased competition from China and challenges for smaller economies.

"Her Strut" is a song written by Bob Seger that was first released on his 1980 album Against the Wind. It was also released as the B-side of his single "The Horizontal Bop." The song was inspired by feminist icon Jane Fonda.

Otto Kirchheimer (1905–1965) was a German-American political scientist, legal scholar, and sociologist known for his critical analyses of constitutional law, political systems, and the relationship between social structures and state power. A member of the Frankfurt School, he contributed to the study of fascism, democracy, and party systems, co-developing the concept of the "catch-all party." Kirchheimer's work remains influential in political and legal theory.
New Berlin is a borough in Union County, Pennsylvania, United States. The population was 791 at the 2020 census.
The Roy model is one of the earliest works in economics on self-selection due to A. D. Roy. The basic model considers two types of workers that choose occupation in one of two sectors.

Coryphodon (from Greek κορῦφὴ, "point", and ὀδοὺς, "tooth", meaning peaked tooth, referring to "the development of the angles of the ridges into points [on the molars].") is an extinct genus of pantodonts of the family Coryphodontidae.

Coryphodon was a pantodont, a member of the world's first group of large browsing mammals. It migrated across what is now northern North America, replacing Barylambda, an earlier pantodont. It is regarded as the ancestor of the genus Hypercoryphodon of Late Eocene Mongolia.

Coryphodon is known from many specimens in North America and considerably fewer in Europe, Mongolia, and China. It is a small to medium-sized coryphodontid who differs from other members of the family in dental characteristics.

Phenoxyethanol is the organic compound with the formula C6H5OC2H4OH. It is a colorless oily liquid. It can be classified as a glycol ether and a phenol ether. It is a common preservative in vaccine formulations.

Fort Frederick was a fortification built in 1676 in Albany, New York, to replace Fort Orange and protect the city from western threats, particularly Native Americans. Initially constructed as a wooden stockade, it was rebuilt with masonry between 1702 and 1735. The fort played a significant role during the Seven Years' War but fell into disrepair afterward. It was dismantled in 1789, and its site is now marked by historical plaques near the New York State Capitol.

*Live in Boston* is a live album by Fleetwood Mac, recorded in February 1970 at the Boston Tea Party venue and released in February 1985. Initially shelved, the recordings were later reissued under various titles, including *Jumping at Shadows* and *Boston Live*. In 1998, Snapper Music released a comprehensive three-volume set, later reissued as *Live at the Boston Tea Party* and repackaged in 2013 as *Boston*.

Peter George Baines is an Australian researcher, academic, and author specializing in geophysical fluid dynamics, climate dynamics, oceanography, and volcano dynamics. He earned degrees in mathematics and physics from Melbourne University and a PhD in geophysical fluid dynamics from Cambridge University. Baines has held academic and research positions at institutions including the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, CSIRO Atmospheric Research, and the University of Melbourne, where he is an Honorary Professorial Fellow.

Baines' research has advanced understanding of stratified fluid flows, ocean tides, climate variability, and volcanic eruptions. He authored the influential book *Topographic Effects in Stratified Flows* and has received numerous accolades, including the AMOS Priestley Medal. He has also contributed to international meteorological organizations and served on editorial boards, making significant contributions to atmospheric and oceanic sciences.

Ruby Romaine is a fictional character portrayed by Tracey Ullman in the television series *Tracey Takes On...* and its spin-off *Tracey Ullman in the Trailer Tales*. A brash, hard-living Hollywood makeup artist, Ruby has worked in the film and television industry for over 50 years, with over 720 credits to her name. Known for her colorful personality, Ruby recounts her tumultuous life, including her early days in Hollywood, numerous affairs with actors, and her unconventional family history. The character was inspired by real-life Hollywood makeup artists and figures from Ullman’s experiences, blending humor and satire to create a vivid portrayal of "Hollywood white trash."
Sergio Garavini (18 May 1926 – 7 September 2001) was an Italian politician, syndicalist, and trade union leader.

Raquel Torres Cerdán (born November 19, 1948) is a Mexican chef, restaurateur, and researcher known for preserving and promoting the indigenous cuisine of Veracruz. She operates two restaurants and teaches workshops on traditional Mexican cooking.

Samira Ćosović (Serbian Cyrillic: Самира Ћосовић; born 19 November 1973) is a politician in Serbia. She was a member of the National Assembly of Serbia from 2020 to 2022 and is currently the deputy mayor of Prijepolje. Ćosović is a member of the Social Democratic Party of Serbia (SDPS) and also of its regional affiliate, the Sandžak Democratic Party.

Anthony Di Pietro (born 15 February 1969) is an Australian businessman and sports executive, serving as the CEO and Director of Premier Fresh Australia since 2003 and Chairman of Melbourne Victory FC since 2011. Under his leadership, Melbourne Victory became one of the most successful clubs in A-League history, achieving multiple championships and premierships.

Norma Aleandro (born 2 May 1936) is an Argentine actress. She is considered one of the most celebrated and prolific Argentine actresses of all time and is recognized as a cultural icon in her home country.

Aleandro starred in the 1985 film The Official Story, a role that earned her the Cannes Award for best actress. She has performed in other successful films like The Truce (1974), Cousins (1989), Autumn Sun (1996), The Lighthouse (1998), Son of the Bride (2001) and Cama Adentro (2005). For her performance as Florencia Sánchez Morales in the 1987 film Gaby: A True Story, she received a Golden Globe nomination and an Academy Award nomination for Best Supporting Actress.

Aleandro wrote the 1970 film The Inheritors and performed in various plays such as August: Osage County. Aleandro also appeared in the Argentine adaptation of BeTipul, the critically successful En terapia.

An Ziwen (Chinese: 安子文; pinyin: Ān Zǐwén; 25 September 1909 – 25 June 1980), born as An Zhihan (安之瀚), was a Chinese politician and member of the Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party. He served as minister of the CCP Central Committee Organization Department, the Central People's Government Minister of Personnel, deputy secretary of the Central Discipline Inspection Commission, the Standing Committee of the CPPCC National Committee, among other roles. He authored the book "Revolutionary successors training is a strategic task of the party".

Hiroko Anzai (born February 9, 1979, in Kanagawa, Japan) is a Japanese actress, model, and former gravure idol. She debuted in 1996 and gained popularity in the late 1990s as a fashion model and television personality. Known for her work in teen magazines and music collaborations, Anzai briefly retired from show business before returning in 2008. She is also skilled in judo and swimming.
Mama Qadeer is a Baloch human rights activist known for his protests against enforced disappearances in Balochistan, including his son's killing.

Daniel J. Berthiaume (born January 26, 1966) is a Canadian former professional ice hockey goaltender who played six seasons in the National Hockey League (NHL) with the Winnipeg Jets, Minnesota North Stars, Los Angeles Kings, Boston Bruins, and Ottawa Senators.

Georgy Vasilyevich Pigulevsky (1888–1964) was a Russian chemist and biochemist known for his research on essential oils, resins, and vegetable fats, particularly their chemical properties and practical applications. A professor at Leningrad State University, he contributed significantly to organic chemistry, including pioneering the use of Raman spectroscopy in the USSR. Pigulevsky played a vital role in organizing medicine production during the Siege of Leningrad and authored over 200 publications, including influential monographs. He was awarded the Order of Lenin and other honors for his scientific achievements.

*Swing Kids* is a 1993 American drama film directed by Thomas Carter and written by Jonathan Marc Feldman. Set in 1939 Nazi Germany, the film follows a group of teenagers in Hamburg who embrace American swing music and culture while grappling with the pressures of the Hitler Youth. Starring Robert Sean Leonard, Christian Bale, and Frank Whaley, the film explores themes of resistance, friendship, and ideological conflict. Despite its historical backdrop and a soundtrack featuring swing music, *Swing Kids* received mixed reviews, with a 56% score on Rotten Tomatoes and criticism for its screenplay, though some praised its performances and research. The film grossed $5.6 million against a $12 million budget.

Life is a 1999 American buddy comedy-drama film directed by Ted Demme. The film stars Eddie Murphy and Martin Lawrence. It is the second film featuring Murphy and Lawrence together, the first being Boomerang. The supporting cast includes Ned Beatty, R. Lee Ermey, Obba Babatundé, Bernie Mac, Anthony Anderson, Miguel A. Núñez Jr., Bokeem Woodbine, Guy Torry, Michael Taliferro and Barry Shabaka Henley. The film's format is a story being told by an elderly inmate about two of his friends, Ray (Murphy) and Claude (Lawrence), who are both wrongfully convicted of murder and sentenced to life in prison. It received an Oscar nomination for Best Makeup at the 72nd Academy Awards. Life failed to meet the studio's expectations at the box office, and received mixed reactions from critics. The film later found a strong cult following among Murphy and Lawrence’s fans, establishing Life as a cult classic.

The 2020–21 UEFA Women's Champions League knockout phase began on 9 December 2020 with the round of 32 and ended with the final on 16 May 2021 at the Gamla Ullevi in Gothenburg, Sweden, to decide the champions of the 2020–21 UEFA Women's Champions League. A total of 32 teams competed in the knockout phase.

The hired armed cutter *Sandwich* was a British naval vessel acquired on 22 May 1798 and armed with 2 4-pounder guns and 10 12-pounder carronades. Under British command, she captured the Dutch hoy *Hoop* in 1798 but was herself captured by the French on 14 June 1799. The French Navy retained her name and used her until 1803, when she was recaptured by the Royal Navy. Purchased in 1804, *Sandwich* served briefly before being sold in Jamaica in 1805.

Ibex Peak, is a 2,039-metre (6,690-foot) granitic horn located in the North Cascades of southwestern British Columbia, Canada. It is situated 13 km (8 mi) west-northwest of Coquihalla Summit, and 1 km (1 mi) northwest of Steinbok Peak. Its nearest higher peak is Alpaca Peak, 6.2 km (4 mi) to the east. Precipitation runoff from the peak drains into tributaries of the Anderson River. The mountain was named for the ibex, as part of the ungulate theme for several other nearby peaks that were submitted by Philip Kubik of Vancouver. The mountain's name was officially adopted on February 5, 1976, by the Geographical Names Board of Canada.

Douglas A. Keszler is a distinguished professor in the Department of Chemistry at Oregon State University, adjunct professor in the Physics Department at OSU and adjunct professor in the Department of Chemistry at University of Oregon. He is also the director of the Center for Sustainable Materials Chemistry, and a member of the Oregon Nanoscience and Microtechnologies Institute (ONAMI) leadership team.

Burlington Street is a partially at grade and elevated roadway in Hamilton, Ontario, stretching along the south shore of Hamilton Harbour in Lake Ontario. Burlington Street has four different statuses along the route. In its western terminus, it is a collector route ending at Bay Street North. Upon the intersection at Wellington Street, the road becomes an arterial route with four lanes. The Street ends at Parkdale Avenue. On October 14, 2015, city council approved the renaming of the upper portion of Burlington Street from 165 meters east of Ottawa St to the QEW to Nikola Tesla Boulevard.

Nikola Tesla Boulevard is the official exit #90 from QEW. The signage along the QEW for Burlington Street was eventually replaced between October 22-23, 2016 to reflect the name change. After this change, the westbound lanes of Burlington street begin from the first exit on Nikola Tesla Boulevard, heading westbound.

The International Pool Tour was a professional sports tour created in 2005 by Kevin Trudeau and hosted by Rebecca Grant. It aimed to elevate pool to the level of other modern sports. Closely modeled on the PGA Tour, the IPT offered the largest prize funds in pool history in its first year. The tour attracted the top pool players in the world. It differed from the many nine-ball tournaments, as all IPT events were eight-ball matches. The company was based in Hinsdale, Illinois.

Many pool enthusiasts were initially skeptical, but the first event was successful, and at the time was the biggest tournament in billiards history. However, by the end of 2006, the tour was in serious financial trouble, and was forced to stop staging major tournaments.

Oksana Alexandrovna Kostina (Lang-ru; 15 April 1972 – 11 February 1993) was a Soviet and Russian individual rhythmic gymnast. She was the 1992 World All-around champion and 1992 European All-around bronze medalist.

Bygdøy Royal Estate is a historic Baroque estate in Oslo, Norway, owned by the Norwegian royal family. Originally linked to a Cistercian monastery, it became royal property in the 13th century. The current main building, completed in 1733, has served as a royal summer residence and underwent significant restorations in recent years. The estate features gardens, organic farming, and public access to certain areas, including a café and farm facilities.

The Technicolor Time Machine is a 1967 science fiction novel by American writer Harry Harrison. It is a time travel story with comedic elements, which satirizes Hollywood. The story first appeared in Analog Science Fiction and Fact magazine, where it was serialized in three parts in the March–May 1967 issues, under the title "The Time-Machined Saga."

Exponentiation by squaring is an efficient algorithm for computing large integer powers of a number. It is based on the observation that powers can be calculated recursively by squaring and multiplying, reducing the number of operations to a logarithmic scale compared to the linear approach. The method can be implemented recursively or iteratively, with variations that optimize memory usage or computational efficiency. It is widely used in applications such as modular arithmetic, cryptography, and matrix exponentiation, where fast computation of powers is essential. Advanced techniques, like the 2k-ary method and Montgomery's ladder, further enhance its efficiency and security.
USS R-14 was an R-class submarine of the United States Navy, commissioned in 1919 and decommissioned in 1945.

Operation Northern Falcon is an ongoing joint operation between the Ukrainian and Danish defense ministries, initiated in 2009, to transport fuel to Greenland. Conducted annually, it involves Ukrainian Air Force personnel operating in extreme Arctic conditions to deliver fuel and cargo between Thule Air Base and Station Nord.

Olwen Williams is a Welsh physician and healthcare leader, currently serving as the Royal College of Physicians' Vice President for Wales. A Fellow of the Learned Society of Wales, she has held prominent roles in medical organizations and advised the Welsh Government.

Dan Dotson (born November 23, 1962) is an American auctioneer and television personality, best known for his role on A&E's reality series *Storage Wars*. Active in auctioneering since 1974, he founded American Auctioneers in 1983, specializing in storage unit and estate auctions. Alongside his wife and business partner, Laura Dotson, he has become a prominent figure in the auction industry. In 2014, Dotson survived a double aneurysm.

Association football during World War II was significantly disrupted, with many domestic leagues and international competitions suspended or altered. In countries like England, Scotland, and Germany, regional leagues and unofficial matches replaced regular tournaments, while players often served in the military, with some careers interrupted or ended. Football was used as a morale booster for soldiers and civilians, though the sport also faced tragedies, including the deaths of Jewish players during the Holocaust and the destruction of clubs. Despite the challenges, some players were decorated for military service, while others collaborated with occupying forces.

In computer engineering, directory-based cache coherence is a type of cache coherence mechanism, where directories are used to manage caches in place of bus snooping. Bus snooping methods scale poorly due to the use of broadcasting. These methods can be used to target both performance and scalability of directory systems.

David Production Co., Ltd. (株式会社デイヴィッドプロダクション) is a Japanese animation studio founded in September 2007 by Kōji Kajita and Taito Okiura. Based in Nishitōkyō, Tokyo, it became a subsidiary of Fuji TV in 2014.

The Eurasian otter (*Lutra lutra*) is a semi-aquatic mammal native to Europe, Asia, and parts of Africa, and is the most widely distributed otter species. It is characterized by its brown fur, cream underside, and adaptations for aquatic life, such as dense bones to reduce buoyancy. Primarily feeding on fish, it also consumes amphibians, crustaceans, and birds. Eurasian otters are solitary, territorial, and breed year-round, with females raising pups alone. Once threatened by pollution, habitat loss, and hunting, populations have rebounded in parts of Europe due to conservation efforts and legal protections.

Parmalat S.p.A. is an Italian multinational food company specializing in milk, dairy products, fruit juices, and tea. Founded in 1961 by Calisto Tanzi in Parma, Italy, the company grew into a global corporation by the 1990s, employing over 30,000 people in 30 countries. Parmalat became a household name through its sponsorship of Formula 1 teams in the 1980s and 1990s. However, in 2003, it was embroiled in one of Europe’s largest financial scandals, with €14.3 billion in hidden debt uncovered. Founder Calisto Tanzi was convicted of fraud, and the company underwent bankruptcy and restructuring under government-appointed administrator Enrico Bondi. 

In 2011, French dairy giant Lactalis acquired Parmalat, eventually delisting it in 2019 and relocating strategic operations to France. Despite its troubled history, Parmalat remains a significant player in the global dairy industry, with 2015 revenues of €6.42 billion and a workforce of over 27,000 employees.

*Up the Elephant and Round the Castle* is a British sitcom that aired on ITV from 30 November 1983 to 7 November 1985. Starring Jim Davidson, the series follows Jim London, a mischievous young man who inherits a dilapidated Victorian house in south London and navigates comedic misadventures involving the police, drinking, and women. The show ran for three series with 22 episodes and featured a theme tune composed by Keith Emerson. Produced by Thames Television, it was later released on DVD, with the complete series reissued in 2016.

The Manned Spaceflight Engineer Program was an effort by the United States Air Force to train American military personnel as payload specialists for United States Department of Defense missions on the Space Shuttle program.

"Tight Connection to My Heart (Has Anybody Seen My Love)" is a song by Bob Dylan that was released on his 1985 album Empire Burlesque. As a single, it was a Top 40 Hit in New Zealand and Belgium. An earlier version of the song, entitled "Someone's Got a Hold of My Heart", was recorded for Dylan's 1983 LP Infidels, but was not included on that album; it later appeared on The Bootleg Series Volumes 1–3 (Rare & Unreleased) 1961–1991.
Sir Stephen George Tallents KCMG CB CBE (20 October 1884 – 11 September 1958) was a British civil servant and public relations expert.

Ports North, the trading name of the Far North Queensland Ports Corporation Limited, is a Queensland Government statutory corporation that is responsible for the Cairns Marlin Marina and the Cairns Cityport project and the ports in Cairns, Cape Flattery, Karumba, Mourilyan, Skardon River, Quintell Beach, Thursday Island, Burketown and Cooktown, in Queensland, Australia. Since 2017, the shareholding Ministers are the Honourable Jackie Trad, Deputy Premier, Treasurer and Minister for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Partnerships, and the Honourable Mark Bailey, Minister for Transport and Main Roads.

Formerly known as the Cairns Port Authority, the corporation was responsible for the operation of the Cairns International Airport until the sale of the airport in December 2008 to a private consortium. The former authority was responsible for the Cairns Seaport and in 2009 became responsible for the regional ports of Mourilyan, Cooktown, Cape Flattery, Quintell Beach, Thursday Island, Skardon River, Karumba and Burketown.

*Anima Yell!* (アニマエール!, *Anima Ēru!*) is a Japanese four-panel manga series written and illustrated by Tsukasa Unohana. It was serialized in *Manga Time Kirara Carat* from February 2016 to August 2020, with five tankōbon volumes published. The story follows Kohane Hatoya, a high school girl who starts a cheerleading club. An anime adaptation by Doga Kobo aired from October to December 2018, running for 12 episodes. The series blends slice-of-life and sports elements, receiving mixed reviews for its characters and humor but praise for its animation and charm. Characters also appear in the mobile game *Kirara Fantasia*.
A by-election to the Spanish Cortes Españolas was held in Spanish Guinea in 1960.

Rupa Goswami (Lang-sa, Lang-bn, IAST: Rūpa Gosvāmī; 1489–1564) was a devotional teacher (guru), poet, and philosopher of the Gaudiya Vaishnava tradition. With his brother Sanatana Goswami, he is considered the most senior of the six Goswamis of Vrindavan associated with Caitanya Mahaprabhu, a hidden avatar (incarnation) of Krishna in Kali Yuga.

Exabyte Corporation was a manufacturer of magnetic tape data storage products headquartered in Boulder, Colorado, United States. Exabyte Corp. is now defunct, but the company's technology is sold by Tandberg Data under both brand names. Prior to the 2006 demise, Exabyte offered tape storage and automation solutions for servers, workstations, LANs and SANs. Exabyte is best known for introducing the Data8 (8mm) magnetic tape format in 1987. At the time of its demise, Exabyte manufactured VXA and LTO based products. The company controlled VXA technology but did not play a large role in the LTO community.

The Virginia Constitutional Convention of 1864 was held from February 13 to April 11 in Alexandria, Virginia, during the American Civil War. Composed of delegates from Union-occupied counties, the convention abolished slavery without compensation, reformed voting procedures, and limited disenfranchisement to Confederate officeholders. Its constitution governed Union-controlled areas until replaced in 1870.

Mike Mineo is an American singer-songwriter from Hollywood, Florida, known for his eclectic style and dynamic performances. He released his debut album *Eccentricity* in 2010, followed by *Beach Season* (2011), *Big Big Star* (2013), and *1* (2016). His music has been featured on college radio, television, and major events like SunFest, and he has opened for the Steve Miller Band.
Dale Albert Kimball (born November 28, 1939) is a Senior United States district judge of the United States District Court for the District of Utah.

Australia's National Reserve System (NRS) is a network of more than 10,000 Commonwealth plus state and territory protected areas which, in combination, on a national scale, protect more than 137 million hectares (340×10^6 acres), greater than 17% of the continent, of unique biodiversity and most significant ecological landscapes for future generations. The aim of the NRS is protect the diversity of all native landscapes, flora and fauna across Australia through strategic habitat protection. It consists of public, indigenous and privately protected areas of land and inland freshwaters.

As part of the National Reserve System Cooperative Program, the Interim Biogeographic Regionalisation for Australia framework was developed as a planning tool to assist in identifying areas of priority.
Come is an American alternative rock band, formed in Boston by Thalia Zedek (vocals, guitar), Chris Brokaw (guitar, vocals), Arthur Johnson (drums), and Sean O'Brien (bass).

Climate change in the Gambia is having impacts on the natural environment and people of The Gambia. Like other countries in West Africa, the impacts of climate change are expected to be varied and complex. Climate change adaptation is going to be important to achieve the Sustainable Development Goals in the country.

Sir John Wedderburn, 5th Baronet of Blackness (died 28 November 1746), was a Scottish landowner and Jacobite who participated in the Jacobite rising of 1745, known as the "Forty Five." A supporter of Charles Edward Stuart, he served as a colonel in the Jacobite army and was captured at the Battle of Culloden. Wedderburn was tried for treason in London, found guilty, and executed at Kennington Common. Born into a family with Jacobite ties, he faced financial hardship before the rebellion. He married Jean Fullerton in 1724 and had several children, including two sons, John and James.

Ahmad Nami (1873 – December 13, 1962) was a Syrian politician and statesman who served as the 5th Prime Minister and 2nd President of Syria from 1926 to 1928 during the French Mandate. Born in Beirut to an affluent family of Ottoman descent, he was educated in military academies and married Ayşe Sultan, daughter of Sultan Abdul Hamid II. As president, Nami sought to preserve Syrian unity, establish a national army, and secure compensation for citizens affected by the Great Syrian Revolt, but his ambitions were curtailed by French authorities, leading to his removal in 1928. After retiring from politics, he lived in Lebanon and occasionally lectured in France.

Alfred Edward Bailey (14 March 1871 – 1 August 1950) was an English cricketer who played as a left-arm orthodox spin bowler. He represented Somerset in 43 first-class matches between 1900 and 1911, taking 133 wickets at an average of 26.62. Bailey's career highlights included several notable performances, such as career-best match figures of 11 for 65 against Warwickshire in 1906 and 8 for 46 against Yorkshire in 1907. He also played for the Scottish national team in first-class matches in 1908 and 1909.

Dragon Ball Xenoverse is an action role-playing fighting game based on the Dragon Ball franchise developed by Dimps and published by Bandai Namco Games. It was released in February 2015 for PlayStation 3, PlayStation 4, Xbox 360, Xbox One, and Microsoft Windows.

A sequel, Dragon Ball Xenoverse 2 was released in 2016.

The Jamaat al Muslimeen coup attempt was an attempt to overthrow the government of Trinidad and Tobago, instigated on Friday, 27 July 1990. Over the course of six days, Jamaat al Muslimeen, a radical extremist Islamist group, held hostages (including Prime Minister A. N. R. Robinson and other government officials) at the Red House and at the headquarters of the state-owned national television broadcaster, Trinidad and Tobago Television (TTT). On 1 August, the insurgents surrendered. They were charged with treason, but were ordered released by the Court of Appeal. Twenty four people were killed and many more were injured in the coup.

*Dangaioh* (破邪大星ダンガイオー, *Haja Taisei Dangaiō*) is a Japanese original video animation (OVA) series in the mecha and science fiction genres, directed by Toshiki Hirano and released from 1987 to 1989. The story follows four psychic warriors who unite their powers to form Dangaioh, a powerful mecha, to combat the tyranny of Captain Galimos and his henchman, Gil Berg. The series gained attention for its dynamic animation, music by Chumei Watanabe, and subsequent international releases, including English subtitled and dubbed versions. It inspired merchandise, a sequel series titled *Great Dangaioh* (2001), and appearances in video games like *Super Robot Wars*.

The Jackson Plan, also known as the Raffles Town Plan, was a city plan for Singapore drawn up in 1822–1823 by Lieutenant Philip Jackson under the instructions of Sir Stamford Raffles. It was created to address the haphazard development of the colony under William Farquhar's administration and to organize the town into functional and ethnic subdivisions. The plan divided Singapore into distinct zones, including a European Town, a Chinese Kampong (now Chinatown), an Indian area (Chuliah Kampong), and Kampong Glam for Malays, Arabs, and Bugis. While the ethnic divisions were not strictly enforced, they influenced the city's development and remain evident in modern Singapore.

The plan also designated areas for government and commercial use, with the latter evolving into today's financial hub at Raffles Place. Raffles personally oversaw land reclamation and infrastructure improvements, such as leveling hills and embanking the Singapore River. Additionally, Raffles proposed architectural guidelines, including fire-resistant masonry buildings with uniform facades and sheltered walkways, which influenced the region's distinctive "five-foot ways." Although not fully implemented at the time, the Jackson Plan laid the foundation for Singapore's urban development and the present-day Downtown Core.

Ida S. Baker High School is a public high school located in Cape Coral, Florida. The school was founded in 2004, and is one of six high schools located in Cape Coral. For the 2011–12 school year it was rated as an "A+" school in the State of Florida.

Dutchman is a play written by African-American playwright Amiri Baraka, then known as LeRoi Jones. Dutchman was first presented at the Cherry Lane Theatre in Greenwich Village, New York City, in March 1964. The play won an Obie Award; it shared this distinction with Adrienne Kennedy's Funnyhouse of a Negro. Baraka's stage play was made into a film in 1967, starring Shirley Knight and Al Freeman Jr. Dutchman was the last play produced by Baraka under his birth name, LeRoi Jones. At the time, he was in the process of divorcing his Jewish wife, Hettie Jones, and embracing Black Nationalism. Dutchman may be described as a political allegory depicting black and white relations during the time Baraka wrote it.

The play was revived in 2007 at the Cherry Lane Theatre starring Dulé Hill, and in 2013 was restaged by Rashid Johnson at the Russian and Turkish Baths in the East Village.
Ya'akov Ekhoiz (born May 29, 1957) is an Israeli former footballer and coach.

Rupa Goswami (c. 1489–1564 CE) was a prominent theologian, scholar, and saint in the Gaudiya Vaishnavism tradition. Born into a Telugu-speaking Brahmin family, he served as a government official before becoming a disciple of Chaitanya Mahaprabhu. Rupa Goswami was instrumental in systematizing Gaudiya Vaishnava theology, rediscovering holy sites in Vrindavana, and composing influential devotional texts. He is regarded as the foremost follower of Chaitanya Mahaprabhu and the leader of the Rupanuga tradition. His samadhi is located at the Radha-Damodara temple in Vrindavana.

Alba Vergés i Bosch (born 3 September 1978) is a Catalan economist and politician who is current serving as the First Vice President of the Parliament of Catalonia since 2021 and as Acting President of the Parliament of Catalonia since 2022.

Marco Pinotti (born 25 February 1976 in Osio Sotto, Lombardy) is an Italian former road racing cyclist, who competed as a professional between 1999 and 2013. An individual time trial specialist, Pinotti was a six-time Italian Time Trial Champion (2005, 2007, 2008, 2009, 2010 and 2013).

Argentina was one of the seventeen nations that competed at the inaugural Summer Paralympic Games in 1960 held in Rome, Italy from September 19 to 24, 1968. Preparations for the Games began two years prior in 1958 to stage what was at the time called the 9th Annual International Stoke Mandeville Games. The team finished tenth in the medal table with a total of six medals, two gold, three silver and one bronze. The Argentinian team consisted of five athletes, one man and four women.

*Alnus glutinosa*, commonly known as the black alder or common alder, is a deciduous tree in the birch family (Betulaceae) native to much of Europe, parts of Asia, and North Africa. It thrives in moist soils near rivers, lakes, and wetlands but can also grow in drier locations. The tree typically reaches heights of 20–30 meters (66–98 feet) and is characterized by its sticky young leaves and shoots, rounded serrated leaves, and distinctive catkins. It is monoecious, with wind-pollinated male and female flowers, and its seeds are adapted for water dispersal.

The species plays a significant ecological role due to its symbiotic relationship with the nitrogen-fixing bacterium *Frankia alni*, which enhances soil fertility. It is also a pioneer species in ecological succession and supports diverse wildlife, including insects, birds, and fungi. However, it is susceptible to diseases such as phytophthora disease of alder.
