*Alnus glutinosa* has been widely introduced outside its native range, where it can become invasive. Its wood is valued for its durability underwater, making it historically significant for construction, including the foundations of Venice. The tree is also used for erosion control, dyeing, tanning, and traditional medicine. Despite its utility, its pollen is a common allergen in Europe. The species is classified as "Least Concern" by the IUCN.

The John Wescott Three-Decker is a historic triple-decker house located at 454 Pleasant Street in Worcester, Massachusetts. Built around 1892 in the Queen Anne style, it features distinctive architectural details, including alternating clapboard and shingle siding and decorative brackets. It was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1990.

Shade is a 2003 American neo-noir crime thriller film directed and written by Damian Nieman and starring Stuart Townsend, Gabriel Byrne, Thandiwe Newton, Jamie Foxx, Melanie Griffith and Sylvester Stallone. The film follows a trio of grifters who attempt to set up a legendary card shark nicknamed "The Dean". The film had a limited release in the United States on May 7, 2004.
Barrington J. Bayley (9 April 1937 – 14 October 2008) was an English science fiction writer.

The 2009 FIA GT3 European Championship was a sports car racing series featuring new entries from Audi with the R8 LMS and Alpina with the B6, while Ferrari introduced the 430 Scuderia GT3. The season saw structural changes, limiting teams to two cars each, with a maximum of three teams per manufacturer. Manufacturers like Ascari, Dodge, Jaguar, Lamborghini, and Morgan were ineligible for the Manufacturers Cup due to not meeting the minimum car supply requirement. Points were awarded to the top eight finishers, with partial points for cars completing at least 75% of the race distance. The Manufacturer Cups were open to manufacturers supplying at least two teams for the full season.

Joseph Hamilton (August 28, 1938 – August 3, 2021) was an American professional wrestler, promoter, and trainer. He was best known as one half of the tag team The Assassins where he was called "Assassin #1". When the Assassins ended, He wrestled as "The Assassin" and "The Flame" both while wearing a mask.

Hamilton teamed up with Tom Renesto in late 1961 to form the masked heel (bad guy) team known as "The Assassins" and, for over a decade, he and Renesto wrestled all over the world with great success. Once Renesto retired, Hamilton kept the Assassins team alive by partnering with wrestlers such as Randy Colley and "Hercules" Hernandez under the trademark gold and black masks of the Assassins.

In the late 1980s, Hamilton founded Deep South Wrestling (DSW), where he also wrestled until a back injury forced him to retire in 1988. After Deep South Wrestling closed, he worked extensively for World Championship Wrestling both on the booking team and later on as a trainer at WCW's Power Plant training facility. In 2005, he reopened Deep South Wrestling, this time as an affiliate of World Wrestling Entertainment (WWE), to train wrestlers the WWE had signed to developmental contracts. In 2007, WWE abruptly ended their contract with DSW and the promotion suspended operations later in the year.

Gareth James Delany (born 28 April 1997) is an Irish cricketer who plays as an all-rounder, batting right-handed and bowling right-arm leg break. He made his international debut for Ireland in 2019 in Twenty20 Internationals (T20Is) and in 2020 in One Day Internationals (ODIs). Domestically, he has played for Leinster Lightning, Leicestershire, and Munster Reds.

*In the Night Room* is a 2004 horror novel by American author Peter Straub. The story follows Timothy Underhill, a writer grappling with the loss of his sister and haunted by emails from deceased acquaintances, and Willy Patrick, an award-winning author struggling with visions of her deceased daughter. Their lives intertwine as they confront unsettling mysteries and supernatural forces. The novel explores themes of trauma, narrative reality, and the boundaries of fiction, employing metafictional techniques. *In the Night Room* received mostly positive reviews, with praise for its emotional depth and narrative complexity, though some critics noted its reliance on genre conventions.

Nnamdi Moweta (born March 27, 1958), is a radio personality, music producer, music supervisor, music promoter and consultant. He is the host of Radio Afrodicia, a radio show on KPFK, a listener-sponsored radio station based in Los Angeles, California. With an audience of approximately 50,000 listeners weekly, Moweta is the only African-born radio host in Los Angeles, California. In 2011, Moweta was featured on Reuters Africa Journal segment about African DJs in the United States.

Moweta is recognized by UCLA's ethnomusicology program as an expert in the field of African and African-influenced music—he has served as a panelist for UCLA's annual ethnomusicology conferences which attempt to educate the public about global arts and cultures.
Cipher runes are cryptographic variations of runic alphabets, primarily preserved in Icelandic manuscripts from the 17th–18th centuries.

Ruby Romaine is a fictional character portrayed by Tracey Ullman on her show Tracey Takes On. The character became so popular that HBO greenlit a pilot for a potential Ruby Romaine spin-off series resulting in the one-off television special, Tracey Ullman in the Trailer Tales in 2003. Ruby is a self-proclaimed "star maker".
Mario Adrián Vázquez (born June 15, 1977) is an American singer from The Bronx, New York City. He competed on American Idol's fourth season, which aired on the FOX network in early 2005.

Vicente Greco (February 3, 1888 – October 5, 1924) was an Argentinian composer, conductor, and bandoneon player of tango music. He had a significant role in the spread of tango music from the suburbs into the cities, where it became very popular.

DXWT (92.3 FM), branded as 92.3 Wild FM, is a radio station in Davao City, Philippines, owned by the UM Broadcasting Network. Established in 1949 as DXMC on AM, it transitioned to FM in 1988 and adopted its current call sign. Known for its dance-leaning Contemporary Hit Radio format, it pioneered innovative programming and promotions, becoming a leading station in Mindanao.

The Coaldale Big Green was an early professional football team based in Coaldale, Pennsylvania. The club played as an independent until joining the Anthracite League in 1924. After leaving the league in 1924, the team spent its 1925 season as an independent, then joined the short-lived Eastern League of Professional Football in 1926. While the most well known Anthracite League team is the Pottsville Maroons, which jumped to the National Football League in 1925 and immediately established itself as a championship contender, the most consistently successful club in the coal region was the Big Green. Coaldale won the coal region's Curran Cup in 1921, 1922 and 1923.

Mathew Street is a street in Liverpool, England, notable as the location of the new Cavern Club, the Beatles having played in the original club on numerous occasions in their early career.

Mathew Street is visited by thousands of tourists a year, who visit the Cavern Club and many surrounding attractions including a statue of John Lennon, a Beatles store, the Liverpool Beatles Museum and several pubs formerly frequented by the band. A wall in Mathew Street is adorned by a sculpture by Arthur Dooley entitled "Four Lads Who Shook the World".
Mike Albert is an American guitarist. He has collaborated with various musicians like Frank Zappa, Don Felder, Ruben Guevara, and L. Shankar, and toured with bands like Megadeth and El Chicano.
Dottle refers to the unburned tobacco residue left in a pipe after smoking. It can result from improper packing, excessive moisture, or rapid smoking.

William Scott Husel is an American intensive/critical care physician who was charged (and later acquitted) with 14 counts of first degree murder relating to the deaths of multiple patients from his care of terminally ill patients at Mount Carmel West and St. Ann's Hospitals in Columbus, Ohio. The prosecution in this case argued that Dr. Husel hastened the death of terminally ill patients. He turned himself in on June 5, 2019. After an extensive trial, Husel was found not guilty on all counts on April 20, 2022.

Blaže Koneski (born Blagoje Ljamević; 19 December 1921 – 7 December 1993) was a Macedonian writer, translator, linguistic scholar, and key figure in the codification of the standard Macedonian language. A professor and rector at Ss. Cyril and Methodius University of Skopje, he contributed significantly to Macedonian literature and linguistics. Koneski authored influential works on Macedonian grammar and history, translated major literary works, and received numerous international awards. Despite his achievements, his role in standardizing Macedonian has been criticized for alleged Serbianization.

The Camp Beauregard Memorial, outside Water Valley, Kentucky on Kentucky state road 2422 northeast of town, marks the site of a Confederate States Army encampment named for General P. G. T. Beauregard. The camp was situated to protect the right flank of the Confederate encampment at Columbus, Kentucky.
Tyrone Kirk (born 2 January 1984) is an English former midfielder who played for clubs including Stamford, Macclesfield Town, and Rhyl.

Associated Equipment Company (AEC) was a British vehicle manufacturer that built buses, motorcoaches and trucks from 1912 until 1979. The name Associated Equipment Company was hardly ever used; instead it traded under the AEC and ACLO brands.

During World War One, AEC was the most prolific British lorry manufacturer; after building London's buses before the great war.

*Svalikhita-jivani* is the autobiography of Bhaktivinoda Thakur, written in 1896 at the request of his son, Lalita Prasad. Covering the first 56 years of his life, the work candidly recounts Bhaktivinoda's struggles with finances, health, doubts, and introspection, which ultimately led him to embrace the teachings of Caitanya Mahaprabhu. Despite his status as a prominent Gaudiya Vaisnava leader, Bhaktivinoda presented himself with humility, avoiding claims of spiritual powers or saintliness. The autobiography offers an honest and modest portrayal of his life and spiritual journey. It was posthumously published by Lalita Prasad in 1916, following Bhaktivinoda's death.

Sir John Wedderburn, 5th Baronet of Blackness, was a Perthshire gentleman who joined the 1745 rebellion of Charles Edward Stuart. He was captured at the Battle of Culloden, taken to London, and convicted of treason. He was hanged, his estates were forfeit to the Crown, and his family was attainted. The Blackness in his title is Blackness House in Dundee rather than Blackness in Lothian just west of Edinburgh. His son John Wedderburn of Ballendean fled after his father's death to Jamaica, where he re-established the family's fortunes via slave sugar, and eventually regained his father's title.

Eblana (Lang-el) is an ancient Irish settlement that appears in the Geographia of Claudius Ptolemaeus (Ptolemy), the Greek astronomer and cartographer, around the year 140 AD. It was traditionally believed by scholars to refer to the same site as the modern city of Dublin. The 19th-century writer Louis Agassiz used Eblana as a Latin equivalent for Dublin. However, more recent scholarship favours the north County Dublin seaside village of Loughshinny due to its proximity to Drumanagh, an important trading site with strong links to Roman Britain; it has even been described as a bridgehead of a possible Roman invasion. However, there is no definitive proof to tie Eblana with any location, so its exact identity is still a matter of speculation.

Heinz Rein (9 April 1906 – 16 January 1991) was a German writer and journalist, best known for his novel *Berlin Finale*, a seminal work of rubble literature depicting the end of World War II and postwar Berlin. Initially active in East Germany, he later moved to West Germany.

Lala Hsu (born December 20, 1984) is a Taiwanese singer-songwriter known for her distinctive lyrical style and contributions to Mandopop. She rose to fame after winning the third season of the singing competition *One Million Star* in 2008, where her performance of "Riding a White Horse" earned her widespread acclaim. Hsu released her debut album in 2009, which garnered six nominations at the 21st Golden Melody Awards, including Best New Artist, and won her the Best New Artist award in 2010. Over her career, she has released six studio albums, with her fifth album, *The Inner Me* (2017), earning her Best Mandarin Album and Best Mandarin Female Singer at the 29th Golden Melody Awards.

Hsu's music blends narrative lyricism with elements of Taiwanese opera and innovative chord progressions. She has also performed theme songs for films and television and participated in notable music programs, including *I Am a Singer*. Married to director Bill Chia since 2018, Hsu continues to influence the Mandopop scene with her unique artistry and storytelling.
Yuda (湯田町, Yuda-machi) was a town located in Waga District, Iwate Prefecture, Japan.

"Lottery" (Lang-hi, Lang-ur) is a Hindustani short story. It was written by Indian author Premchand. The story is told in narrative form from the perspective of an unnamed school teacher. The story has been adapted into play format, with theatrical performances presented by Lalit Parimoo's Natsamaj Theatre Group, as well as Mujeeb Khan's Ideal Drama and Entertainment Academy.

Design Museum Den Bosch, established in 1972 and located in 's-Hertogenbosch, Netherlands, focuses on contemporary design, ceramic art, and jewelry. Originally known as Stedelijk Museum 's-Hertogenbosch, it moved to its current building, designed by Hubert-Jan Henket, in 2013. Renamed in 2018, the museum explores the societal impact of design and gained attention for its 2019–2020 exhibition "Design of the Third Reich." It houses a significant jewelry collection and archives of notable designers.

Kimbolton is a historic town and civil parish in Cambridgeshire, England, with a population of 1,477 as of the 2011 Census, including the hamlet of Stonely. Known for its medieval origins, the town features Kimbolton Castle, once the residence and prison of Catherine of Aragon, now part of Kimbolton School. The town hosts the annual Statute Fair, a tradition dating back to 1200. Other notable features include the Grade I listed St Andrew's Church, remnants of a World War II airfield, and Kimbolton Fireworks.

Ottery St Mary is a historic town and civil parish in East Devon, England, situated on the River Otter. With a population of 4,898 as of 2011, the town is known for its rich history, notable architecture, and unique traditions. Its name derives from the river and its association with St Mary, dating back to its mention in the Domesday Book of 1086. The parish church of St Mary, often compared to a "miniature Exeter Cathedral," features a painted ceiling, medieval misericords, and the Ottery St Mary Astronomical Clock, one of the oldest mechanical clocks in England.

The town is renowned for its annual Tar Barrels event, a centuries-old tradition where flaming barrels are carried through the streets, and Pixie Day, which celebrates a local legend. Ottery is also the birthplace of poet Samuel Taylor Coleridge. Other landmarks include The King's School, founded in 1335, and The Chanter's House, a historic residence tied to the Coleridge family. The town remains a vibrant cultural and historical hub in Devon.

Methionine aminopeptidase 2 is an enzyme that in humans is encoded by the METAP2 gene.

Methionine aminopeptidase 2, a member of the dimetallohydrolase family, is a cytosolic metalloenzyme that catalyzes the hydrolytic removal of N-terminal methionine residues from nascent proteins.

MetAP2 is found in all organisms and is especially important because of its critical role in tissue repair and protein degradation. Furthermore, MetAP2 is of particular interest because the enzyme plays a key role in angiogenesis, the growth of new blood vessels, which is necessary for the progression of diseases including solid tumor cancers and rheumatoid arthritis. MetAP2 is also the target of two groups of anti-angiogenic natural products, ovalicin and fumagillin, and their analogs such as beloranib.

A Zirkel (German for "circle", as in "circle of friends") is a monogram used in European student societies in countries such as Germany, Austria, Switzerland, Belgium, Hungary, Latvia, Estonia (Studentenverbindungen).

James Raymond Jordan Sr. (July 31, 1936 – July 23, 1993) was the father of former basketball player Michael Jordan, and was the grandfather of former basketball players Jeffrey Jordan and Marcus Jordan.

Sir Frederick Wollaston Mann KCMG (2 May 1869 – 29 May 1958) was the chief justice of the Australian state of Victoria between 1 October 1935 and 31 January 1944. He was also Victoria’s lieutenant governor between 12 May 1936 and May 1945. Mann was nicknamed the “Little Gentleman” because of his height (he was 168 cm tall) and he was unfailingly courteous. He had a reputation of being a careful judge delivering decisions of precision and clarity. In 1935, he became the first Australian born person to become chief justice of Victoria.

Dick Anderson (born July 29, 1941) is a former American football player and coach. He played tight end and defensive end at Penn State from 1960 to 1962 and later served as the head coach at Rutgers University from 1984 to 1989, compiling a record of 28–33–4. Anderson spent much of his coaching career at Penn State, where he was an assistant coach under Joe Paterno for over two decades, mentoring numerous All-Americans and future NFL players.

Hualing Nieh Engle (born January 11, 1925) is a Chinese-born writer, editor, and educator, best known as the co-founder of the International Writing Program (IWP) at the University of Iowa. A prolific author of over two dozen works, including the acclaimed novel *Mulberry and Peach*, she has played a significant role in fostering cross-cultural literary exchange. After relocating to Taiwan following the Chinese Communist Revolution, she became a prominent literary editor and educator before moving to the United States in 1964. Alongside her husband, Paul Engle, she developed the IWP into a globally recognized residency for writers. Her contributions to literature and international collaboration have earned her numerous accolades, including an American Book Award and a Nobel Peace Prize nomination.
The 1965–66 Football League First Division was won by Liverpool.

A diatreme, sometimes known as a maar-diatreme volcano, is a volcanic pipe formed by a gaseous explosion. When magma rises up through a crack in Earth's crust and makes contact with a shallow body of groundwater, rapid expansion of heated water vapor and volcanic gases can cause a series of explosions. A relatively shallow crater (known as a maar) is left, and a rock-filled fracture (the actual diatreme) in the crust. Diatremes breach the surface and produce a steep, inverted cone shape.

The term diatreme has been applied more generally to any concave body of broken rock formed by explosive or hydrostatic forces, whether or not it is related to volcanism. The word comes from Ancient Greek δία- (dia-) 'through, across, over' and τρῆμα (trêma) 'hole, aperture'.

Yana Peel (born June 1974) is a Canadian businesswoman, philanthropist, and arts advocate, currently serving as the Global Head of Arts and Culture at Chanel. Born in Leningrad (now St. Petersburg), Russia, she emigrated to Canada in 1978 and later studied at McGill University and the London School of Economics. Peel began her career at Goldman Sachs before co-founding the Outset Contemporary Art Fund in 2003 and Intelligence Squared Asia in 2009. She served as CEO of the Serpentine Galleries from 2016 to 2019, where she advanced digital initiatives and global architectural projects. Peel is also active in philanthropy, supporting contemporary art, design, and child welfare initiatives.

Owain Sebastian Yeoman is a Welsh actor. His credits include The Nine, Kitchen Confidential, AMC's Turn (as Benedict Arnold) and the HBO series Generation Kill. Additionally, he portrayed CBI Agent Wayne Rigsby in The Mentalist and portrayed Benny Gallagher in Emergence.

Colt Lyerla (born November 13, 1992) is a former American football tight end who played college football at the University of Oregon. Known for his athleticism, he was a standout high school player in Oregon and earned national attention before committing to Oregon, where he played from 2011 to 2013. Lyerla went undrafted in the 2014 NFL Draft but briefly signed with the Green Bay Packers and later played in the Arena Football League. His career was overshadowed by legal troubles, including arrests for drug possession and other offenses.
Q.S. Serafijn (born in Roosendaal en Nispen) is a Dutch artist and educator known for his interactive sculptures and public art projects.

*I, Olga Hepnarová* is a 2016 Czech biographical drama film directed by Tomáš Weinreb and Petr Kazda. Starring Michalina Olszańska, the film explores the life and crimes of Olga Hepnarová, a woman who committed a mass murder in 1973. Praised for its stark cinematography and haunting narrative, it received mixed to positive reviews, with a 78% approval rating on Rotten Tomatoes.
Sergio Ferrer (born January 29, 1951) is a former Puerto Rican professional baseball shortstop who played in Major League Baseball (MLB) for the Minnesota Twins and New York Mets from 1974 to 1979.

Medelpad (IPA-sv or IPA-sv) is a historical province or landskap in the north of Sweden. It borders Hälsingland, Härjedalen, Jämtland, Ångermanland and the Gulf of Bothnia.

The province is a part of Norrland and as such considered to be Northern Sweden, although the province geographically is located in the middle of Sweden. It is a common misconception that the name Medelpad ("middle land" or "middle ground") reflects this, but the name actually refers to the fact that most of the province lies between its two rivers Ljungan and Indalsälven.

Aussie Gold Hunters is an Australian factual television show which follows crews of gold prospectors in Australia. The series is produced by Electric Pictures and began on the Discovery Channel on 15 September 2016. The series has been a ratings success and gone on to air in 125 countries including the United Kingdom.

In August 2022 the series was renewed for a eighth and ninth season, with season eight to premiere in 2023. Season seven concluded on 13 October 2022.

2011 MD is a small near-Earth asteroid, approximately 6 meters (20 feet) in diameter, discovered on June 22, 2011, by the Lincoln Near-Earth Asteroid Research (LINEAR) program. Initially mistaken for space debris, it was later confirmed as an asteroid. During its close approach to Earth on June 27, 2011, it passed within 0.1 lunar distances, altering its orbital period. Observations revealed it to be a porous rubble pile with a density similar to water. The asteroid posed no threat to Earth, as it would have burned up in the atmosphere if it entered. In 2014, NASA identified 2011 MD as a potential candidate for the Asteroid Redirect Mission.
Leucocyanidin is a colorless chemical compound that is a member of the class of natural products known as leucoanthocyanidins.

Patricia "Patt" Murphy Derian (née Murphy; August 12, 1929 – May 20, 2016) was an American civil rights and human rights activist who fought racism in Mississippi and went on to serve as Assistant Secretary of State for Human Rights and Humanitarian Affairs from 1977 to 1981. She was, remembered The Times of London, "a courageous champion of civil rights who took on some of the world's most brutal dictators in her role as a senior American diplomat."
Alimi Ballard (born October 17, 1977) is an American television actor. He is best known for his role as FBI agent David Sinclair on the CBS police procedural Numb3rs (2005–2010).

VirusTotal is an online service that analyzes files and URLs for viruses, worms, trojans, and other types of malicious content. Launched in June 2004 by Hispasec Sistemas, the platform allows users to upload files or submit URLs to be scanned by multiple antivirus engines and tools. It supports a wide range of languages and is headquartered in Dublin, Ireland. VirusTotal was acquired by Google in 2012 and later became part of Google's cybersecurity subsidiary, Chronicle, in 2018.

The service offers various tools, including browser extensions, a mobile app for Android, and uploaders for Windows, macOS, and Linux, enabling users to scan files and URLs directly from their devices. It also provides a public API for automating tasks such as file uploads, URL scans, and report retrieval, though usage restrictions apply. VirusTotal retains uploaded files and shares them with anti-malware companies to enhance threat detection. While its Windows Uploader was discontinued in 2017, the platform remains active and widely used for cybersecurity purposes.

Robert Sithole (c. 1944 – 2005) was a South African pennywhistle musician renowned for his contributions to the kwela and mbaqanga styles. Born in District Six, Cape Town, he was displaced by apartheid-era policies and later lived in exile in the United Kingdom. Sithole performed with groups like the Kwela Kids and the Beaters and was celebrated for his virtuosity, though he struggled financially later in life. He served as musician-in-residence at the District Six Museum before his health declined. His legacy includes recordings and a depiction in Vladimir Tretchikoff's painting *Penny Whistlers*.

Cipriano Piccolpasso (1524–1579) was an Italian Renaissance polymath, known for his contributions as a surveyor, civil and military engineer, draughtsman, poet, and writer. He was a member of the literary Accademia degli Eccentrici in Perugia and co-founded the Accademia del Disegno in 1573, one of the earliest academies for Italian artists. Piccolpasso is best known for his treatise *Li tre libri dell'arte del vasaio* (The Three Books of the Potter’s Art), a detailed manual on the production of majolica pottery, written at the request of Cardinal François de Tournon, possibly to aid French faience production. 

He also authored *Le piante ed I ritratti delle Città e Terre dell'Umbria*, an illustrated topography of Umbria commissioned by Pope Pius IV, who later knighted him. A manuscript of *Li tre libri*, featuring Piccolpasso's drawings of pottery techniques and decorative motifs, is housed in the Victoria and Albert Museum. Acquired in 1861, it was first published in 1857 and has since been reissued multiple times. Piccolpasso was buried in the church of San Francesco in Castel Durante, leaving a legacy as a key figure in the documentation of Renaissance art and craftsmanship.

Chester Creek is a tributary of the Delaware River in southeastern Pennsylvania, formed by its East and West Branches. Historically significant, it hosted early paper mills and Swedish settlements, and it remains ecologically healthy, supporting native species like brook trout and freshwater eels.

"You're a Big Girl Now" is a song by Bob Dylan, released on his 15th studio album, Blood on the Tracks, in 1975. It is one of five songs on the album that Dylan initially recorded in New York City in September 1974 and then re-recorded in Minneapolis in December that year. The latter recording, made on December 27, 1974, became the album track.

*The Complete Beat* is a box set by the British ska and new wave band The English Beat, released on July 10, 2012, by Shout! Factory. Spanning recordings from 1979 to 1982, the collection includes the band’s three studio albums, extended mixes, dub versions, live recordings, and a 24-page booklet by Alex Ogg detailing the band’s history. While no previously unreleased songs are included, many rarities make their CD debut in this set.

The box set received critical acclaim, earning a score of 90/100 on Metacritic based on eight reviews. Critics praised its comprehensive nature and the quality of the live recordings, which showcase the band’s energetic performances and political themes. AllMusic and Rolling Stone both rated it 4.5/5, with reviewers highlighting the visceral force of the recordings and the band’s enduring impact. Paste magazine also lauded the live tracks, calling them the most compelling aspect of the collection.

The Taylorcraft Auster was a British military light observation aircraft developed during World War II from the American Taylorcraft Model A. Initially adapted for civilian use as the Taylorcraft Plus C, it was later modified for Air Observation Post (AOP) roles, directing artillery fire for the British Army. Various models, including the Auster III, IV, and V, were widely used by the Royal Air Force, Royal Canadian Air Force, and other Allied forces in Europe, North Africa, and the Pacific. Postwar, the Auster evolved into civilian and military variants, with its roles eventually replaced by helicopters.

Hans Klumbach (26 April 1904 – 14 December 1992) was a German archaeologist and expert on Roman helmets. He served as director of the Romano-Germanic Central Museum in Mainz and was an honorary professor at Johannes Gutenberg University Mainz. Known for his work on late Roman ridge helmets, his 1973 publication *Spätrömische Gardehelme* established a typology for these artifacts.
Arnold V de Looz, (died August 22, 1327) was Count of Loon from 1279 to 1323 and Count of Chiny (Arnulf III) from 1299 to 1310. He was the son of John I, Count of Looz and Mathilde Jülich.

Henry Nicholas John Gunther (June 6, 1895 – November 11, 1918) was an American soldier and the last known combat death of World War I. Born in Baltimore, Maryland, to a German-American family, he was drafted in 1917 and served in the 313th Infantry Regiment. Demoted from sergeant to private, Gunther sought to redeem himself and was killed charging a German machine gun position one minute before the Armistice took effect. Posthumously, he was restored to the rank of sergeant and awarded the Distinguished Service Cross. Memorials honor him in both France and the United States.

"Winter" is a song by the Irish rock band U2, written for the 2009 war film *Brothers* directed by Jim Sheridan. Composed by the band with lyrics by Bono, the song underwent several iterations, evolving from a rock arrangement to a stripped-down version inspired by The Edge's original piano composition. The lyrics, influenced by Tobey Maguire's character in the film, explore themes of hidden emotions and the experiences of soldiers in Afghanistan. Although initially intended for U2's album *No Line on the Horizon*, it was ultimately excluded but featured in the film's closing credits. "Winter" was nominated for a Golden Globe Award for Best Original Song in 2010.

Vesna Škare-Ožbolt (IPA-sh; born 20 June 1961) is a Croatian politician. She was the 10th Minister of Justice of Croatia from 2003 until 2006. She was the second female holder of that office, as well as the second consecutive woman after Ingrid Antičević-Marinović.
Diether Ocampo (born July 19, 1974) is a Filipino actor, singer, model, and Philippine Coast Guard Auxiliary officer.
Linda Gottlieb is an American producer, professor, and Emmy nominee.

The divisions of Punjab, Pakistan, are the first-level administrative units of the province, currently numbering 10 as of 2022. Originating during the Mughal and British eras, divisions were restructured post-independence, with their number gradually increasing. Each division is overseen by a commissioner and further subdivided into districts, tehsils, and union councils.

The original Citroën Rosalie was a light-weight racing car that established a succession of records at the Montlhéry racing circuit. More generally the Rosalie was a range of three models/sizes of automobile that comprised the core of Citroën's model range between 1932 and 1938. The three models were originally designated respectively the 8CV, the 10CV and the 15CV, the numbers defining the cars' fiscal horsepower which in turn defined the approximate engine size of each model. After the introduction of the new Traction Avant, the lineup was modified and at the 1934 Paris Salon the two smaller models became the 7UA and the 11UA, now with the overhead-valve engines from the Traction. Starting with the 1933 model year Citroen offered the 1,766 cc 11UD diesel engine option.

The Moldavian Autonomous Soviet Socialist Republic (MASSR) was an autonomous republic within the Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic, established on October 12, 1924, and dissolved on August 2, 1940. It was created as part of Soviet efforts to promote Moldavian identity and assert territorial claims over Bessarabia, then part of Romania. The MASSR's capital was initially Balta, later moved to Tiraspol in 1929. Although the republic was intended to represent the Moldavian population, Moldavians constituted less than one-third of its population, with significant Ukrainian and Russian communities.

The MASSR served as a political and propaganda tool to promote Soviet influence in Bessarabia and Romania. Policies included the promotion of a distinct Moldavian identity, the use of Cyrillic script for the Moldavian language, and industrialization efforts. The republic faced challenges such as famines, anti-Soviet uprisings, and forced collectivization. It was dissolved in 1940 when the Moldavian Soviet Socialist Republic was established, incorporating parts of Bessarabia and the MASSR.

Yankee Dutch crossing is the name of a dance performed in a 4-by-4 square formation of 8 couples (16 dancers). The original version, called simply Dutch Crossing, was choreographed in the English country dance style by Ernst van Brakel of the Netherlands in 1990. In 2004, contra dance caller Joseph Pimentel modified the choreography in the American style, sometimes called Yankee Dutch crossing to distinguish it from the original. Any number of squares can perform the dance simultaneously. It is not normally found at monthly, bi-monthly or weekly dances. The most likely venues are multi-day dance weekends or weeks, where it is taught in sessions of one hour or longer.
The Museum of Russian Impressionism is an art museum in Moscow dedicated to Russian art at the turn of the twentieth century.

Mapoutahi was the site of a historic massacre in southern New Zealand, involving a conflict between Kāti Māmoe chiefs Taoka and Te Wera. The dispute began when Taoka, based near present-day Timaru, visited Te Wera at his pā (fortified village) at Huriawa, near the Waikouaiti River. During a visit to another relative, Kapo, an argument escalated, leading to Te Wera killing Taoka's son. In retaliation, Taoka raised a war party and besieged Huriawa for a year without success. 

Taoka then turned his attention to Te Wera's ally, Pakihaukea, at Mapoutahi Pā. Exploiting a lapse in security, Taoka's forces attacked at night, breaching the pā's defenses and massacring approximately 250 inhabitants. The aftermath of the slaughter was so devastating that the nearby settlement of Pūrākaunui was named in reference to the sight of bodies piled in a heap outside the pā. This event remains a significant episode in the history of intertribal warfare in the region.

Barasat Junction railway station is the primary rail hub in the city of Barasat It is one of the major suburban city railway stations in the district of North 24 Parganas. Generally, the people commuting daily use this station for travelling to Kolkata's Central Business District or other towns, with the busiest times at the station being the morning and evening peak periods. This station provides the facility of separate rest rooms for both male & female passengers. Barasat Junction Railway station consists of 6 permanent platforms (Platform no: 1–6).

Recently, the government-owned Indian Railways corporation approved a proposal for an underground metro railway station connecting Barasat Junction to Noapara metro station. The construction of the project has started in October 2011.

An EMU carshed is situated in the vicinity of Barasat Junction railway station.

The Mourne Mountains (Irish: na Beanna Boirche) are a granite mountain range in County Down, Northern Ireland, known for their natural beauty and cultural significance. The range's highest peak is Slieve Donard, standing at 850 meters (2,790 feet). The Mournes are renowned for their hiking trails, rock climbing, and scenic views, attracting visitors for outdoor activities and Duke of Edinburgh's Award expeditions. Notable features include the Mourne Wall, a 31.4 km dry stone wall built to protect Belfast's water supply, and reservoirs like Silent Valley and Ben Crom. The mountains have inspired literature, music, and film, including C.S. Lewis's *The Chronicles of Narnia* and HBO's *Game of Thrones*.

*Millennium Fantasy* is a composition for solo piano and orchestra by American composer Ellen Taaffe Zwilich. Written in two movements with a performance duration of approximately 20 minutes, the piece is based on the folk song "Come All You Fair and Tender Ladies," a melody Zwilich's grandmother sang to her in childhood. The work premiered to critical acclaim, with reviewers praising its vibrant dialogue between piano and orchestra and its broad appeal. A recording featuring pianist Jeffrey Biegel and the Florida State University Symphony Orchestra was released by Naxos Records in 2010.

Olwen Williams OBE is a Consultant Physician in Genitourinary/HIV Medicine based at Betsi Cadwaladr University Health Board, North Wales. She was appointed an OBE for services to medicine in Wales in 2005. and Welsh Woman of the Year in 2000.

Alba Vergés i Bosch (born 3 September 1978) is a Catalan economist and politician, currently serving as the acting President and First Vice President of the Parliament of Catalonia. A member of the Republican Left of Catalonia (ERC), she was previously the Minister of Health from 2018 to 2021, overseeing the region's healthcare system during significant political and social challenges. Vergés has also held various roles in the Parliament of Catalonia, including Fourth Secretary and Member of Parliament for the Province of Barcelona. Before entering politics, she worked in economics and administration, particularly in elder care management.

As of the 2010 U.S. Census there were 11,813 ethnic Koreans in Harris County, Texas, in the Houston area, making up 4.2% of the county's Asian population. In 2015 Haejin E. Koh, author of "Korean Americans in Houston: Building Bridges across Cultures and Generations," wrote in regards to the census figure that "community leaders believe the number is twice as large."
FC Ingolstadt 04 II is the reserve team of FC Ingolstadt 04, founded in 2004. The team competes in the Bayernliga Süd and has previously played in the Regionalliga Bayern.

Elizabeth Parr-Johnston CM (born 1939) is an American-born Canadian economist, academic, and former university administrator. She served as the 16th president of the University of New Brunswick (1996–2002) and the 8th president of Mount Saint Vincent University (1991–1996). Parr-Johnston has held prominent roles in academia, the private sector, and the Canadian federal government, including her involvement in the Iran hostage crisis. She was appointed to the Order of Canada in 2008 for her contributions to education. Parr-Johnston resides in Nova Scotia and continues to be recognized for her leadership and public service.

Andrew Lackfi (Lang-hu; c. 1310 – October 1359) was an influential nobleman and a successful military leader in the Kingdom of Hungary. He was Count of the Székelys between 1343 and 1350, Ban of Macsó from 1355 to 1356, and Voivode of Transylvania between 1356 and 1359.

Nagorik Natya Sampradaya is a Bangladeshi theatre company. Established in 1968, it was the first theatre group in Dhaka and started performing plays on stage in 1972. The chairperson of the group is the actor and director Aly Zaker and his wife, actress Sara Zaker, is the vice-chairperson. As of December 2018, Nagorik had performed 46 different productions.

Amyntaio (Lang-el, before 1928: Σόροβιτς - Sorovits; Bulgarian/Macedonian: Суровичево, Сорович), is a town and municipality in the Florina regional unit of Macedonia, Greece. The population of Amyntaio proper is 4,306, while that of the entire municipality is 16,973 (2011). The town is named after the ancient king of Macedon and grandfather of Alexander the Great, Amyntas III.

*Collar × Malice* is a visual novel developed by Idea Factory under their Otomate brand and published in Japan in 2016 for the PlayStation Vita, with subsequent releases on the Nintendo Switch. The game follows Ichika Hoshino, a rookie police officer in Shinjuku, Tokyo, who becomes entangled in the "X-Day Incident," a series of murders orchestrated by the terrorist group Adonis, after a poison-filled collar is placed around her neck. The game received praise for its suspenseful narrative and character development. A fan disc, *Collar × Malice: Unlimited*, and an anime film adaptation were later released.

*Fiction* is an EP by Canadian rock band Suuns, released on October 30, 2020, through Secret City and Joyful Noise. Featuring avant-rock, noise rock, and experimental elements, the six-track project explores new sonic directions, blending curation and creation. The EP includes singles "Pray," "Breathe" (featuring Jerusalem In My Heart), and the title track "Fiction." Critics praised its experimental approach and atmospheric sound, highlighting its innovative departure from the band's earlier work.
Ruth Cameron was a singer, producer, and Charlie Haden's manager.

Carman George Blough (1895–1979) was an American accountant, educator, and the first Chief Accountant of the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission. He held academic positions at institutions including the University of North Dakota and Columbia University and served as research director at the American Institute of Certified Public Accountants.

Pinecone Burke Provincial Park is a provincial park in British Columbia, Canada. It extends from the southwest corner of Garibaldi Provincial Park, west of Pitt Lake and Pitt River to include Burke Mountain in the City of Coquitlam. Most of the park is unserviced wilderness with very rough trails such as the Fools Gold Trail in the Boise Valley. There is canoe access to Widgeon Slough and from there hiking trails that lead to Widgeon Lake, and a network of old logging roads, hiking and mountain biking trails on Burke Mountain. The parks boundaries were established in 1995 by the provincial government and covers 38,000 hectares. Parts of the parks boundaries are located in the Katzie First Nation, Kwikwetlem First Nation, and Stó:lō First Nations territories. The parks diverse ecosystems supports several different species on land and in its inlet. There are 6 species that have been labeled as sensitive or vulnerable that live in the park. Before the parks protected status the area was logged and mined. It also was used for recreational purposes such as hiking and rock climbing. Visitors can use the park for hiking, camping, back country skiing and snowshoeing.

John Hull Campbell (October 10, 1800 – January 19, 1868) was an American politician from Pennsylvania who served as an American Party member in the U.S. House of Representatives for Pennsylvania's 3rd congressional district from 1845 to 1847.

The Gonzaga Bulldogs (/ɡənˈzæɡə/) (also known unofficially as the Zags) are the intercollegiate athletic teams representing Gonzaga University, located in Spokane, Washington, United States. Gonzaga competes in the National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA) Division I as a member of the West Coast Conference.

The 2022 Torneo Federal A was the 10th season of the third tier of Argentine football under its current format. The competition featured 34 teams divided into two zones, with each team playing home and away matches within their group. The top eight teams from each zone advanced to the final stages, where Racing (Córdoba) emerged as champions, securing promotion to the Primera Nacional. The tournament saw 559 matches played and 1,151 goals scored, with an average of 2.06 goals per match. Four teams—Atlético Paraná, Deportivo Camioneros, Desamparados, and Juventud Unida (G)—were relegated to the Torneo Regional Federal Amateur. The season concluded on November 13, 2022.

Come is an American alternative rock and blues rock band formed in Boston, Massachusetts, in 1990. Known for their dark, emotionally intense sound and the interplay between Thalia Zedek's distressed vocals and the guitar work of Zedek and Chris Brokaw, the band released four critically acclaimed albums, including their debut *11:11* (1992). They toured with notable acts like Sonic Youth and Nirvana and received praise from artists such as Kurt Cobain and Bob Mould. After disbanding in 2001, Come reunited for several performances, including a 2013 tour celebrating the 20th anniversary of *11:11*.

Uriah F. Abshier (September 17, 1849 – May 23, 1934) was an American politician, businessman, and farmer. He served as a Republican member of the Oregon House of Representatives from 1885 to 1886, representing Lake and Klamath counties. Abshier was also active in local politics, serving as Lake County commissioner, assessor, and postmaster. Later, he became a successful hardware store owner in Washington and a fruit farmer in California.

The Pacific geoduck ("gooey-duck"; /ˈɡuːiˌdʌk/; Panopea generosa) is a species of very large saltwater clam in the family Hiatellidae. The common name is derived from the Lushootseed (Nisqually) word gwídəq.

The geoduck is native to the coastal waters of the eastern North Pacific Ocean from Alaska to Baja California. The shell of the clam ranges from 15 centimetres (6 in) to over 20 centimetres (8 in) in length, but the extremely long siphons make the clam itself much longer than this: the "neck" or siphons alone can be 1 metre (3 ft 3 in) in length. The geoduck is the largest burrowing clam in the world. It is also one of the longest-living animals of any type, with a typical lifespan of 140 years; the oldest has been recorded at 179 years old. The precise longevity of geoducks can be determined from annual rings deposited in the shell which can be assigned to calendar years of formation through crossdating. These annual rings also serve as an archive of past marine variability.

Samira Ćosović is a Serbian language and literature teacher and politician. She has been a member of the Prijepolje municipal assembly since 2008 and served as vice-president of the Sandžak Democratic Party's municipal board. Ćosović was a member of the National Assembly of Serbia from 2020 to 2022 and was appointed deputy mayor of Prijepolje in December 2021.

Aadmi (transl. Man) is a 1993 Indian Hindi-language action film directed by Arshad Khan, starring Mithun Chakraborty, Gautami, Harish and Ajit Khan. The film features a score and soundtrack composed by Jatin–Lalit, while Anwar Sagar wrote the lyrics.

Bustard Island is Alberta's largest sand island, It is located in Lake Athabasca in the part of the Fidler-Greywillow Wildland Park. The Lucas Islands lie northeast from the island, the other major island in the park; Burntwood Island lays 11.8 kilometres (7.3 mi) to the north.
Jean-Philippe Mateta (born 28 June 1997) is a French professional footballer who plays as a striker for Premier League club Crystal Palace. He previously played for Lyon, Le Havre, and Mainz 05.

Front Royal, Virginia, is a town in Warren County, founded in 1788 and located at the confluence of the North and South Forks of the Shenandoah River. Known as the "Canoe Capital of Virginia," it has a rich history tied to the Shenandoah Valley, including its role in the Civil War and its industrial past, notably the American Viscose rayon plant. The town features historic sites, such as the Confederate Museum and Warren Heritage Society buildings, and is home to institutions like Randolph-Macon Academy and Christendom College. Front Royal also hosts the Virginia Inland Port and the Smithsonian Conservation Biology Institute.

The **Antasena-class combat boat** is an Indonesian catamaran-type combat vessel developed by North Sea Boats and first unveiled in 2016. Designed for amphibious operations in shallow waters, it features advanced composite materials, a Cockerill 3030 turret, and optional integrated drones. The boat can carry up to 60 troops and is powered by twin MTU diesel engines, achieving speeds of up to 50 knots. The first prototype underwent sea trials in 2021.

"Maximum Carnage" is a 1993 Marvel Comics crossover storyline spanning 14 issues across multiple Spider-Man titles. Written by Tom DeFalco, J.M. DeMatteis, Terry Kavanagh, and David Michelinie, it follows Spider-Man and allies as they battle Carnage and his villainous gang during a violent rampage through New York City.

Qin Shengxiang (born February 1957) is a retired Chinese admiral and politician who served as Political Commissar of the People's Liberation Army Navy from 2017 to 2022. He previously held key roles, including Director of the General Office of the Central Military Commission from 2012 to 2017.

An ultrasonic motor is a type of electric motor that uses ultrasonic vibrations in a stator to generate motion in a rotor, typically through dry friction. These motors operate by modulating frictional forces at the stator-rotor interface, enabling motion beyond a single vibration cycle. Two primary vibration methods are used: traveling-wave and standing-wave. Early designs in the 1970s utilized standing-wave vibration, while later advancements favored traveling-wave vibration for improved efficiency and reduced wear. High-torque hybrid transducer motors combine axial and torsional vibrations for enhanced performance.

Ultrasonic motors are capable of operating at high frequencies, with vibration displacements ranging from micrometers to nanometers, depending on the frequency. Most motors are contact-based, though non-contact versions using working fluids like air have been developed.

The technology has been widely adopted in consumer electronics, particularly in autofocus systems for photographic lenses, with Canon pioneering its use in the late 1980s.

The Zhdanov Doctrine (also called Zhdanovism or Zhdanovshchina; Lang-ru) was a Soviet cultural doctrine developed by Central Committee secretary Andrei Zhdanov in 1946. It proposed that the world was divided into two camps: the "imperialistic", headed by the United States; and "democratic", headed by the Soviet Union. The main principle of the Zhdanov Doctrine was often summarized by the phrase "The only conflict that is possible in Soviet culture is the conflict between good and best". Zhdanovism soon became a Soviet cultural policy, meaning that Soviet artists, writers and intelligentsia in general had to conform to the party line in their creative works. Under this policy, artists who failed to comply with the government's wishes risked persecution. The policy remained in effect until the death of Joseph Stalin in 1953.

*Dutchman* is a one-act play written by Amiri Baraka, first performed in March 1964 at the Cherry Lane Theatre in New York City. The play explores themes of race, identity, and power dynamics through an intense and symbolic interaction between two characters: Lula, a white woman, and Clay, a young Black man, aboard a New York City subway. Their flirtatious conversation escalates into a confrontation, with Lula manipulating and mocking Clay before ultimately murdering him. The play ends with Lula targeting another Black man, suggesting a cyclical pattern of violence and oppression.

The title *Dutchman* alludes to the transatlantic slave trade and the myth of the Flying Dutchman, symbolizing the inescapable cycles of racial history. The subway car serves as a metaphor for this unending journey. The play is a powerful critique of racism and assimilation during the Civil Rights Movement, blending realism with allegory.

Stewardson Township is a township in Potter County, Pennsylvania, United States. The population was 64 at the 2020 census. Ole Bull State Park, a Pennsylvania state park, is located in Stewardson Township.
John Leslie Sissons (born 30 September 1945) is an English former footballer who played for West Ham United, Sheffield Wednesday, Norwich City and Chelsea.

Mercury Meltdown is a puzzle-platform game for the PlayStation Portable. It is the sequel to Archer Maclean's Mercury. Like the first game, the goal is to tilt the stage in order to navigate one or more blobs of mercury to the destination. In contrast to the original, Ignition Banbury had more time and experience developing the game and listened to player feedback, allowing the game to be easier and provide players with more freedom to choose levels. The game has new hazards, enemies, and minigames.

The game received a port to the PlayStation 2 titled Mercury Meltdown Remix released a month after the original, with improved graphics, new levels and optimized for PlayStation 2's controller. A second port for the Wii titled Mercury Meltdown Revolution was released in 2007 and also changing the levels, improving the graphics further from the Remix version and making use of Wii's motion controls.

All versions of Mercury Meltdown were well-received among critics. The game was praised by critics for being an overall improvement from the original in terms of difficulty and art style. Mercury Meltdown Remix received mixed reviews in regards to the PS2 controls with some criticism of removing multiplayer. Mercury Meltdown Revolution also criticized for lack of multiplayer but was praised for its motion controls.

Ward No. 8 of the Kolkata Municipal Corporation covers the neighborhoods of Bagbazar and Shobhabazar in Kolkata, West Bengal, India. It is part of Borough No. 1 and falls under the Shyampukur Assembly constituency and Kolkata Uttar parliamentary constituency. As of the 2011 Census, the ward had a population of 18,760 with a literacy rate of 93.57%. It is bordered by the Hooghly River and several streets, and is served by Shyampukur police station. The ward forms a municipal corporation council electoral constituency.

Andrew Lackfi (c. 1310–1350s) was a Hungarian nobleman and military leader from the Hermán kindred. The son of Lack, Count of the Székelys, Andrew succeeded his father in 1343 and held key administrative roles in Transylvania. He led a successful military campaign against the Tatars in 1345.
Lars Eric Mattsson is a guitarist, songwriter and producer living in Åland, Finland.

"Love Song" is the fourteenth single by Japanese rock band Luna Sea, released on November 8, 2000. It turned out to be the band's last single, as they held a press conference on the day of its release and announced they would be disbanding. They later reunited in 2010, and released "The One -Crash to Create-" in 2012. "Love Song" reached number 4 on the Oricon Singles Chart, and charted for five weeks.

The Wenceslas Bible (Lang-de) or the Bible of Wenceslaus IV (Lang-cs) is a multi-volume illuminated biblical manuscript written in the German language. The manuscript was commissioned by the King Wenceslaus IV of Bohemia (that time also the King of the Romans) and made in Prague in the 1390s. The Wenceslas Bible is unique and very precious not only because of its text, which is one of the earliest German translations of the Bible, but also because of its splendid illuminations. This oldest German deluxe Bible manuscript remained uncompleted.

"Tight Connection to My Heart (Has Anybody Seen My Love)" is a song by American singer-songwriter Bob Dylan, released in 1985 as the opening track on his album *Empire Burlesque* and as a single. Originally developed as "Someone's Got a Hold of My Heart" during sessions for his 1983 album *Infidels*, the song underwent significant lyrical changes, incorporating references to classic Hollywood films. The single achieved modest chart success and received critical praise for its engaging melody and production. A music video directed by Paul Schrader was filmed in Tokyo. The song has been covered and featured in various performances, including the musical *Girl from the North Country*.
John Nestor (1913–1999) was an American physician, FDA whistleblower, and advocate for strict adherence to speed limits while driving.

Tõnis Kõiv (born 1970) is an Estonian lawyer, politician, and member of the Estonian Reform Party. He served in the Riigikogu from 2005 to 2015, held leadership roles in parliamentary committees, and was secretary general of the Reform Party from 2017 to 2018. Kõiv has also been active in local government, serving as mayor of Paide (1996–2004) and holding leadership positions in various organizations, including the Association of Estonian Cities and the Estonian delegation to the IPU.

*The Time* is the second studio album by Chinese singer and actor Xu Weizhou, released in four parts from 2017 to 2018 by Baina Entertainment. The album features a mix of C-pop, rock, and EDM genres and includes singles such as "Leave Me Alone," "So What," and "Orange Sky." It achieved significant commercial success, reaching double platinum status shortly after release and topping charts on QQ Music. Xu actively participated in the album's production, including songwriting and designing the cover. The album received positive critical reception for its creativity and Xu's musical growth.

Tracy Reiner (born Tracy Henry, July 7, 1964) is an American actress known for her roles in films such as *A League of Their Own* (1992) and *Apollo 13* (1995). The daughter of actress Penny Marshall and adoptive daughter of filmmaker Rob Reiner, she has appeared in nearly 30 films and worked as an associate producer. Reiner also led efforts to fund and market medical software in 2013. She is married and has five children.

Janie Dee (born 20 June 1962) is an English actress and singer. She won the Olivier Award for Best Actress, Evening Standard Award and Critics'Circle Theatre Award for Best Actress in a Play, and in New York the Obie and Theatre World Award for Best Newcomer, for her performance as Jacie Triplethree in Alan Ayckbourn's Comic Potential.

She also won the Olivier Award for Best Supporting Performance in a Musical for her performance as Carrie Pipperidge in Nicholas Hytner's acclaimed production of Rodgers & Hammerstein's Carousel at the National Theatre.

In 2013, Dee won the TMA Theatre Award UK for Best Performance in a Musical for her performance as Dolly Levi in Hello Dolly at Curve, Leicester.

Jasmina Obradović (Serbian Cyrillic: Јасмина Обрадовић; born 29 January 1961) is a politician in Serbia. She has served in the National Assembly of Serbia since 2012 as a member of the Serbian Progressive Party.

HMS Fairy was a three-funnel, 30-knot destroyer of the First World War. One of three similar ships built by Fairfields for the Royal Navy, she was ordered under the 1896–1897 Naval Estimates and the sixth Royal Navy ship to carry this name. She was classified, along with other similar ships, as a C-class destroyer in 1913. She sank in 1918 from damage inflicted by ramming and sinking the German submarine UC-75.

Georgia Douglas Johnson (September 10, 1880 – May 15, 1966) was an American poet, playwright, and key figure of the Harlem Renaissance. One of the first African-American female playwrights, she wrote over 200 poems, 28 plays, and 31 short stories, often addressing themes of race, gender, and social justice. Her works, including *The Heart of a Woman* (1918) and *Bronze* (1922), gained national recognition, and she was a prominent anti-lynching activist. Johnson hosted the influential S Street Salon in Washington, D.C., fostering creativity among African-American artists and intellectuals.

Shaarey Zedek Cemetery is a Conservative Jewish burial ground in the North End of Winnipeg, Manitoba, Canada. Operated by the Shaarey Zedek Synagogue, it is the largest Jewish cemetery in the Canadian Prairies, with more than 8,000 graves as of 1996. In 2012, a Jewish interfaith burial ground was installed in a fenced-off section with a separate entrance to accommodate interment of Jews alongside their non-Jewish spouses. The cemetery features a war memorial honoring Winnipeg residents who fell in World War I and World War II.

Hyper Combat Unit Dangaioh (破邪大星ダンガイオー, Haja Taisei Dangaiō) is a Japanese anime OVA series produced and animated by AIC and Artmic and released in Japan in 1987. Dangaioh featured character designs by creator Toshiki Hirano, mechanical designs by Shoji Kawamori, and animation direction by Masami Ōbari.

Frederick Rodney "Rod" Holt (born 1934) is an American engineer best known for developing the switching power supply for the Apple II, which contributed to its lightweight design. Initially skeptical of Apple, Holt joined the company as its fifth employee after being recruited by Steve Jobs during the computer's early development. He served as Chief Engineer and Vice President of Engineering, contributing to innovations like the Apple II's floppy disk system and addressing radio interference issues. Holt received four patents for his work at Apple before departing under new management. Known for his political activism and passion for motorcycles, Holt's portrayal in the 2013 film *Jobs* was met with amusement by those who knew him.

Victor Lenard Shapiro (16 October 1924, Chicago – 1 March 2013, Riverside, California) was an American mathematician, specializing in trigonometric series and differential equations. He is known for his two theorems (published in 1957) on the uniqueness of multiple Fourier series.

Franciszek Piłsudski (1713-1791) Piłsudski coat of arms, was a Lithuanian nobleman, Colonel and Commander in the Samogitian division of the Grand Ducal Lithuanian army, Deputy cup-bearer, member of the Sejm of the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth and recipient of the Order of Saint Stanislaus (1779) and the Great Cross of Divine Providence.

Infanta Beatriz of Spain, Princess of Civitella-Cesi (Beatriz Isabel Federica Alfonsa Eugénie Cristina Maria Teresia Bienvenida Ladislàa de Borbón y Battenberg; 22 June 1909 – 22 November 2002) was a daughter of King Alfonso XIII of Spain and Victoria Eugenie of Battenberg, wife of Alessandro Torlonia, 5th Prince di Civitella-Cesi. She was a paternal aunt of King Juan Carlos I.

Jatavarman Sundara I, also known as Sadayavarman Sundara Pandyan, was a emperor of the Pandyan dynasty who ruled regions of Tamilakkam (present day South India) between 1250–1268 CE. He is remembered for his patronage of the arts and tamil architecture, along with refurbishment and decoration of many Kovils (temple) in the Tamil continent. He oversaw a massive economic growth of the Pandyan empire. On the eve of his death in 1268 CE, the second Pandyan empire's power and territorial extent had risen to its zenith.
Barysaw (Lang-be, IPA-be) or Borisov (Lang-ru, IPA-ru) is a city in Belarus near the Berezina River in the Minsk Region 74 km north-east from Minsk. Its population is around 145,000.

NFL Blitz is a series of American football themed video games originally released by Midway featuring National Football League (NFL) teams. It began as a 1997 arcade game NFL Blitz that was ported to home consoles and spawned a series of sequels. Rather than being designed as a realistic interpretation of the sport of football, like Madden NFL or NFL 2K, the Blitz series was created as an over-the-top, exaggerated version of the sport, inspired by Midway's own NBA Jam basketball games.

In 2005, after losing the NFL license, Midway relaunched the Blitz series as Blitz: The League, depicting fictional players and teams in a fictional league with slightly more realistic (though still exaggerated) on-field play and a focus on the seedy behind-the-scenes lives of the players.

Following the dissolution of Midway, EA Sports acquired the rights to the Blitz name and relaunched the series on PlayStation Network and Xbox Live with a new entry in the series simply titled NFL Blitz. The game took the series back to the original style of Blitz, but removed the late hits due to input by the NFL.

KushCo Holdings, formerly known as Kush Bottles, is a publicly traded company (OTCQB: KSHB) founded in 2010 by Nicholas Kovacevich and Dallas Imbimbo in Santa Ana, California. Specializing in cannabis-related packaging and ancillary products, the company has expanded through acquisitions, including Dank Bottles, CMP Wellness, and Summit Innovations. KushCo played a key role in advocating for child-resistant cannabis packaging regulations and launched an eCommerce platform in 2015. By 2017, it had sold over 100 million products and served 4,000 cannabis clients. The company continues to grow, entering the Cannabidiol (CBD) market and expanding its distribution network.
The Secours Populaire Français (SPF), or French Popular Relief, is a French non-profit organization founded in 1945, dedicated to fighting poverty and discrimination in public life.

Terranova is a German band based between Berlin and Paris made up of current members Fetisch and &me. Terranova are signed to Kompakt records. Terranova was formed in 1996 by Fetisch, Marco Meister and Kaos. They previously went under the moniker Turntable Terranova on the Compost label and sometimes as Edition Terranova. Their music is a fusion of punk rock, electronic, hip hop and House. They currently reside between Paris and Berlin and perform DJ sets worldwide. They have been constant collaborators with artists in the past, Cath Coffey, Nicolette Krebitz, Tricky, Ari Up, Mike Ladd, Khan, Snax, Billie Ray Martin e.a.
Crystina Poncher is an American sports broadcaster and journalist, known as the only female play-by-play boxing broadcaster with Top Rank. She has also worked with the NFL and Fox Sports.

Molding sand, primarily used in metal casting, is a mixture of sand, bentonite clay, pulverized coal, and water, with sand as the main component. Green sand, the most common type, is used in its wet state for creating molds. This method, dating back to ancient Mesopotamia around 3200 B.C., remains one of the simplest and most cost-effective casting techniques. Modern advancements in sand quality and metallurgy have significantly improved the casting process, supporting large-scale production of various metal castings worldwide.
Jasaw Chan Kʼawiil I also known as Ruler A, Ah Cacao and Sky Rain, (before 682-734), was an ajaw of the Maya city of Tikal. He took the throne on May 3, 682 and reigned until his death.
Martyrs Bus Service is a bus operator based in Lilydale, Victoria, serving eastern Melbourne. Established in 1927, it operates one route with a fleet of 42 buses.

Michael Trend CBE (born 19 April 1952) is a British journalist and former Conservative Party politician who served as Member of Parliament (MP) for Windsor and Maidenhead from 1992 to 1997, and Windsor from 1997 to 2005. He held various party roles, including Deputy Chairman and Chief Executive, and was involved in democracy-building efforts internationally. In 2003, he was found guilty of improperly claiming parliamentary expenses, leading to his suspension and decision not to seek re-election. Trend is also a writer on music and a former church organist.

Belopolskya (minor planet designation: 1004 Belopolskya), provisional designation 1923 OS, is a dark Cybele asteroid from the outermost region of the asteroid belt, approximately 75 kilometers (47 mi) in diameter. It was named for Russian astrophysicist Aristarkh Belopolsky.

Multiple complex developmental disorder (MCDD) is a proposed neurodevelopmental condition characterized by a combination of psychotic, affective, behavioral, autistic, and neurological symptoms. It is not officially recognized in the DSM-V or ICD-10, and its diagnostic criteria remain unclear and debated. MCDD may serve as a diagnosis for individuals whose symptoms do not align with other specific disorders, though it has been criticized as vague. The condition is believed to have genetic causes, with affected individuals often displaying unique symptom profiles and familial links to neuropsychological disorders.

Oksana Kostina (15 April 1972 – 11 February 1993) was a Russian rhythmic gymnast and 1992 World All-around champion. She won five gold medals at the 1992 World Championships, including all apparatus titles. Despite her success, Kostina was overshadowed by Ukrainian gymnasts earlier in her career and was not selected for the 1992 Olympics. She aimed to compete at the 1996 Olympics but tragically died in a car crash in 1993 at the age of 20.

Binghamstown (Irish: An Geata Mór) is a townland and village in County Mayo, Ireland, located on the Mullet Peninsula near Saleen Harbour. Established in 1796 by Major Denis Bingham as a "landlord village" for the Bingham estate, it was originally named Ballymacshedon before being renamed. The village features historical connections to the Bingham family, including the now-ruined Binghamstown House, and is associated with the Irish name meaning "the Big Gate."

Marko Čakarević (born May 13, 1988) is a Serbian professional basketball player, currently playing 3x3 basketball for Partizan Belgrade. A 2.00 m (6 ft 7 in) small forward, he has played for numerous teams across Europe, including ASVEL, Partizan, and Igokea, and has won multiple league and cup titles. Čakarević was named Serbian Super League MVP in 2017 and has represented Serbia internationally, winning gold at the 2007 FIBA Under-19 World Championship and the 2008 FIBA Europe Under-20 Championship.
Enrico Cruciani Alibrandi (died 1921) was an Italian engineer, politician, and mayor of Rome (1905–1907), later serving as a senator.
Carola Richards (1916–2004) was an English artist and member of the Eastbourne Group.

Lord Forbes was launched at Chester in 1803 as a West Indiaman. She soon became an "armed defense ship", but by 1805 had returned to being a West Indiaman. She made two voyages as an "extra" ship for the British East India Company (EIC). She continued trading with India until 1817 when she sustained damage on her way to Bengal. There she was surveyed, condemned and sold.

*Our Revolution: A Future to Believe In* is a 2016 book by U.S. Senator Bernie Sanders, detailing his 2016 presidential campaign and progressive political agenda. The memoir addresses issues such as climate change, income inequality, free education, and healthcare reform, while recounting his campaign's challenges and successes. The book became a bestseller and received critical acclaim, including a Grammy nomination for its audiobook.

Man Mountain Dean, born Frank Simmons Leavitt (June 30, 1891 – May 29, 1953), was an American professional wrestler, actor, and soldier. Known for his imposing size and signature beard, he had a successful wrestling career spanning over 6,700 matches and appeared in several films. Dean also served in both World Wars, retiring as a master sergeant, and later studied journalism at the University of Georgia. He died of a heart attack in Norcross, Georgia, in 1953.

The Hong Kong Film Award for Best Action Choreography is presented annually by the Hong Kong Film Awards to honor excellence in action choreography. Established in 1983, it recognizes the integral role of action scenes in Hong Kong cinema.

The Wilson's snipe (*Gallinago delicata*) is a small, well-camouflaged wading bird in the family Scolopacidae, found across North America and parts of Russia. Measuring 23–28 cm (9.1–11.0 in) in length, it has a long, straight bill, mottled brown plumage, and pointed wings. It breeds in wetlands, tundra, and meadows in northern regions and migrates to the southern United States, the Caribbean, and South America, though some populations are year-round residents on the U.S. Pacific coast. Known for its unique "winnowing" courtship display, the species forages in soft mud for insects, earthworms, and plant material. Despite past population declines, it is currently listed as Least Concern by the IUCN.

Edith Guerrier (1870–1958) was a pioneer in the field of library science. Guerrier is best known for developing progressive library programs in the 1890s, including a reading program and a pottery studio for girls of Boston's North End, an urban immigrant center during the Progressive Era.

Callington was a UK parliamentary borough constituency in Cornwall, represented in the House of Commons from 1585 until its abolition by the Great Reform Act in 1832. It was a rotten borough with a small electorate, heavily influenced by the Rolle and Coryton families, who controlled its parliamentary representation. The borough's voting rights were clarified in 1821, significantly increasing the electorate, but it remained a pocket borough until its disfranchisement.
On November 16, 2015 the beta version of the European Data Portal was launched. The European Data Portal is an initiative of the European Commission, and is part of the Digital Single Market.
Komsomolets Ukrainy was the lead ship of Kashin-class destroyer of the Soviet Navy.
Reliance was an American automobile manufacturer founded in 1904 in Detroit, Michigan, producing cars and trucks before its 1909 acquisition by General Motors.

Jessie Joe Parker (born 22 August 1985) is a Papua New Guinean rugby league player and coach. He has represented Papua New Guinea in multiple international tournaments, including the Rugby League World Cups and the Four Nations. Parker played for clubs such as the Northern Pride, Featherstone Rovers, and Whitehaven, where he reached 100 appearances in 2016. He became player-coach of Hensingham ARLFC in 2022 and remains the club's top scorer.

Annie Brisset is a Canadian scholar and expert in translation studies, semiotics, and intercultural communication. She has held prominent academic and administrative roles, published extensively, and received numerous accolades, including election to the Royal Society of Canada.

*Mirage: Arcane Warfare* is a multiplayer action game developed and published by Torn Banner Studios, released for Windows on May 23, 2017. Built on Unreal Engine 4, the game featured melee-focused combat with magical abilities and various game modes. Despite receiving mixed reviews for its gameplay and design, it struggled commercially due to low player activity. Torn Banner supported the game with updates but ultimately removed it from sale and shut down its servers in May 2018.

*Give It Up* is the second studio album by American singer-songwriter Bonnie Raitt, released in September 1972 by Warner Bros. Records. Recorded at Bearsville Studios in New York and produced by Michael Cuscuna, the album blends folk, blues, R&B, and soft rock, showcasing a mix of sentimental ballads and energetic folk rock tracks. Featuring guest musicians such as John Hall, Freebo, and Paul Butterfield, *Give It Up* is noted for its polished sound compared to Raitt's debut album. The album includes three original songs by Raitt and covers by artists like Chris Smither and Jackson Browne, with lyrics exploring themes of femininity, relationships, and self-assurance.

*Give It Up* received critical acclaim for Raitt's confident vocal performance and musical versatility. It peaked at number 138 on the *Billboard* Top LPs & Tapes chart and was certified gold in 1985. Retrospective reviews have praised its influence, with *Rolling Stone* ranking it among the 500 Greatest Albums of All Time in 2012.

Rüdiger Sagel (born 1955) is a German politician and activist known for his involvement in environmental and social causes. Initially a member of the Green Party, he served in the Landtag of North Rhine-Westphalia from 1998 to 2007, focusing on budgetary and financial issues. After leaving the Greens in 2007, he joined the Left Party, where he continued his political career until 2012. Sagel has been active in anti-nuclear protests, surface mining opposition, and Latin American cultural projects.

The Vladimir Lenin monument in Azov, located in Rostov Oblast, Russia, was unveiled on April 30, 1978, to commemorate Lenin's 108th birthday. Designed by sculptor Yuri Orekhov and architects Vladimir Peterburzhtsev and Alexander Stepanov, the 10.8-meter-tall monument features a half-figure of Lenin carved from Azerbaijani granite, an uncommon design for Lenin monuments. The surrounding square was renovated during its construction under the direction of architect Vladimir Fomenko.

Code R is an American action-adventure television series that aired on CBS from January 21 to June 10, 1977. Code R focuses on the emergency services (police, fire, and ocean rescue) of the California Channel Islands. The series starred James Houghton, Martin Kove and Tom Simcox and ran for a single season of thirteen episodes.

Faerie Tale Theatre (also known as Shelley Duvall's Faerie Tale Theatre) is an American live-action fairytale fantasy drama anthology television series of 27 episodes, that originally aired on Showtime from September 11, 1982 until November 14, 1987. It is a retelling of 26 fairy tales, particularly those written by The Brothers Grimm, Charles Perrault and Hans Christian Andersen, an additional episode is based on the poem "The Pied Piper of Hamelin".

The 27th episode was a reunion episode of cast and crew titled "Grimm Party", in which in Fairytale style, celebrating the series they attended a gala in fancy dress.

The series follow a similar style to an earlier series narrated by Shirley Temple, titled Shirley Temple's Storybook, that ran from 1958 and 1961 and also featured numerous Hollywood stars in roles.

The Canajoharie Creek (/ˌkænədʒəˈhɛəri/) is a river that flows into the Mohawk River in the Village of Canajoharie in the U.S. State of New York. The name "Canajoharie" is a Mohawk language term meaning "the pot that washes itself", referring to the "Canajoharie Boiling Pot", a 20-foot (6.1 m) wide and 10-foot (3.0 m) deep pothole in the Canajoharie Creek, just south of the village of Canajoharie. Bowmans Creek is one main tributary that enters the creek east of the Hamlet of Sprout Brook. The other main tributary is Brimstone Creek which enters the creek north-northwest of the Village of Ames.

The Petaluma and Santa Rosa Railroad (P&SR) was an interurban electric railroad that operated in Sonoma County, California, from 1904 to 1932. Formed through the consolidation of several horse car lines in 1903, it connected Petaluma, Santa Rosa, Sebastopol, and Forestville, with ferry service to San Francisco. The railroad faced significant challenges, including the "Battle of Sebastopol Road," a conflict with a competing steam railroad over track crossings. Despite early expansion and success, including freight and passenger services, the P&SR declined due to competition and economic shifts. It was acquired by the Northwestern Pacific Railroad in 1932, with operations ceasing by the mid-20th century.

The 1988 San Francisco Giants season was the franchise's 106th season and their 31st in San Francisco. The team played their home games at Candlestick Park and competed in the National League's Western Division. Managed by Roger Craig and owned by Bob Lurie, the Giants' games were broadcast on KTVU and KNBR, with additional coverage on GiantsVision and KIQI.
