Drexel-Alvernon was a census-designated place (CDP) in Pima County, Arizona, United States. The population was 4,192 at the 2000 census.

George Ege (March 9, 1748 – December 14, 1829) was an American ironmaster, politician, and Federalist who served in the U.S. House of Representatives from Pennsylvania's 5th district (1796–1797) and in the Pennsylvania House of Representatives (1783). Born in Germantown, Pennsylvania, he became a prominent ironmaster, owning the Charming Forge and other properties, and was one of Berks County's wealthiest citizens. Ege also served as an associate judge in Berks County from 1791 to 1818.

The Secret of Roan Inish is a 1994 independent fantasy-adventure film written and directed by John Sayles. It is based on the 1957 novel Secret of the Ron Mor Skerry, by Rosalie K. Fry.

It is centered on the Irish and Orcadian folklores of selkies—seals that can shed their skins to become human. The story, set on the west coast of Ulster in the north-west of Ireland, is about Fiona, a young girl who is sent to live with her grandparents and her cousin Eamon near the island of Roan Inish, where the selkies are rumored to reside. It is a family legend that her younger brother was swept away in his infancy and raised by a selkie. Part of the film takes place in Donegal Town.

Fredrik Backman (born 2 June 1981) is a Swedish author, blogger, and columnist. He wrote A Man Called Ove (2012), Things My Son Needs to Know about the World (2012), My Grandmother Asked Me to Tell You She's Sorry (2013), Britt-Marie Was Here (2014), Beartown (2017), Us Against You (2018), Anxious People (2020), and The Winners (2022). The books were number one bestsellers in his home country of Sweden. Backman's books have been published in more than twenty-five languages.

The *Anne* and *Little James* were two ships that arrived at Plymouth Colony in 1623, bringing new settlers and supplies. The *Anne*, a 140-ton supply ship captained by William Peirce, carried the majority of the passengers and arrived on July 10, 1623. The *Little James*, a 44-ton pinnace captained by Emmanuel Altham, arrived weeks later and was intended to remain in New England for trade and fishing. However, the *Little James* faced numerous challenges, including a mutinous crew, inadequate trade goods, a shipwreck, and eventual capture by Barbary pirates. Despite efforts to salvage and repair the ship, it failed to fulfill its intended purpose, contributing to the financial struggles of the colony. The combined passenger list of the two ships included approximately 60 settlers, though exact records remain unclear.
Fitchville Township is one of the nineteen townships of Huron County, Ohio, United States. Established in 1828, it is named after Colonel Fitch, a Connecticut landowner.

Barasat Junction railway station is a major railway station in Barasat, North 24 Parganas, West Bengal, India. Operated by Eastern Railway, it serves as a key junction on the Kolkata Suburban Railway network, connecting the Sealdah–Bangaon line and the Barasat–Hasnabad and Ranaghat–Bangaon branch lines. Opened in 1906 and electrified in 1972, the station has five platforms and six tracks but lacks parking facilities. 

The station features a reservation counter, smart card ticket booths, and a railway car shed for maintenance. Historically, it was part of the Barasat Basirhat Railway, a narrow-gauge line established in 1914 and later converted to broad gauge. Barasat is also part of the proposed Noapara–Barasat metro extension, which aims to improve connectivity but has faced delays due to land encroachment and contractor withdrawal.

Joanna Mytkowska (born 1970) is a Polish curator, art critic, and museum director. She studied at the University of Warsaw and has curated notable exhibitions, including at the Venice Biennale and Centre Georges Pompidou. In 2018, she received the Igor Zabel Award.

The Necedah Shrine is a religious site in Necedah, Wisconsin, associated with reported Marian apparitions experienced by Mary Ann Van Hoof between 1949 and 1950. Van Hoof claimed to receive messages from the Virgin Mary, which included warnings of global chastisement and support for the Tridentine Mass. Despite attracting thousands of pilgrims, the Roman Catholic Church declared the apparitions false in 1955, and in 1975, Van Hoof and her followers were placed under interdict, leading to a schism with the Church. The shrine now operates independently, affiliated with a conservative Old Catholic organization, and includes a school, visitor center, and ongoing construction of a "House of Prayer."

Jimmy McCulloch (4 June 1953 – 27 September 1979) was a Scottish musician and songwriter, best known as the lead guitarist for Paul McCartney's band Wings from 1974 to 1977. A prodigious talent, McCulloch began playing guitar at age 11 and gained early recognition with the psychedelic rock band One in a Million and later with Thunderclap Newman, whose 1969 hit "Something in the Air" topped the UK charts. He also played with Stone the Crows, John Mayall's Bluesbreakers, and the reformed Small Faces, among others.

During his time with Wings, McCulloch contributed notable tracks such as "Medicine Jar" and "Wino Junko." He later formed the band White Line and collaborated with various artists. Known for his melodic, blues-infused guitar style, McCulloch tragically died of heart failure due to morphine and alcohol poisoning at age 26. His legacy includes contributions to rock and blues music across multiple bands and projects.

The First Congregational Church and Parsonage is a historic church complex at 23 Pepperrell Road (Maine State Route 103) in the Kittery Point section of Kittery, Maine. Built in 1730 for a congregation first organized in 1653, the church is the oldest in Kittery, and one of the oldest in the state of Maine. It is accompanied by a parsonage house, built in 1729, and a small cemetery, established in 1733. The buildings were listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1978; the cemetery was added to the listing in 1997.

*Bitter Tears: Ballads of the American Indian* is a 1964 concept album by American country singer Johnny Cash, addressing the struggles and injustices faced by Native Americans. Inspired by Native American activism and his belief in having Cherokee ancestry (later disproven), Cash collaborated with songwriter Peter La Farge, who contributed five of the album's eight tracks. The album includes "The Ballad of Ira Hayes," a poignant story of a Pima Marine who raised the flag at Iwo Jima but faced poverty and alcoholism upon returning home. Other songs, such as "As Long as the Grass Shall Grow," highlight the loss of Seneca land due to the Kinzua Dam, while "Custer" critiques the glorification of General George Custer.

Despite initial resistance from radio stations and fans, Cash's advocacy helped the album reach No. 2 on the Billboard Country Albums chart, with "The Ballad of Ira Hayes" peaking at No. 3. The album was later reissued and inspired a 2014 tribute album and a 2016 PBS documentary, *We're Still Here: Johnny Cash's Bitter Tears Revisited.*

Rangi Ruru Girls'School is a New Zealand private girls' day and boarding secondary school located in Merivale, an inner suburb of Christchurch. The school is affiliated to the Presbyterian Church, and serves approximately 698 girls from Years 7 to 13 (ages 10 to 18).

Quintus Pompeius Falco (c. 70 – after 143) was a Roman senator, general, and governor who held several prominent positions during the reigns of Trajan and Hadrian. He served as governor of Judea, Moesia Inferior, and Britain, where he suppressed a rebellion. Falco also oversaw the construction of the Via Traiana and later governed the province of Asia. He married Sosia Polla, linking him to prominent Roman families, and retired to his estate near Tusculum.

Pakistan's second Presidential Elections were held on 2 January 1965. The voting was to be indirect, as the President had to be elected by the 80,000 "basic democrats", who made up the Electoral College. These basic Democrats were basically democratically elected public representatives who served in the Divisional, District, Tehsil or Union councils.

There were two major parties contesting the elections: the Convention Muslim League and the Combined Opposition Parties. The Combined Opposition Parties consisted of five major opposition parties, and had a nine-point program, which included restoration of parliamentary democracy and introduction of direct elections and adult franchise. The Combined Opposition Parties had no single leadership and were therefore, unable to select a presidential candidate from amongst themselves. Hence, they chose Fatima Jinnah as their candidate who was seen as an undisputed leader due to her relationship with Muhammad Ali Jinnah. Meanwhile, the candidate for Convention Muslim League was the incumbent President Mohammed Ayub Khan.and other party Muhammad Irfan sameja

Route 225 is a state highway in the U.S. state of Massachusetts. The highway runs 31.100 miles (50.051 km) from Route 2A in Lunenburg in northeastern Worcester County east to Route 2 in Lexington. Route 225 serves the western Middlesex County towns of Shirley, Groton, Westford, and Carlisle. The highway also passes through Bedford along its concurrency with Route 4, which terminates at the same junction with Route 2.

Melchior Inchofer (1584–1648) was a Hungarian Jesuit theologian, writer, and professor known for his role in the 1633 trial of Galileo, where he concluded that Galileo's *Dialogue* supported Copernicanism. Born to Lutheran parents, he converted to Catholicism and joined the Jesuits, teaching philosophy and theology. Inchofer authored significant works on ecclesiastical history, Latin, and astronomy, and contributed to the study of historical sources.

A capsule hotel is a type of affordable, minimalist accommodation featuring small, enclosed sleeping spaces called capsules, which are roughly the size of a single bed. These capsules, often made of fiberglass or plastic, are stacked in rows and include basic amenities such as a small television, air conditioning, and power outlets. Shared facilities, including bathrooms, lounges, and dining areas, are common, and some hotels offer additional features like communal baths, saunas, and vending machines. Capsule hotels, which vary in size and often cater primarily to men, are popular in Japan for their low cost and convenience, particularly among salarymen and travelers. However, they have been criticized for their cramped spaces and lack of privacy.

"Winter" is a song by rock band U2. The track was originally planned to be included on the band's 2009 album No Line on the Horizon, but it was cut at the end of the recording sessions as it did not fit the album's theme. The song does appear on the accompanying film Linear. "Winter" was written for the 2009 war film Brothers at the request of director Jim Sheridan, and it plays over the closing credits. Several different versions of the song have been recorded, including an uptempo rock arrangement on Linear and a slower acoustic version in Brothers. The song was nominated for the Golden Globe Award for Best Original Song.
Amaan Reza (born May 20, 1986) is a Bangladeshi actor and entrepreneur.

The *Insurance Act, 1938* is an Indian law that consolidated and amended regulations for the insurance industry. Initially influenced by British law, it established regulatory frameworks, including the Controller of Insurance. Over time, its significance evolved with the nationalization of insurance companies and the formation of the Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India (IRDAI) in 1999. The Act governs insurance operations in India, requiring companies to be Indian-registered and limiting foreign ownership, with the foreign direct investment cap increased to 74% in 2021.

The Acts of Parliament of the United Kingdom relating to the European Communities and the European Union encompass legislation governing the UK's accession to the European Communities (later the European Union), its membership, and subsequent developments. Key acts include the European Communities Act 1972, which facilitated the UK's entry in 1973, and later legislation addressing referendums, European Parliament elections, the European Economic Area, EU treaty amendments, and the UK's withdrawal from the EU.

Chenani is a town in the Udhampur district of Jammu and Kashmir, India, located at an elevation of 1,062 meters (3,484 feet) along the Tawi River. Historically, it was a princely state ruled by the Chandel Rajputs until 1947. As of the 2011 Census, it has a population of 2,620 with a literacy rate of 88.91%. Chenani is well-connected by road, including the nearby Dr. Syama Prasad Mookerjee Tunnel, India's longest road tunnel, which reduces travel time on the Jammu–Srinagar National Highway. It serves as a gateway to nearby shrines such as Sudhmahadev and Mantalai.

Sir Robert James Buckland KBE KC MP (born 22 September 1968) is a British Conservative Party politician and barrister who has served as the Member of Parliament (MP) for South Swindon since 2010. He has held several ministerial roles, including Secretary of State for Justice and Lord Chancellor (2019–2021) under Boris Johnson, and Secretary of State for Wales (July–October 2022) under Johnson and Liz Truss. Buckland also served as Solicitor General for England and Wales (2014–2019) and Minister of State for Prisons (2019). 

Before entering politics, Buckland practiced as a barrister specializing in criminal law and was appointed Queen's Counsel in 2014. Known for his advocacy on autism and special educational needs, he has received several awards for his work. Buckland was knighted in 2022 and returned to the backbenches later that year.

*Ultraman Powered* is a 1994 fighting video game developed by Tose and published by Bandai for the 3DO console. Based on the 1993 home video series *Ultraman: The Ultimate Hero* (known in Japan as *Ultraman Powered*), the game features the titular superhero defending Earth from alien and monster threats. Players engage in one-on-one battles using basic attacks and special moves powered by a replenishing energy meter. The game includes single-player modes such as Visual Mode, which features FMV clips from the series, and Battle Mode, which combines rail-shooter segments with traditional fighting gameplay. A multiplayer Versus Mode allows two players to compete.

The game was a launch title for the Japanese 3DO market and was praised for its digitized graphics and character sprites, which were adapted from the series. However, its slow gameplay and poor controls received criticism. Despite mixed reviews, *Ultraman Powered* remains notable for its connection to the *Ultraman* franchise and its early use of FMV and pre-rendered visuals.

Marie Fillunger (27 January 1850 – 23 December 1930) was an Austrian classical singer and voice teacher, known for her interpretations of lieder by Schubert and Brahms. A student of Mathilde Marchesi and Amalie Joachim, she performed across Europe, Australia, and South Africa, later teaching at the Royal Manchester College of Music. Fillunger had a long-term relationship with Eugenie Schumann, daughter of Clara and Robert Schumann, and spent her later years in Switzerland, where she died.

Down High School is a grammar school in Downpatrick, County Down, Northern Ireland, established in 1933. The school serves approximately 1,080 students aged 11 to 18 and is situated on a rural campus. Its motto, *Floreat Dunum, Absque Labore Nihil* ("May Down flourish, nothing is achieved without effort"), reflects its commitment to academic and personal growth. The school’s crest, inspired by the badge of 12th-century Norman soldier John de Courcy, symbolizes its historical ties to the region. Notable features of the campus include a gatehouse and stone walls, remnants of the former county gaol, and five student houses fostering community spirit.

Air Marshal Sir Peter Edward Bairsto (3 August 1926 – 24 October 2017) was a senior Royal Air Force officer who served from 1944 to 1984, including as Deputy Commander of Strike Command. He was a decorated veteran of the Second World War, receiving honors such as the Air Force Cross and being appointed Knight Commander of the Order of the British Empire. After retiring, he became an advisor to the Ferranti Group and served as Deputy Lieutenant of Fife.

Pneumodesmus newmani is a species of myriapod that lived in the Paleozoic. Its exact age is uncertain; it was originally interpreted as living 428 million years ago, in the Late Silurian; however, subsequent research dates it to around 414 million years old, in the Early Devonian (Lochkovian). It is one of the first myriapods, and among the oldest creatures to have lived on land. It was discovered in 2004, and is known from a single specimen from Stonehaven, Aberdeenshire, Scotland.

Maitreya-nātha (c. 270–350 CE) is a name whose use was pioneered by Buddhist scholars Erich Frauwallner, Giuseppe Tucci, and Hakuju Ui to distinguish one of the three founders of the Yogacara school of Buddhist philosophy, along with Asanga and Vasubandhu. Some scholars believe this Maitreya to be a historical person in India. The traditions themselves have held that it is referring to Maitreya, the future buddha.

Gabby La La (born Gabby Lang, May 23, 1979) is an American experimental musician, singer, and songwriter from Petaluma, California. Known for her high-pitched vocals and eclectic performances, she plays a variety of instruments, including sitar, ukulele, accordion, theremin, and toy piano. A protégé of Les Claypool, she has performed in his Fancy Band and collaborated with artists such as Snoop Dogg, Jane Wiedlin, and The Coup. La La has also performed at Bonnaroo and taught music to young girls. She is recognized for her colorful stage presence and unique musical style.
Todd Scott is a New Zealand media executive, former television host, and butcher, owning the *National Business Review*.

Tulane University Law School, established in 1847, is a private law school located in New Orleans, Louisiana, and is part of Tulane University. It is one of the oldest law schools in the United States and is ranked 55th nationally by *U.S. News & World Report* (2023). The school is housed in John Giffen Weinmann Hall on Tulane's Uptown campus, which features a law library, classrooms, and faculty offices. Tulane Law offers a Juris Doctor (J.D.) program, six optional concentrations, and graduate degrees, including Master of Laws (LL.M.) and Doctor of Juridical Science (S.J.D.) programs. It is renowned for its programs in maritime, environmental, and international law, as well as its study-abroad opportunities. The school also offers joint degree programs, including JD/MBA and JD/MHA. Tulane Law emphasizes experiential learning through clinical programs and externships and was the first U.S. law school to require pro bono service for graduation.
Louis François Auguste Cauchois-Lemaire (August 28, 1789 in Paris – August 9, 1861 in Paris) was a French journalist.

Essert-Pittet (IPA-fr) is a former municipality in the district of Jura-Nord Vaudois of the canton of Vaud in Switzerland. In 2017 the former municipalities of Essert-Pittet and Corcelles-sur-Chavornay merged into the municipality of Chavornay.

Atlantic Coast Line 1504 is a 4-6-2 USRA Light Pacific steam locomotive built in March 1919 by the American Locomotive Company (ALCO) of Richmond, Virginia, for the Atlantic Coast Line Railroad (ACL) as a member of the P-5-A class. It was assigned to pull ACL's mainline passenger trains until it was retired in 1952 and subsequently donated to the city of Jacksonville, Florida, where it sat on static display as the only original USRA Light Pacific steam locomotive to be preserved. In 1990, No. 1504 was designated as a National Historic Mechanical Engineering Landmark by the American Society of Mechanical Engineers (ASME).

As of 2022, the locomotive is being restored to operating condition for use in excursion service on the South Central Florida Express shortline railroad in Clewiston, Florida as part of U.S. Sugar's (USSC) heritage tourist passenger train named the Sugar Express, where it will eventually ran alongside ex-Florida East Coast Railway (FEC) No. 148.

The POW/MIA flag is a symbol of the United States' commitment to resolving the fates of American prisoners of war (POWs) and those missing in action (MIA). Designed in 1971 by Newt Heisley for the National League of Families, the flag features a silhouette of a POW, a guard tower, and barbed wire, with the words "POW/MIA" and "You Are Not Forgotten." Officially recognized by Congress in 1990, it is flown on federal properties and military sites to honor and remember POW/MIAs from all U.S. conflicts.

Tbilisoba (Georgian: თბილისობა) is an annual October festival which celebrates the diversity and history of Tbilisi, the capital of Georgia. It was first held on October 28, 1979, and has since become an established tradition. The festival features open-air concerts of traditional music and dancing and various cultural events, centered on Old Tbilisi, the historical part of the city. Beyond celebrating the city's past and present, people from all over Georgia represent their region at the fair of the harvest and Rtveli. Awarding honorary citizenship of Tbilisi by the city government also occurs in the framework of Tbilisoba.
Drammen Museum of Art and Cultural History (Drammens Museum for kunst og kulturhistorie) is a museum in Drammen in Viken county, Norway.
Maria Clara Eimmart (27 May 1676 – 29 October 1707), was a German astronomer, engraver and designer. She was the daughter and assistant of Georg Christoph Eimmart the Younger.

*I Feel Pretty* is a 2018 American comedy film written and directed by Abby Kohn and Marc Silverstein, starring Amy Schumer, Michelle Williams, Rory Scovel, and Emily Ratajkowski. The film follows Renee Bennett, a woman struggling with low self-esteem, who gains newfound confidence after a head injury leads her to believe she is stunningly beautiful. Her transformation impacts her personal and professional life, culminating in a message of self-acceptance. Produced on a $32 million budget, the film grossed $94.5 million worldwide. While it received mixed reviews, with praise for its premise but criticism for its execution, it sparked discussions on body image and inspired a 2021 French television remake, *Belle, belle, belle*.

Anna Magdalena Appel (8 February 1846 – 19 December 1917), later Freifrau von Hochstädten, was a German ballet dancer and the morganatic wife of Louis III, Grand Duke of Hesse. Born in Darmstadt, she became a soloist at the Hoftheater before marrying Louis III in 1868, after being ennobled. She influenced the artistic affairs of the Grand Duchy and cared for her husband until his death in 1877. Following his passing, she resided in Wiesbaden and remained respected by the Grand-Ducal family.
Thomas Hourston (February 12, 1854 – April 11, 1905) was a politician from Alberta, Canada and a municipal councillor in Edmonton.

Scott L. Thoele is a United States Army officer and National Guard member who has served in various capacities since 1980, including deployments in the Gulf War, Iraq War, and War in Afghanistan. He has held commands in the 20th Special Forces Group and served at the Pentagon. Thoele's military decorations include the Legion of Merit and Bronze Star Medal, among others. In civilian life, he is a vice president and credit analyst at First Bankers Trust Company in Quincy, Illinois.

Parmalat S.p.A. is a dairy and food corporation which is a subsidiary of French multinational company Lactalis. It was founded by Calisto Tanzi in 1961.

Having become the leading global company in the production of long-life milk using ultra-high-temperature processing, the company collapsed in 2003 with a €14bn ($20bn; £13bn) hole in its accounts in what remains Europe's biggest bankruptcy. Since 2011, it has been a subsidiary of French group Lactalis (with fuller control since 2019). Today, Parmalat is a company with a global presence, having operations in Europe, North America, South America, Australia, China, and South Africa.

Still specializing in UHT milk and milk derivatives (varieties of yogurt, cheese, butter, ice cream, etc.), the group also sells fruit juices distributed under the brand names Lactis, Santal, Malù, and Kyr. Its worldwide operations include almost 140 production centres and some 16,000 employees, with 5,000 Italian dairy farms supplying it. Its shares were listed on the Borsa Italiana.

Ben Christo (born 22 March 1980) is an English rock musician, guitarist, songwriter, vocalist, lyricist and session musician, he is best known for his tenures with The Sisters of Mercy, Night By Night and Diamond Black.

Under the Whyte notation for the classification of steam locomotives, 0-8-0 represents the wheel arrangement of no leading wheels, eight powered and coupled driving wheels on four axles and no trailing wheels. Locomotives of this type are also referred to as eight coupled.

A customer data platform (CDP) is a type of software that centralizes and standardizes customer data from various sources to create a unified customer view. Unlike data management platforms (DMPs) or data lakes, CDPs collect identifiable customer data in real-time and enable personalized marketing and analytics. Emerging in 2010, CDPs evolved from customer databases and gained popularity for their integration capabilities, with the industry reaching $1.9 billion in revenue by 2022.

Christian College, Geelong is an independent, co-educational school located in Geelong, Victoria, Australia. Established in 1980 in the suburb of Highton, the school has grown from an initial enrollment of 40 students to over 1,900 by 2007. The college operates multiple campuses, including the Middle School in the former St. Augustine's Orphanage, the Junior School in the former Glastonbury Orphanage, and the Senior Campus, which opened in 2000 on the former Highton campus of Geelong Grammar School. The Bellarine Campus, serving students on the Bellarine Peninsula, was established in 1996 on the site of the former Rural City of Bellarine council offices.

The school features a house system with four houses: Penman Panthers, Flynn Falcons, Taylor Tigers, and Burrows Bears, named after notable figures such as Eva Burrows and John Flynn. Christian College is also known for its extensive music program, offering instruction in various instruments and hosting bands, choirs, and orchestras that compete and perform internationally, including at the Sydney Opera House and the Pacific Basin Music Festival in Hawaii.

In 2015, the school faced controversy when three Year 12 students arrived at a "Celebration Day" event in an unauthorized helicopter, leading to the cancellation of the following year's event.

The Ricoh 2A03 or RP2A03 (NTSC version) / Ricoh 2A07 or RP2A07 (PAL version) is an 8-bit microprocessor manufactured by Ricoh for the Nintendo Entertainment System video game console. It was also used as a sound chip and secondary CPU by Nintendo's arcade games Punch-Out!! and Donkey Kong 3.

Nathan Sawaya (born July 10, 1973) is an American artist known for creating large-scale sculptures and installations using LEGO bricks. A former attorney, he gained recognition in 2004 after leaving his legal career to pursue art full-time. Sawaya's work, including his signature human form sculptures and large-scale replicas, has been exhibited globally in venues such as the Lancaster Museum of Art, Federation Square in Melbourne, and the ArtScience Museum in Singapore. His touring exhibition, *The Art of the Brick*, is one of the first major museum exhibitions to focus exclusively on LEGO as an art medium. Sawaya operates studios in Manhattan and Los Angeles, each housing over 1.5 million LEGO bricks, and has been recognized as one of the world's top LEGO builders. His work has been widely acclaimed, featured in major media outlets, and commissioned by collectors, celebrities, and institutions worldwide.

*Life* is a 1999 American comedy-drama film directed by Ted Demme and starring Eddie Murphy and Martin Lawrence. Written by Robert Ramsey and Matthew Stone, the film follows Ray Gibson and Claude Banks, two men wrongfully convicted of murder in 1932 Mississippi and sentenced to life imprisonment. Over several decades, the pair navigate hardships, form friendships, and attempt to escape, ultimately finding freedom in their old age. The film explores themes of resilience, friendship, and injustice, blending humor with poignant moments.

Produced by Imagine Entertainment and distributed by Universal Pictures, *Life* was released on April 16, 1999. Despite its $80 million budget, it grossed $73.3 million worldwide, making it a financial disappointment. Critical reception was mixed, with praise for the performances of Murphy and Lawrence. The film's soundtrack, featuring hip hop and R&B, was a commercial success, achieving platinum certification. Filming took place in California, though the story is set in Mississippi.

"I Am Real" is the debut single by Australian hotelier Simon van Kempen. It was released on 21 April 2011, through JSM Music, during his wife Alex McCord's tenure on the American reality television series The Real Housewives of New York City. The song was written by Allison Beth Simon, Jon Jason Appleton, and Koki Saito, while production was handled by Joel Simon, Ross Hopman, and Saito. During its development, van Kempen and McCord met with the team of writers to ensure that the song's lyrics would represent them accurately. A dance-pop and EDM track, "I Am Real" contains tongue-in-cheek lyrics that address van Kempen's critics. One lyric references a comment McCord used to describe fellow cast member Luann de Lesseps.

"I Am Real" received mostly unfavourable reviews from music critics, who dismissed van Kempen as a television personality who tried launching a singing career. It also received comparisons to "Money Can't Buy You Class" (2010) by de Lesseps. The song was unsuccessful, selling just 1,000 copies in the United States, as of September 2011. van Kempen has since performed "I Am Real" on Watch What Happens Live with Andy Cohen and at Manhattan's annual "Fashion Night Out" event in 2011.

Neodiprion abietis, commonly known as the balsam fir sawfly, is a species of insect in the family Diprionidae. It is found in North America from Canada to northern Mexico and is phytophagous, feeding on the needles of coniferous trees.

The Postcard Killings is a 2020 American crime film directed by Danis Tanović, starring Jeffrey Dean Morgan, Famke Janssen and Cush Jumbo, and based on the 2010 novel The Postcard Killers by James Patterson and Liza Marklund. The film was released on March 13, 2020, receiving negative reviews from critics.

Petro Pysarchuk (born June 6, 1955) is a Ukrainian politician, entrepreneur, and former member of the Verkhovna Rada, representing the Party of Regions. He also founded Lviv's largest trade complex, Pivdennyj Market.

The handscroll is a long, narrow, horizontal scroll format in East Asia used for calligraphy or paintings. A handscroll usually measures up to several meters in length and around 25–40 cm in height. Handscrolls are generally viewed starting from the right end. This kind of scroll is intended to be read or viewed flat on a table, in sections. The format thus allows for the depiction of a continuous narrative or journey.

The traditional alternative format in East Asian painting is the vertical hanging scroll, which is rarely as long.

*Chenopodium fremontii* is a species of annual herb in the family Amaranthaceae, native to North America. It grows erect, reaching heights of 50 to 80 centimeters, with powdery-textured leaves and flowers. The plant produces small, tightly packed flowers from June to October. Historically, it has been used by Native American tribes as a food source, with its greens consumed as a vegetable and its seeds used to make bread and porridge.
Diether Pascual Ocampo (born July 19, 1974) is a Filipino actor, singer, and model.

The Port of Gaza is a small port near the Rimal district of Gaza City. It is the home port of Palestinian fishing-boats and the base of the Palestinian Naval Police, a branch of the Palestinian National Security Forces. Under the Oslo II Accord, the activities of the Palestinian Naval Police are restricted to 6 nautical miles from the coast. Since 2007, the Port of Gaza has been under an Israeli-imposed naval blockade as part of a blockade of the Gaza Strip, and activities at the port have been restricted to small-scale fishing.

David Upthegrove (born May 3, 1971) is an American politician. He is a member of the King County Council, representing the 5th district since 2014. A member of the Democratic Party, he was a member of the Washington House of Representatives, representing the 33rd district from 2002 to 2013.

A lord proprietor was an individual granted control over a proprietary colony by the British Crown during the colonial era, with powers to govern, create laws, and manage land. This system, established through charters like the 1663 Carolina Charter, allowed proprietors to oversee colonies such as Maryland, Carolina, and New Jersey. Proprietary governance varied by colony, with some achieving stability while others faced mismanagement and conflict. Over time, dissatisfaction with proprietors' rule led to many colonies transitioning to royal control. The system also influenced land distribution and governance structures in early American colonies.

UFOria is a science fiction comedy film written and directed by John Binder and starring Fred Ward, Harry Dean Stanton, Harry Carey, Jr. and Cindy Williams. The film includes small appearances by Peggy McKay, Joe Unger, Hank Worden, Charlotte Stewart, and Alan Beckwith. Filming was completed in 1981, but the film was not theatrically released until 1985. It was released only on VHS in 1987 by MCA Home Video, now known as Universal Studios Home Entertainment.

WUFO (1080 AM), branded as "Power 96.5," is a radio station based in Amherst, New York, serving the Buffalo metropolitan area. Established in 1948, it operates on a frequency of 1080 kHz and simulcasts on FM translator W243DX at 96.5 MHz. Owned by Sheila L. Brown's Vision Multi Media Group, WUFO is notable for being the first radio station in Western New York programmed for the African American community, a format it adopted in 1962 under the ownership of Dynamic Broadcasting. Over the years, it has featured prominent African American radio personalities and transitioned through various formats, including gospel music. In 2017, WUFO rebranded as "Power 96.5," adopting an Urban AC and Classic Hip Hop format, further solidifying its cultural significance in the region.

Collar × Malice is a visual novel video game developed and published by Idea Factory under their Otomate brand. It was released for PlayStation Vita on August 18, 2016, in Japan, and by Aksys Games in North America and Europe on July 28, 2017. A Nintendo Switch port was also released in 2020. The game was well received by critics, and sold well in Japan for a niche game of its genre and platform. A fan disc, Collar × Malice: Unlimited, was released on July 26, 2018. An anime film adaptation is set to premiere in 2023.

The 2010 conservatism-psychoticism correlation error refers to a mistake in a study by Brad Verhulst, Peter K. Hatemi, and Nicholas G. Martin, which incorrectly reported positive correlations between psychoticism and conservative attitudes. The error, later repeated in subsequent publications, reversed the actual negative correlations. While the mistake did not affect the study's primary thesis, it caused confusion in later research and public discourse. The error was eventually corrected after scrutiny by researchers, sparking debates over data transparency and the interpretation of psychoticism in political psychology.

Marcus Furius Camillus (c. 26 BC – after AD 18) was a Roman senator and a close friend of the emperor Tiberius. Despite being without previous military experience, he enjoyed several successes against the Numidian rebel Tacfarinas while serving as governor of Africa, and was even praised in public by the Emperor and awarded triumphal honours. The historian Tacitus, in his Annales (published AD 109), joked that Camillus subsequently lived invisibly enough to survive this great honour (an allusion to the endless series of executions of prominent senators on spurious treason charges under Tiberius).

Margaret Bernadine Hall (10 March 1863 – 2 January 1910) was an English painter who spent most of her career in Paris. Few of her works have survived, but she is notable for her 1886 painting Fantine, which hangs in the Walker Art Gallery, Liverpool, England. The subject of the painting is Fantine, a character in Victor Hugo's 1862 novel Les Misérables.

The Taylor Titch is a British single-seat, low-wing monoplane designed by John Taylor in the 1960s as a high-performance aircraft for amateur construction. Developed from the Taylor Monoplane, it was created for the 1964 Midget Racer Design Competition, where it placed second. The prototype first flew in 1967, but Taylor tragically died in a crash later that year. Despite this, plans for the Titch were marketed by his family, and as of 2011, 40 examples had been completed and flown. The Titch is aerobatic, capable of cross-country touring, and can be powered by either Continental or Lycoming engines.

Te Mapoutahi or simply Mapoutahi is a peninsula on the coast of Otago, New Zealand, between the townships of Waitati and Pūrākaunui, within the limits of Dunedin City. It lies some 20 kilometres (12 mi) north of Dunedin's city centre.

Known also, confusingly, as "Goat Island", the peninsula's narrow isthmus and rugged coastline made it the perfect defensive location for a pā, a fortified settlement. The headland was, indeed, occupied by a Kāti Māmoe pā during the century before the arrival of Europeans in the early 1800s, and was the site of a major massacre in around 1750 when the warrior Taoka attacked the fortifications and killed those within.

The peninsula is now a scenic and historic reserve and is the site of a popular walking track.

The adjacent beach, Mapoutahi Cove, is used by naturists for nude walks and sunbathing. New Zealand has no official nude beaches, as public nudity is legal on any beach where it is "known to occur".
John Barnard (1705–1784) was an English shipbuilder and Master Shipwright for the Royal Navy, known for his work at Harwich Dockyard and his private shipyards in Deptford and Rotherhithe.

*NBA Street Vol. 2* is a 2003 sports video game developed by NuFX and EA Canada and published by EA Sports BIG for PlayStation 2, GameCube, and Xbox. The sequel to *NBA Street*, it features 3-on-3 street basketball gameplay with four modes: Pick Up Game, NBA Challenge, Be a Legend, and Street School. Players can unlock NBA and street legends, including three versions of Michael Jordan, and earn rewards like players, jerseys, and courts. The game introduced new trick moves, dunks, and a two-level "Gamebreaker" mechanic. It received critical acclaim for its gameplay, soundtrack, and presentation.

Georgia Blanche Douglas Camp Johnson, better known as Georgia Douglas Johnson (September 10, 1880 – May 15, 1966), was a poet. She was one of the earliest female African American playwrights, and an important figure of the Harlem Renaissance.
Paul Alden Brinegar Jr. (December 19, 1917 – March 27, 1995) was an American character actor best known for his roles in three Western series: The Life and Legend of Wyatt Earp, Rawhide, and Lancer.

The New York Regents Examinations are a series of standardized tests administered by the New York State Education Department (NYSED) to assess high school students' proficiency in core subjects. First introduced in 1866, the exams were expanded in 1878 to serve as high school end-of-course assessments. Over time, the exams evolved to reflect changes in educational priorities, transitioning from a wide array of subject-specific tests to more comprehensive exams. Vocational exams were discontinued by the 1970s, and foreign language exams were largely eliminated by 2011 due to budget cuts.

Regents exams are typically three hours long and are offered in January, June, and August, with exceptions like the Earth Science lab component. Students must pass at least five exams with a score of 65 or higher to earn a Regents Diploma, with additional requirements for Advanced Designation. Special provisions exist for students with disabilities and English language learners.

The exams have undergone significant changes, including alignment with Common Core Standards and adjustments due to the COVID-19 pandemic. While public schools are required to administer Regents exams, some private and alternative schools opt for different assessment methods.

Kfar Dounin (Arabic: كفردونين) is a village in the Bint Jbeil District of the Nabatieh Governorate in southern Lebanon, situated at an elevation of 580 meters. Historically, it was noted in the late 19th century for its Metawileh population and remnants of a demolished church.

The London Midland and Scottish Railway (LMS) Hughes Crab or Horwich Mogul is a class of mixed-traffic 2-6-0 steam locomotive built between 1926 and 1932. They are noted for their appearance with large steeply-angled cylinders to accommodate a restricted loading gauge.

The new areas or new districts of the People's Republic of China are new urban districts that are given special economic and development support by the Chinese Central Government or regional government. New areas are divided into two varieties: administrative or management and further divided into levels: state-level, provincial-level, and prefectural-level.

In the United Kingdom and the Isle of Man, the term "institute" is a protected designation, reserved for organizations conducting research at the highest level or professional bodies of the highest standing. The use of "institute" in a company or business name requires approval from the Secretary of State, in accordance with regulations such as the Business Names Act. Unauthorized use of the term without approval constitutes a criminal offence.
Marko Čakarević (Serbian Cyrillic: Марко Чакаревић, born May 13, 1988) is a Serbian professional basketball player for Beograd (3x3) team.

Our Revolution: A Future to Believe In is a book by U.S. Senator from Vermont Bernie Sanders, published by Thomas Dunne Books in November 2016.

It was released on November 15, 2016, a week after the election of Donald Trump. The book was written in the context of Sanders's 2016 presidential campaign and aimed to explain some of its rationale.

Publius Sulpicius Quirinius (c. 51 BC – AD 21) was a Roman statesman and military leader who served as consul in 12 BC and later as governor of Syria. He is known for his military campaigns, including victories over the Marmaridae and the Homanades, and for conducting the controversial Census of Quirinius in Judea in 6 AD, which incited unrest. Quirinius was a close associate of Emperor Augustus and later Tiberius, and he was honored with a public funeral upon his death. He is also mentioned in the Gospel of Luke in connection with the nativity of Jesus.

Moses Rischin was an American historian and professor, known for his contributions to American Jewish history. Born and raised in Brooklyn, New York, he earned his Ph.D. from Harvard University in 1957 and became a professor at San Francisco State University in 1964. Rischin directed the Western Jewish History Center at the Judah L. Magnes Museum from its founding in 1967. He also served on the boards of prominent historical organizations and was honored with a collection of essays published in 1996.

The Underground Railroad is a historical fiction novel by American author Colson Whitehead, published by Doubleday in 2016. The alternate history novel tells the story of Cora, a slave in the Antebellum South during the 19th century, who makes a bid for freedom from her Georgia plantation by following the Underground Railroad, which the novel depicts as a rail transport system with safe houses and secret routes. The book was a critical and commercial success, hitting the bestseller lists and winning several literary awards, including the Pulitzer Prize for Fiction, the National Book Award for Fiction, the Arthur C. Clarke Award, and the 2017 Andrew Carnegie Medal for Excellence. A TV miniseries adaptation, written and directed by Barry Jenkins, was released in May 2021.

Gina Bachauer (1913–1976) was a renowned Greek pianist, celebrated as one of the greatest pianists of the 20th century. Born in Athens, she showed early talent for the piano, giving her first recital as a child and graduating from the Athens Conservatory in 1929. She studied under Alfred Cortot and Sergei Rachmaninoff, the latter of whom she followed around the world for lessons. Despite interruptions to her career due to financial difficulties and World War II, she achieved international acclaim, performing over 100 concerts annually across Europe and the Americas. Known as the "queen of pianists," she was particularly admired for her interpretations of Romantic piano concertos.

Bachauer recorded extensively and supported young pianists through mentorship and competitions. She was a close collaborator with the Utah Symphony Orchestra and held an honorary doctorate from the University of Utah. The Gina Bachauer International Piano Competition, established in 1976, continues her legacy. She died of a heart attack in 1976, moments before a scheduled performance in Athens.

Cécile Rol-Tanguy (10 April 1919 – 8 May 2020) was a French communist who was a Resistance fighter during World War II. She participated in the liberation of Paris serving as secretariat and a liaison officer, conducting clandestine operations and relaying confidential communications.

The City of Greenwood Village is a home rule municipality located in Arapahoe County, Colorado, United States. The city population was 15,691 at the 2020 United States Census. Greenwood Village is a part of the Denver–Aurora–Lakewood, CO Metropolitan Statistical Area and the Front Range Urban Corridor.
London Bourne (1793–1869) was a Barbadian businessman, former slave, and civil rights advocate.

Pennsylvania Route 934 (PA 934) is a 10.9-mile (17.5 km) state highway located in Lebanon County, Pennsylvania. The route begins at an intersection with U.S. Route 322 (US 322) and PA 241 in South Annville Township and runs north to its terminus at an interchange with Interstate 81 (I-81) in Fort Indiantown Gap. PA 934 is primarily a two-lane undivided road, passing through agricultural areas, residential communities, and notable landmarks such as Lebanon Valley College in Annville. The route also crosses several waterways, including the Quittapahilla Creek and Swatara Creek, and transitions to a divided highway near its northern terminus.

Designated in 1928, PA 934 originally ran from Annville to Bellegrove, with subsequent extensions south to US 322 and north to I-81 over the decades. The route was realigned in the 1960s to serve the Pennsylvania National Guard's Edward Martin Military Reservation. By the 1970s, its northern terminus was shortened to the I-81 interchange. PA 934 remains an important connector in Lebanon County, linking rural areas with major highways.

Nancy Duff Campbell is an American lawyer and a founder and co-president of the National Women's Law Center. Campbell has focused on women's law and public policy issues and has participated in the development of legislative initiatives and litigation regarding women's rights, emphasizing issues affecting low‐income women, and has authored articles on women's legal issues.
Lugazi University was a private university in Lugazi, Uganda, active from 2007 to 2010, offering degrees, diplomas, and certificates.

John Hull Campbell (October 10, 1800 – January 19, 1868) was an American lawyer and politician who served as a U.S. Representative from Pennsylvania's 3rd district from 1845 to 1847. A member of the American Party, he also served in the Pennsylvania House of Representatives in 1831. After declining renomination to Congress, he resumed his law practice in Philadelphia, where he lived until his death.

Azerbaijan—France relations are the bilateral relations between the Republic of Azerbaijan and the French Republic in the political, socio-economic, cultural, and other spheres.

Cooperation is carried out in such areas as energy (Total), environment (Suez CNIM), transport (Alstom, Thales, Iveco, SYSTRA), agriculture (Lactalis, Danone), space industry (Airbus, Thales), gas sector, security, culture, hotel business (Accor), banking (Societe Generale), aviation, etc.

Mong-Lan is a Vietnamese-American poet, artist, musician, dancer, and educator, known for her multidisciplinary contributions to the arts. Born in Saigon, South Vietnam, she fled the country during the fall of Saigon and later became a Stegner Fellow at Stanford University and a Fulbright Scholar. She has published eight books and chapbooks of poetry and art, earning accolades such as the Pushcart Prize and the Juniper Prize. Her work has been featured in prominent anthologies, including *Best American Poetry* and *The Pushcart Book of Poetry*.

As a visual artist, Mong-Lan's paintings and photographs have been exhibited in major institutions like the Dallas Museum of Art and internationally in cities such as Tokyo and Buenos Aires. A skilled musician and tango dancer, she has released ten albums blending jazz, poetry, and tango. Mong-Lan has also taught at various universities and continues to perform and present her work globally.

Sir George Lockhart (1673 – 17 December 1731) was a Scottish politician, lawyer, and writer, known for his opposition to the 1707 Treaty of Union between Scotland and England. A member of the Tory party, he served as a commissioner in the Parliament of Scotland and later as a Member of Parliament for Edinburgh and Wigtown Burghs in the British Parliament. Lockhart was a prominent Jacobite, involved in the 1715 rising, and was briefly imprisoned for his activities. He authored *Memoirs of the Affairs of Scotland* and the influential "Lockhart Papers."

Goodwin Park is a 237-acre urban park located in Hartford and Wethersfield, Connecticut, established in 1901. Originally named South Park, it was renamed in honor of Reverend Francis Goodwin, a key advocate for Hartford's park system. Designed by Frederick Law Olmsted, the park features a grand meadow, tree plantations, and water features. The park's central area, once an airstrip, now houses the Goodwin Park Golf Course, a 27-hole facility with historic roots dating back to the late 1800s.
The unicameral National Assembly (Portuguese: Assembleia Nacional) is the legislative body of the Republic of Cabo Verde.

Kfar Dunin (Lang-ar) is a village in Lebanon, 102 km from the capital Beirut and 580 m from sea coast remote. Adjacent villages: Deir Kifa, Alkaline, Khirbat Silm, Der Ntar. It is considered first of villages in Bent Jbeil district of Nabatieh Governorate. Its population is 6000 people.

A quick coupler is a device used on construction and earth-moving machinery to enable the rapid exchange of tools or attachments, such as buckets. Commonly found on hydraulic excavators, backhoe loaders, and other equipment, quick couplers come in various designs, including manual, semi-automatic, and automatic types. Safety concerns, particularly with semi-automatic couplers, have led to differing regulatory approaches worldwide. The market is supplied by numerous manufacturers, including original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) and independent producers. Efforts to standardize safety guidelines have resulted in the development of international standards, such as ISO 13031.

Mutational meltdown is a process in population genetics where the accumulation of deleterious mutations in a small population leads to a decline in population size and growth rate, ultimately resulting in extinction. This occurs as harmful mutations become fixed in the population, reducing fitness and increasing mortality rates. The process is particularly rapid in small populations due to genetic drift and the shorter time required for mutations to become fixed.

The effects of mutational meltdown differ between asexual and sexual populations. Asexual populations are more vulnerable due to the lack of genetic recombination, which allows mutations to accumulate more quickly. In contrast, sexual populations benefit from genetic diversity and recombination, which can slow or prevent mutational meltdown. However, under certain conditions, such as low birth rates or population bottlenecks, even large sexually reproducing populations can experience mutational meltdown.

The Prosecution Ministry (Spanish: Ministerio Fiscal) is a constitutional body (Part VI § 124) integrated into the Judiciary of Spain, but with full autonomy. It is entrusted with defending the rule of law, the rights of the citizens, and public interest, as well as watching over the independence of the courts of justice.

The requirements to enter into a career of prosecution are a public exam that can only be taken by those with a law degree who meet the required capacity requirements (EOMF § 42). The exam for admission to the judicial and prosecution careers are connected, so that anyone who passes the tests must proceed to the entry position of their chosen career. Those who choose prosecution must complete a training course at the Center for Legal Studies, after which they become a prosecutor by swearing the corresponding oath and taking possession of their jurisdiction (EOMF § 45).

The Japanese submarine *Ro-45* was a Kaichū-type (K6 subclass) submarine of the Imperial Japanese Navy during World War II. Commissioned in January 1944, it operated briefly before being sunk on April 30, 1944, near Truk by U.S. Navy forces during airstrikes by Task Force 58. All 74 crew members were lost, and the submarine was officially declared presumed lost in May 1944. *Ro-45* was stricken from the Navy list in July 1944.

The Irving–Williams series is an empirical rule that describes the relative stabilities of complexes formed by divalent first-row transition metal ions. The series follows the order Mn < Fe < Co < Ni < Cu > Zn, indicating that copper forms the most stable complexes, while manganese forms the least stable. This trend is attributed to a combination of factors, including covalent and electrostatic contributions to metal–ligand binding energies, as well as crystal field stabilization energy (CFSE). The series played a significant role in the development and acceptance of crystal field theory, which later evolved into ligand field theory.

The Irving–Williams series also applies to natural proteins' metal-binding affinities. However, recent research has demonstrated a protein-design approach that can overcome the series' restrictions, enabling proteins to bind metals in a manner contrary to the series, such as favoring metals other than copper. This finding has implications for bioinorganic chemistry and protein engineering.

Quick couplers (also called quick hitches) are used with construction machines to allow the rapid change of buckets and attachments on the machine. They remove the need to use hammers to manually drive out and insert the mounting pins for attachments. They also bring with them additional safety risks that must be overcome by careful design and manufacture, and proper use.
Gatillazo is a Spanish punk band formed in the Basque Country in 2005 by the former La Polla Records singer Evaristo Páramos.

Anas al-Kandari (1981 – October 8, 2002) was a Kuwaiti citizen. He died in a firefight on Faylaka Island, with United States Marines on October 8, 2002. A U.S. Marine was also killed in the incident as was Al Kandari's fellow fighter Jassem al-Hajiri.

USS Plunger (SS-2) was one of the earliest submarines of the United States Navy. She was the lead boat of her class and was later renamed A-1 when she was designated an A-type submarine. She is not to be confused with the experimental submarine Plunger which was evaluated by the U.S. Navy from 1898 to 1900, but not accepted or commissioned.

Zoya Yunoevna Semenduyeva (October 20, 1929 – April 9, 2020) was a Mountain Jewish poet known for her works in Juhuri and Russian. She published numerous poetry collections, often focusing on themes of love, her people, and cultural heritage. Born in Derbent, Dagestan, USSR, she began her literary career in 1960 and gained recognition with her first collection in 1967. After immigrating to Israel in 1992, she continued writing and contributed to translating the Tanakh into Juhuri.

The pluperfect (shortening of plusquamperfect), usually called past perfect in English, is a type of verb form, generally treated as a grammatical tense in certain languages, relating to an action that occurred prior to an aforementioned time in the past. Examples in English are: "we had arrived"; "they had written".

The word derives from the Latin plus quam perfectum, "more than perfect". The word "perfect" in this sense means "completed"; it contrasts with the "imperfect", which denotes uncompleted actions or states.

In English grammar, the pluperfect (e.g. "had written") is now usually called the past perfect, since it combines past tense with perfect aspect. (The same term is sometimes used in relation to the grammar of other languages.) English also has a past perfect progressive (or past perfect continuous) form: "had been writing".

Dan Erickson (born January 26, 1984) is an American television screenwriter, producer, and showrunner, best known as the creator of the critically acclaimed Apple TV+ series *Severance* (2022). Originally from Olympia, Washington, Erickson's pilot script for *Severance* gained recognition in 2016 and was later developed by Ben Stiller's Red Hour Productions. The series premiered to widespread acclaim, earning Erickson a spot on the 2022 *TIME100 Next* list, which highlights emerging global leaders.

William Howard Ashton (born 19 August 1943), known professionally as Billy J. Kramer, is an English pop singer. With The Dakotas, Kramer was managed by Brian Epstein during the 1960s and scored hits with several Lennon–McCartney compositions never recorded by the Beatles, among them the UK number one "Bad to Me" (1963). Kramer and the Dakotas had a further UK chart-topper in 1964 with "Little Children" and achieved U.S. success as part of the British Invasion. Since the end of the beat boom, Kramer has continued to record and perform. His autobiography, Do You Want to Know a Secret, was published in 2016.

The Port of Gaza, located in the Gaza Strip and operated by the Palestinian National Authority, serves as a small port primarily used by Palestinian fishing boats. Historically, the area was home to the ancient port of Maiuma, with archaeological finds such as Roman columns and artifacts discovered in recent years. Since 2007, the port has been under an Israeli naval blockade, with limited access for ships. Efforts to expand and develop the port, including plans for a larger seaport since the 1993 Oslo I Accord, have been hindered by the ongoing Israeli–Palestinian conflict.

Anna Magdalena Appel, (later known as Freifrau Anna Magdalena von Hochstädten), (Darmstadt, 8 March 1846-Wiesbaden, 19 December 1917) was a German ballet dancer, morganatic wife to Louis III, Grand Duke of Hesse.

PRNP (prion protein) is the human gene encoding for the major prion protein PrP (prion protein, Pr for prion, and P for protein), also known as CD230 (cluster of differentiation 230). Expression of the protein is most predominant in the nervous system but occurs in many other tissues throughout the body.

The protein can exist in multiple isoforms: the normal PrPC form, and the protease-resistant form designated PrPRes such as the disease-causing PrPSc(scrapie) and an isoform located in mitochondria. The misfolded version PrPSc is associated with a variety of cognitive disorders and neurodegenerative diseases such as in animals: ovine scrapie, bovine spongiform encephalopathy (BSE, mad cow disease), feline spongiform encephalopathy, transmissible mink encephalopathy (TME), exotic ungulate encephalopathy, chronic wasting disease (CWD) which affects cervids; and in humans: Creutzfeldt–Jakob disease (CJD), fatal familial insomnia (FFI), Gerstmann–Sträussler–Scheinker syndrome (GSS), kuru, and variant Creutzfeldt–Jakob disease (vCJD). Similarities exist between kuru, thought to be due to human ingestion of diseased individuals, and vCJD, thought to be due to human ingestion of BSE-tainted cattle products.

Edwin "Honest Ed" Mirvish, OC CBE OOnt (July 24, 1914 – July 11, 2007) was an American-Canadian businessman, philanthropist and theatrical impresario who lived in Toronto, Ontario. He is known for his flagship business, Honest Ed's, a landmark discount store in downtown Toronto, and as a patron of the arts, instrumental in revitalizing the theatre scene in Toronto.

This is Orson Welles is a 1992 book by Orson Welles (1915–1985) and Peter Bogdanovich that comprises conversations between the two filmmakers recorded over several years, beginning in 1969.: xxiv The wide-ranging volume encompasses Welles's life and his own stage, radio, and film work as well as his insights on the work of others. The interview book was transcribed by Bogdanovich after Welles's death, at the request of Welles's longtime companion and professional collaborator, Oja Kodar.

In addition to more than 300 pages of interviews, the book includes an annotated chronology of Welles's career, a summary of the alterations made in Welles's 1942 masterpiece, The Magnificent Ambersons, and notes on each chapter by film scholar Jonathan Rosenbaum, who edited the volume.

A second edition of This is Orson Welles was published in paperback in 1998, with a new introduction by Bogdanovich and excerpts of a 58-page memo Welles wrote Universal Pictures about the editing of his 1958 film Touch of Evil. The memo was used to create a director's cut of the film released in 1998.

The 1992 audiobook version of This is Orson Welles was nominated for a Grammy Award for Best Spoken Word or Non-Musical Album.
Frank Simmons Leavitt (June 30, 1891 – May 29, 1953) was an American professional wrestler of the early 1900s, known by the ring name Man Mountain Dean.

HIV/AIDS in Pakistan is a growing public health concern, with the epidemic evolving from a low-prevalence phase to a concentrated epidemic since the early 2000s. The disease is primarily concentrated among high-risk groups, including people who inject drugs (27.2% prevalence), transgender sex workers (5.2%), and male sex workers (1.6%), while prevalence among female sex workers remains low (0.6%). Geographic trends show the epidemic expanding from major urban centers to smaller cities and peripheral areas. Contributing factors include unsafe therapeutic injection practices, with Pakistan having one of the highest rates of injection use globally, and cultural stigmas that hinder HIV testing and surveillance.

Pakistan's response began in 1987 with the establishment of the National AIDS Control Program, which has since implemented prevention, surveillance, and treatment initiatives. Efforts were bolstered in 2004 with international funding, enabling targeted interventions for high-risk populations and the provision of free HIV treatment. However, challenges persist, including low service coverage, stigma, and limited integration of HIV programs into broader health systems. Future strategies focus on improving monitoring, expanding access to care, and addressing systemic inefficiencies. Despite these efforts, the epidemic remains concentrated in high-risk groups, with limited data on its spread in the general population due to cultural barriers and inadequate surveillance.

Argentina competed at the inaugural 1960 Summer Paralympics in Rome, Italy, with a delegation of five athletes, all participating in swimming events. The team achieved significant success, winning a total of six medals: two gold, three silver, and one bronze, ranking 10th in the medal table. Notable performances included Sznitowski, who won a gold and a silver medal, and Perazzo, who claimed a gold medal in the women's 50 metres crawl incomplete class 3. All athletes competed in events for individuals with spinal cord injuries requiring wheelchairs.

Helene Hewitt OBE is a British climate scientist specializing in ocean and sea ice modeling. She is the Head of Ocean Modelling at the Met Office Hadley Centre, where she develops climate models, including HadGEM3. Hewitt contributed to the IPCC Sixth Assessment Report and was honored with an OBE in 2022.

Paul Speratus (13 December 1484 – 12 August 1551) was a Swabian Catholic priest who became a Protestant preacher, reformer and hymn-writer. In 1523, he helped Martin Luther to create the First Lutheran hymnal, published in 1524 and called Achtliederbuch.

Mesa/Boogie is an American company that designs and manufactures amplifiers for guitars and basses. Founded in 1969 by Randall Smith in Petaluma, California, the company gained recognition for its innovative amplifier designs, including the high-gain Mark I and the Rectifier series, which became staples in modern rock music. Originally starting as a small repair shop, Mesa/Boogie evolved into a prominent amplifier manufacturer, with Smith pioneering advancements such as cascading gain stages. The company relocated to Petaluma in 1980 and continued to expand its product line over the decades. In 2021, Mesa/Boogie was acquired by Gibson, with Smith joining Gibson as a "Master Designer." The company remains a significant name in the music industry.

Rod Diridon Sr. (born February 8, 1939) is an American transportation and environmental leader, retired public official, and non-profit advocate, often referred to as the "father of modern transit service" in Silicon Valley. He served on the Santa Clara County Board of Supervisors for 20 years, chaired numerous regional transportation projects, and played a key role in developing light rail and commuter rail systems in the San Francisco Bay Area. Diridon was the founding executive director of the Mineta Transportation Institute and has been involved in various philanthropic, environmental, and educational initiatives. San Jose's Diridon Station is named in his honor.

The 2014 WNBA Finals was the playoff series for the 2014 season of the Women's National Basketball Association (WNBA), and the conclusion of the season's playoffs. The Phoenix Mercury, champions of the Western Conference, faced the Chicago Sky, champions of the Eastern Conference.

The WNBA Finals were under a 2–2–1 rotation. The Mercury held home-court advantage as they had a better regular season record (29–5) than the Sky (15–19). The Mercury swept the Sky in three games to win their third title in franchise history.
Waingaro is a rural community in the Waikato District and Waikato region of New Zealand's North Island, on the banks of the Waingaro River, where it is fed by a hot spring.

*Clepsicosma* is a genus of moths in the family Crambidae, containing the single described species *Clepsicosma iridia*. Both the genus and species were first described by Edward Meyrick in 1888, based on a female specimen collected in the Waitākere Ranges, Auckland, New Zealand. The species is endemic to New Zealand, where it is commonly found in native forests across the North Island and parts of the South Island. Adult moths are nocturnal, active from late December to May, and are attracted to light. They rest on the underside of leaves during the day. The larval host plant is believed to be species of cutty grass, particularly *Gahnia setifolia* and *Gahnia xanthocarpa*, though this has not been confirmed through rearing in captivity.
Abderahmane Hachoud (Lang-ar; born July 2, 1988, in El Attaf) is an Algerian professional football player.

Deccani Architecture refers to the architectural styles developed during the Deccan sultanate period. The Deccan sultanates were five dynasties that ruled late medieval kingdoms, namely, Bijapur, Golkonda, Ahmadnagar, Bidar, and Berar in south-western India. The Deccan sultanates were located on the Deccan Plateau. Their architecture was a regional variant of Indo-Islamic architecture, heavily influenced by the styles of the Delhi Sultanate and later Mughal architecture, but sometimes also directly from Persia and Central Asia.

The rulers of five Deccan sultanates had a number of cultural contributions to their credit in the fields of art, music, literature and architecture. Deccan sultanates have constructed many grand and impregnable forts. Bidar and Golconda forts are classic example of military planning of Deccan sultanates. Apart from forts, they have constructed many tombs, mosques and madrasas. Gol Gumbaz (tomb of Mohammed Adil Shah), was the second largest dome in the world.

In 2014, UNESCO put a group of buildings on its "tentative list" to become a World Heritage Site under the name Monuments and Forts of the Deccan Sultanate (despite there being a number of different sultanates). These are:

Nippu Ravva (transl. Fire Spark) is a 1993 Telugu-language action film, produced by M.V.Srinivas Prasad on Yuvarathna Arts banner and directed by A. Kodandarami Reddy. The film stars Nandamuri Balakrishna, Vijayashanti, and music composed by Bappi Lahiri & Raj–Koti with A. R. Rahman's background score. Nippu Ravva and Bangaru Bullodu, also starring Balakrishna, were released on the same day and both ran for over 100 days. It was dubbed into Tamil and Hindi as Suriya and Mawaali Raaj, respectively.

Freeze Frame is the third album by Godley & Creme. The album was recorded at Nigel Gray's Surrey Sound Studios, Leatherhead, Surrey. The cover art, designed by Hipgnosis, identifies the duo as'Godley Creme'.

*A Beautifully Foolish Endeavor* is a 2020 speculative fiction novel by Hank Green and the sequel to *An Absolutely Remarkable Thing*. The story follows the aftermath of April May's apparent death, as her friends navigate a world transformed by the mysterious Altus Space, a virtual reality system threatening humanity's free will. The novel explores themes of technology, addiction, and human connection, culminating in a high-stakes effort to dismantle Altus and restore autonomy. Praised for its humor and thought-provoking narrative, the book received mostly positive reviews, with critics highlighting its nuanced exploration of escapism and hope.

Haemulinae is a subfamily of the Haemulidae and consists of the genera of that family which are regarded as being of New World origin, although they are now widespread. The subfamily is distinguished from the Plectorhynchinae by having a short dorsal fin which contains 13-16 soft rays, as opposed to the long dorsal fin with 17-26 soft rays of the subfamily Plectorhynchinae.

China's Special Envoy on the Middle East Issue is a special diplomatic position appointed by the Chinese Foreign Ministry. The Special Envoy focuses primarily on facilitating the Arab-Israeli peace process, although at various times, the Special Envoy has also addressed other major issues including the conflict in Syria.

Homoisoflavonoids are a class of naturally occurring flavonoid-related compounds found in various plants, including Portulaca oleracea, Caesalpinia sappan, and members of the Asparagaceae family. They exhibit diverse biological activities, such as anti-angiogenic, antimycobacterial, and cytotoxic properties. Homoisoflavonoids can be synthesized from compounds like 2'-hydroxydihydrochalcones and are categorized into subtypes, including homoisoflavanones, which are prominent in plants like Hyacinthaceae. These compounds have potential applications in cancer treatment and other medicinal uses.

Cécile Rol-Tanguy (10 April 1919 – 8 May 2020) was a French Resistance fighter and communist activist who played a key role in the liberation of Paris during World War II. Working alongside her husband, Henri Rol-Tanguy, she served as a liaison officer and secretary for the Forces Françaises de l’Intérieure (FFI), using her resourcefulness to support the Résistance while raising her family. After the war, she dedicated herself to preserving the memory of the Resistance and advocating for the recognition of women’s contributions to the struggle. She was awarded the Resistance Medal and became a Grand Officer of the Legion of Honour in 2008. Rol-Tanguy passed away at the age of 101 on 8 May 2020, the 75th anniversary of VE Day.

The Holy Saviour Monastery of Julfa (Armenian: Սուրբ Ամենափրկիչ վանք) was an Armenian Apostolic monastery located in Jugha, Nakhichevan, dating back to the 9th–10th centuries. Believed to have been founded no later than the 10th century, it was destroyed between 1997 and 2003, along with its adjacent cemetery containing 47 tombstones.
Oakbank School is a coeducational free school in Ryeish Green, Berkshire, England, established in 2012 on the former Ryeish Green School site.

Araxá is a municipality in the state of Minas Gerais, Brazil, located at an elevation of 973 meters (3,192 ft) with a population of 107,337 (2020). Known for its spa with medicinal mud and mineral waters, it is a center for tourism, mining, and agriculture. The city hosts the Grande Hotel, a historic landmark, and is a major producer of niobium and phosphate. Araxá is also a regional hub for education and healthcare, with a high Human Development Index (HDI) of 0.772 (2010).

Hannah Mary Bardell (born 1 June 1983) is a Scottish National Party (SNP) politician serving as the Member of Parliament (MP) for Livingston since 2015. She has held various spokesperson roles, including for Work and Pensions and Digital, Culture, Media and Sport. Bardell is openly LGBT and has advocated for rehabilitation and inclusivity in society.

*Tome VI* is a 1968 jazz album by Gil Mellé, notable for being described as "the first album of electronic jazz." It features Mellé's experimentation with live electronic instruments alongside modal and free jazz styles.

The Koza riot was a violent civil disturbance that occurred on December 20, 1970, in Koza, Okinawa, during the U.S. military occupation of the island. Sparked by a traffic accident involving a U.S. serviceman and an Okinawan pedestrian, the riot escalated due to long-standing tensions between Okinawans and the American military presence. Thousands of Okinawans participated, resulting in the destruction of over 70 vehicles, injuries to 56–60 American servicemen and 27 Okinawans, and 82 arrests. The event highlighted local resentment ahead of Okinawa's 1972 reversion to Japanese sovereignty.

Publius Sulpicius Quirinius (c. 51 BC – AD 21), also translated as Cyrenius, was a Roman aristocrat. After the banishment of the ethnarch Herod Archelaus from the tetrarchy of Judea in AD 6, Quirinius was appointed legate governor of Syria, to which the province of Judaea had been added for the purpose of a census.

Chenani is a town and tehsil in the Udhampur district, in the Indian union territory of Jammu and Kashmir. It is governed by a notified area council (city council). Before 1947, it was an internal jagir ruled by a Chandel (Rajput) ruler in the princely state of Jammu and Kashmir.

Pieter Woortman (23 March 1700 – 14 April 1780) was a Dutch administrator and merchant who served as Director-General of the Dutch Gold Coast from 1767 to 1780. He had a significant career with the Dutch West India Company, engaging in the slave trade and establishing influential Euro-African family lineages, including the prominent Plange family.

Milnrow Cricket Club is an English cricket team that has competed in the Central Lancashire League. Despite a modest record in the league, the club has attracted several notable professional players, including international Test cricketers and former county players. Among its most prominent professionals are Australian all-rounder George Tribe, West Indies Test player Derick Parry, Zimbabwean cricketer Kevin Curran, Indian bowler Chetan Sharma, and New Zealand off-spinner Paul Wiseman, who played for the club in 2005. 

George Tribe holds the club's record for the most wickets in a single season, taking 148 wickets in 1949, a figure that was also a league record at the time. However, his performance was noted to have been hindered by "indifferent fielding" at Milnrow, according to league historian John Kay. Tribe's record was later surpassed in 1955 by Dattu Phadkar of Rochdale. Milnrow Cricket Club's history reflects its ability to attract high-caliber players, contributing to its legacy within the league.

The 2015–16 Pittsburgh Penguins season was the 49th season for the National Hockey League franchise that was established on June 5, 1967. Their regular season games began on October 8, 2015 against the Dallas Stars. On December 12, the team had a record of 15–10–3. The organization then fired head coach Mike Johnston and replaced him with Mike Sullivan, head coach of the organization's American Hockey League affiliate in Wilkes-Barre.

The Penguins qualified for the playoffs for the tenth consecutive season. They earned second place in the Metropolitan Division with 104 points. They began the 2016 Stanley Cup playoffs on April 13 against the New York Rangers, the team they were eliminated by in the Stanley Cup playoffs both of the previous two seasons. They went on to beat the Rangers in five games. In the second round the Pens defeated the Washington Capitals, the winners of the Presidents'Trophy, in six games. The team then played in their first Eastern Conference Final since 2013 against the Tampa Bay Lightning. The Pens defeated the Lightning in seven games, despite trailing the series 3–2 after Game 5, to earn the franchise's fifth berth in the Stanley Cup Final. In the Final, the Penguins defeated the San Jose Sharks in six games to win the franchise's fourth league title.

The Indian River is a river in Lanark County, Ontario, Canada, that flows from an unnamed lake in Lanark Highlands to its mouth at the Mississippi River in Mississippi Mills. Part of the Saint Lawrence River drainage basin, the river passes through notable landmarks such as the Mill of Kintail Conservation Area, which houses museums dedicated to R. Tait McKenzie and James Naismith.

Arti Rana (born c. 1976) is an Indian social entrepreneur. She helps fellow Tharu women to set up groups to make and sell handicrafts. In 2022, she received the Nari Shakti Puraskar, the highest civilian award for women in India.

Don Sweeney (born August 17, 1966) is a Canadian former professional ice hockey player and current executive. A defenseman, he played 16 seasons in the NHL, primarily with the Boston Bruins, appearing in 1,115 games and recording 273 points. Sweeney also played briefly for the Dallas Stars before retiring in 2004. He was named general manager of the Bruins in 2015, overseeing key trades and guiding the team to the 2019 Stanley Cup Finals. Sweeney was awarded the NHL General Manager of the Year Award in 2019. He is married to former figure skater Christine Hough.

Eskişehirspor Kulübü is a Turkish professional football club based in Eskişehir, founded on June 19, 1965, through the merger of three local teams.
