Known as "Red Lightnings" and "Star of Anatolia," the club has a rich history, including winning the Turkish Cup in 1969–70 and the President's Cup in 1970–71. Eskişehirspor has experienced fluctuating success, with periods in the Süper Lig and lower divisions. The team plays at the New Eskişehir Stadium, supported by passionate fans, including the renowned BandoESES group, known for creating a vibrant match atmosphere.

Eduard Deisenhofer (27 June 1909 – MIA 31 January 1945) was a German commander in the Waffen-SS of Nazi Germany. He was an early member in the SS, and served with the Leibstandarte SS Adolf Hitler and at the Dachau concentration camp in 1930s. During World War II, Deisenhofer served with several combat divisions on both the Eastern and Western fronts, earning the Knight's Cross of the Iron Cross. He held a PhD in political economy.

Jasmina Obradović is a Serbian economist and politician serving as a member of the National Assembly of Serbia since 2012 as a member of the Serbian Progressive Party. She has held various committee roles, including in defense, cultural affairs, and agriculture, and has been active in parliamentary friendship groups with several countries. Previously, she served in the Novi Sad city assembly from 2012 to 2016.

Gene Winstead (born 1951) is an American politician and businessman from Minnesota. Winstead was first elected Mayor of Bloomington, Minnesota in 2000. He had previously served as a city council member for 5 years. His 20 year tenure as mayor is the longest in Bloomington history.

Donald Joel Aronow (March 3, 1927 – February 3, 1987) was an American designer, builder and racer of the famous Magnum Marine, Cary, Cigarette, Donzi, and Formula speedboats. He built speedboats for the Shah of Iran, Charles Keating, Robert Vesco, Malcolm Forbes, and George H. W. Bush. Retired President Lyndon B. Johnson owned several 16 ft. Donzi speedboats on his Texas ranch with which he would race his Secret Service agents.
Thomas Hourston (February 12, 1854 – April 11, 1905) was a Scottish-born Canadian merchant, farmer, and alderman on Edmonton's Town Council from 1896 to 1898.

The Wenceslas Bible is a richly illuminated German translation of the Bible, created in the 1390s in Prague, Bohemia, under the patronage of King Wenceslaus IV and his wife, Sophia. Commissioned by Martin Rotlev, a wealthy Prague burgher, it represents one of the earliest German Bible translations from the Latin Vulgate. Despite Emperor Charles IV's earlier ban on vernacular Bible translations, Wenceslaus supported this monumental project, reflecting ties to the Bohemian Reformation. The incomplete manuscript contains 654 miniatures and was intended to be one of the most elaborate medieval manuscripts.
Carola Richards (1915–2004) was a British artist known for her vibrant, experimental works.
Rikuchū-Noda Station (陸中野田駅, Rikuchū-Noda-eki) is a railway station on the Rias Line in the village of Noda, Iwate, Japan, operated by the third-sector railway operator Sanriku Railway.
Phlebiella is a genus of fungi in the order Polyporales.

The Jack Rabbit Trading Post, established in 1949 near Joseph City, Arizona, is a historic Route 66 attraction known for its iconic "HERE IT IS" billboard, fiberglass jackrabbit, and ties to the 2006 film *Cars*.
Michelle Stephenson (born 3 January 1977) is a British pop singer and television presenter. At the age of 17, she was briefly a member of the girl group that would go on to become the Spice Girls.
GE Power is a subsidiary of General Electric, specializing in energy industry products like turbines, generators, and nuclear reactors.

Michael Cobbins (born August 9, 1992) is an American professional basketball player for Basket Brescia Leonessa of the Lega Basket Serie A (LBA). A power forward and center, Cobbins played college basketball at Oklahoma State, where he was a two-time Big 12 All-Defensive Team selection. After going undrafted in the 2015 NBA Draft, he began his professional career with the Oklahoma City Blue and has since played for teams in Greece, Germany, Croatia, Israel, and Italy. Cobbins also competed in The Basketball Tournament as part of the Stillwater Stars, a team of Oklahoma State alumni.
In Search of Sunrise (abbreviated ISOS) is a trance music mix-compilation music CD series released on SongBird, a subdivision of Black Hole Recordings originally founded by Tiësto.

Blaže Koneski (Lang-mk; 19 December 1921 – 7 December 1993) was a Macedonian poet, writer, literary translator, and linguistic scholar.

His major contribution was to the codification of standard Macedonian. He is the key figure who shaped Macedonian literature and intellectual life in the country however he has also been accused of serbianizing the Macedonian standard language.

A pressurized heavy-water reactor (PHWR) is a nuclear reactor that uses heavy water (deuterium oxide D2O) as its coolant and neutron moderator. PHWRs frequently use natural uranium as fuel, but sometimes also use very low enriched uranium. The heavy water coolant is kept under pressure to avoid boiling, allowing it to reach higher temperature (mostly) without forming steam bubbles, exactly as for pressurized water reactor. While heavy water is very expensive to isolate from ordinary water (often referred to as light water in contrast to heavy water), its low absorption of neutrons greatly increases the neutron economy of the reactor, avoiding the need for enriched fuel. The high cost of the heavy water is offset by the lowered cost of using natural uranium and/or alternative fuel cycles. As of the beginning of 2001, 31 PHWRs were in operation, having a total capacity of 16.5 GW(e), representing roughly 7.76% by number and 4.7% by generating capacity of all current operating reactors.

Nagorik Natya Sampradaya (নাগরিক নাট্য সম্প্রদায়) is one of Bangladesh's oldest and most prominent theatre groups, founded in 1968 by Fazle Lohani, Ataur Rahman, and Zia Haider. Known for staging iconic productions of both Bangla and international plays, the troupe performed Bangladesh's first ticketed play in 1973. In 2018, it celebrated its Golden Jubilee with cultural events and new initiatives.

The Nieuport-Macchi Parasol was a single-engine observation aircraft produced by the Italian aeronautical company Nieuport-Macchi in the early 1910s. It was the first aircraft designed independently by Macchi, which until then had only produced aircraft under license. The Parasol operated in front-line service in 1915 during the early stages of Italy's participation in World War I.

The National League of Families POW/MIA flag, often referred to as the POW/MIA flag, was adopted in 1972 and consists of the official emblem of the National League of Families of American Prisoners and Missing in Southeast Asia in white on a black background. In 2019 the National POW/MIA Flag Act was signed into law, requiring the POW/MIA flag to be flown on certain federal properties, including the U.S. Capitol Building, on all days the U.S. flag is flown.

Tech4 Carmen Contreras-Bozak, (December 31, 1919 – January 30, 2017) was the first Puerto Rican woman to serve in the U.S. Women's Army Corps (WAC) where she served as an interpreter and in numerous administrative positions.

Wilson's snipe (Gallinago delicata) is a small, stocky shorebird. The genus name gallinago is New Latin for a woodcock or snipe from Latin gallina, "hen" and the suffix -ago, "resembling". The specific delicata is Latin for "dainty".

This species was considered to be a subspecies of the common snipe (G. gallinago) until 2003 when it was given its own species status, though not all authorities recognized this immediately. Wilson's snipe differs from the latter species in having a narrower white trailing edge to the wings, and eight pairs of tail feathers instead of the typical seven of the common snipe. Its common name commemorates the American ornithologist Alexander Wilson.
John Barnard (1705–1784) was an 18th century English shipbuilder serving the Royal Navy.

Soul Power is the debut studio album by American singer Curtis Harding, released May 6, 2014, by Burger Records. It encompasses a wide breadth of genres, though it is primarily inspired by soul music and his mother's gospel singing. The album received generally favorable reviews from music critics.

Georgy Vasilyevich Pigulevsky (Lang-ru; 10 December [O.S. 28 November] 1888 – 19 September 1964) was a soviet organic chemist, specializing in natural product chemistry. He studied essential oils and resins of plants, as well as terpene compounds extracted from them.

Antasena-class combat boat is a combat boat built by North Sea Boats and designed by LOMOcean Design for the Indonesian armed forces. The manufacturer designated it as Combat Boat X-18 / CB X-18. Initially, this class of ships was developed from a tank boat program from a consortium consisting of PT Lundin (North Sea Boats), PT Pindad, PT LEN, and PT Hariff Daya Tunggal Engineering, with John Cockerill as the turret supplier.

Andrew Keary (Irish: Aindriú Ó Ciara; born 1987) is an Irish former hurler who played as a left wing-forward. He achieved success with Galway's minor and under-21 teams, winning multiple All-Ireland titles.

The Church of Nossa Senhora do Rosário (Lang-pt) is a 16th-century Azorean temple in the civil parish of Nossa Senhora do Rosário, municipality of Calheta, being reconstructed after the Mandado de Deus earthquake that affected the islands of São Jorge and Pico.

GABA transporter type 2 (GAT2) is a protein involved in the transport of gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) and taurine across cell membranes. It is expressed in various tissues, including the brain, liver, and kidneys, though its physiological roles remain partially understood. In the brain, GAT2 facilitates the efflux of GABA and taurine through the blood-brain barrier, but it has a lower affinity for GABA compared to GAT1 and GAT3, which are more influential in neurotransmitter activity. In the liver, GAT2 is primarily involved in taurine transport and may play a role in GABA uptake from the intestine. In the kidneys, it is localized to the basolateral membranes of proximal tubules, though its function is unclear. While GAT2's clinical significance is undetermined, GABA transporters are linked to epilepsy and other neurological disorders.

Raymond Cannon (born Ulises Tildmann Cannon;: 270 September 1, 1892 – June 7, 1977) was an American actor, film director, screenwriter, journalist, and author known for his work with D. W. Griffith and Buster Keaton.
Thomas Grantham (1634–1692) was an English General Baptist minister, and theologian. He had access to Charles II of England, and made petitions on behalf of Baptist beliefs.

Alliance française or AF (Lang-fr, IPA-fr; The French Alliance), is an international organization that aims to promote the French language and francophone culture around the world. Created in Paris on 21 July 1883 under the name Alliance française pour la propagation de la langue nationale dans les colonies et à l'étranger (French alliance for the propagation of the national language in the colonies and abroad), known now simply as L'Alliance française, its primary goal is teaching French as a second language. Headquartered in Paris, the Alliance had 850 centers in 137 countries on every inhabited continent in 2014.

The 1952 Hyderabad State Legislative Assembly election was the first election held in Hyderabad State after its integration into India. The Indian National Congress won a majority, securing 93 of 175 seats with 41.86% of the vote. The People's Democratic Front, representing communists, won all seats in Nalgonda district. Following the States Reorganisation Act of 1956, Hyderabad State was merged into Andhra Pradesh.

The Aeroprakt A-22 Foxbat is a Ukrainian light aircraft designed and manufactured by Aeroprakt in Kyiv. Development began in 1990, with the first prototype flying on October 21, 1996, and production starting in 1999. The aircraft features a mostly metal structure with fabric-covered wings and control surfaces, and its large glazed cockpit provides excellent visibility. It uses a 3-axis control system with flaperons, enabling a low stall speed of 52 km/h (32 mph). The A-22 is powered by various engine options, including the Rotax 912 series, and offers dual control configurations.
The Dartmouth Jack-O-Lantern (also known as the Jacko) is a college humor magazine, founded at Dartmouth College in 1908.

Echo Lawn Estate, also known as Stonegate after the Great War, is a historic estate located at Balmville in Orange County, New York. The main house was built about 1860 and is a two-story brick dwelling in the Second Empire style. It features sweeping concave mansard-type roofs. Also on the property is a cluster of mid-19th-century service buildings, an early 20th-century formal garden, and a substantial set of Arts and Crafts inspired gateposts and stone walls.

It was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 2009.

Entrenching battalions were temporary units formed in the armies of the British Empire during the First World War. Entrenching Battalions were trained as infantry, but were primarily utilized for manual labour duties such as trench repair, wire laying, road making and assisting tunnellers, pioneers, engineers and signals. The Battalions were also used as pools of men from which drafts of replacements could be drawn by conventional infantry battalions.

In various science/engineering applications, such as independent component analysis, image analysis, genetic analysis, speech recognition, manifold learning, and time delay estimation it is useful to estimate the differential entropy of a system or process, given some observations.

The simplest and most common approach uses histogram-based estimation, but other approaches have been developed and used, each with its own benefits and drawbacks. The main factor in choosing a method is often a trade-off between the bias and the variance of the estimate, although the nature of the (suspected) distribution of the data may also be a factor.

The red flour beetle (Tribolium castaneum) is a species of beetle in the family Tenebrionidae, the darkling beetles. It is a worldwide pest of stored products, particularly food grains, and a model organism for ethological and food safety research.

The S9 is a line of the Zürich S-Bahn, operated by the Zürcher Verkehrsverbund (ZVV) in Switzerland. It connects Schaffhausen and Uster, serving 23 stations, including Zürich Hauptbahnhof. Operated with Re 450 locomotives and double-deck coaches, it runs every 30 minutes, with extended service during peak times.

The National Trust for Scotland for Places of Historic Interest or Natural Beauty, commonly known as the National Trust for Scotland (Lang-gd), is a Scottish conservation organisation. It is the largest membership organisation in Scotland and describes itself as "the conservation charity that protects and promotes Scotland's natural and cultural heritage for present and future generations to enjoy".

Rod Diridon Sr. (born February 8, 1939) is an American politician known for his leadership in mass transportation. He served as chair of the California High-Speed Rail Authority from 2001-2003. He currently chairs the advisory board of the United States High Speed Rail Association, and he chairs the National Heritage Area Project for Santa Clara County, California. The New York Times calls Diridon "a tireless advocate of public transport for the Bay Area."

Diridon has also been active in Silicon Valley in leadership on environmental issues, the arts and culture, higher education, community organizations, and philanthropy.

Hostile vehicle mitigation (HVM) is a generic term that covers a suite of anti-terrorist protective measures that are often employed around buildings or publicly accessible spaces/venues of particular significance. The design of these various vehicle security barriers and landscape treatments came about as security authorities across the globe sought to mitigate the effects of vehicle borne improvised explosive devices (VBIED). The sorts of places that warrant consideration as potential terrorist targets in need of HVM include: government buildings, airports, large railway stations, sports venues, concentrations of entertainment and crowded night time economy, etc.

MyPertamina is a digital financial service platform from Pertamina that integrated with the apps LinkAja. This application is used for non-cash fuel oil payments at Pertamina's public fueling stations.

A lord proprietor is a person granted a royal charter for the establishment and government of an English colony in the 17th century. The plural of the term is "lords proprietors" or "lords proprietary".

Directory-based cache coherence is a mechanism used in multiprocessor systems to maintain consistency between cached copies of shared memory. It employs a directory to track the state of cache lines across processors. Various formats, including full bit vector, coarse bit vector, sparse directory, number-balanced binary tree, chained directory, and limited pointer, optimize storage and performance by balancing scalability, memory overhead, and communication efficiency.

The Additional Civil Lord of the Admiralty was a position on the Board of Admiralty in the United Kingdom, established in 1882 and held by individuals who were neither naval officers nor politicians. Initially created to assist with shipbuilding and dockyard management, the role was abolished in 1885 but reinstated in 1912. During World War I, additional civil lords were appointed to manage the increasing workload, with responsibilities including ship repairs and procurement. The position was permanently abolished in 1919.

Comproportionation or synproportionation is a chemical reaction where two reactants containing the same element but with different oxidation numbers, form a compound having an intermediate oxidation number. It is the opposite of disproportionation.

The shaking tent ceremony is a traditional Indigenous ritual practiced by various tribes across Canada and the northern United States, including the Algonquin, Ojibwe, Cree, and Blackfeet. Conducted by a shaman, the ceremony involves summoning spirits into a cylindrical tent to seek guidance, knowledge, or healing. The tent shakes violently as spirits communicate with the shaman, who relays their messages to the tribe. Historically used for purposes such as curing illnesses or resolving mysteries, the ceremony remains a significant cultural practice, though its methods and interpretations vary among tribes. It emphasizes spiritual connection, community identity, and ancestral traditions.
Sergio Ferrer Marrero (born January 29, 1951) is a former Major League Baseball shortstop.

The 2017 Ole Miss Rebels football team represented the University of Mississippi in the 2017 NCAA Division I FBS football season. The Rebels played their home games at Vaught–Hemingway Stadium in Oxford, Mississippi and competed in the Western Division of the Southeastern Conference (SEC). They were led by then-interim head coach Matt Luke. They finished the season 6–6, 3–5 in SEC play to finish in sixth place in the Western Division.

On February 22, 2017, the University of Mississippi self-imposed a one-year ban from post-season play on the Ole Miss football team for the 2017 season, due to alleged NCAA violations. On July 20, 2017, head coach Hugh Freeze resigned in the wake of allegations about repeated calls to a female escort service from his school-issued cell phone. He was given the ultimatum, either resign or be fired.

Vicente Greco (1888–1924) was an Argentine tango musician, composer, and bandleader, credited with forming the first *orquesta típica*, a traditional tango orchestra. A self-taught bandoneonist, he composed influential tangos like "Rodríguez Peña" and "El flete," and collaborated with Carlos Gardel.

Hasan Arat is a Turkish sports administrator, businessman, and former professional basketball player. He led Istanbul's bid for the 2020 Olympics, holds prominent roles in international sports organizations, and chairs various business ventures.

Lloyd John Quinan (born 1957) is a Scottish broadcaster and pro-independence politician. He was a Scottish National Party (SNP) Member of the Scottish Parliament (MSP) for the West of Scotland region from 1999 to 2003.

Born and raised in Edinburgh, Quinan is a longtime campaigner for Scottish independence, initially joining the Scottish National Party in 1974, later leaving in 2003. In 2015 the SNP selected him as a list candidate for the Lothian region.

Massachusetts Route 225 is a 31.1-mile (50.1 km) state highway running through Worcester and Middlesex counties. Established in 1957, it begins at Route 2A in Lunenburg and ends at Route 2 in Lexington. The route passes through several towns, including Groton, Westford, Carlisle, and Bedford, featuring notable intersections with Route 119, Route 4, and I-95. It was renumbered from Route 25 following the construction of Interstate 495.

Henry Stuart Russell (1818 – 5 March 1889) was an English-born Australian explorer, pastoralist, and politician. He is known for his exploration of Queensland, including the discovery of the Mary and Boyne Rivers, and for his book *The Genesis of Queensland* (1888), which documented the region's early settlement and pastoral industry. Russell also served in the New South Wales Legislative Council and held various pastoral interests before returning to England later in life.

True Liberty is a Christian hardcore band from Richmond, Virginia, formed in 2005. The band consists of lead vocalist and guitarist Mike Liberty, bassist Tom Liberty, and drummer and background vocalist Aaron Liberty. Known for their blend of Christian hardcore, hardcore punk, and punk rock, True Liberty has released several albums through various labels, including Raging Storm Records, Wounded Records, and Thumper Punk Records. Their discography includes *This Is War* (2007), *Brotherhood* (2008), *City of the One* (2010), and *Marked for Life* (2012). They also released a live album, *Give Me True Liberty or Give Me Death*, in 2014. The band remains active, continuing to contribute to the Christian punk and hardcore music scene.

Exabyte Corporation was an American data storage company founded in 1985 in Boulder, Colorado, by Juan Rodriguez, Harry Hinz, Kelly Beavers, and former StorageTek engineers. The company pioneered the use of consumer videotape technology for data storage and introduced the Data8 magnetic tape format in 1987, which advanced computer backup technology. Exabyte went public on NASDAQ in 1989 under the symbol EXBT. In 2001, it merged with Ecrix, a company co-founded by two of Exabyte's original founders, gaining access to Ecrix's VXA Packet Technology. Exabyte was acquired by Tandberg Data in 2006.

Entrenching battalions were temporary units formed during World War I by the British, Canadian, and New Zealand armies to perform labor-intensive tasks such as improving defenses and serving as reserve forces. The British Army established them in 1916, disbanding them in 1918 after the German spring offensive. The Canadian Expeditionary Force created similar units in 1916, disbanding them in 1917, while New Zealand formed entrenching battalions in 1918, disbanding them later that year.

*Two from Galilee* is a 1972 Christian novel by Marjorie Holmes that fictionalizes the love story of Mary and Joseph, culminating in the birth of Jesus. Despite mixed critical reception, it became a bestseller and inspired a musical adaptation performed in churches.
London Bourne (1793–1869) was a former Barbadian slave who became a wealthy merchant and abolitionist.

Fuxi (伏羲), a prominent figure in Chinese mythology, is regarded as one of the Three Sovereigns and a cultural hero. Often depicted with a human face and a snake's body, he is said to have been born alongside his sister Nüwa and played a key role in the creation of humanity. Fuxi is credited with introducing marriage, cooking, fishing, hunting, and the I Ching trigrams. He is also associated with the invention of calligraphy and the Guqin musical instrument.

Air Marshal Sir Peter Edward Bairsto, KBE, CB, AFC, DL (3 August 1926 – 24 October 2017) was a Royal Air Force officer who served as Deputy Commander of Strike Command from 1981 until his retirement in 1984.

Paul Lawrence Smith (June 24, 1936 – April 25, 2012) was an American-Israeli actor. Burly, bearded and imposing, he appeared in feature films and occasionally on television since the 1970s, generally playing "heavies" and bad guys. His most notable roles include Hamidou, the vicious prison guard in Midnight Express (1978), Bluto in Robert Altman's Popeye (1980), Gideon in the ABC miniseries Masada (1981), Glossu Rabban in David Lynch's Dune (1984) and Falkon in Red Sonja (1985).
Robert C. Morgan is an American art historian, critic, curator, and artist known for his contributions to conceptual and post-conceptual art, as well as his extensive academic career.

An Ziwen (Chinese: 安子文; September 25, 1909 – June 25, 1980) was a Chinese Communist revolutionary and politician who held key roles in the Chinese Communist Party (CCP). He served as head of the CCP Central Organization Department from 1956 to 1966 and as deputy secretary of the Central Commission for Discipline Inspection. An participated in the Second Sino-Japanese War and the Chinese Civil War. Persecuted during the Cultural Revolution, he was rehabilitated in 1978 under Deng Xiaoping and later served as vice president of the Central Party School.

Arkady Adamovich Brish (May 14, 1917 – March 19, 2016) was a Soviet and Russian physicist, a designer of nuclear weapons, Doctor of Sciences, professor, Hero of Socialist Labour, Laureate of the Lenin Prize and the USSR State Prize.

John A. Lafore Jr. (May 25, 1905 – January 24, 1993) was an American politician, businessman, and dog breeder. A Republican, he served in the Pennsylvania House of Representatives (1950–1957) and the U.S. House of Representatives (1957–1961).

The Jamaat al Muslimeen coup attempt was an armed insurrection in Trinidad and Tobago from July 27 to August 1, 1990, led by Yasin Abu Bakr and the Jamaat al Muslimeen. The group stormed the Red House, the seat of Parliament, taking Prime Minister A. N. R. Robinson and other officials hostage, while also seizing a television station. The coup resulted in 24 deaths, widespread looting, and significant property damage. After six days, the insurgents surrendered under an amnesty agreement. The event exposed political, economic, and security vulnerabilities in the country, leading to long-term investigations and reforms.

Cycling in Prague has evolved significantly over time, transitioning from a city with minimal cycling infrastructure to one with growing support for cyclists. The first bicycle lanes appeared in 1956, but modern cycling paths, such as the one in Stromovka park, were only introduced in 1986. Following the Velvet Revolution, car traffic surged, but leisure cycling gained popularity, and plans for extensive cycling routes were initiated, though many were not completed. Prague now features bike-sharing systems, with several operators active as of 2022. Bicycles are also accommodated on public transport, including the metro, ferries, and select buses.

Kirkthorpe Hydro is a hydroelectric power station located on the River Calder in Kirkthorpe, Wakefield, England. Built on the grade II listed Kirkthorpe Weir, the facility was constructed by Barn Energy at a cost of £5.3 million and became operational in March 2017. It has a capacity of 500 kW, generating 2.3 million units of electricity annually, enough to power over 800 homes. The site includes a fish pass, eel pass, and a 1,200 kWh lithium-ion battery for energy storage. In 2019, Kirkthorpe Hydro was sold to JLEN as part of a £4.3 million deal.
Live in Boston (a.k.a. Boston Live and Jumping at Shadows) is a live album by British blues-rock band Fleetwood Mac that was first released in 1985.

The supine is a grammatical form found in various languages, often derived from a verbal noun and used to express purpose, respect, or other functions. In Latin, there are two supines: the first, ending in *-tum*, is used with verbs of motion or to indicate fate, while the second, ending in *-ū*, is used with adjectives to express respect or extent. Similar forms exist in Sanskrit, Germanic, Finnic, Romance, Slavic, and Baltic languages, with varying uses and functions.
Year of the Gun is a 1991 American thriller film directed by John Frankenheimer and starring Andrew McCarthy, Sharon Stone and Valeria Golino.

Essert-Pittet is a former municipality in the canton of Vaud, Switzerland, located in the district of Jura-Nord Vaudois. First mentioned in 1100, it covered an area of 2.8 km², primarily used for agriculture. With a population of 164 in 2015, the municipality featured a mix of residential and agricultural life. Essert-Pittet was part of the Yverdon District until 2006, when it joined Jura-Nord Vaudois. The local economy included agriculture, construction, and education, while the population predominantly spoke French and adhered to the Swiss Reformed Church.

Clyde Lee Chambliss Jr. (born April 16, 1969) is an American politician serving as a Republican member of the Alabama Senate from the 30th district since 2014. In 2019, he sponsored the Human Life Protection Act, a controversial abortion law banning most abortions in Alabama, with exceptions only for cases where the mother’s health is endangered.

Cipriano di Michele Piccolpasso (1524 – 21 November 1579) was a member of an Italian patrician family of Bologna that had been settled since the mid-fifteenth century in Castel Durante, which was an important center for the manufacture of maiolica. Today he is remembered for writing Li tre libri dell'arte del vasajo ("The three books of the potter's art"), which are a storehouse of information on the techniques of maiolica from the choice of clays and their refinement, the shaping of the body, the composition of the glazes, to the preparation of the colors. The work"is now widely accepted as the first comprehensive account of the manufacture of any kind of pottery ever produced in Europe".

His brother operated a maiolica workshop, but it is not clear how much hands-on potting experience Cipriano himself had. He mentions that he had never used lustreware pigments, which might imply that he had used other types. His account of workshop techniques is in places unclear and hard to follow, either because he did not understand the process himself, or had difficulty forming a written description. The date of the book is uncertain, but most writers follow Bernard Rackham who suggested it (or at least the manuscript in the Victoria and Albert Museum) was written between around 1556 and 1559.
Joseph Buck (born December 10, 2002) is an American soccer player who plays as a midfielder for Georgetown Hoyas.

Leif Carlsen was Danish football forward. During his career he played for Boldklubben Frem, Hvidovre IF, and Landskrona BoIS.

Carlsen was born in 1943 in Denmark, and died in 2018.
The Fury of the Aquabats! is the second studio album by American rock band the Aquabats, released on October 28, 1997 by Goldenvoice Records and Time Bomb Recordings.

The 1975 Council of Ministers of East Timor was the inaugural cabinet of the Democratic Republic of East Timor, formed by Fretilin on 1 December 1975 following its unilateral declaration of independence from Portuguese rule. Led by President Francisco Xavier do Amaral and Prime Minister Nicolau dos Reis Lobato, the council was short-lived, as Indonesia launched a full-scale invasion on 7 December 1975, leading to the establishment of a Provisional Government under Indonesian control.

Zoya Yunoevna Semenduyeva (Lang-ru; Lang-he; born 20 October 1929 – 9 April 2020) was a Soviet and Israeli poet. She wrote in a language of the Mountain Jew (Juhuri). She was a member of Dagestan Writers' Union.

Robert N. Bellah (February 23, 1927 – July 30, 2013) was an American sociologist and influential scholar of religion, best known for coining the term "American civil religion" and for his works *Habits of the Heart* (1985) and *Religion in Human Evolution* (2011). A Harvard graduate and professor at the University of California, Berkeley, Bellah explored the intersection of religion, morality, and society, earning numerous accolades, including the National Humanities Medal. His career was marked by significant contributions to the sociology of religion and communitarian thought.

Building officials of developed countries are generally the jurisdictional administrator of building and construction codes, engineering calculation supervision, permits, facilities management, and accepted construction procedures.
Satannish is a powerful Marvel Comics demon, created by Roy Thomas and Gene Colan.

A handscroll (Chinese: 手捲) is a traditional East Asian art format used for paintings and texts, designed to be viewed horizontally by unrolling it section by section. Originating in ancient China, handscrolls were initially used for text documents on bamboo or wooden slips during the Spring and Autumn period (770–481 BCE) and later adapted for paper and silk by the Eastern Han period (25–220 CE). They became a standard format for paintings by the Three Kingdoms period (220–280). Handscrolls were introduced to Japan through Buddhism, where they evolved into emakimono, often depicting narrative themes.

Ariyalur (IPA-ta) is a town and district headquarters of Ariyalur district in the South Indian state of Tamil Nadu and is rich in limestone, surrounded with seven cement factories and two sugar factories. The town is located at a distance of 310 km (190 mi) from the state capital Chennai.

Ariyalur was a part of the erstwhile Trichirapally District until India's independence in 1947 and Tiruchirappalli district until 1995, Perambalur district until 2007 and subsequently a part of the newly formed Ariyalur district. The town is a part of the fertile Cauvery Delta and the major profession in the town is agriculture.

Ariyalur is administered by a municipality established in 1994. As of 2011, the municipality covered an area of 7.62 km2 (2.94 sq mi) and had a population of 28,902. Ariyalur comes under the Ariyalur assembly constituency which elects a member to the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly once every five years and it is a part of the Chidambaram constituency which elects its Member of Parliament (MP) once in five years. Roadways are the major mode of transportation to the town and it also has rail connectivity which acts as a main station after Tiruchirappalli junction. The nearest seaport is Karaikal port, located 95 km (59 mi) away, while the nearest airport is the Tiruchirappalli International Airport, located 76 km (47 mi) away from the town.

Rosa Amelia Guzmán (later Rosa Amelia Guzmán de Araujo) was a Salvadoran feminist, activist, and politician who played a key role in advancing women's rights in El Salvador. She co-founded the journal *Tribuna Femenina* and the Salvadoran Feminist League, advocating for women's suffrage and legal equality. In 1956, she became one of the first female deputies in El Salvador's Legislative Assembly.
Duško Tarbuk (Serbian Cyrillic: Душко Тарбук; born 21 August 1972) is a politician in Serbia. He has served in the National Assembly of Serbia since 2016 as a member of the Serbian Progressive Party.

Major General عبدالقیوم طوطاخیل (Abdul Qayum Tutakhail or Totakhil) was born in Sayed-Karam district of Paktia province Afghanistan. He was Assistant Minister of Defense for Health Affairs in Afghanistan. Prior to this General Tutakhail served as Surgeon General of the Afghan National Army and as a faculty lecturer of AFAMS teaching Combat Surgery, Tactics, Organization and Medical Ethics. Since the 1970s General Tutakhail has served in the Afghan Military in different capacities having a distinguished career. He is an ethnic Pashtun from the southern Paktia province.

George Frederick Blanda (September 17, 1927 – September 27, 2010) was an American football placekicker and quarterback who played professionally in the American Football League (AFL) and National Football League (NFL). Blanda played 26 seasons of professional football, the most in the sport's history, and had scored more points than anyone in history at the time of his retirement.

Blanda retired from pro football in August 1976 as the oldest player to ever play at the age of 48. One of only two players to play in four different decades (the other being John Carney), he holds the record for most extra points made (943) and attempted (959). During his career, he played under head coaches Bear Bryant, George Halas, Clem Crowe, Lou Rymkus, Wally Lemm, Pop Ivy, Sammy Baugh, Hugh Taylor, John Rauch, and John Madden.

The Tikhvinskaya water system was a historic waterway in Russia, connecting the Volga River to St. Petersburg via a network of rivers, lakes, and canals. Spanning 902 kilometers, it included the Tikhvin Canal and several locks and sluices, facilitating navigation through the region. Proposed multiple times in the 18th century, its construction was approved by Alexander I in 1802 following a severe drought that disrupted other waterways. Today, the system is in a state of disrepair, with destroyed gates, reduced water levels, and pollution, while architectural monuments along its route have fallen into neglect.

The Limestone Coast is a name used since the early twenty-first century for a South Australian government region located in the south east of South Australia which immediately adjoins the continental coastline and the Victorian border. The name is also used for a tourist region and a wine zone both located in the same part of South Australia.

The Charleston Dirty Birds are an American professional baseball team based in Charleston, West Virginia. They are a member of the South Division of the Atlantic League of Professional Baseball, a partner league of Major League Baseball. The Dirty Birds have played their home games at GoMart Ballpark since 2005.
Crystina Poncher (born July 20, 1984) is currently a commentator, host and reporter for Top Rank Boxing on ESPN. She previously served as host, reporter and correspondent for NFL Network and NFL.com.

The Katkari also called Kathodi, are an Indian tribe from Maharashtra. They have been categorised as a Scheduled tribe. They are bilingual, speaking the Katkari language, a dialect of the Marathi-Konkani languages, with each other; they speak Marathi with the Marathi speakers, who are a majority in the populace where they live. In Maharashtra the Katkari have been designated a Particularly vulnerable tribal group (PVTG), along with two other groups included in this sub-category: the Madia Gond and the Kolam. In the case of the Katkari this vulnerability derives from their history as a nomadic, forest-dwelling people listed by the British Raj under the Criminal Tribes Act of 1871, a stigma that continues to this day.

Vesna Škare-Ožbolt (born 20 June 1961) is a Croatian politician and former Minister of Justice, serving from 2003 to 2006 in the cabinet of Prime Minister Ivo Sanader. A lawyer by profession, she played a key role in the peaceful reintegration of Eastern Slavonia into Croatia during the 1990s. Initially a member of the Croatian Democratic Union (HDZ), she later joined the Democratic Centre (DC), becoming its leader. As Minister of Justice, she pursued judicial reforms, including efforts to digitalize land registries. After her dismissal in 2006, she ran unsuccessfully in the 2009–10 Croatian presidential election.
1980 in Japan saw notable achievements in film, manga, music, sports, and international events like Miss International.

KNBN (channel 21), branded as NewsCenter 1, is a television station in Rapid City, South Dakota, affiliated with NBC and MyNetworkTV. Owned by Rapid Broadcasting Company, it began broadcasting on May 14, 2000, after securing the NBC affiliation for the area. KNBN produces 16 hours of local newscasts weekly and operates several subchannels. The station transitioned to digital broadcasting in 2009. Its history includes the acquisition of a low-power station in 1996 and the launch of its full news service in 1997.

A zograscope is an optical device for magnifying flat pictures that also has the property of enhancing the sense of the depth shown in the picture. It consists of a large magnifying lens through which the picture is viewed. Devices containing only the lens are sometimes referred to as graphoscopes. Other models have the lens mounted on a stand in front of an angled mirror. This allows someone to sit at a table and to look through the lens at the picture flat on the table. Pictures viewed in this way need to be left-right reversed; this is obvious in the case of writing. A print made for this purpose, typically with extensive graphical projection perspective, is called a vue d'optique or "perspective view".

Zograscopes were popular during the later half of the 18th century as parlour entertainments. Most existing ones from that time are fine furniture, with turned stands, mouldings, brass fittings, and fine finishes.

According to Michael Quinion, the origin of the term is lost, but it is also known as a diagonal mirror, as an optical pillar machine, or as an optical diagonal machine.

In Japan, the zograscope became known as 和蘭眼鏡 (Oranda megane, 'Dutch glasses') or 覗き眼鏡 (nozoki megane 'peeping glasses'), and the pictures were known as 眼鏡絵 (megane-e, 'optique picture') 繰絵 (karakuri-e 'tricky picture').

The 2014 Mountain Province Bus accident was a bus accident that occurred when a Florida Trans bus fell off a road in Sitio Paggang, Talubin, Bontoc, Mountain Province of the Philippines. The accident occurred on February 7, 2014. Fourteen people were killed and more than 32 people were injured. Comedian Tado and two more artists died in this accident.

Alexis Cohen (October 17, 1983 – July 5, 2009) was a two-time reality TV show contestant and singer on American Idol who directed an expletive-filled televised rant at the show's judges after comparing her singing style to vocalists Grace Slick, Janis Joplin, and Pat Benatar. She was called "Glitter Girl" in the media.

The slender-billed gull (Chroicocephalus genei) is a mid-sized gull which breeds very locally around the Mediterranean and the north of the western Indian Ocean (e.g. Pakistan) on islands and coastal lagoons. Most of the population is somewhat migratory, wintering further south to as far as North Africa and India. A few birds have wandered to western Europe. A vagrant individual was reportedly seen on Antigua, April 24, 1976 (AOU, 2000).

The genus name Chroicocephalus is from Ancient Greek khroizo, "to colour", and kephale, "head". The specific genei commemorates Italian naturalist Giuseppe Gené.

Mongolia competed at the 2004 Summer Olympics in Athens, Greece, from August 13 to 29, with a delegation of 20 athletes participating in seven sports. The team was led by flag bearer Damdinsürengiin Nyamkhüü. Mongolia won one bronze medal, placing 71st in the overall medal rankings. 

The nation’s sole medal was earned in judo by Khashbaataryn Tsagaanbaatar, who competed in the lightweight division. Tsagaanbaatar advanced to the semifinals but lost to his Japanese opponent in under thirty seconds. In the bronze medal match, he secured victory during an additional period, marking Mongolia's only podium finish at the Games. 

Mongolia also competed in athletics, boxing, shooting, and weightlifting, with judo being the largest contingent, featuring eight athletes. The 2004 Games continued Mongolia's consistent participation in the Summer Olympics since its debut in 1964. The Mongolian National Olympic Committee oversaw the team’s participation.

Hannah Mary Bardell (born 1 June 1983) is a Scottish politician who has served as Member of Parliament (MP) for Livingston since 2015. A member of the Scottish National Party (SNP), she served as its spokesperson for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport from 2018 to 2019.

Wrocław Global Forum (WGF) is an annual transatlantic conference centered on topics related to democracy, prosperity and security. Launched in 2010, the event is held in Wrocław, Poland. It is organized by the Atlantic Council, the Polish Institute of International Affairs and the city of Wrocław. The most recent Forum took place on June 2–3, 2016.

Svalikhita-jivani is an autobiography written in 1896 by Kedarnatha Datta (also known as Bhaktivinoda Thakur), a prominent thinker of Bengali Renaissance and a leading philosopher, savant and spiritual reformer of Gaudiya Vaishnavism who effected its resurgence in India in late 19th and early 20th century and was hailed by contemporary scholars as the most influential Gaudiya Vaisnava leader of his time.

Written in Bengali on the request of Bhaktivinoda's son Lalita Prasad, Svalikhita-jivani gives a detailed autobiographical account that spanned most of his life from his birth in 1838 until retirement in 1894. The book was published by Lalita Prasad in 1916, after Bhaktivinoda's death.

*Vijenac* is a Croatian biweekly magazine focused on literature, arts, and science, published by Matica hrvatska since 1993. It is considered the successor to the 19th-century literary magazine *Vienac*.

William Henry Belpitt (May 3, 1860 – November 29, 1912) was an Australian-born sailor in the United States Navy who received the Medal of Honor for rescuing a man from drowning in Fuzhou, China, in 1884.

Marcq-en-Barœul (IPA-fr; Lang-pcd; older Lang-nl) is a commune in the Nord department in the Hauts-de-France region in northern France.

It is a suburb of the city of Lille, and is immediately adjacent to it on the northeast. It is the sixth-largest component of the Métropole Européenne de Lille. Marcq-en-Barœul is known as the most affluent suburb city of Lille, it is part of the 'Triangle d'or' (Golden triangle) of Lille. It features expensive real estate, especially alongside the Grand Boulevard and Hippodrome des Flandres racecourse. The city is also home to one of the campuses of the elite international private collège and lycée active bilingue Jeannine-Manuel.

Hanauer Straße is a street in Munich, Germany, extending from Dachauer Straße near Westfriedhof and crossing Georg-Brauchle-Ring. Historically, it housed the Moosach gasworks and the 1972 Olympic press city. Today, it features cultural monuments, major retail centers like the Olympia-Einkaufszentrum, and public transport connections, including underground stations and tram stops. Future developments include residential neighborhoods and office spaces. Hanauer Straße was the site of the 2016 Munich shooting, commemorated by a memorial honoring the victims.

The Maas–Hoffman model is a mathematical tool to characterize the relation between crop production and soil salinity. It describes the crop response by a broken line of which the first part is horizontal and the second is sloping downward. The breakpoint (Pb) or threshold is also called tolerance because up to that point the yield is unaffected by the salinity, so the salt is tolerated, while at greater salinity values the crops are affected negatively and the yield goes down.

*Kachche Heere* is a 1982 Hindi-language film directed and written by Narender Bedi and produced by Netra Pal Singh. The film stars Feroz Khan, Reena Roy, Danny Denzongpa, Aruna Irani, and Shakti Kapoor in pivotal roles, with music composed by Rahul Dev Burman. Set in rural Madhya Pradesh, the story revolves around dacoits, feuds among the ruling class, and themes of redemption. It follows Feroz Khan's character, Ajnabi, as he uncovers his true identity and, alongside five reformed petty thieves, fights against the oppressive dacoit Lukka Singh to save a village.

The City of Lost Children (Lang-fr) is a 1995 science fantasy film directed by Marc Caro and Jean-Pierre Jeunet, written by Jeunet and Gilles Adrien, and starring Ron Perlman. An international co-production of companies from France, Germany, and Spain, the film is stylistically related to the previous and subsequent Jeunet films, Delicatessen and Amélie.

The musical score was composed by Angelo Badalamenti, with costumes designed by Jean-Paul Gaultier. It was entered into the 1995 Cannes Film Festival.

Henry Nicholas John Gunther (June 6, 1895 – November 11, 1918) was an American soldier and possibly the last soldier of any of the belligerents to be killed during World War I. He was killed at 10:59 a.m., about one minute before the Armistice was to take effect at 11:00 a.m.

Gunther had recently been demoted, and was seeking to regain his rank just before the war ended.

The Siege of Ypres (1383) was a key event during Despenser's Crusade and the Revolt of Ghent (1379–1385). Lasting from June 8 to August 8, 1383, the siege saw English forces, led by Henry le Despenser, and their Ghent allies unsuccessfully attempt to capture the Flemish city of Ypres, which remained loyal to France. Despite sustained attacks and artillery bombardments, the city's well-prepared defenses, led by John d'Oultre, withstood the siege until a French relief army forced the attackers to withdraw. The siege marked a turning point in the Revolt of Ghent and contributed to Ypres's long-term economic decline.

Pudliszki Sp. z o.o. is a brand and producer in the food industry of fruit and vegetables. Presently, the brand is under the American company Heinz, which holds considerable shares in the company. Pudliszki focuses on the production of tomato goods (ketchup and concentrate) and vegetables (peas, beans and corn).

Deryan Aktert, chairman of Dicle district for the Justice and Development Party (AKP), was shot by a group of seven or eight people at a gas station owned by him in Dicle on 10 October 2016, around 22:35 TSİ.

Hualing Nieh Engle (born 11 January 1925), née Nieh Hua-ling (Chinese: 聶華苓; pinyin: Niè Huálíng; Wade–Giles: Nieh4 Hua2-ling2), is a Chinese novelist, fiction writer, and poet. She is a professor emerita at the University of Iowa.

A pressurized heavy-water reactor (PHWR) is a type of nuclear reactor that uses heavy water (deuterium oxide) as a neutron moderator and coolant, enabling the use of natural uranium as fuel. This design eliminates the need for expensive uranium enrichment facilities, making it more cost-effective in certain contexts. PHWRs, such as the CANDU reactor, are highly efficient due to their ability to moderate neutrons at lower temperatures, which increases the likelihood of fission reactions. They can also utilize a variety of fuels, including reprocessed uranium, spent nuclear fuel, and mixed oxide (MOX) fuel.

However, PHWRs have drawbacks, including the high cost of heavy water, increased spent fuel volume, and the production of tritium, a radioactive isotope. Additionally, their ability to produce weapons-grade plutonium poses a nuclear proliferation risk, as demonstrated by India's use of a heavy-water reactor to develop its first nuclear weapon. Despite these challenges, PHWRs remain a significant technology in nuclear energy production.

Michael St John Trend, CBE (born 19 April 1952) is a British former Conservative politician and journalist who was a member of parliament from 1992 to 2005, when he stood down after an expenses scandal.

Port Dock railway station – named Port Adelaide until 1916 – was located in the commercial centre of Port Adelaide, South Australia at the corner of St Vincent Street and Lipson Street. It was the original terminus of the railway between Adelaide and Port Adelaide, which opened in 1856.

After closure in 1981, the passenger station site was redeveloped as the Port Adelaide Police Station and Magistrates' Court. The former goods yard, adjacent to Lipson Street, is now occupied by the National Railway Museum. Several proposals have been advanced to build a new station, including a budgeted project by the government of South Australia in 2019, but as of 2021 none has proceeded to funding.

Alan Arnett McLeod, VC (20 April 1899 – 6 November 1918) was a Canadian soldier, aviator, and a recipient of the Victoria Cross, the highest award for gallantry in the face of the enemy that can be awarded to British and Commonwealth forces. McLeod served as a pilot in the Royal Flying Corps and later the Royal Air Force during the First World War.

Barry Ono, born Frederick Valentine Harrison (21 May 1876 – ?), was a British comedian, songwriter, and performer known for his one-man routine "The Old Time Music Hall." He gained prominence in 1929 and published works like *The Great Barry Ono Song Book*. Later in life, he became an avid collector of penny fiction and operated a lending library as a child.
Hillhead High School is a day school in Glasgow, Scotland, on Oakfield Avenue, neighbouring the University of Glasgow.

*Going Blank Again* is the second studio album by British shoegaze band Ride, released on 9 March 1992 through Creation and Sire Records. Produced by Alan Moulder and the band, it features ten tracks, including the singles "Leave Them All Behind" and "Twisterella." The album received critical acclaim for its layered vocals and shoegaze sound, and it has been reissued multiple times, including a 20th-anniversary edition in 2012.

Paleontology in New Hampshire explores the state's limited but notable fossil record, which spans from the Silurian period to the Quaternary. Due to the predominance of igneous and heavily metamorphosed rocks, New Hampshire lacks fossils from the early Paleozoic and Mesozoic eras. However, Silurian fossils, including brachiopods, bryozoans, corals, crinoids, and trilobites, have been discovered in the Fitch Formation near Littleton and other sites along the Ammonoosuc River. Devonian fossils, such as brachiopods, crinoids, and gastropods, have been found at locations like Tip Top Farm and Mormon Hill.

The state's fossil record is sparse for the Carboniferous, Permian, and Mesozoic due to extensive erosion and geological uplift. During the Quaternary, glaciers covered much of New Hampshire, leaving behind plant fossils, pollen, and remains of megafauna like woolly mammoths and mastodons. Notable discoveries include a mastodon fossil near the Isles of Shoals in 2013 and a mammoth tooth in 2014.

The study of New Hampshire's paleontology began in 1873 with C. H. Hitchcock's report on Silurian fossils. Later contributions include F. H. Lahee's documentation of Devonian fossils in the early 20th century and the discovery of a unique brachiopod-rich horizon in the Clough Formation in 1958. Despite its limited fossil record, New Hampshire provides valuable insights into ancient marine and glacial environments.

Nactus kunan is an extant species of slender-toe geckos described in 2012, and indigenous to the Admiralty Islands of Papua New Guinea. It is brightly coloured, and its specific name means "bumblebee" in the local Nali language.

**Kalopanax** is a genus of flowering plants in the family Araliaceae, with its sole species, *Kalopanax septemlobus*. Native to East Asia, it is a large deciduous tree growing up to 30 meters tall, with spiny stems and palmately lobed leaves. Cultivated as an ornamental tree for its tropical appearance, it is hardy to −40 °C. In Korea, its young shoots are consumed as a blanched dish.

Section 101 of the Constitution Act, 1867 (Lang-fr) is a provision of the Constitution of Canada giving the federal Parliament the power to create the Supreme Court of Canada and the federal courts. Although Parliament created the Supreme Court by an ordinary federal statute in 1875, the Court is partially entrenched by the amending formula set out in the Constitution Act, 1982. The composition of the Court can only be changed by a unanimous constitutional amendment, passed by the two houses of Parliament, and all of the provincial legislative assemblies.

The Constitution Act, 1867 is the constitutional statute which established Canada. Originally named the British North America Act, 1867, the Act continues to be the foundational statute for the Constitution of Canada, although it has been amended many times since 1867. It is now recognised as part of the supreme law of Canada.

Clyde Lee Chambliss Jr. (born April 16, 1969) is an American Republican politician who has served in the Alabama Senate from the 30th district since 2014. He completed his degree in Civil Engineering from the University of Alabama in 1992, after which he started his civil engineering firm. Chambliss and his wife Tara Herring Chambliss have three daughters, Kristen, Lauren, and Madison.

Vesna Györkös Žnidar (born 29 December 1977) is a Slovenian politician who was Minister of Interior in the Cabinet of Miro Cerar between 18 September 2014 and 13 September 2018. She is a member of the Modern Centre Party.

The Vidarbha movement is a long-standing demand for the creation of a separate state of Vidarbha, comprising the eastern region of Maharashtra, India. The demand dates back over a century, with the Central Provinces legislature passing a resolution for a separate "Mahavidarbha" state in 1938. Despite recommendations by the State Reorganization Commission in 1956 for a separate Vidarbha state, it was merged with Maharashtra in 1960. The movement has persisted due to perceived regional neglect, economic disparities, and political marginalization, with support from various political groups and organizations.

The Charleston Dirty Birds are a professional baseball team based in Charleston, West Virginia. Founded in 1987, they play in the Atlantic League of Professional Baseball, an independent MLB Partner League. The team, originally known as the Charleston Wheelers, has undergone several name changes and affiliations before adopting its current name in 2021. The Dirty Birds play home games at GoMart Ballpark, which opened in 2005. The name "Dirty Birds" references the canary in a coal mine, symbolizing the region's mining heritage. The franchise has a rich history of minor league baseball in Charleston, dating back to 1910.

The Secret Committee was a group of loyal psychoanalysts formed by Sigmund Freud in 1912 to safeguard psychoanalytic theory. Proposed by Ernest Jones, the committee initially included Jones, Sándor Ferenczi, Otto Rank, Hanns Sachs, and Karl Abraham, each receiving a golden ring symbolizing their commitment. Max Eitingon joined in 1919. The group aimed to address potential deviations from psychoanalytic principles and solidify Freud's inner circle, partly isolating Carl Jung. Despite functioning effectively for a decade, internal conflicts led to its dissolution in 1924. It was later reconstituted with Anna Freud replacing Rank, continuing its influence on psychoanalytic discourse.
Barrington J. Bayley (9 April 1937 – 14 October 2008) was a British science fiction writer, influential in the New Wave movement.

The Moldavian Autonomous Soviet Socialist Republic (Lang-ro, Moldovan Cyrillic: Република Аутономэ Советикэ Cочиалистэ Молдовеняскэ; Lang-uk), shortened to Moldavian ASSR, was an autonomous republic of the Ukrainian SSR between 12 October 1924 and 2 August 1940, encompassing the modern territory of Transnistria (today de jure in Moldova, but de facto functioning as an independent state; see Transnistria conflict) as well as much of the present-day Podilsk Raion of Ukraine. It was an artificial political creation inspired by the Bolshevik nationalities policy in the context of the loss of larger Bessarabia to Romania in April 1918. In such a manner, the Bolshevik leadership tried to radicalize pro-Soviet feelings in Bessarabia with a goal to return it in the presence of favorable conditions and creation of geopolitical "place d'armes" (bridgehead) to execute a breakthrough in the Balkan direction by projecting influence upon Romanian Bessarabia, which was eventually occupied and annexed in 1940 after the signing of the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact.

*Wally Bear and the NO! Gang* is a 1992 side-scrolling video game developed by American Game Cartridges and published by American Video Entertainment for the Nintendo Entertainment System. The game follows Wally Bear, a skateboarding bear, as he promotes anti-drug and alcohol messages while traveling to his uncle's party. Despite mixed reviews at release, the game gained attention for its educational themes. Wally Bear and related characters were later used by the National Clearinghouse for Alcohol and Drug Information (NCADI) for substance abuse prevention campaigns, evolving into the "Wally Bear and the KNOW Gang" initiative. The franchise was eventually retired in 2004 and replaced by the "Building Blocks for a Healthy Future" program.

The slender-billed gull (*Chroicocephalus genei*) is a medium-sized gull in the family Laridae, found in regions around the Mediterranean Sea and northwestern Indian Ocean. It breeds in lagoons and lakes, often forming colonies, and overwinters along coasts, estuaries, and bays. Recognizable by its pale grey body, white head, dark red bill, and long-necked appearance, it feeds on fish, marine invertebrates, and insects. The species is gregarious and nests on the ground, laying up to three eggs. Classified as Least Concern by the IUCN, it is protected under the AEWA agreement.
Washington Bogart Cooper (September 18, 1802 – March 30, 1888) was an American portrait painter, sometimes known as "the man of a thousand portraits".

Beach 90th Street station is an elevated station on the IND Rockaway Line of the New York City Subway, located in Rockaway Beach, Queens. Originally opened in 1880 by the Long Island Rail Road, it became a subway station in 1956. The station has two side platforms and two tracks, with exits to Rockaway Freeway and Beach 90th Street. It served as a temporary terminal for the H shuttle after Hurricane Sandy until full service was restored in 2013.

Age of War is the debut studio album by Pakistani progressive rock band Black Hour, released on 25 October 2011 in the United States by Yourlabel Records, and in Pakistan by Afterwind Records. It was reissued in 2020 as a remastered version by Rearts Records. The recording sessions occurred at the 11/8 Studios in Islamabad, Pakistan with production by Black Hour alongside Jonathan Jones. The album sold over 10,000 copies worldwide and helped the band gain success both locally and internationally.

Phenoxyethanol is a colorless, oily liquid with germicidal and preservative properties, widely used in cosmetics, pharmaceuticals, and organic synthesis. It is produced via hydroxyethylation of phenol and is effective against bacteria and yeast.

WTRF-TV (channel 7) is a television station licensed to Wheeling, West Virginia, United States, serving the Wheeling, West Virginia–Steubenville, Ohio market as an affiliate of CBS, MyNetworkTV, and ABC. The station is owned by Nexstar Media Group and maintains studios on 16th Street in downtown Wheeling; its transmitter is located in Bridgeport, Ohio.

WTRF-TV was the first station to specifically serve Wheeling and Steubenville. It was originally an NBC affiliate before switching to CBS in January 1980. In the 2000s, it expanded to add two new subchannel services.

Kerry Condon (born 9 January 1983) is an Irish actress. She is best known for her role as Octavia of the Julii in Rome, Stacey Ehrmantraut in Better Call Saul, and the voice of F.R.I.D.A.Y. in various films in the Marvel Cinematic Universe. She is also the youngest actress ever to play Ophelia in a Royal Shakespeare Company production of Hamlet.

Chenopodium fremontii is a species of flowering plant in the family Amaranthaceae known by the common name Frémont's goosefoot. Both the species' specific epithet, and the common name derive from the 19th century western pioneer John C. Frémont.

It is native to much of the western half of North America from Canada through California to Mexico. It grows in many types of habitat from open desert, to shady forest, at 700–3,100 metres (2,300–10,200 ft).
Malcolm Neville Lockyer (5 October 1923 – 28 June 1976) was a British film composer and conductor.
Olia Lialina (Lang-ru, May 4, 1971, Moscow) is an Internet artist and theorist, an experimental film and video critic and curator.

The First Congregational Church and Parsonage in Kittery Point, Maine, is a historic church complex listed on the National Register of Historic Places. The church, built in 1729 after the original structure burned down, is a modest wood-frame building featuring Greek Revival and Colonial architectural elements, with significant renovations in 1840. The adjacent parsonage, constructed in 1729 and replaced in 1910, served as a community center after its replacement. The site also includes a cemetery established in 1733, which contains the graves of many prominent local figures and features a historic stone wall and iron gate.

Joachim Herrmann (born 21 September 1956) is a German politician. He is a member of the CSU party. Herrmann is an MP in the Landtag, the parliament of Bavaria since 1994. Since 16 October 2007 he has been Minister of the Interior of the state of Bavaria.

The Borscht Corporation is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit that creates short films and videos in and about the city of Miami, Florida. In addition to hosting a quasi-annual film festival that screens their work, Borscht Corp. provides financial and technical support to its commissioned filmmakers.

Since receiving a challenge grant from the John S. and James L. Knight Foundation in 2010, Borscht Corp. has spearheaded productions by untested moviemakers and provided a creative platform for underrepresented (often Latin American) identities in film.

The center of gravity (CG) of an aircraft is the point over which the aircraft would balance. Its position is calculated after supporting the aircraft on at least two sets of weighing scales or load cells and noting the weight shown on each set of scales or load cells. The center of gravity affects the stability of the aircraft. To ensure the aircraft is safe to fly, the center of gravity must fall within specified limits established by the aircraft manufacturer.

WNED-FM (94.5 MHz) is a non-commercial classical music radio station licensed to Buffalo, New York, and serving Western New York and the Toronto area. Owned by Buffalo Toronto Public Media, it operates alongside sister stations WBFO and WNED-TV. The station, branded as "WNED Classical," first signed on as WEBR-FM in 1960 and adopted its current call sign in 1976, transitioning to a classical music format the following year. WNED-FM is a grandfathered "superpower" Class B FM station, broadcasting at 94,000 watts, with a signal that extends into Canada, including Toronto and the Niagara Peninsula.

The station produces extensive local programming, collaborates with the Buffalo Philharmonic Orchestra, and features unique segments like "JoAnn's Classical Corner." It also operates in HD Radio, with its HD2 subchannel airing JazzWorks. In recent years, WNED-FM has engaged listeners through initiatives like its Classical Top 100 playlist, with Beethoven's Symphony No. 9 topping the list in 2020, 2021, and 2022.

A publicist is a professional responsible for managing the public image and media coverage of individuals, brands, or organizations. Their primary role is to generate positive press coverage, often by crafting press releases, networking with journalists and media professionals, and organizing press tours. Publicists also handle crisis management, ensuring their clients' reputations are protected during controversies. They play a crucial role in shaping public perception through traditional media, social media, and blogs.

Publicists often work in industries such as entertainment, sports, and business, with Hollywood publicists managing relationships between celebrities and media outlets. They help stars maintain visibility and navigate the challenges of modern entertainment news. Publicists typically have backgrounds in communications, journalism, or public relations, and their salaries vary widely, with celebrity publicists earning more based on their clientele. The profession requires strong writing, networking, and crisis management skills, as well as adaptability to the evolving media landscape.

Alan Arnett McLeod VC (April 20, 1899 – November 6, 1918) was a Canadian First World War flying ace and the youngest Canadian to receive the Victoria Cross. Serving with the Royal Flying Corps, he displayed extraordinary bravery during a mission in March 1918, saving his observer and surviving a fiery crash despite severe injuries. McLeod died at age 19 from the Spanish flu. He is commemorated through numerous tributes, including a street, a school building, and his induction into the Canadian Aviation Hall of Fame.

Helmut Rahn (16 August 1929 – 14 August 2003), known as Der Boss (The Boss), was a German footballer who played as a forward. He became a legend for having scored the winning goal in the final of the 1954 FIFA World Cup (West Germany vs. Hungary 3–2). Rahn, along with the German team, were decorated by the President of the Federal Republic of Germany in 1972.

TIME'S UP is a non-profit organization that raises money to support victims of sexual harassment. The organization was founded on January 1, 2018, by Hollywood celebrities in response to the Weinstein effect and the Me Too movement. As of January 2020, the organization had raised $24 million in donations.

Time's Up collaborated with the National Women's Law Center to create the Time's Up Legal Defense Fund (TULDF), which provides legal and media support to individuals who have been subject to workplace sex discrimination, such as sexual harassment. The Time's Up Foundation raises money for the TULDF.

The organization came under fire after its leadership's involvement in the attempted cover-up of the Andrew Cuomo sexual harassment allegations came to light. In September 2021, Time's Up dissolved its 71-member advisory board, which included several prominent actors, as a result of the continued fallout from the group's handling of the controversy. It was also reported that its entire governing board would resign and be replaced.

In late 2022, the three board members were Ashley Judd, Nina Shaw, and financial executive Gabrielle Sulzberger.

William W. Arnold (commonly "W. W."; August 28, 1843 – March 30, 1923) was a physician better known across the country for his avocation as an ornithologist. Born in Connersville, Indiana, he initially practiced medicine near Rushville, Indiana. In 1886, he visited Colorado and decided he would like to move there and he settled in Colorado Springs in October of that year. He operated a practice in downtown Colorado Springs and compounded medicine for his patients. He is best known as the founder and operator of a bird hospital, where he cared for ill and injured birds. He took care of domestic birds and had a large influx of wild birds after bad weather, like hail storms. He wrote articles about nature and birds, and he gave talks about them to school children in Colorado and Indiana. He worked as a physician and caregiver for the bird hospital until shortly before his death at age 79.

David Production Inc. (Japanese: 株式会社デイヴィッドプロダクション, Hepburn: Kabushiki-gaisha Deividdo Purodakushon) is a Japanese animation studio founded in September 2007 and is based in Nishitōkyō, Tokyo. Notable works from the studio include JoJo's Bizarre Adventure, Cells at Work!, and Fire Force.

Donald Aronow (March 1, 1927 – February 3, 1987) was an American boat designer, builder, and racer, known for founding several successful powerboat companies, including Donzi Marine, Magnum Marine, and Cigarette Racing Team. A two-time world champion and three-time U.S. champion, his boats won over 350 offshore races and were used by the U.S. Customs Service. Aronow was murdered in 1987 in a case linked to business disputes and drug smuggling.

Louis François Auguste Cauchois-Lemaire (1789–1861) was a French journalist, historian, and political activist known for his liberal and oppositional writings during the Restoration and July Monarchy.

Barysaw (Belarusian: Барысаў; Russian: Борисов) is a city in Belarus, located in the Minsk Voblast. Founded in 1102, it has a rich history, including periods under the Grand Duchy of Lithuania, the Russian Empire, and Soviet rule. The city played a significant role during the Napoleonic Wars and World War II. Today, Barysaw is an industrial hub with a population of approximately 145,000 (as of 2015) and is known for its football team, BATE Borisov, which has achieved national and international success.

Yana Peel (born June 1974) is a Canadian executive, businesswoman, children's author and philanthropist who is currently global head of arts and culture at Chanel. She was CEO of the Serpentine Galleries from 2016 to 2019, and was previously a board member.

Peel is a co-founder of the Outset Contemporary Art Fund (with Candida Gertler), and Intelligence Squared Asia, and was CEO of Intelligence Squared Group from 2013 to 2016.

Peel has several advisory positions including the Tate International Council, V-A-C Foundation, and the NSPCC therapeutic board. She has been an advisor to the British Fashion Council, Asia Art Archive, Lincoln Center, Para Site and the Victoria and Albert Museum, where she founded the design fund.

Haemulinae is a subfamily of marine ray-finned fishes in the family Haemulidae, commonly known as grunts. Established in 1885, it includes several genera, though their classification has been subject to revision based on molecular studies. These studies have led to the reclassification of some species and the revival of certain genera to resolve paraphyly. Taxonomic differences exist between Catalog of Fishes and FishBase.
Li Shuai (simplified Chinese: 李帅; traditional Chinese: 李帥; pinyin: Li Shuài, born 18 August 1982) is a Chinese former footballer who played as a goalkeeper.

Rosa Amelia Guzmán was a Salvadoran journalist, feminist and suffragette. Her 1950 speech to the Constituent Assembly was instrumental in women gaining, not just the right to vote, but the rights of citizenship on 14 September. She was one of the first four women elected to serve in the Legislative Assembly of El Salvador.

May Song Vang (February 5, 1951 – August 5, 2013) was a Laotian-born Hmong American leader, midwife, nurse, and physician assistant. A pioneer for women in Laos, she defied societal norms to pursue education and later married General Vang Pao, a prominent Hmong military leader. After immigrating to the United States, she became a key figure in the Hmong American community, advocating for education and founding the General Vang Pao Foundation.

East Rennell, located on Rennell Island in the Solomon Islands, is the largest raised coral atoll in the world and a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1998. Covering 37,000 hectares, it includes Lake Tegano, surrounding forests, and marine areas. Known for its rich biodiversity, it is home to numerous endemic species, including birds, reptiles, and insects. The site is managed in collaboration with local communities, but logging and environmental threats led to its inclusion on the List of World Heritage in Danger in 2013.

Nathan Sawaya (born July 10, 1973) is an American artist who builds custom three-dimensional sculptures and large-scale mosaics from popular everyday items and is best known for his work with standard LEGO building bricks.

China's Special Envoy on the Middle East Issue is a diplomatic position established in 2002 to support peace efforts in the region, particularly the Israeli–Palestinian conflict. Rooted in China's historical support for the Palestinian cause, the envoy promotes China's Five Principles for Middle East peace and addresses regional issues, including Syria.

Veronika Ivanovskaia (born 1 February 1995) is a German pool player. She began to play in 2003, and later became part of the German national team. She was the winner of the 2018 European Pool Championships eight-ball event, and the runner-up at the 2017 Austria Open.

Arkady Adamovich Brish (May 14, 1917 – March 19, 2016) was a Soviet and Russian physicist and engineer. A graduate of Belarusian State University, he contributed to resistance efforts during World War II and later worked in Moscow. Brish received numerous prestigious awards, including the Hero of Socialist Labour and the Lenin Prize. A memorial plaque in his honor was installed at the NL Dukhov All-Russian Research Institute of Automation in 2017.

Royalty Exchange is an American company that operates an online platform for buying and selling royalty assets of any type, mostly music, where royalty owners can sell their future payments to investors as alternative assets. The company hosts a centralized marketplace and online auction platform that connects a community of over 22,500+ investors with owners of royalty-based assets.

All types of assets can be monetized and invested in including music royalties, film royalties, and tax credits, patent and intellectual property royalties, trade secret royalties, renewable energy land leases, technology licensing royalties, book publishing royalties, franchise royalties, copyright royalties and agriculture royalties.

Sir Frederick Mann KCMG (2 May 1869 – 29 May 1958) was an Australian jurist who served as Chief Justice of Victoria from 1935 to 1944 and Lieutenant Governor of Victoria from 1936 to 1945. Born in Mount Gambier, South Australia, he pursued a legal career after studying at the University of Melbourne. Mann served in the Boer War before becoming a barrister and later a justice of the Supreme Court of Victoria in 1919. Knighted in 1933, he was known for his judicial clarity and significant rulings, including his interpretation of legal terminology.

The Bab Ballads is a collection of light verses by W. S. Gilbert (1836–1911), illustrated with his own comic drawings. The book takes its title from Gilbert's childhood nickname. He later began to sign his illustrations "Bab". Gilbert wrote the "ballads" collected in the book before he became famous for his comic opera librettos with Arthur Sullivan. In writing these verses Gilbert developed his "topsy-turvy" style in which the humour is derived by setting up a ridiculous premise and working out its logical consequences, however absurd. The ballads also reveal Gilbert's cynical and satirical approach to humour.

They became famous on their own, as well as being a source for plot elements, characters and songs that Gilbert recycled in the Gilbert and Sullivan operas. They were read aloud at private dinner-parties, at public banquets and even in the House of Lords. The ballads have been much published, and some have been recorded or otherwise adapted.

Jackson Township is one of the eighteen townships of Richland County, Ohio, United States. It is a part of the Mansfield Metropolitan Statistical Area. The 2000 census found 3,861 people in the township, 2,774 of whom lived in the unincorporated portions of the township.

*The Harold Peary Show* was an American radio situation comedy that aired on CBS from 1950 to 1951. Starring Harold Peary as "Honest Harold" Hemp, the show struggled to replicate the success of *The Great Gildersleeve*, with which it shared notable similarities. Despite its humor and ensemble cast, the program failed to gain a significant audience and ended after one season.
