The proposed nation would encompass parts of five U.S. states and operate as a confederation with libertarian principles. The movement has not been recognized by the U.S. government, tribal governments, or the international community.

Ludvík Podéšť (1921–1968) was a Czech composer, conductor, and musicologist known for his operas, orchestral works, and politically engaged songs. His style blended classical influences, folklore, and later Moroccan musical elements.

Société Belge des Transports par Air SA, known by its short form Sobelair, was a Belgian charter airline from that operated from 1946 to 2004. It was headquartered in Brussels (later in Zaventem) and operated mostly non-scheduled passenger and cargo flights out of Brussels Airport.
Bing Yeh is a Taiwanese-American entrepreneur and engineer, known for founding Silicon Storage Technology and Greenliant Systems, specializing in memory technologies.

The 500-series format is a standardized format for a modular signal processor system consisting of a combination of 500-series modules installed within a 500-series chassis (or rack), with the chassis providing power and audio connections for the individual modules. Originally invented by Automated Processes, Inc. co-founder Saul Walker, today numerous companies manufacture 500-series format products.

The modular nature of the 500-series format allows individual modules to be combined to create a customized signal processing chain, with individual modules added or changed as budget allows. Many 500-series chassis also offer portability, allowing a recording engineer to bring their desired signal processing chain to any recording session.

Richard E. Anderson (born July 29, 1941) is a former American football player and coach. He served as the head football coach at Rutgers University from 1984 to 1989, compiling a record of 28–33–4. His highlight victory during that span was 21–16 win over Penn State in 1988—Rutgers' first win over the Nittany Lions in 70 years. He played college football at Penn State, and served there as an assistant football coach for many years under Joe Paterno.

Jody Hamilton (born Joseph Nicholas Zwaduk III; August 28, 1938 – August 3, 2021) was an American professional wrestler, promoter, and trainer, best known for his work as "The Assassin" and later "The Flame." Debuting in 1955, Hamilton achieved fame as part of the masked tag team "The Assassins" alongside Tom Renesto, becoming one of the most notorious and successful tag teams in wrestling history. The duo held multiple championships, including a record 12 NWA Georgia Tag Team Championships, and headlined events worldwide. Hamilton also found success as a singles competitor, holding titles such as the NWA Central States Heavyweight Championship.

After retiring from in-ring competition in the 1980s due to a back injury, Hamilton became a prominent trainer, running World Championship Wrestling's Power Plant, where he trained stars like Bill Goldberg and Kevin Nash. He later partnered with WWE to establish Deep South Wrestling as a developmental territory. Hamilton also worked with Rampage Pro Wrestling and was involved in a legal dispute with WWE, which was settled in 2010. 

Hamilton's son, Nick Patrick, followed in his footsteps as a wrestling referee. Hamilton passed away on August 3, 2021, at the age of 82, leaving behind a legacy as a wrestler, trainer, and influential figure in professional wrestling.

Great Wishford is a village and civil parish in the Wylye Valley in Wiltshire, England, about three miles (5 km) north of Wilton and five miles (8 km) northwest of Salisbury. The village lies west of a bend in the River Wylye and has a triangular street layout comprising South Street, West Street and Station Road.

Wawne is a village and civil parish in the East Riding of Yorkshire, England, located within the Yorkshire and the Humber region. It had a population of 975 according to the 2011 census. The village, historically mentioned in the Domesday Book as "Wagene," has roots dating back to the Middle Ages and features the Grade I listed St Peter's Church. Wawne was historically significant for its ferry across the River Hull, which operated until 1946. The village has seen modern development, including new housing and community enhancements, while maintaining its historic connections to nearby Sutton on Hull.

Medelpad is a historical province in northern Sweden, located in the southern part of Västernorrland County. Covering an area of 7,058 km², it is bordered by the rivers Ljungan and Indalsälven and features a varied landscape with elevations averaging 200–300 meters above sea level. Sundsvall, the province's largest city, was granted city status in 1624 and remains its cultural and economic center. Medelpad is notable for its coastline along the Gulf of Bothnia, part of the UNESCO-listed High Coast, shaped by post-glacial rebound. Its provincial symbols include the spruce, mountain hare, and perch.

Major-General Sir James Syme Drew KBE CB DSO MC DL (1 September 1883 – 27 June 1955) was a decorated British Army officer who saw service during both the world wars. He served as aide-de-camp to the King, commanded the 52nd (Lowland) Infantry Division, was Colonel to the Queen's Own Cameron Highlanders, and became Director-General of the Home Guard and Territorial Army.

Lau is a socken (district) on the eastern coast of Gotland, Sweden, encompassing the village of Lau kyrkby and the medieval Lau Church. The area is rural, featuring farms, forests, and the Lau Cove. Historically significant, Lau contains Bronze and Iron Age archaeological sites, including grave mounds, stone ships, and a hillfort. Nearby are the Laus Holmar islets, known for bird reserves and grazing.

Lieutenant Colonel Henning Bertil Stjernfelt (24 July 1917 – 21 January 2017) was a Swedish Army officer and military historian. He wrote several books about World War II and the Swedish coastal defence. Stjernfelt was also a part of the formation of the Swedish Coastal Rangers in 1956.

The Walt Whitman House is a historic building in Camden, New Jersey, United States, which was the last residence of American poet Walt Whitman, in his declining years before his death. It is located at 330 Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. Boulevard, known as Mickle St. during Whitman's time there.

Charles Jackson "Bud" Wildman (born June 3, 1946) is a Canadian politician. He served in the Legislative Assembly of Ontario as a New Democratic Party Member of Provincial Parliament (MPP) from 1975 to 1999, representing the riding of Algoma, and was a cabinet minister in the government of Bob Rae.

The Finse Tunnel is a 10.3-kilometer (6.4-mile) railway tunnel on the Bergen Line in Norway, located near Finse. Opened on May 16, 1993, it was constructed to address the severe winter weather conditions that frequently disrupted rail operations in the area. The tunnel, owned by the Norwegian National Rail Administration and operated by Norges Statsbaner and other freight companies, is electrified and allows trains to travel at speeds of up to 170 kilometers per hour (110 mph). It is situated at an elevation of 1,237 meters (4,058 feet), making it one of the highest railway tunnels in Europe.

The project was initiated in the 1980s to improve reliability and reduce operational costs associated with snow clearance. Construction began in 1990 and was completed in 1992, with the tunnel officially opening the following year at a total cost of 480 million kr. The tunnel includes a passing loop and safety features, though concerns about fire safety and evacuation have been raised due to its remote location. The Finse Tunnel significantly improved travel times and operational efficiency on the Bergen Line.

An institute is an organisational body created for a certain purpose. They are often research organisations (research institutes) created to do research on specific topics, or can also be a professional body.

In some countries, institutes can be part of a university or other institutions of higher education, either as a group of departments or an autonomous educational institution without a traditional university status such as a "university institute" (see Institute of Technology). In some countries, such as South Korea and India, private schools are sometimes referred to as institutes, and in Spain, secondary schools are referred to as institutes.

Historically, in some countries institutes were educational units imparting vocational training and often incorporating libraries, also known as mechanics' institutes.

The word "institute" comes from a Latin word institutum meaning "facility" or "habit"; from instituere meaning "build", "create", "raise" or "educate".

Tom Gorman (born January 19, 1946) is a retired ATP tour American tennis player and coach. He won 7 singles and 9 doubles titles and reached semi-finals in the 3 of the 4 ATP tour grand slam events. His ATP ranking peaked at 8 in 1973.

The Katkari people are a Scheduled Tribe primarily residing in the Indian states of Maharashtra, Gujarat, Rajasthan, and Karnataka, with a population of over 300,000. Historically a forest-dwelling community in the Western Ghats, their name derives from their traditional livelihood of producing catechu (katha) from the khair tree. Over time, restrictions on forest-based activities and shifting cultivation forced many Katkari to migrate to agricultural villages, where they now live in scattered hamlets, often as landless laborers. Many work in brick kilns and charcoal units, facing social exclusion and land insecurity. Despite these challenges, the Katkari retain unique cultural practices, including their knowledge of forest resources and a diet that includes rodents, which contributes to their marginalization.

Dave Upthegrove (born May 3, 1971) is an American politician and member of the Democratic Party, serving on the King County Council since 2014. Previously, he was a member of the Washington House of Representatives from 2002 to 2013, where he chaired the Environment Committee and led environmental and education initiatives.

Dance of the Tiger (Swedish: Den Svarta Tigern) is a novel by Finnish palaeontologist Björn Kurtén, published in 1978 and English translation in 1980. It is a prehistoric novel dealing with the interaction between Neanderthals and Cro-Magnons. A sequel, Singletusk, published in 1982, continues the story of the family.

Red God (1954–1979) was a Thoroughbred race horse foaled in Kentucky who competed in England and the United States but who is best known as the sire of Blushing Groom who prominent turfman Edward L. Bowen calls one of the great international sires of the 20th century.

Howard Ehmke (April 24, 1894 – March 17, 1959) was an American professional baseball pitcher who played 15 seasons in Major League Baseball (MLB) from 1915 to 1930. He pitched for the Buffalo Blues, Detroit Tigers, Boston Red Sox, and Philadelphia Athletics, compiling a career win–loss record of 166–166 with a 3.75 earned run average (ERA). Ehmke is best known for his performance in Game 1 of the 1929 World Series, where he struck out a then-record 13 batters to lead the Athletics to victory over the Chicago Cubs. He also pitched a no-hitter for the Red Sox in 1923 and was a key contributor to the Athletics' 1929 World Series championship.

Beyond baseball, Ehmke was an entrepreneur credited with inventing the tarpaulin to protect sports fields from rain. He founded the Howard Ehmke Company, which manufactured tarps, tents, and other products, and later contributed to defense work during World War II. Ehmke died in Philadelphia in 1959 at the age of 64.
Statistics of Football League First Division in the 1965–66 season.

Tony Waddington (9 November 1924 – 21 January 1994) was an English football player and manager, best known for his 17-year tenure as manager of Stoke City from 1960 to 1977. Born in Manchester, he began his playing career as a wing half with Crewe Alexandra, making 193 league appearances before transitioning into coaching. After joining Stoke City as a coach in 1952, he rose to become manager in 1960, tasked with revitalizing the struggling club.

Waddington is celebrated for transforming Stoke City, notably signing legendary players such as Stanley Matthews, Gordon Banks, and George Eastham. Under his leadership, Stoke achieved promotion to the First Division in 1963 and won their first major trophy, the League Cup, in 1972. His tenure also saw the club reach two FA Cup semi-finals and enjoy one of the most successful periods in its history. However, financial difficulties following storm damage to the Victoria Ground in 1976 led to the sale of key players and Waddington's departure in 1977.

After a brief managerial stint at Crewe Alexandra (1979–1981), Waddington returned to Stoke as an associate director in 1991, a role he held until his death in 1994. He is widely regarded as Stoke City's greatest manager, leaving a lasting legacy in English football.
"Like a Man" is a song recorded by Onhel, featuring American rapper Lil Wayne. It was released as a single on August 8, 2017.
When World War II was declared in 1939, it had a negative effect on association football; competitions were suspended and players signed up to fight, resulting in the deaths of many players.
Mirage: Arcane Warfare was a multiplayer action game developed by Torn Banner Studios. The game launched on Windows PCs in May 2017, but was shut down one year later.

The Yale Glee Club is a mixed chorus of men and women, consisting of students of Yale University in New Haven, Connecticut. Founded in 1861, it is the third oldest collegiate chorus in the United States after the Harvard Glee Club, founded in 1858, and the University of Michigan Men's Glee Club, founded in 1859. The Glee Club performs several concerts each year in New Haven and goes on tour each January. According to music critic Zachary Woolfe of the New York Times, it is "one of the best collegiate singing ensembles, and one of the most adventurous." Its members are "world famous for their harmonic precision" per New York Times music critic Robert Sherman.

Bygdøy Royal Estate (Lang-no), also known as the Bygdø Royal Farm, is a Kongsgård estate and manor house that occupies a large part of the northwestern part of the Bygdøy peninsula in Oslo, Norway. It is the official summer residence of the King of Norway.

The ZBD-03 is a Chinese airborne infantry fighting vehicle (IFV) developed to replace the BMD-3 and has been in service since 2002. Designed for air-dropping from aircraft like the Xi'an Y-20, it features a lightweight, amphibious design with hydropneumatic suspension and a Dongfeng diesel engine. Armed with a 30mm autocannon, HJ-73C anti-tank guided missiles, and a coaxial machine gun, it also includes a computerized fire control system. Despite its limited armor for air-drop compatibility, the ZBD-03 offers mobility, versatility, and firepower for rapid deployment operations.

Nerima Wako-Ojiwa (born 1989) is a Kenyan political analyst and corporate executive, serving as the executive director of Siasa Place, a youth-led organization she co-founded in 2015. She holds a Bachelor of Arts in Journalism and Sociology and a Master of Public Administration from Jacksonville State University. Born in Nairobi, Kenya, she developed her political acumen through campus leadership roles while studying in the United States. Nerima is married to politician Billian Ojiwa and is the niece of former Kenyan Senator Amos Wako.
Leucocyanidin is a flavonoid compound found in various plants, involved in procyanidin synthesis and studied for its metabolic pathways.

Eucalyptus largiflorens, commonly known as black box, is a tree species in the family Myrtaceae native to Australia. It grows up to 20 meters tall, with rough, dark grey bark and lance-shaped leaves. Flowering occurs in most months, especially in autumn and spring, producing white flowers. First described in 1855 by Ferdinand von Mueller, the species is widespread across Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, and South Australia, particularly in grassy woodlands on the floodplains of the Murray–Darling basin. Indigenous Australians utilized its seeds as a food source, processing them to remove bitterness before consumption.

Gorky Central Park of Culture and Leisure (Russian: Центральный парк культуры и отдыха имени Горького, romanized: Tsentralny park kultury i otdykha imeni Gorkogo, IPA: [tsɨnˈtraljnɨj ˈpark kʊljˈturɨ i ˈoddɨxə ˈimɪnɪ ˈɡorkova]) is a central urban park in Rostov-on-Don, Rostov oblast, Russia, named after Maxim Gorky. It is the eldest park in Rostov-on-Don. The park was established in 1813 on the skirts of the city. Now it is located in center of Rostov-on-Don between Pushkinskaya street (Gorky Park North), Bolshaya Sadovaya street (Gorky Park South), Budyonovskiy avenue (Gorky Park West) and Semashko lane (Gorky Park East). Since 1998 the park is protected as an object of cultural heritage of regional value.
The Insurance Act, 1938 is a law originally passed in 1938 in British India to regulate the insurance sector. It provides the broad legal framework within which the industry operates.

Michael Van Wijk (born 30 September 1952) is an English bodybuilder, actor, and former television personality, best known for his role as "Wolf" on the British TV series *Gladiators*. He ran gyms in London, later moved to New Zealand, and competed in Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu.

Ariyalur is a town and the administrative headquarters of Ariyalur district in the state of Tamil Nadu, India. Located in the Chola Nadu region, it is historically significant as part of the Chola dynasty, with Gangai Konda Cholapuram serving as the capital during the late Chola period. The town later came under the rule of the Vijayanagar Nayaks, Marathas, and eventually the British. Ariyalur is known for its rich limestone resources, which support a thriving cement industry, with major companies like UltraTech Cement and Dalmia Cement operating in the area. Agriculture, particularly sugarcane, cashew, and rice cultivation, also plays a significant role in the local economy.

Ariyalur has a hot semi-arid climate and is connected by National Highway NH136 and a railway station on the Chennai–Tiruchirappalli line. The municipality, established in 1943, oversees local governance and infrastructure, including water supply, waste management, and healthcare. As of the 2011 census, the town had a population of 28,902, with a literacy rate of 76.04%. Ariyalur is part of the Chidambaram Lok Sabha constituency and the Ariyalur State Assembly constituency. The town is also home to historical landmarks, including temples built during the Vijayanagar period, and serves as a hub for transportation and commerce in the region.

Duško Tarbuk is a Serbian politician and member of the Serbian Progressive Party. He has served in the National Assembly of Serbia since 2016 and previously held roles in Zemun's municipal government.

Thomas Grantham (1634–1692) was an English General Baptist minister, theologian, and advocate for religious liberty. Born in Lincolnshire, he became a prominent leader among the General Baptists, known for his Arminian theology and efforts to establish congregations across England. Grantham frequently faced persecution, including multiple imprisonments, for his nonconformist beliefs. He served as a "messenger," an office dedicated to evangelism and church oversight, and engaged in theological debates with Anglicans, Presbyterians, Quakers, and Roman Catholics. Grantham was a prolific writer, defending Baptist doctrines and religious freedom, and his works were widely read in the 17th century.

*Speshow* is the fourth studio album by Taiwanese Mandopop artist Show Lo, released on November 17, 2006, by Avex Taiwan. The album achieved significant commercial success, debuting at number one on Taiwan's G-Music Weekly Top 20 Mandarin and Combo Charts, as well as the Five Music Chart, and maintaining strong chart performance for several weeks. Notable tracks include "好朋友" (Good Friends) and "精舞門" (Dance Gate), both of which were featured on Hit Fm Taiwan's Annual Top 100 Singles Chart for 2006 and received nominations for Songs of the Year at the 2007 Metro Radio Mandarin Music Awards. "精舞門" also won a Top 10 Song of the Year award at the 2007 HITO Radio Music Awards.

Judith Arnold is the pen name of Barbara Keiler (born April 7, 1953), an American romance novelist who has written over 85 novels since 1983. A three-time Romantic Times award-winner and former president of Novelists, Inc., she has also been nominated for the RITA Award and the RWA Lifetime Achievement Award.

The BB 1800 were motor luggage vans of SNCF. These were the so-called "first-series" Z 5000 locomotives ordered by the Chemins de fer de l'Ouest. They were put into service, with running numbers Z 5001 to Z 5010, on 12 April 1900, on the line from Paris-Invalides to Issy-Plaine, which was extended to Meudon on 1 July 1901 then to Versailles RG on 31 May 1902.

Sir George Lockhart of Lee (1673 – 17 December 1731), of Carnwath, South Lanarkshire, also known as Lockhart of Carnwath, was a Scottish writer and Jacobite politician who sat in the Parliament of Scotland from 1702 to 1707 and as a Tory in the House of Commons from 1708 to 1715. He was a member of the Commission on the Union before 1707 but acted as an informant to his Jacobite colleagues and later wrote an anonymous memoir of its dealings. He supported the Stuart cause in the Jacobite rising.

Oleg Vladimirovich Losev (May 10, 1903 – January 22, 1942) was a Russian physicist and inventor, recognized as a pioneer in semiconductor physics and the inventor of the light-emitting diode (LED). Despite lacking formal higher education, Losev conducted groundbreaking research on point-contact crystal detectors, leading to his discovery in 1924 that silicon carbide junctions emitted light when a current was applied. He published extensively on the phenomenon, theorizing its quantum mechanical basis and envisioning applications in telecommunications. His work, however, was largely overlooked during his lifetime.

Losev also explored negative resistance in diodes, using zincite crystals to develop solid-state amplifiers, oscillators, and radio receivers decades before the invention of the transistor. His contributions to solid-state electronics were underappreciated, overshadowed by the dominance of vacuum tube technology.

Losev worked at several Soviet institutions, including the Nizhny Novgorod Radio Laboratory and the Ioffe Physical-Technical Institute. He died of starvation during the Siege of Leningrad in 1942. Modern researchers credit him as a visionary in semiconductor technology.

Eastoft is a village and civil parish in North Lincolnshire, England. It is situated within the Isle of Axholme, 3 miles (5 km) north-east from Crowle, and on the A161 road.

The 2001 census recorded a parish population of 378, increasing to 431 at the 2011 census.

Joseph Buckland (born 27 October 1992) is a British ice dancer from Nottingham, England. He has competed with multiple partners, including Olivia Smart and Robynne Tweedale, achieving national and international success. Buckland is the younger brother of ice dancer Nicholas Buckland.
Waingaro is a rural hamlet in the Waikato District of New Zealand, known for its hot springs, marae, and historical significance as a transportation hub. It has a population of 189 (2018 census).

*Clidastes* is an extinct genus of small mosasaurs that lived during the Late Cretaceous period, approximately 85 to 80 million years ago. Belonging to the subfamily Mosasaurinae, *Clidastes* was one of the smallest mosasaurs, typically measuring 2–4 meters (6.6–13.1 feet) in length, with some specimens reaching up to 6.2 meters (20 feet). Known for its streamlined body and interlocking vertebrae, *Clidastes* was a highly agile swimmer adapted for hunting fast-moving prey in shallow marine environments. Its teeth and tooth replacement system were similar to other mosasaurs, featuring thecodont implantation and specialized resorption pits.

The genus was first described by Edward Drinker Cope in 1868, with *Clidastes propython* later designated as the type species. Fossils of *Clidastes* have been found primarily in the United States, with additional remains reported from Sweden and other regions. *Clidastes* is considered one of the most basal hydropedal mosasaurines, bridging the evolutionary gap between earlier and more derived mosasaurs.
Freienfeld (IPA-de; Lang-it IPA-it) is a comune (municipality) in the province of South Tyrol in northern Italy, located about 45 kilometres (28 mi) north of the city of Bolzano.

Eblana is a settlement mentioned in Ptolemy's 2nd-century work *Geographia*, which some early antiquarians, such as Sir John Ware and Walter Harris, speculated to be an early reference to Dublin. They suggested that Eblana might be a corruption of "Deblana," derived from the Gaelic "Dubh Linn" (Black Pool), the origin of Dublin's name. However, this theory is debated, as no evidence of a significant settlement at Dublin's site exists before the Norse fortifications in 841. Scholars like Eoin MacNeill argue that Eblana may have been farther north, possibly in County Louth. Other theories propose locations such as the Delvin River or the promontory fort of Drumanagh, leaving the true identity of Eblana uncertain and speculative.

*Shade* is a 2003 American neo-noir crime thriller film written and directed by Damian Nieman. Featuring an ensemble cast including Stuart Townsend, Gabriel Byrne, Thandiwe Newton, Jamie Foxx, and Sylvester Stallone, the film follows a group of hustlers navigating high-stakes poker games and elaborate cons. Premiering at the CineVegas International Film Festival, it had a limited theatrical release in 2004, grossing $458,144. Despite mixed reviews, it holds a 67% rating on Rotten Tomatoes.
Ellis Bitter is a mid-19th-century English cider apple variety, originating near Crediton, Devon. Known for its bittersweet flavor, it remains popular in traditional and commercial orchards.

"Like a Surgeon" is a song by American singer Ciara from her third studio album, *Fantasy Ride* (2009). Written by Christopher Stewart and Terius Nash, and produced by Tricky Stewart and The-Dream, the R&B track features metaphorical lyrics with a medical theme. Although planned as a single in North America, its release was canceled due to the album's poor sales. The song received mixed reviews from critics, with some praising its bold production and Ciara's delivery, while others criticized its metaphors and lack of a strong hook.

Natalia Maliszewska (born 16 September 1995) is a Polish short track speed skater. She won silver medal at the 2018 World Short Track Speed Skating Championships and gold medal at the 2019 European Short Track Speed Skating Championships.

Jagadishpur is a census town in Howrah district, West Bengal, India, located at 22°39′N 88°17′E. As of the 2011 Census, it had a population of 16,259 with a literacy rate of 85.46%. The town is connected by Benaras Road and the nearby Kona railway station.
Karl Hermann Heinrich Benda (1748–1836) was a German violinist, composer, and piano teacher, known for his virtuosity and contributions to Berlin's musical life.

In evolutionary genetics, mutational meltdown is a sub class of extinction vortex in which the environment and genetic predisposition mutually reinforce each other. Mutational meltdown (not to be confused with the concept of an error catastrophe) is the accumulation of harmful mutations in a small population, which leads to loss of fitness and decline of the population size, which may lead to further accumulation of deleterious mutations due to fixation by genetic drift.

A population experiencing mutational meltdown is trapped in a downward spiral and will go extinct if the phenomenon lasts for some time. Usually, the deleterious mutations would simply be selected away, but during a mutational meltdown, the number of individuals thus suffering an early death is too large relative to the overall population size so that mortality exceeds the birth rate.
Prakash Vir Shastri (30 December 1923 – 23 November 1977) was a Member of the Parliament of India (Sansad) and was also a leader in the Arya Samaj movement.

Kerry Condon (born 9 January 1983) is an Irish actress known for her work in film, television, and theater. She gained recognition for her stage roles, including Mairead in *The Lieutenant of Inishmore* and Ophelia in *Hamlet* with the Royal Shakespeare Company, and won awards for *The Cripple of Inishmaan*. Condon's film credits include *Ned Kelly*, *Unleashed*, and *The Last Station*. She is also known for voicing F.R.I.D.A.Y. in the Marvel Cinematic Universe and for her roles in the HBO/BBC series *Rome* and *The Walking Dead*.

Dragan Bender (born November 17, 1997) is a Croatian professional basketball player for Monbus Obradoiro of the Spanish Liga ACB. A versatile power forward and center, Bender began his professional career at age 15 with KK Split in Croatia before playing for Maccabi Tel Aviv in Israel. He was selected fourth overall by the Phoenix Suns in the 2016 NBA Draft, making him the highest-drafted Croatian player in NBA history. Bender also played for the Milwaukee Bucks and Golden State Warriors before returning to Europe, where he won multiple Israeli League titles. Internationally, he has represented Croatia at various youth and senior levels.

Gina Bachauer (Greek: Τζίνα Μπαχάουερ; May 21, 1910, Athens – August 22, 1976, Athens), was a Greek classical pianist who toured extensively in the United States and Europe. Interested in piano at a young age, Bachauer graduated from the Athens Conservatory and studied under Alfred Cortot and Sergei Rachmaninoff. She is best known for playing Romantic piano concertos. She played hundreds of concerts for the Allied troops in the Middle East during World War II while she lived in Egypt. She spent a lot of time touring the United States and Europe, giving over 100 concerts each year. Bachauer also recorded extensively, both as a soloist and with orchestras. She received an honorary doctorate from the University of Utah. During her career she was called the "queen of pianists". The Gina Bachauer International Piano Foundation was named in honor of her contributions to the musical world. In her personal life, Bachauer married music conductor Alec Sherman, who became her manager. She died at the age of 66 at the Athens Festival.

Hastings, Iowa, is a small city in Mills County, United States, with a population of 152 as of the 2020 census. Established in 1872 following the construction of the Chicago, Burlington and Quincy Railroad, the city covers 0.41 square miles of land. It is part of the East Mills Community School District, formed in 2011 through a merger.

David Peter Ryan (born 5 January 1957) is an English former footballer who played as a goalkeeper. He began his career at Manchester United and later played for clubs including Port Vale, Southport, and Northwich Victoria. After retiring, he served as Head Goalkeeping Coach at the Manchester United Academy and worked in community development for the club from 1992 to 2013.
William Henry Belpitt (born May 3, 1860, date of death unknown) was a United States Navy sailor and a recipient of the United States military's highest decoration, the Medal of Honor.

"Charles" is a short story by Shirley Jackson that follows a mother’s observations of her son Laurie’s behavioral changes after starting kindergarten. Laurie tells his parents about a mischievous classmate named Charles, whose antics captivate and concern them. However, a twist at the end reveals that Charles does not exist, suggesting Laurie invented him to explain his own misbehavior. The story is widely interpreted as a commentary on childhood and deception, with some alternative readings suggesting a supernatural element. It remains a popular piece in school curricula and is Jackson's second most famous short story after "The Lottery."
Serbs in Germany (Lang-sr; Lang-de) refers to persons living in Germany who have total or partial Serbian ancestry. They form the seventh largest group of foreigners in Germany.
Fitchville Township is one of the nineteen townships of Huron County, Ohio, United States. As of the 2010 census the population of the township was 1,056.

The Fruitmarket Gallery is a contemporary art gallery in Edinburgh, Scotland, established in 1974 in a former fruit and vegetable market building. Renovated in 1994 and expanded in 2021, it hosts international and Scottish art exhibitions, alongside events, a café, and a bookshop. Admission is free.

The 0-8-0 is a steam locomotive wheel arrangement with eight driving wheels and no leading or trailing wheels. Initially used for mainline freight haulage, it later became popular for heavy switching duties. Known for its high tractive effort and adhesive weight, the 0-8-0 was widely used in countries such as the United States, Germany, Russia, and South Africa. Notable examples include the USRA 0-8-0 in the United States, the Prussian G8.1 in Germany, and the O-class in Russia. The design was less common in lighter railways, where smaller configurations like the 0-6-0 were preferred.
Salvatore Grippi (September 30, 1921 – November 30, 2017) was an American painter, printmaker, and educator.
Tyrone Charles Kirk (born 2 January 1984, in Scunthorpe) is an English footballer.

El Valle de Arroyo Seco is a census-designated place (CDP) in Santa Fe County, New Mexico, United States. It is part of the Santa Fe, New Mexico Metropolitan Statistical Area. The population was 1,440 at the 2010 census.
Hanauer Straße in Munich is an arterial road about one and a half kilometers long extending from south to north in the Moosach district. It was named after the Hesse city of Hanau.

TransPeshawar or Peshawar BRT is a bus rapid transit system in Peshawar, capital of Pakistan's Khyber Pakhtunkhwa (KP) province. TransPeshawar BRT system consists of two parts: the first encompasses an east–west corridor served by 32 stations on a dedicated lane for exclusive use by buses, while the second part consists of a network of feeder routes in which buses can enter and exit the system to travel on city streets. The system was inaugurated on August 13, 2020, and is the fourth BRT system in Pakistan.

*Faces of Ground Zero* is a photographic exhibition and collection of portraits capturing the individuals impacted by the September 11, 2001, terrorist attacks. Created by photographer Joe McNally, the exhibit features life-sized Polaroid images of first responders, survivors, and others connected to the events of 9/11. The exhibition debuted in 2002 and toured several major cities, including New York, Boston, London, Chicago, San Francisco, and Los Angeles. It returned to New York for significant anniversaries of the attacks, including the first, fifth, and tenth anniversaries, with notable displays at Rockefeller Center, the New York City Fire Museum, and the Time Warner Center. The tenth-anniversary exhibition, titled *Faces of Ground Zero: 10 Years Later*, included more than 50 images from the original collection alongside new portraits.  

The portraits from the exhibit were also published in photobooks, preserving the stories and faces of those affected by the tragedy. The project has been widely recognized for its emotional impact and its role in commemorating the resilience and humanity of those connected to 9/11.

Franco-Albertans are French-speaking residents of Alberta, Canada, and form the largest francophone community in Western Canada. As of the 2016 Canadian Census, 86,705 Albertans reported French as their mother tongue, while 268,615 were bilingual in English and French. The community is diverse, with only 25% of Franco-Albertans born in Alberta, while others have migrated from other Canadian provinces or abroad, particularly Africa and Europe. French is the sixth most commonly reported ethnic origin in Alberta.

The Franco-Albertan community has historical roots dating back to the late 18th century, when French Canadian fur traders and missionaries arrived in the region. French was once a prominent language in Alberta, but its status declined after the 19th century due to English-only policies. Despite this, Franco-Albertans have preserved their language and culture through institutions, education, and advocacy. The 1982 Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms strengthened their educational rights, leading to the establishment of francophone school boards in 1994.

Today, Franco-Albertans are represented by the Association canadienne-française de l’Alberta and supported by the provincial Francophone Secretariat. The community is centered in Greater Edmonton and the Calgary Region, with four officially bilingual municipalities. Cultural contributions include francophone media, arts, and festivals, while the University of Alberta’s Campus Saint-Jean offers French-language post-secondary education. The Franco-Albertan flag was officially recognized in 2017, symbolizing the community’s enduring presence and contributions to Alberta.

HMS *Fairy* was a Fairfield three-funnel, 30-knot destroyer of the Royal Navy, launched in 1897 and commissioned in 1898. Initially serving with the Channel Fleet, she was later classified as a C-class destroyer in 1913. During World War I, *Fairy* was engaged in convoy escort duties. On 31 May 1918, she sank after ramming the German submarine SM UC-75 off Flamborough Head. She earned the battle honour "Belgian Coast 1914–17."

An ultrasonic motor is a type of piezoelectric motor powered by the ultrasonic vibration of a component, the stator, placed against another component, the rotor or slider depending on the scheme of operation (rotation or linear translation). Ultrasonic motors differ from other piezoelectric motors in several ways, though both typically use some form of piezoelectric material, most often lead zirconate titanate and occasionally lithium niobate or other single-crystal materials. The most obvious difference is the use of resonance to amplify the vibration of the stator in contact with the rotor in ultrasonic motors. Ultrasonic motors also offer arbitrarily large rotation or sliding distances, while piezoelectric actuators are limited by the static strain that may be induced in the piezoelectric element.

One common application of ultrasonic motors is in camera lenses where they are used to move lens elements as part of the auto-focus system. Ultrasonic motors replace the noisier and often slower micro-motor in this application.

*Oreophrynella quelchii* is a species of toad in the family Bufonidae, described by George Albert Boulenger in 1895 and named after J. J. Quelch, who collected specimens from Mount Roraima. Endemic to high montane tepui environments, it is diurnal and commonly found on open rock surfaces. Listed as Vulnerable, it is protected within Canaima and Monte Roraima National Parks.

Carfilzomib, sold under the brand name Kyprolis, is an anti-cancer medication acting as a selective proteasome inhibitor. Chemically, it is a tetrapeptide epoxyketone and an analog of epoxomicin. It was developed by Onyx Pharmaceuticals.

The U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved it on 20 July 2012, for use in people with multiple myeloma who have received at least two prior therapies, including treatment with bortezomib and an immunomodulatory therapy (such as lenalidomide) and have demonstrated disease progression on or within 60 days of completion of the last therapy. Initial approval was based on response rate. Data demonstrating an Overall Survival (OS) benefit was later demonstrated in the ENDEAVOR trial and approved by the FDA.

The abbreviation CFZ is common for referring to carfilzomib, but abbreviating drug names is not best practice in medicine.

*The Historical Illuminatus Chronicles* is a series of historical novels by Robert Anton Wilson. The trilogy follows the adventures of Sigismundo Celine, an ancestor of Hagbard Celine from *The Illuminatus! Trilogy*, as he navigates Europe and America during the Enlightenment. The series explores themes of secret societies, mysticism, and historical intrigue, with Sigismundo encountering Freemasons, imprisonment, and mysterious captors who claim he is a descendant of Jesus Christ. The novels blend historical events with fictional elements, offering a satirical and philosophical take on history and power.

The series gained additional notoriety in 1989 when a mentally ill man, Kenneth Lamar Noid, held hostages at a Domino's Pizza and requested a copy of the second novel, *The Widow's Son*, during the incident.

György Szondy (1500 – July 9, 1552) was a Hungarian military captain and national hero known for his defense of Drégely Castle during the Ottoman–Hungarian Wars. Despite being vastly outnumbered, Szondy and his small garrison resisted a 12,000-strong Ottoman force led by Hadim Ali Pasha in the 1552 siege. Refusing to surrender, Szondy fought valiantly until his death in battle. His bravery earned the respect of his Ottoman adversaries, who buried him with full military honors. Szondy's legacy endures as a symbol of courage, celebrated annually in Drégelypalánk, Hungary.

The Aleutian tern (Onychoprion aleuticus) is a migratory bird living in the subarctic region of the globe most of the year. It is frequently associated with the Arctic tern, which it closely resembles. While both species have a black cap, the Aleutian tern may be distinguished by its white forehead (although juvenile Arctic terns also have white foreheads). During breeding season, the Arctic terns have bright red bills, feet, and legs while those of the Aleutian terns are black.

Onychoprion aleuticus has not been thoroughly studied yet. If its winter migratory range was completely unknown until the late 1980s; it is now known that many Aleutian terns spend the winter near the Equator in the western Pacific. The Aleutian tern breeds in wide-ranging coastal colonies only in Alaska and eastern Siberia. Although Alaskan and Siberian populations are not well monitored, both are thought to be in significant decline.

Lee Franklin Hanmer (October 24, 1871 - April 27, 1961) was the director of the recreation department of the Russell Sage Foundation from 1912 to 1937. In that capacity, he helped to found the Boy Scouts of America. He later helped to form the Campfire Girls of America in 1912.

Petro Pysarchuk (Lang-uk, born June 6, 1955, in Chemeryntsi, village, Peremyshliany Raion, Lviv Oblast, Ukrainian SSR) is a Ukrainian Politician and Entrepreneur. People's Deputy to the Verkhovna Rada of Ukraine 4th, 5th and 6th convocations.

Berlin, also known as the Long-a-Coming Depot, is located in Berlin, Camden County, New Jersey, United States, between East Taunton and Washington Avenues, abutting the New Jersey Transit Atlantic City Line tracks. The station was built in 1856 and added to the National Register of Historic Places on February 14, 1997 for its significance in transportation.

The Walt Whitman House is a historic home in Camden, New Jersey, where poet Walt Whitman lived from 1884 until his death in 1892. Designated a National Historic Landmark in 1962, the six-room house is now a museum operated by the New Jersey Division of Parks and Forestry, featuring artifacts from Whitman's life, including the bed where he died.

Sage Cullen Kotsenburg (born July 27, 1993) is an American professional snowboarder and Olympic gold medalist. He won the inaugural gold medal in slopestyle snowboarding at the 2014 Winter Olympics in Sochi, becoming the first gold medalist of those Games and the first U.S. medalist. Known for his creativity, Kotsenburg performed a trick he had never attempted before, featuring his signature grab, the "Holy Crail." Born in Coeur d'Alene, Idaho, and raised in Park City, Utah, he began snowboarding at age five and turned professional in 2010.

Radovan Pankov (born 5 August 1995) is a Serbian professional footballer who plays as a centre-back or right-back for Red Star Belgrade. A product of Vojvodina's youth system, he has also played for Ural Yekaterinburg, AEK Larnaca, and Radnički Niš. Pankov was part of Serbia's squad that won the 2015 FIFA U-20 World Cup and has been recognized for his performances in domestic and European competitions, including a standout role in Red Star Belgrade's 2019–20 UEFA Champions League campaign.

The terpene synthase C terminal domain is a protein domain involved in the biosynthesis of terpenoids, a large class of metabolic molecules. It catalyzes the cyclization of geranyl diphosphate, stabilizing reactive intermediates. Found in the terpene synthase family, it exhibits structural conservation and is classified into six phylogenetic subgroups.

Down High School is a controlled co-educational grammar school located in Downpatrick, County Down, Northern Ireland. The school has students from the ages of eleven to eighteen in the senior school — of which there are approximately 970 pupils. On 21 July 2014, John O'Dowd, the education minister, approved a development proposal to close the preparatory department of Down High School and it closed on 30 June 2014. There are roughly 250 pupils engaged in A-level study in the sixth form. In July 2015, some £20 million was granted by the education minister to begin the new build project in 2017.

"The Things That I Used to Do" is a 1953 blues song by Guitar Slim, written by Eddie Jones and produced by Ray Charles. Recorded at J&M Studio in New Orleans, the song features a gospel-influenced arrangement and lyrics of regret, with Slim's distinctive guitar work and a rising and falling melody. It became a major commercial success and a standard in the blues genre, influencing the development of soul music and the "electric sound" of rock. The song's use of distorted guitar tones predated similar techniques by artists like Jimi Hendrix, who cited Guitar Slim as an influence.

Recognized as one of the "500 Songs That Shaped Rock and Roll" by the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame, the track has been covered by artists such as James Brown, Tina Turner, and Pee Wee Crayton. James Brown's 1964 version reached number 99 on the Billboard Hot 100. The song remains a classic of New Orleans blues and a landmark in music history.
Black Noise: Rap Music and Black Culture in Contemporary America is a 1994 book by Tricia Rose. It was released in hardback on April 29, 1994 through Wesleyan University Press.

Hyundai Chungun High School (Korean: 현대청운고등학교; HCU), is a private boarding high school located in Dong-gu, Ulsan, South Korea. It is one of the six independent private high schools (자율형 사립고) in South Korea that require an application of nationwide scale and admissions test to the applicant students. Since students who want to get in HCU usually get straight A when they are at middle school and take a test to get in HCU, they usually get rank 1 or rank 2 for their grades of CSAT(대학수학능력시험)College Scholastic Ability Test statistically. For example, there was a research for those who took CSAT in 2016(2017학년도 대학수학능력시험), and HCU placed the 4th in nationwide for the average CSAT grade for Korean, Mathematics, and English. The percentage of rank 1 and rank 2 is 4%, 7% respectively. The aggregate percentile for rank 2 is 11%. Academic grading in South Korea 72.6% of students at HCU in 2016 got rank 1 or rank 2 for their CSAT grade.

Kenneth Francis (born December 1, 1969) is an American NASCAR crew chief. He began his racing career at the age of eight, racing go-karts. He continued to drive the race car until 1996 when he began working on engines for Butch Mock Motorsports. Six years later, Francis became a crew chief for the first time while he was employed at Robert Yates Racing. He is currently employed at Hendrick Motorsports as the Vehicle Technical Director organization-wide.

Jhajhar is a village, situated in the erstwhile province of Shekhawati of Rajasthan, India. It is located in the district of Jhunjhunu, approximately 7 km from Nawalgarh. Formally it was the part of Pentalisa of Bhojyana.

The 1986 New York Mets season was one of the most memorable in Major League Baseball history, culminating in the team winning the World Series. Managed by Davey Johnson, the Mets dominated the National League East with a franchise-record 108 wins, finishing 21.5 games ahead of the second-place Philadelphia Phillies. The team featured a mix of rising stars like Dwight Gooden and Darryl Strawberry, and seasoned veterans such as Keith Hernandez and Gary Carter. 

The Mets' postseason run included a dramatic National League Championship Series victory over the Houston Astros and a legendary World Series against the Boston Red Sox. Game 6 of the World Series, highlighted by Mookie Wilson's ground ball through Bill Buckner's legs, remains one of baseball's most iconic moments. The Mets ultimately defeated the Red Sox in seven games, securing their second championship in franchise history. The season was later chronicled in the ESPN documentary *Once Upon a Time in Queens*.

Design studies is an interdisciplinary field that examines the practices, impacts, and cultural significance of design. It explores design's role in shaping society, ethics, and the environment, while addressing issues like decolonization, sustainability, and agency. Emerging in the 20th century, it integrates methodologies from design history, research, and social sciences to analyze design as both a practice and a reflection of societal values. Key areas of study include design ethics, the artificial, and actor-network theory, with research methods such as ethnography and semiotics. Prominent organizations like the Design Research Society and Design History Society support the field's development.

Romy Simpkins (born 2 November 1993) is an English mental health advocate, beauty pageant titleholder, and model. She has held several titles, including Miss Dorset 2015, Miss Global International UK 2015, Miss International United Kingdom 2016, and Miss Supranational England 2018. Simpkins has also appeared in television and film productions and worked as a part-time model.

Donald Clarke Sweeney (born August 17, 1966) is a Canadian former ice hockey defenceman who played over 1,100 games in the National Hockey League (NHL), mostly with the Boston Bruins. He ranks among the top ten in many Bruins team statistics, including fourth overall in total games played. After retiring from hockey following the 2003–04 season, he worked briefly as a broadcaster before rejoining the Bruins as a team executive in 2006.

He is the current general manager of the Bruins, a position he has held since May 2015 after replacing Peter Chiarelli.

The South African Reserve Bank (SARB) is the central bank of South Africa. It was established in 1921 after Parliament passed an act, the "Currency and Bank Act of 10 August 1920", as a direct result of the abnormal monetary and financial conditions which World War I had brought. The SARB was only the fourth central bank established outside the United Kingdom and Europe, the others being the United States, Japan and Java. The earliest suggestions for the establishment of the Central Bank in South Africa date back to 1879. A select committee, of ten members of Parliament, was established on 31 March 1920 to examine the benefits to the national interest of the establishing of the central bank.

Following on the recommendations of the committee, the South African Reserve Bank opened for business on 30 June 1921, making it the oldest central bank in Africa. The first banknotes were issued to the public by the Bank on 19 April 1922. Set of ZAR notes 2012 to present R 104 000 000 000.00 and Set of ZAR notes 2018 to present 400 000 000 print.

Unlike the Bank of England, which provided the model for establishing the SARB, the SARB is privately owned.

VirusTotal is a website created by the Spanish security company Hispasec Sistemas. Launched in June 2004, it was acquired by Google in September 2012. The company's ownership switched in January 2018 to Chronicle, a subsidiary of Google.

VirusTotal aggregates many antivirus products and online scan engines called Contributors. In November, 2018, the Cyber National Mission Force, a unit subordinate to the U.S. Cyber Command became a Contributor. The aggregated data from these Contributors allows a user to check for viruses that the user's own antivirus software may have missed, or to verify against any false positives. Files up to 650 MB can be uploaded to the website, or sent via email (max. 32MB). Anti-virus software vendors can receive copies of files that were flagged by other scans but passed by their own engine, to help improve their software and, by extension, VirusTotal's own capability. Users can also scan suspect URLs and search through the VirusTotal dataset. VirusTotal uses the Cuckoo sandbox for dynamic analysis of malware. VirusTotal was selected by PC World as one of the best 100 products of 2007.

In particle physics, annihilation is the process that occurs when a subatomic particle collides with its respective antiparticle to produce other particles, such as an electron colliding with a positron to produce two photons. The total energy and momentum of the initial pair are conserved in the process and distributed among a set of other particles in the final state. Antiparticles have exactly opposite additive quantum numbers from particles, so the sums of all quantum numbers of such an original pair are zero. Hence, any set of particles may be produced whose total quantum numbers are also zero as long as conservation of energy and conservation of momentum are obeyed.

During a low-energy annihilation, photon production is favored, since these particles have no mass. High-energy particle colliders produce annihilations where a wide variety of exotic heavy particles are created.

The word "annihilation" takes use informally for the interaction of two particles that are not mutual antiparticles – not charge conjugate. Some quantum numbers may then not sum to zero in the initial state, but conserve with the same totals in the final state. An example is the "annihilation" of a high-energy electron antineutrino with an electron to produce a W boson-.

If the annihilating particles are composite, such as mesons or baryons, then several different particles are typically produced in the final state.

Edith Guerrier (1870–1958) was an American librarian, social reformer, and advocate for immigrant communities. She is best known for founding the Saturday Evening Girls, a reading club for immigrant girls in Boston's North End, which evolved into the Paul Revere Pottery, providing artistic and economic opportunities. Guerrier's innovative library programs and community initiatives emphasized education, cultural integration, and gender equality.

William Henry Seaman (November 15, 1842 – March 8, 1915) was an American lawyer and judge from Wisconsin. He served as a United States federal judge for 22 years, first in the Eastern District of Wisconsin, then on the Seventh Circuit.

In an international context Denmark is viewed as a somewhat peculiar country when it comes to internet access. The former state owned telephone company TDC owns the entire last mile infrastructure in terms of copper telephone lines and the vast majority of the coaxial cable infrastructure as well. Even though the Danish telecommunications infrastructure is very heavily dominated by one company, Danish internet customers still enjoy fair prices and a wide availability of different next generation access internet connections in comparison with most other EU countries. Furthermore, TDCs de facto monopoly on last mile infrastructure has come under attack. In the last decade regional power companies have formed national business alliances aimed at implementing FTTH for private and business end users.

In 2012, Denmark was ranked third by OECD in terms of wired broadband subscriptions per 100 inhabitants (see the bar chart below). The same year 99,9 % of all households and companies were able to connect to the internet via a broadband connection of at least 2 Mbit/s. As of 2015, 1.3 million Danish households are connected to the internet via TDC's coax and fiber, all of whom will soon have the opportunity to receive one gigabit per second connection.

In 2012, Denmark performed poorly in terms of next generation access in rural areas compared to several other countries in the EU and the US.

The second season of *Top Chef Masters* premiered on Bravo from April 7 to June 9, 2010, featuring 22 chefs competing in culinary challenges to win money for their chosen charities. Hosted in a 10-episode format, the season included Quickfire and Elimination challenges, with the latter determining progression to the Champions' Round. Marcus Samuelsson emerged as the winner, earning $100,000 for his charity.

The Störtebeker Festival (Lang-de) is an yearly open-air theatre festival in Germany. It is based on stories around the medieval German privateer Klaus Störtebeker and his Victual Brothers, who later turned to pirates.

Founded in 1959 as part of an East German cultural initiative, the festival has become Germany's most successful open-air theatre event, and is broadcast by public television network NDR. It is held in the small town of Ralswiek on the isle of Rügen.
Nikos Filis (born 1960) is a Greek politician, journalist, and member of the Hellenic Parliament for Syriza since 2015. He served as Minister of Education and Religious Affairs from 2015 to 2016.

The Long-a-Coming Depot, built in 1856 in Berlin, New Jersey, is a historic Italianate-style train station originally serving the Camden and Atlantic Railroad. Renamed Berlin Station in 1867, it closed in the 1960s. Restored in 1994 by the Long-a-Coming Historical Society, it now serves as a meeting space. It was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1997.
The *Dartmouth Jack-O-Lantern* is a student-run humor magazine at Dartmouth College, founded in 1908 and published quarterly.

*Myrica cerifera*, commonly known as southern bayberry or wax myrtle, is a versatile evergreen shrub or small tree native to the southeastern United States, Central America, and the Caribbean. Adaptable to various habitats, it is valued for its aromatic leaves, wax-coated fruit, and nitrogen-fixing roots. Historically, its wax was used for making candles and soap, and it has applications in herbal medicine and ornamental gardening.

*The Underground Railroad* is a 2016 novel by American author Colson Whitehead. It reimagines the historical Underground Railroad as a literal subterranean train system used by enslaved people to escape to freedom. The story follows Cora, an enslaved woman in Georgia, as she flees northward, encountering various dangers and moral dilemmas along the way. The novel explores themes of slavery, freedom, and systemic racism, drawing parallels to historical and contemporary issues.

The book received widespread critical acclaim, winning the 2017 Pulitzer Prize for Fiction, the 2016 National Book Award for Fiction, and other honors. It was also selected for Oprah's Book Club. In 2021, a television adaptation directed by Barry Jenkins was released on Amazon Prime Video.

Joseph Biddle Wilkinson Jr. (February 20, 1845 – October 23, 1915) was an American Confederate Army officer, planter, and federal judge. He served on the Board of General Appraisers from 1890 to 1899, appointed by President Benjamin Harrison. A great-grandson of James Wilkinson, he was also Chief Clerk of the New Orleans Customs House and a Virginia Military Institute graduate.
E11EVEN Miami is a multilevel nightclub located in downtown Miami. The night club was opened February 5, 2014.

WU polyomavirus (WU virus), also known as Human polyomavirus 4, is a species of virus in the family *Polyomaviridae* and genus *Betapolyomavirus*. Discovered in 2007 at Washington University, it was identified through shotgun sequencing of respiratory secretions from a pneumonia patient. The virus has a circular double-stranded DNA genome encoding five viral proteins. WU virus is globally distributed, with seroprevalence rates ranging from 54% to 97% in various populations, suggesting primary infection occurs in childhood. While its pathogenicity remains unclear, it may have potential relevance in immunocompromised individuals.

William Henry Seaman (November 15, 1842 – March 8, 1915) was an American lawyer, judge, and Civil War veteran who served as a federal judge on the United States Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit from 1905 to 1915. Previously, he was a United States district judge for the Eastern District of Wisconsin and the 22nd mayor of Sheboygan, Wisconsin. A Democrat, Seaman also served in local government and was a delegate to the 1888 Democratic National Convention.
The Taylor Titch is a British fixed-wing homebuilt aircraft, developed in the 1960s by J.F. Taylor. As of 2015, examples are still being built and flown.

The Chicago Ballers were a professional basketball team based in Chicago, Illinois, that competed in the Junior Basketball Association (JBA) during its inaugural 2018 season. Founded by LaVar Ball, the team was coached by Nicolas Colon.

According to Theravada Buddhism's Pali canon's Buddhavamsa and its commentary, Phussa is the twenty-first of twenty-seven Buddhas who preceded the historical Gotama Buddha. He was also the second Buddha of the Maṇḍa kalpa.

In the Buddhavamsa, he is described as:

Phussa Buddha removed the darkness, calmed the world and gave the Dhamma rain.

Phussa was 58 cubits, or 87 feet tall and was frequently compared with the sun and the moon.

Four Lane Ends Interchange is a transport hub in Longbenton, North Tyneside, England, serving as both a Tyne and Wear Metro station and a bus station. Opened in 1980, it features step-free access, a multi-storey car park, cycle facilities, and art installations. It provides frequent Metro and bus services connecting Newcastle and North Tyneside.

"Eu Quero Tchu, Eu Quero Tcha" (I Want Tchu, I Want Tcha), full title "Eu Quero Tchu Eu Quero Tcha (Tchu Tcha Tcha)" is a single by Brazilian Sertanejo universitário duo João Lucas & Marcelo, released in 2012, and written by Shylton Fernandes. The song was a hit in Brazil, following the success of similar songs such as Michel Teló's "Ai Se Eu Te Pego" and Gusttavo Lima's "Balada", though not as successful in Europe as them.

CubETH is a Swiss satellite project. As of 2015, it is being developed by several Swiss academic and industrial partners. The spacecraft is a single unit CubeSat and therefore 10 x 10 x 10 cm (3.9 x 3.9 x 3.9 in) in size. Its main goal is to prove the feasibility of attitude and orbit determination with low cost commercial off-the-shelf GNSS-receivers.

True Liberty is an American Christian hardcore band, and they primarily play hardcore punk and punk rock. They come from Richmond, Virginia. The band started making music in 2005, and their members are lead vocalist and lead guitarist, Mike Liberty, bassist, Tom Liberty, and drummer and background vocalist, Aaron Liberty. Their first studio album, This Is War, was released in 2007 by Raging Storm Records. They independently released, Brotherhood, that came out in 2008. The studio album, City of the One, was released by Wounded Records, in 2010. Their subsequent studio album, Marked for Life, was released by Thumper Punk Records, in 2012. They released a live album, Give Me True Liberty or Give Me Death, with Thumper Punk Records, in 2014.

Royalty Exchange is an American online marketplace founded in 2011 in Raleigh, North Carolina, that connects investors with royalty owners seeking to sell their income streams. The company facilitates auctions and private syndicates for music royalties and other intellectual property. Acquired by private investors in 2015, Royalty Exchange relocated to Denver, Colorado, and expanded its operations under new leadership. In 2017, it launched Royalty Flow, a subsidiary aimed at acquiring high-profile music catalogs, though its IPO plans were later canceled. The platform has also introduced innovative investment models, such as multi-unit auctions and private syndicates, attracting accredited investors to music royalty assets.

Pennsylvania Route 934 (PA 934) is a 10.9-mile-long (17.5 km) state route located in Lebanon County, Pennsylvania. The southern terminus is at a junction with U.S. Route 322 (US 322)/PA 241 in the South Annville Township hamlet of Fontana. Its northern terminus is at Interstate 81 (I-81) at the southern edge of the Fort Indiantown Gap Military Reservation. The route passes through rural areas along with the community of Annville in between. Along its routing, PA 934 intersects US 422 in Annville and US 22 in the East Hanover Township hamlet of Harper Tavern.

PA 934 was first designated in 1928 to run from US 22 (now US 422) in Annville north to Bellegrove. By 1946, the southern terminus was extended to US 322/PA 241 while the northern terminus was extended to US 22 and PA 343. The northern terminus was extended to PA 72 in Lickdale in 1963 and realigned to PA 72 and PA 443 within the Edward Martin Military Reservation in the late 1960s. In the 1970s, the north end of PA 934 was moved to its current location at I-81.

Eclipse Island is a granite island located off the south coast of Western Australia, covering 99 hectares (245 acres). Named by Captain George Vancouver in 1791 after observing a solar eclipse, it is a Class 1A Nature Reserve and an Important Bird Area, supporting significant populations of seabirds. The island features a lighthouse, weather station, and a historic landing stage.

The International Pool Tour (IPT) was a professional pool tour founded by Kevin Trudeau, aiming to elevate the sport's profile through high-stakes tournaments and a professional dress code reminiscent of pool's earlier eras. The IPT sought to promote the sport by live-streaming matches featuring top players and implementing a formal dress code requiring suits and leather shoes for male competitors. Despite initial success, the tour faced financial difficulties, including the cancellation of events and failure to secure funding. In 2006, an acquisition deal with billionaire Stanley Ho's company fell through, leading to the IPT's demise. While the tour deviated from some traditional pool governing body rules, it was supported by organizations like the Women's Professional Billiards Association and the U.S. Professional Pool Players Association.

Gilad Efrat (born 1969) is an Israeli painter known for his innovative techniques that explore the intersection of abstraction and figuration, often addressing themes of identity, memory, and geopolitical landscapes. A graduate of the Bezalel Academy of Arts and Design and the Hebrew University of Jerusalem, Efrat has exhibited internationally and held teaching positions at institutions such as Shenkar College and Rice University. His works, which include landscapes, portraits, and abstract compositions, often employ a unique method of layering and erasing paint to reveal images. Efrat's notable exhibitions include a large-scale solo show at The Israel Museum in 2019.

John Ricus Couperus (24 February 1816 – 13 October 1902) was a Dutch lawyer, colonial administrator, and composer. Born in the Dutch East Indies, he held various legal and administrative positions, including membership in the High Military Court and the Council of Justice in Padang. Couperus was also a vocal critic of colonial policies, publishing influential brochures on land ownership and economic reforms. After retiring, he returned to the Netherlands, where he oversaw the construction of his family home in The Hague. He was the father of renowned Dutch author Louis Couperus.
Masʿūd II of Ṭur ʿAbdin (d. 1509 or 1512) was a Syriac Orthodox patriarch, bishop, abbot, and mystical writer.

Charles Van Dell Johnson (August 25, 1916 – December 12, 2008) was an American film, television, theatre and radio actor. He was a major star at Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer during and after World War II.

Johnson was described as the embodiment of the "boy-next-door wholesomeness" which made him a popular Hollywood star in the 1940s and 1950s, playing "the red-haired, freckle-faced soldier, sailor, or bomber pilot who used to live down the street" in MGM films during the war years, with such films as Thirty Seconds Over Tokyo, A Guy Named Joe, and The Human Comedy. He made occasional World War II films through the end of the 1960s, and he played a military officer in one of his final feature films in 1992. At the time of his death in 2008, he was one of the last surviving matinee idols of Hollywood's "golden age".

Paul Speratus (13 December 1484 – 12 August 1551) was a German Lutheran minister, hymn writer, and a key figure in the early Reformation. Originally a Roman Catholic priest, he became a follower of Martin Luther and contributed to the first Lutheran hymnal, the *Achtliederbuch* (1524). Speratus is best known for his hymn *Es ist das Heil uns kommen her*, which emphasizes salvation by faith alone. After facing persecution and excommunication, he served as court chaplain to Albert, Duke of Prussia, and later as the Protestant bishop of Pomesania, where he defended Lutheran theology until his death.

Shaarey Zedek Cemetery (Hebrew: בית החיים של שערי צדק) is a Jewish cemetery located in Winnipeg, Manitoba, Canada. Established in 1894 by the Shaarey Zedek Synagogue, it is the largest Jewish cemetery in the Canadian Prairies, spanning 4 hectares (9.9 acres). The cemetery contains over 8,000 graves, including those of prominent rabbis and the Bronfman family. It features a war memorial honoring Jewish servicemen and an interfaith burial ground opened in 2012. Notable events include a 1918 "black wedding" during the Spanish flu pandemic and instances of vandalism in 1968 and 1993.

179 Klytaemnestra is a stony asteroid in the outer region of the main asteroid belt, discovered on November 11, 1877, by James Craig Watson at the Ann Arbor Observatory. It is the largest member of the Telramund family of asteroids and orbits the Sun every 5.12 years at a distance of 2.6–3.3 AU. Classified as an S-type asteroid, it has a rotation period of 11.17 hours and a diameter ranging from 64 to 90 kilometers. The asteroid is named after Clytemnestra, a figure from Greek mythology.
"Like a Man" is a 2017 tropical house single by Onhel featuring Lil Wayne. Produced by Onhel, it features Auto-Tuned vocals and was officially released following an early leak.

Stanstead is a village and civil parish in the Babergh district of Suffolk, England, with a population of 319 as of the 2011 Census. Located in the Glem Valley northeast of Sudbury, the village's name derives from the Anglo-Saxon term for "stony place," reflecting its glacial flint-strewn fields. The village features a "T-shaped" layout and is served by local schools and limited bus services.

Carroll Ashmore Campbell Jr. (July 24, 1940 – December 7, 2005), was an American Republican Party politician who served as the 112th governor of South Carolina from 1987 to 1995. Prior to this, he served as a member of the South Carolina Senate representing the 2nd district from 1977 to 1978 and as a congressman representing South Carolina's 4th district from 1979 to 1987.

Qualified teacher status (QTS) or Qualified teacher learning and skills status (QTLS) is required in England and Wales to work as a teacher of children in state schools under local authority control, and in special education schools. A similar status exists under a different name in Scotland and Northern Ireland.

William Scott Husel (born November 11, 1975) is an American physician who was accused of prescribing excessive doses of fentanyl to patients during his tenure as an intensive-care doctor at Mount Carmel West Hospital in Columbus, Ohio. Between 2015 and 2018, 34 patient deaths were linked to his prescriptions, leading to his indictment on 25 counts of murder in 2019. His trial began in February 2022, with the defense arguing the medications were intended for palliative care. On April 20, 2022, Husel was acquitted of all charges. He later surrendered his medical license.
Ellis Bitter, also called Ellis's Bitter or Ellis's Bitter-Sweet, is an English cider apple originating in Devon, but now widely planted across the west of England.
Bill Long (28 April 1932 – 21 May 2010) was an Irish writer and broadcaster. He often featured on RTÉ Radio 1. He was also Ireland's longest surviving heart transplant patient.

Arti Rana (born c. 1976) is an Indian social entrepreneur and member of the Tharu community, known for empowering women through self-help groups in the Terai region near the Dudhwa Tiger Reserve. Under the National Rural Livelihood Mission, she established initiatives producing handicrafts from materials like moonj grass and jute, training and employing hundreds of women. Rana has received several honors, including the Rani Laxmibai Bravery Award, Gram Swaraj Award, and the Nari Shakti Puraskar, India’s highest award for women, in 2022.

Doğuş Balbay (IPA-tr; born 21 January 1989) is a Turkish professional basketball player and the team captain for Anadolu Efes Istanbul of the Turkish Basketbol Süper Ligi (BSL) and the EuroLeague. He is 6 ft 1 in (1.85 m) tall and he plays at the point guard position.

The Dickinson Micropolitan Statistical Area, as defined by the United States Census Bureau, is an area consisting of two counties in North Dakota, anchored by the city of Dickinson. As of the 2010 census, the μSA had a population of 24,982 (a July 1, 2019 estimate placed the population at 32,417).

The Society for the Rise of Kurdistan (SAK) was a Kurdish political organization established in 1919 to advocate for Kurdish self-determination, cultural preservation, and political rights. Led by figures such as Abdulkadir Ubeydullah and Emin-Ali, the society sought Kurdish autonomy and aligned its goals with the Wilsonian Fourteen Points. It represented Kurdish interests at the Paris Peace Conference and supported uprisings like the Koçgiri rebellion. The society also promoted Kurdish language and culture through publications like *Jîn*. Internal disputes and Turkish opposition led to its dissolution and the execution of key leaders in 1925.

Jack Wong Sue, OAM, DCM, JP (12 September 1925 – 16 November 2009), also known as Jack Sue(Chinese: 黄如彩), was a Chinese Australian from Perth, Western Australia. Wong Sue served as a member of the commando/special reconnaissance section, Z Special Unit, during the Second World War and was decorated with the Distinguished Conduct Medal. After the war, Wong Sue was a businessman, owning a diving store in the Perth suburb of Midland. He was also an author, a guide for tours of Borneo and a musician, who performed with bands in Perth for about 60 years.

Mike Albert is an American guitarist and songwriter known for his work across genres such as heavy metal, rock, blues, and jazz fusion. Active since the 1970s, he has collaborated with notable artists and bands, including Ruben and the Jets, El Chicano, and Megadeth. Albert has also worked as a session musician for commercials, movie soundtracks, and live performances, and has led several projects, including the Mike Albert Project and the Albert/Schroeder Experience. His albums, such as *Afterlife* (2009) and *Crime of Passion* (2014), have received critical acclaim. Albert continues to perform and collaborate internationally, including with Indian violinist L. Shankar.

Lydia Sokolova (born Hilda Tansley Munnings; 4 March 1896 – 5 February 1974) was an English ballerina, best known as the principal character dancer of Sergei Diaghilev's Ballets Russes from 1913 to 1929. Renamed Sokolova by Diaghilev, she gained acclaim for her role as the Chosen Maiden in *The Rite of Spring* and other notable performances. After the Ballets Russes disbanded, she returned to England to teach, choreograph, and write her memoir, *Dancing for Diaghilev*.
Hastings is a city in Mills County, Iowa, United States. The population was 152 at the time of the 2020 census.

Thomas Alexander Fraser, 12th Lord Lovat and 1st Baron Lovat, KT (17 June 1802 – 28 June 1875) was a Scottish peer. He was also the 21st MacShimidh, the traditional Gaelic Patronym for the Chiefs of the Clan Fraser of Lovat.

The Corps Franc de la Montagne Noire was a French Resistance unit active from April to September 1944 during World War II. Formed in the Montagne Noire region under the direction of the Special Operations Executive, it consisted of diverse nationalities, including French, Spanish Republicans, Russian deserters, and a Jewish platoon. The unit conducted ambushes and raids against German forces but faced heavy losses during a German assault in July 1944. Survivors later joined the First Army in France's liberation.

*Gutter Phenomenon* is the third studio album by American metalcore band Every Time I Die, released on August 23, 2005, through Ferret Records. Produced by Machine, the album blends metalcore with southern rock and features guest vocals from Gerard Way of My Chemical Romance and Daryl Palumbo of Glassjaw. It received critical acclaim, with *Revolver* naming it Album of the Year, and was supported by extensive touring, including appearances at Warped Tour and international shows.

Steve Levy (born August 25, 1959) is an American politician who served as the 7th County Executive of Suffolk County, New York, from 2004 to 2011. A former Democrat who switched to the Republican Party in 2010, Levy previously served in the New York State Assembly (2001–2003) and the Suffolk County Legislature (1985–2000). As County Executive, he focused on fiscal conservatism, affordable housing, environmental preservation, and economic development, including revitalizing Gabreski Airport and attracting Canon's U.S. headquarters to Suffolk County. Levy also prioritized public safety, achieving a significant reduction in violent crime, and maintained flat general fund taxes while improving the county's bond rating. In 2010, he unsuccessfully sought the Republican nomination for New York Governor.

The Mourne Mountains (/mɔːrn/ MORN; Lang-ga), also called the Mournes or Mountains of Mourne, are a granite mountain range in County Down in the south-east of Northern Ireland. They include the highest mountains in Northern Ireland, the highest of which is Slieve Donard at 850 m (2,790 ft). The Mournes are designated an Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty and it has been proposed to make the area Northern Ireland's first national park. The area is partly owned by the National Trust and sees many visitors every year. The Mourne Wall crosses fifteen of the summits and was built to enclose the catchment basin of the Silent Valley and Ben Crom reservoirs.

Titus Haterius Nepos was a Roman official and military officer who served as the prefect of Roman Egypt from 120 to 121 CE. His career, documented in an inscription from Fulginiae and a letter from Vindolanda, included roles such as military tribune, procurator of Roman Armenia, and praefectus vigilum. While governor of Egypt, he famously visited the Colossi of Memnon and heard the statue "sing."

Barry Took (19 June 1928 – 31 March 2002) was an English comedian, writer, and broadcaster, best known for his work in radio and television comedy.
