He co-wrote popular shows such as *The Army Game*, *Bootsie and Snudge*, and *Round the Horne*, often collaborating with Marty Feldman. Took played a key role in bringing together the performers of *Monty Python's Flying Circus* and later hosted *The News Quiz* and *Points of View*. He also authored several books, including his autobiography.
Washington Bogart Cooper (September 18, 1802 – March 30, 1888) was an American portrait painter known for his works depicting prominent Tennesseans, including governors and historical figures.
The Harold Peary Show is a radio situation comedy broadcast in the United States September 17, 1950-June 13, 1951 on CBS. Some sources refer to the program as Honest Harold or The Hal Peary Show.

Give It Up is the second studio album by American musician Bonnie Raitt. Released in 1972 by Warner Bros. Records, Give It Up is an amalgamation of various genres, including folk, blues, R&B, and soft rock. Seven of the ten tracks on the album are covers, and the tracks are either soft sentimental ballads or fast-paced folk rock pieces. Lyrically, Give It Up revolves around Raitt's femininity, relationships, and being comfortable with herself. Raitt recorded the album at Bearsville Studios with producer Michael Cuscuna.

Give It Up was Raitt's first album to reach the Billboard Top LPs & Tapes chart, where it peaked at number 138. The initial reception was positive, as critics liked the vocals and the musicianship throughout the album. Contemporary reviews are also positive, with some critics calling Give It Up a stand-out album within Raitt's discography. In 2012, Rolling Stone ranked Give It Up at number 495 on its list of the 500 Greatest Albums of All Time.

*Madden NFL 25* is a 2013 sports video game developed by EA Tiburon and published by EA Sports as part of the *Madden NFL* series. Released for PlayStation 3, Xbox 360, iOS, and Android on August 27, 2013, it later launched as a title for PlayStation 4 and Xbox One. The game introduced the Ignite engine for next-gen consoles, offering enhanced graphics, real-time physics, and improved gameplay mechanics, including more realistic player movements and environmental interactions. It also marked the return of team relocation in the Connected Franchise mode.

Barry Sanders, a Hall of Fame running back, was featured on the cover for seventh-generation consoles, while Adrian Peterson appeared on the next-gen versions. The game received mostly positive reviews for its gameplay improvements but faced criticism for commentary and repetitive elements. Despite mixed reception for its mobile versions, *Madden NFL 25* sold over 3 million copies.

The divisions of Punjab (Lang-ur), are the first-order administrative bodies of the Punjab Province of Pakistan. In total, there are 10 divisions, which are further divided into districts ranging from three to six per division, depending upon area. Divisions are governed by Commissioners while districts are governed by Deputy Commissioners.

Prostitution in the Democratic Republic of the Congo is legal but related activities are prohibited. The Congolese penal code punishes pimping, running a bawdy house or brothel, the exploitation of debauchery or prostitution, as well as forced prostitution. Activities that incite minors or promote the prostitution of others have been criminalised. The government does little to enforce the law. During the colonial era and the years that followed independence, the Ministry of Health issued calling cards identifying professional sex workers and provided them with medical health checks. However, this system was abandoned in the 1980s. Public order laws are sometimes used against sex workers. Street prostitutes report harassment, violence and extortion from the police. UNAIDS estimated there are 2.9 million sex workers in the country.

Food insecurity and extreme poverty are now the main reasons why women in the DRC become prostitutes. Traders make up the majority of clients, along with officials working for national and international NGOs. Many sex workers earn between $2 and $5 and payment is sometimes made in the form of food or other goods. Prostitutes working in bars and nightclubs receive between $10 and $20, and are known as "Londoners" as they dress like British girls on a Saturday night out. "VIP prostitution" operates from hotels, with sex workers earning between $50 and $100. Some Congolese prostitutes are from abroad or homeless children who have been accused of witchcraft.
San Michele, locally called in dialect, Gésa Granda, is the Roman Catholic parish church located in the city center at Piazza Dante Alighieri in the town Cameri, province of Novara, Piedmont, Italy.

In the Night Room is a 2004 horror-thriller novel by American author Peter Straub, a sequel to his 2003 book Lost Boy, Lost Girl. The work was first published in hardback on October 26, 2004 through Random House and it won the 2004 Bram Stoker Award for Novel. Straub encountered some difficulties while writing In the Night Room and had written several different passages for the work before growing bored with each version before writing it using the same technique he used for its predecessor in which he "reached down inside the book and turned it inside out."

"Just Got Started Lovin' You" is a song co-written and recorded by American country music artist James Otto. It was released in July 2007 as the first single from his album Sunset Man. On the Hot Country Songs chart dated for May 17, 2008, the song has also become Otto's first (and to date, his only) number-one hit. The song was also the number one song on Billboard's year-end 2008 Hot Country Songs chart.

William J. Maddox Jr (May 22, 1921 – January 5, 2001) was a United States Army Aviator and a major general in the United States Army. During his career he amassed over 10,500 flying hours, almost 4,000 of those in combat over Korea, Vietnam and Cambodia.

He served three tours in Vietnam, the last as the commander of an armored division. In 1976, he commanded the United States Army Aviation Center. Maddox was instrumental in identifying the need for air mobility, developing a management structure that ensured the Army met its aviation objectives. Maddox is one of army's most highly decorated aviators.

*The Secret of Roan Inish* is a 1994 independent fantasy drama film written and directed by John Sayles, based on Rosalie K. Fry's novel *Secret of the Ron Mor Skerry*. Set in 1946 Ireland, the story follows Fiona, a young girl sent to live with her grandparents in a coastal village, where she uncovers family lore about selkies—mythical seal-people—and the mysterious disappearance of her baby brother. The film explores themes of folklore, family, and the connection between humans and nature.

Shot primarily in County Donegal, Ireland, the film features cinematography by Haskell Wexler and a traditional Irish score by Mason Daring. Praised for its atmospheric visuals and storytelling, it holds a 96% approval rating on Rotten Tomatoes. A digitally restored version was released in 2020 by the UCLA Film and Television Archive.

Scott L. Thoele (born 1958) was a major general in the National Guard of the United States. His final assignment was as Deputy Commanding General for the Reserve Component of United States Army Forces Command. Thoele previously served as Deputy Commanding General, Army National Guard, United States Army Combined Arms Center, Fort Leavenworth. He retired on 30 September 2015 after 14 years of service in the U.S. Army and 21 years of service in the Illinois Army National Guard.

Hadia Tajik (born 18 July 1983) is a Norwegian politician, lawyer, and journalist who has served as a Member of Parliament for the Labour Party since 2009. She was the youngest minister in Norwegian history and the first Muslim to serve in the cabinet, holding the position of Minister of Culture from 2012 to 2013. She later served as Minister of Labour and Social Inclusion from 2021 to 2022. Tajik has also been the deputy leader of the Labour Party (2015–2022). Born to Pakistani immigrants in Rogaland, she holds degrees in journalism, human rights, and law.

Romy Simpkins (born 2 November 1993) is a British actress, model, Mental health ambassador and beauty pageant titleholder who competed in several beauty pageants. She has represented her country & the United Kingdom internationally in beauty pageants. She competed at the Miss International 2016 pageant in Tokyo, Japan. Romy competed in the Miss Supranational 2018 pageant in Krynica-Zdrój, Poland.

Bertil Stjernfelt (24 July 1917 – 21 January 2017) was a Swedish Navy lieutenant colonel, military historian, and author. Serving from 1940 to 1977, he held key positions, including chief of staff in Gotland Coastal Artillery Defence and commander of the Swedish UN contingent in Cyprus. Stjernfelt was instrumental in establishing Sweden's Coastal Rangers and authored several military history works, including *Alarm i Atlantvallen*. After retiring, he continued research and writing on military history and served in local government.

Condensation polymers are a class of polymers formed through a condensation reaction, where monomers combine and release small molecules such as water as byproducts. Two significant types of condensation polymers are polyamides and polyesters. Polyamides, such as nylons and proteins, are produced through the reaction of carboxylic acids with amines, with water as a byproduct. For example, nylon 66 is synthesized from diamines and dicarboxylic acids, releasing two water molecules per repeat unit. Polyesters, such as polyethylene terephthalate (PET), are formed through the reaction of carboxylic acids with alcohols.

Condensation polymers are notable for their environmental and safety characteristics. They tend to be more biodegradable than addition polymers due to the presence of hydrolyzable bonds, such as peptide or ester linkages, which can be broken down by catalysts or bacterial enzymes. This property makes them more environmentally friendly compared to many other types of synthetic polymers.

The England Boxing National Amateur Championships, formerly known as the ABA Championships, is an annual amateur boxing competition in England, inaugurated in 1881. Organized by England Boxing, it features multiple weight categories and has undergone numerous changes, including rebranding in 2019. The event has historically been a key pathway for English boxers.

The South African Reserve Bank (SARB) is the central bank of South Africa, headquartered in Pretoria. Established on June 30, 1921, it is one of the few central banks globally that is privately owned, with 2 million issued shares held by 696 shareholders as of 2018. Shareholders are limited to owning a maximum of 10,000 shares each and receive a capped annual dividend of 10 South African cents per share, with the remaining profits directed to the South African government. The government has announced plans to nationalize the Reserve Bank.

The SARB is governed by a board of directors, including a Governor, currently Lesetja Kganyago, three Deputy Governors, and eleven other directors. The Governor and Deputy Governors are appointed by the President of South Africa in consultation with the Minister of Finance, while other directors are appointed by the President or elected by shareholders.

The SARB oversees the issuance of the South African rand (ZAR) and manages reserves of approximately 38.92 billion USD. It is responsible for maintaining monetary stability and implementing monetary policy in South Africa. The bank's official website is resbank.co.za.
The National Assembly of Cape Verde is the unicameral legislative body of the country, established on 4 July 1975.
Chester Creek is a 9.4-mile-long (15.1 km) tributary of the Delaware River in Delaware County, Pennsylvania in the United States.

The Ricoh 2A03 is an 8-bit microprocessor launched in 1983, co-developed by Nintendo and Ricoh for the Nintendo Entertainment System (NES). Based on the MOS Technology 6502, it features a modified instruction set, integrated sound hardware with five audio channels, and support for game controller polling. Regional variations, such as the Ricoh 2A07, were adapted for PAL systems.
Lev Dodin (born 1944) is a Russian theater director, known for his long tenure as artistic director of the Maly Drama Theater since 1982.

Wally Bear and the NO! Gang is an educational Nintendo Entertainment System game that was released in 1992 exclusively for a North American audience. It was not licensed by Nintendo. The game teaches children to say no to potentially harmful drugs like tobacco, alcohol and marijuana. Wally Bear and the NO! Gang was designed in cooperation with, and endorsed by, the American Medical Association and the National Clearinghouse for Alcohol and Drug Information.

Kanyaka is an Indian magazine in Malayalam primarily addressing women's issues. It is a fortnightly magazine published by the Mangalam Publications India Pvt. Ltd., Kottayam. Claramma Varghese is Chief Editor and Toshma Biju Varghese is the Managing Editor. Sajil Sreedhar is the Editor-In-Chief. The headquarters is in Mumbai.

The magazine contains features on current affairs, stories, poems, cartoons, interviews of celebrities and uncelebrated common personalities. The magazine is dedicated to the uplifting women in Indian society as well as in the societies of other countries.
Desiderio Macías Silva was a Mexican poet, professor, and university president. He won the National Poetry Prize Aguascalientes and inspired the motto of the Autonomous University of Aguascalientes.

The Roy model is an economic framework introduced by Andrew Roy to analyze self-selection in occupational choices based on individual skills and technology. It was later formalized mathematically by George Borjas to study immigration, showing how selection mechanisms influence expected wages. The model has since been applied in various fields, including labor economics, where it underpins methods like the Heckman correction and serves as an alternative to the LATE framework.

The Irish Centre for High-End Computing (ICHEC), established in 2005, is Ireland's national high-performance computing center, headquartered in Dublin and Galway. Operated by NUI Galway, ICHEC manages supercomputers, including Kay and its predecessor Fionn, and conducts research in computational science, hardware acceleration, and big data analytics.

Hopanoids are a class of pentacyclic triterpenoid lipids produced by various bacteria and some eukaryotes. Structurally similar to sterols in eukaryotes, hopanoids play critical roles in bacterial plasma membranes, enhancing rigidity, reducing permeability, and aiding adaptation to extreme environments. They are synthesized from squalene through a series of enzymatic reactions, including cyclization by squalene-hopene cyclase, and are often further functionalized by additional enzymes. Hopanoids are particularly abundant in nitrogen-fixing bacteria, where they contribute to stress tolerance and membrane stability, and in survival structures like akinetes in cyanobacteria.

Hopanoids are geologically significant due to their stability, persisting as molecular fossils (hopanes) over billions of years. They have been used as biomarkers to study ancient life and environmental conditions, though their association with specific processes, such as oxygenic photosynthesis, has been debated. Recent research suggests broader roles for hopanoids in microbial communities and environmental adaptation.

Beyond biology, hopanoids have applications in agriculture as biofertilizers and are being explored for their role in multidrug resistance mechanisms in bacteria. Their biosynthetic pathways and enzymatic mechanisms have also inspired advancements in chemical engineering.

Steven A. Levy (pronounced LEE-vee; born August 25, 1959) is an American politician and lawyer who served as the seventh County Executive of Suffolk County, New York, elected on November 4, 2003. Originally a fiscally conservative Democrat, Levy joined the Republican Party in an unsuccessful bid for the Republican nomination for governor.

Shaking tent ceremony is a ritual of some of the indigenous people in North America that is used to connect the people with the spirit realm and establish a connection and line of communication between the spirit world and the mortal world. These ceremonies require special tents or lodges to be made, and are performed under the direction of a shaman, or spiritual leader, who uses different practices, rituals, and materials to perform the ceremony. This ceremony is more commonly used by specific indigenous tribes long ago but is still practiced around the continent today.

Tori Bowie (August 27, 1990 – May 2023) was an American track and field athlete specializing in sprints and the long jump. A three-time Olympic medalist, she won gold in the 4×100 meter relay, silver in the 100 meters, and bronze in the 200 meters at the 2016 Rio de Janeiro Olympics. Bowie also claimed gold in the 100 meters at the 2017 World Championships in London, solidifying her status as one of the world's top sprinters.

Born in Sand Hill, Mississippi, Bowie excelled in track and field during high school and earned an athletic scholarship to the University of Southern Mississippi. Competing in NCAA Division I, she won multiple Conference USA titles and two NCAA long jump championships, setting school records in the process.

Turning professional in 2013, Bowie quickly rose to prominence, achieving personal bests of 10.78 seconds in the 100 meters, 21.77 seconds in the 200 meters, and 6.95 meters in the long jump. She competed on the IAAF Diamond League circuit and represented the United States at numerous international competitions, earning a total of six major championship medals during her career.
Joseph Buck (born December 10, 2002) is an American soccer player who plays as a midfielder for the Georgetown Hoyas.
The Rechtboomssloot, or Recht Boomssloot, is a canal in the Lastage (Nieuwmarktbuurt) neighborhood in the center of Amsterdam.

KGGF (690 AM) is a talk radio station licensed to Coffeyville, Kansas, serving Southeast Kansas, Eastern Oklahoma, Southwest Missouri, and Northwest Arkansas. Established in 1930, it originally broadcast at 1010 kHz before moving to its current frequency of 690 kHz in 1941 under the North American Regional Broadcasting Agreement. The station operates at 10,000 watts during the day and 5,000 watts at night. Owned by SEK Media, LLC, KGGF features a mix of local news, farm reports, and nationally syndicated conservative talk shows, with hosts such as Sean Hannity and Dave Ramsey. Weekend programming includes specialty shows on gardening, technology, and old-time radio.

Carmen Contreras Bozak (December 31, 1919 – January 30, 2017) was a Puerto Rican U.S. Army technician and the first Hispanic woman to serve in the Women's Army Corps during World War II. She transmitted encoded messages in General Dwight D. Eisenhower's headquarters in North Africa. After the war, she became a community leader, founding chapters of WAC Veterans and the Society of Military Widows. Contreras Bozak was buried in Arlington National Cemetery.

Marcus Furius Camillus was a Roman senator and consul in 8 AD, belonging to the ancient gens Furia, which had declined in prominence by his time. He served as Proconsul of Africa in 17 AD, where he successfully suppressed a major revolt led by the Numidian insurgent Tacfarinas, despite facing significant challenges in guerrilla warfare. For his victory, Emperor Tiberius awarded him triumphal ornaments, a rare honor for someone outside the Julio-Claudian dynasty. Camillus was the father of Livia Medullina Camilla and Lucius Arruntius Camillus Scribonianus, the latter known for leading a failed coup against Emperor Claudius.

Michelle Stephenson (born 3 January 1977) is an English singer and television presenter, best known as an original member of the girl group Touch, which later became the Spice Girls. She left the group in 1994 after a brief tenure, citing personal reasons, including her mother's illness, though other accounts suggest differing explanations. Following her departure, Stephenson pursued a career in television presenting and music, working as a session singer and releasing an EP titled *SHESongs* in 2003.

The 2010 South Carolina gubernatorial election took place on November 2, 2010. Incumbent Republican Governor Mark Sanford was term limited and unable to seek re-election. Primary elections took place on June 8, 2010, and a runoff election, as was necessary on the Republican side, was held two weeks later on June 22.

Republican Nikki Haley defeated Democrat Vincent Sheheen in the general election by a margin of 4.5%. As of 2018, this is the closest that the Democrats have come to winning the governorship of South Carolina since their last victory in 1998. This is the 1st open seat election since 1994.

The French Covered Court Championships its original name also known as the French Covered Court Open Championships and the French Indoors was a tennis event held from 1895 through 1971 in Paris, France and Lyon, France.

Abu Thumama al-Sa'idi (died October 10, 680) was a companion of Ali ibn Abi Talib, Hassan ibn Ali, and Hussain ibn Ali, known for his role in the events leading to the Battle of Karbala, where he was killed. A prominent Shi'a figure in Kufa, he supported Hussain ibn Ali, managed financial affairs, and fought valiantly on the day of Ashura.
Abderahmane Hachoud (born July 2, 1988) is an Algerian footballer who plays as a defender.

David John Lee (born 26 November 1969) is an English football coach and former professional player.

Lee played as a defender from 1988 until 2004, notably played in the Premier League for Chelsea and in the Australian top flight for Parramatta Power. He also played in the Football League for Reading, Plymouth Argyle, Portsmouth, Sheffield United, Bristol Rovers, Crystal Palace, Colchester United and Exeter City. He then went on to play in Non-league for Havant & Waterlooville, Forest Green Rovers, Weston-super-Mare, Yate Town and Mangotsfield United. He was capped ten times by England U21.

After retiring became a scout for Bristol City and later assistant manager of Northampton Town where he briefly managed in a caretaker role.

Slaughter Studios is a 2002 comedy/horror film. It was directed by Brian Katkin, with the screenplay by John Huckert and Dan Acre, from a story by Huckert, Acre, and Damian Akhavi. The film, 86 minutes long and rated R, was released through New Concorde.

It was the last film shot at Roger Corman's studios in Venice Beach, California, and it only had a 12-day shooting schedule. The studio was being torn down during the production.

The film began life as a remake of Slumber Party Massacre, and writers John Huckert and Dan Acre were hired to pen the script. But after visiting the soon-to-be-demolished studio they came up with this idea instead. Producer Damien Akhavi helped them write the story. Footage from the original Slumber Party Massacre can still be seen during a murder sequence as an in-joke for the production crew.

Though principal photography took place in February 2001, the final sequences were not filmed until July 2002. The long break was necessitated by the fact that the scenes were to be shot in Malibu, but the rainfall was unusually heavy, so the completion of the film was postponed. Director Katkin shot another film during the break in this movie's production.

"Like a Surgeon" is a song recorded by American recording artist Ciara. Written by The-Dream and produced by Tricky Stewart, the song is included on her third studio album, Fantasy Ride. It was planned to be released as the third single from the album in North America, but its release was cancelled. It still manage to chart on the lower half of US R&B charts.

The song's promotion began on MTV. During a commercial for the album, Ciara sang part of the song in a cappella.

Allobates femoralis (common name brilliant-thighed poison frog, brilliant-thighed poison-arrow frog) is a species of frog in the family Aromobatidae. It is found in Bolivia, Brazil, Colombia, Ecuador, French Guiana, Guyana, Peru, and Suriname. Its natural habitat is tropical lowland forests.

Titus Haterius Nepos was an eques who held a number of imperial Roman positions during the reigns of Trajan and Hadrian. The two most important posts were praefectus vigilum or commander of the vigiles or nightwatch, and praefectus or governor of Egypt (120-124). According to Fergus Millar, Nepos was the first eques who was promoted from regular procuratorial posts into a proper secretarial appointment.

Jean Messiha (born Hossam Boutros Messiha on 10 September 1970) is a French economist, civil servant, political advisor, and commentator of Egyptian origin. Born in Cairo to a Coptic Christian family, he moved to France at the age of 8 and became a naturalized French citizen in 1990, changing his first name to Jean. A graduate of Sciences Po and the École nationale d'administration, he began his career as a high-ranking civil servant and later became an advisor to Marine Le Pen and a prominent figure in the National Rally. Known for his controversial views, including support for the Great Replacement theory, Messiha left the National Rally in 2020 and joined Éric Zemmour's Reconquête party in 2022.

*Nactus kunan* is a species of gecko in the family Gekkonidae, described in 2012. Endemic to Manus Island in the Admiralty Islands, it is known from two specimens and likely inhabits forested highlands. Distinguished by its striking black and golden yellow banding and unique head pattern, *Nactus kunan* is bisexual, unlike the parthenogenetic *Nactus pelagicus*. Its conservation status is listed as Data Deficient.

Zeitenspiegel is a German journalism agency founded in 1976 by Uli Reinhardt and headquartered in Weinstadt, Germany. Specializing in print and news publishing, the agency reported a revenue of €1.7 million in 2008. Zeitenspiegel operates the Zeitenspiegel-Reportageschule Günter Dahl, a journalism academy that offers one-year courses to train aspiring feature journalists. The academy, taught by Zeitenspiegel members and renowned professionals, has been recognized as one of the top journalism schools in Germany.

Since 1998, Zeitenspiegel has awarded the Hansel Mieth Prize, honoring its honorary member Johanna "Hansel" Mieth, for outstanding reporting on socially relevant topics. The prize is given to a reporter-photographer team, while the Gabriel Grüner Grant supports emerging journalists in researching promising stories. Both awards reflect Zeitenspiegel's commitment to high-quality, socially conscious journalism.

Charles Percy Abbott (born 10 June 1939) is a former Australian rules footballer who played 17 games for Hawthorn in the Victorian Football League (VFL) from 1961 to 1963. Educated at Caulfield Grammar School and the University of Melbourne, Abbott excelled in academics, sports, and amateur football before joining Hawthorn. After retiring from football in 1964 to focus on his legal career, he became a prominent lawyer and corporate leader, holding various chairmanships and directorships. Abbott was also a skilled polo player, serving as President of the Victorian Polo Association and receiving the Australian Sports Medal in 2000 for his contributions to the sport.

Dimitar Dobrev (February 12, 1868 – April 11, 1944) was a Bulgarian naval officer and lawyer who served in the Bulgarian Navy, Russian Imperial Navy, and participated in key conflicts, including the Serbo-Bulgarian War, Russo-Japanese War, and Balkan Wars.

Woodford Island is an inland island in the Far-North Coast of New South Wales, Australia. It is formed by the Clarence River where it splits into the South Arm and North Arm at the small village of Brushgrove then reforms at the town of Maclean. The island is the largest in NSW, either inland or offshore, at 37 km2.

*When Hope Calls* is an American family drama and Western television series created by Alfonso H. Moreno. A spin-off of *When Calls the Heart*, the series follows orphaned sisters Lillian and Grace, who reunite as adults in 1916 to open an orphanage in the fictional Canadian town of Brookfield. The show premiered on August 30, 2019, as Hallmark Movies Now's first original streaming series and later moved to GAC Family for its second season in 2021. Filmed in Powassan, Ontario, it stars Morgan Kohan, Jocelyn Hudon, and Lori Loughlin, among others.

Marcq-en-Barœul is a commune in the Nord department in northern France, located within the Hauts-de-France region and part of the Métropole Européenne de Lille. Covering an area of 14.04 km², it had a population of 39,943 in 2022. The town is governed by Mayor Bernard Gérard (2020–2026) and is known for its economic contributions, including being the headquarters of Groupe Holder, which owns Ladurée and Paul. Marcq-en-Barœul features a racecourse, two golf courses, and a prominent rugby club, Olympique Marcquois Rugby, established in 1971. The club is affiliated with Stade Toulousain and supports over 600 players and 350 youth participants.

Jessie Joe Nandye (born 22nd Aug 1985), also commonly known by his former name Jessie Joe Parker, is a Papua New Guinean rugby league footballer who plays as a centre for Whitehaven in Betfred League 1.

He is a Papua New Guinean international representative.

In the theory of von Neumann algebras, a part of the mathematical field of functional analysis, Tomita–Takesaki theory is a method for constructing modular automorphisms of von Neumann algebras from the polar decomposition of a certain involution. It is essential for the theory of type III factors, and has led to a good structure theory for these previously intractable objects.

The theory was introduced by Minoru Tomita (1967), but his work was hard to follow and mostly unpublished, and little notice was taken of it until Masamichi Takesaki (1970) wrote an account of Tomita's theory.

WUFO (1080 kHz) is a commercial AM radio station licensed to Amherst, New York, and serving the Buffalo metropolitan area. It is owned by the Visions Multi Media Group headed by Sheila. L. Brown and it broadcasts a hybrid Urban AC and Classic Hip Hop radio format. Its studios are on Broadway Avenue in Buffalo.

WUFO transmits 1,000 watts non-directional. WUFO is a daytimer station because 1080 AM is a clear-channel frequency reserved for Class A stations WTIC Hartford and KRLD Dallas, so WUFO must sign off at sunset to avoid interference. The transmitter is on Genesee Street in Cheektowaga. Programming is heard around the clock on 35-watt FM translator W243DX at 96.5 MHz. It uses the FM translator frequency in its moniker, "Power 96.5."
Wahyt Sähetmyradowiç Orazsähedow (born 26 January 1992) is a Turkmen professional footballer and forward.

Joseph Harmatz (January 23, 1925 – September 22, 2016) was a Lithuanian-born Jewish partisan, Holocaust survivor, and leader of Nakam, a group dedicated to avenging the deaths of six million Jews during the Holocaust. After surviving the Vilnius Ghetto and joining anti-Nazi guerrillas, Harmatz participated in efforts to poison Nazi war criminals, including a failed plot to poison German water supplies. He later emigrated to Israel, leading World ORT from 1960 to 1994 and aiding Jewish resettlement.
Dar Hammuda Pasha is an old palace in the medina of Tunis. It is considered one of the oldest and biggest palaces of the medina that kept their original architecture.

Owain Sebastian Yeoman (born 2 July 1978) is a Welsh actor known for his roles in film and television. He made his film debut in *Troy* and gained prominence as Wayne Rigsby in *The Mentalist*. Yeoman has also appeared in *Generation Kill*, *American Sniper*, and *Turn: Washington's Spies*. A graduate of Oxford University and the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art, he initially pursued a career in banking before transitioning to acting. Yeoman is a vegetarian and has been featured in a PETA campaign.

Kimbolton is a town and civil parish in England. Kimbolton is about 9 miles (14 km) west of Huntingdon and 14 miles (23 km) north of Bedford. Kimbolton is administered as part of Cambridgeshire; however it is situated within Huntingdonshire, which is an historic county of England and is now a non-metropolitan district of Cambridgeshire. The parish includes the hamlet of Stonely.

Catherine of Aragon, after her divorce from Henry VIII, died at Kimbolton Castle in 1536.

The red flour beetle (*Tribolium castaneum*) is a small beetle of the family Tenebrionidae, known for its reddish-brown color and global distribution in stored food products. Originating from the Indo-Australian region, it is a major pest of grains, flour, and other stored commodities, causing significant economic losses. The species exhibits complex mating behaviors, including polyandry and polygamy, which enhance genetic diversity and reproductive success. It has also been extensively studied as a model organism in genetics and developmental biology, with its genome sequenced in 2008.

The railroad brotherhoods are labor unions of railroad workers in the United States. They first appeared in 1863 and they are still active. Until recent years they were largely independent of each other and of the American Federation of Labor.

Natalia Maliszewska (born 16 September 1995) is a Polish short track speed skater. She is a World Championship silver medalist (2018) and a European Champion (2019) in the 500 metres. Maliszewska also won the 2018–19 ISU Short Track Speed Skating World Cup 500m title, becoming the first Polish skater to achieve this feat. She has competed in multiple Winter Olympics, including the 2018 Games, and was a medal contender in 2022 before testing positive for COVID-19. Her older sister, Patrycja Maliszewska, is also a short track speed skater.
St. Dominic's Abbey is a medieval Dominican abbey and National Monument located in Cashel, Ireland.

The hôtel de la Marine (also known as the hôtel du Garde-Meuble) is an historic building located on place de la Concorde in Paris, to the east of rue Royale. It was designed and built between 1757 and 1774 by the architect Ange-Jacques Gabriel, on the newly created square first called Place Louis XV. The identical building to its west, constructed at the same time, now houses the hôtel de Crillon and the Automobile Club of France.

The Hôtel de la Marine was originally the home of the royal Garde-Mobile, the office managing the furnishing of all royal properties. Following the French Revolution it became the Ministry of the French Navy, which occupied it until 2015. It was entirely renovated between 2015 and 2021. It now displays the restored 18th century apartments of Marc-Antoine Thierry de Ville-d'Avray, the King's Intendant of the Garde-Meuble, as well the salons and chambers later used by the French Navy. A separate part displays the Al Thani Collection presenting international and inter-cultural works of art from the collection of Sheik Hamad bin Abdullah Al Thani.

The National Reserve System (NRS) is Australia's network of protected areas, established in 1992 to conserve the nation's biodiversity and fulfill commitments under the international Convention on Biological Diversity. Spanning over 137 million hectares, it includes public, private, and Indigenous lands, with private landowners entering into perpetual conservation covenants. Indigenous Protected Areas (IPAs) form a significant part of the system, protecting ecologically valuable landscapes.

The NRS was developed with cooperation between the Australian Government and states and territories, with substantial investments through programs like the National Heritage Trust. It has been praised by organizations such as WWF Australia and is considered a key tool for biodiversity conservation and addressing climate change impacts.

Jagadishpur is a census town in Bally Jagachha CD Block of Howrah Sadar subdivision in Howrah district in the Indian state of West Bengal. It is a part of Kolkata Urban Agglomeration.

Jagadishpur is under the jurisdiction of Liluah Police Station of Howrah City Police.

"You're a Big Girl Now" is a song by American singer-songwriter Bob Dylan, featured on his 1975 album *Blood on the Tracks*. Recorded on December 27, 1974, in Minneapolis, the folk rock track explores themes of heartbreak and longing, with critics often linking it to Dylan's personal life, though he denied such interpretations. Praised for its emotional depth, the song has been included in several Dylan compilations and performed live over 200 times.

Annie Buller (December 9, 1895 – January 19, 1973) was a Canadian labor organizer, political activist, and founding member of the Communist Party of Canada. Known for her union organizing and advocacy for workers' rights, she played a key role in strikes and labor movements across Canada, including the 1931 Toronto dressmakers' strike and the Estevan Coal Miners Strike. Buller was imprisoned for her activism and later became a prominent figure in the Labor-Progressive Party. She remained active in political organizing, anti-war efforts, and women's campaigns until her retirement in the late 1950s.

Callington was a rotten borough in Cornwall which returned two Members of Parliament to the House of Commons in the English and later British Parliament from 1585 to 1832, when it was abolished by the Reform Act 1832.

1004 Belopolskya is a Cybele asteroid in the outer main belt, discovered on September 5, 1923, by Sergey Belyavsky at Simeiz Observatory. Named after Russian astrophysicist Aristarkh Belopolsky, it measures approximately 72–80 kilometers in diameter.

Nabû-mukin-apli (reigned c. 974–939 BC) was a king of Babylon during the Dynasty of E, ruling in the midst of the Babylonian dark age. His reign is poorly documented, with limited historical sources. The Religious Chronicle notes interruptions to the Akitu festival due to Aramaean hostilities, and a kudurru from his reign records a legal settlement involving land disputes. He was succeeded by his sons, Ninurta-kudurri-uṣur II and Mar-biti-aḫḫe-idinna.

The Fairchild 9440 MICROFLAME, also known as the F9440 and μFLAME, was a 16-bit microprocessor introduced by Fairchild Semiconductor in 1977. The 9440 implemented the Data General Nova 2's instruction set in a single-chip 40-pin DIP. The name "MICROFLAME" was part of a wider branding exercise called "FIRE", which was a development software system.

An updated version, the 9445, was announced in 1978 but did not reach market until late 1981. By this time the 16-bit designs were being surpassed by 32-bit designs and hybrids like the Motorola 68000, and Fairchild began turning their attention to their own 32-bit Fairchild Clipper design. The underlying core of the 9445 was also used to implement the 9450, which used new microcode to implement the MIL-STD-1750A instruction set.

The 9440 and 9445 were subject to constant lawsuits from Data General (DG) that dragged on both companies. DG finally settled all ongoing litigation in September 1986 by paying Fairchild over $52 million. Fairchild was purchased and sold several times during this period, ending up at National Semiconductor in 1987. National ended production of the 9445, leaving several users in the lurch. A final version, the IDC9445, was introduced to fill this need.

*Primitive Love* is the second English-language studio album by the American band Miami Sound Machine, released on August 13, 1985, by Epic Records. Produced by Emilio Estefan Jr., the album marked the band's breakthrough into mainstream success, reaching #23 on the *Billboard* 200 and ending 1986 at #10 on the *Billboard* Year-End Charts. It featured a blend of dance-pop rhythms and Gloria Estefan's distinctive vocals, which helped the band achieve significant crossover appeal. Four singles were released, including the hits "Conga" (#10), "Bad Boy" (#8), "Words Get in the Way" (#5), and "Falling in Love (Uh-Oh)" (#25).

Trichomonas gallinae is a cosmopolitan parasite of birds including finches, pigeons, doves, turkeys, chickens, parrots, raptors (hawks, golden eagle, etc.). The condition in birds of prey is called frounce. It is believed to be an ancient pathogen causing frounce-like symptoms in theropod dinosaurs. The same condition in pigeons is commonly called canker.

The protozoa are small (5 to 20 μm size) oval or spherical flagellates with four free flagella found on the anterior aspect of the parasite. They also have an axostyle found on the posterior end as well as an undulating membrane on one side. An important diagnostic feature is the lack of a free posterior flagellum.

*Mysterious Girlfriend X* (Japanese: 謎の彼女X, *Nazo no Kanojo X*) is a Japanese romantic comedy manga series written and illustrated by Riichi Ueshiba. It was serialized in *Monthly Afternoon* from 2006 to 2014, with 12 volumes published. The story follows the unconventional relationship between Akira Tsubaki and Mikoto Urabe, centered around a mysterious bond involving Urabe's drool. The series was adapted into a 13-episode anime by Hoods Entertainment in 2012, along with an original video animation.

Annie Buller (9 December 1895 – 19 January 1973), also known as Annie Buller-Guralnick, was a union organizer as well as co-founder of the Communist Party of Canada (CPC) and manager of many CPC publications.

Ecological sanitation (ecosan) is an approach to sanitation that emphasizes the safe recycling of nutrients from human excreta and wastewater back into agriculture, minimizing the use of non-renewable resources. First introduced in 1995, ecosan systems aim to reduce health risks, prevent water pollution, and promote resource recovery, particularly as fertilizers. While often associated with urine-diverting dry toilets (UDDTs), ecosan encompasses a variety of technologies and practices tailored to local conditions.

Ecosan has historical roots in traditional agricultural societies and has been promoted for its potential to address issues like phosphorus scarcity and malnutrition. Despite its benefits, challenges include user acceptance, cost, and scalability. The concept has influenced modern sustainable sanitation initiatives globally.

Benjamin Hoffman (April 15, 1864 – May 20, 1922) was an American lawyer, politician, and judge. A Democrat and Tammany Hall member, he served in the New York State Assembly from 1895 to 1899 and as a Municipal Court Justice until his death.

Mongolia competed at the 2004 Summer Olympics in Athens, Greece, from 13 to 29 August 2004. This was the nation's tenth appearance at the Olympics, except the 1984 Summer Olympics in Los Angeles, because of its partial support to the Soviet boycott.

The Mongolian National Olympic Committee sent a total of 20 athletes, 13 men and 7 women, to compete in 7 different sports, tying its delegation count with Sydney four years earlier. In the wake of Munkhbayar Dorjsuren's transfer to the German team, three Mongolian athletes had previously competed in Sydney, including shooter Otryadyn Gündegmaa in the women's pistol events. Judoka and world champion Damdinsürengiin Nyamkhüü was appointed by the committee to become Mongolia's flag bearer in the opening ceremony, dressed in a traditional costume.

After failing to achieve a single Olympic medal from Sydney, Mongolia left Athens with only a bronze from twenty-year-old judoka Khashbaataryn Tsagaanbaatar on the first day of the Games.

Nabû-mukin-apli, typically inscribed dAG-DU-A, “Nabû (is) establisher of a legitimate heir,” ruled c. 974–939 BC, founded Babylon’s 8th dynasty, the so-called Dynasty of E, and ruled for thirty-six years. The Synchronistic Kinglist records him as a contemporary of the Assyrian king Tiglath-Pileser II. His reign was plagued by Aramean invasions, resulting in Babylon being cut off from its agricultural hinterland for several years and consequently being unable to celebrate the new year festival.

Phantom borders are historical political boundaries that continue to influence contemporary social, cultural, and political landscapes. The concept, articulated through the Phantomgrenzen project, highlights how these former borders persist or re-emerge as markers of historical conquests and annexations. In Germany, the divide between East and West reflects in voting patterns, economic disparities, and cultural preferences, rooted in the legacy of Prussia and East Germany. In Poland, historical partitions by Prussia, Austria, and Russia have shaped regional political tendencies, with western regions favoring liberal parties and central-southern areas leaning conservative. Romania's phantom borders reflect differences between Ottoman and Habsburg rule, influencing ethnic diversity and political behavior. Similarly, Ukraine's historical divisions under various empires manifest in regional electoral and cultural divides.

Petaluma and Santa Rosa Railroad was a 600 volt DC electric interurban railway in Sonoma County, California, United States. It operated between the cities of Petaluma, Sebastopol, Forestville, and Santa Rosa. Company-owned steamboats provided service between Petaluma and San Francisco.

Portions of the original right-of-way have been acquired by Sonoma County government for the West County Trail and Joe Rodota Trail, managed by the Sonoma County Regional Parks Department. A portion of the original right-of-way can be found along the waterfront in Petaluma. Efforts have been made by volunteers to re-activate this line to become a trolley line once again.
Lev Abramovich Dodin (Lang-ru, born 1944) is a modern Russian theater director, the leader of Saint Petersburg Maly Drama Theater.

Dimitar Dobrev Dobrev was a 20th-Century Bulgarian naval officer and an I-Class Admiral. He was a participant in the Serbo-Bulgarian War, Russo-Japanese War, First Balkan War and the Second Balkan War.

The Vilnius Girls' Gymnasium was a secondary school for girls established on January 1, 1860, in Vilnius, then part of the Russian Empire. It was one of the first girls' schools in the region, funded by the Office of the Institutions of Empress Maria. Initially aimed at promoting Russification, the school taught in Russian and faced low enrollment, particularly among non-Russian communities. Over time, the curriculum expanded to include electives like French, German, and drawing, preparing students for limited professional roles. The school admitted Jewish students from 1861 and later allowed Judaism classes. By the late 19th century, enrollment grew significantly, though separate Jewish schools reduced Jewish attendance. Following the Russian Revolution of 1905, parents petitioned for broader inclusivity and curriculum reforms, leading to the introduction of Polish and Lithuanian language lessons. The gymnasium played a significant role in the education of women in the region during the Russian Empire.

The 1982 Massachusetts elections, part of the broader United States elections, were held on November 2, 1982. Key outcomes included the election of Democrats Michael Dukakis as Governor and John Kerry as Lieutenant Governor, alongside victories for incumbents in several statewide offices. Voters also decided on four ballot questions, addressing issues such as public funding for private schools, the death penalty, nuclear power regulations, and a beverage container deposit law.

Milnrow Cricket Club, based in Milnrow, an area of the Metropolitan Borough of Rochdale, Greater Manchester, are an English cricket team who as of 2022 play in the Greater Manchester Cricket League (GMCL).

The club is over 150 years old and celebrated its centenary in 1957 with the publication of a club history. The first professional player for the club was James Rigby, in 1884, but the club has never had a great deal of sporting success when compared to its local rivals. It was a founder member of the Central Lancashire League in 1892.

In 2010 the club received a £57,000 grant from the England & Wales Cricket Board. This was towards the costs of providing artificial practice areas for coaching purposes. Earlier in that year it had also received a smaller grant from Sport England. It has also received funding from, for example, the Co-op.

The club has a junior section and has in the past developed players who have been selected for the Academy of Lancashire Cricket Club, which exists to further develop young talent with the aim of seeing its members become professional players.

For the 2018 and 2019 seasons Milnrow played in the Lancashire League.

*American Zombie* is a 2007 mockumentary horror film directed by Grace Lee that explores the zombie subculture in Los Angeles. Blending satire and social commentary, the film follows a documentary crew investigating the lives of sentient zombies. It premiered at the Slamdance Film Festival and received mixed reviews, with praise for its originality and humor but criticism for its uneven tone and ending.

The center of gravity (CG) of an aircraft is a critical factor in ensuring safe and stable flight. It is calculated along the longitudinal axis, with some helicopters also requiring lateral CG calculations. Proper weight distribution, including fixed and removable items, is essential to keep the CG within manufacturer-prescribed limits. An out-of-range CG can lead to severe control issues, affecting stability, maneuverability, and safety. Aircraft operators must account for fuel burn, passenger distribution, and cargo placement to maintain balance throughout the flight. Helicopters are particularly sensitive to CG shifts.

The Chalfont Viaduct, also known as the Chalfont No. 1 Viaduct or Misbourne Viaduct, is a brick arch railway bridge in Gerrards Cross, England. Built between 1902 and 1906 by the Great Western Railway, it was designed by James Charles Inglis and Robert Cherry Sikes to carry the Chiltern Main Line across the River Misbourne. The viaduct is 104 meters long, 12.5 meters high, and features five semi-elliptical arches. In the 1980s, the M25 motorway was constructed to pass through its arches, with minimal alterations to the structure.

The viaduct gained local fame for its graffiti, including the slogan "give peas a chance," which became a beloved landmark. Subsequent modifications to the graffiti sparked public debate and media attention.
Annie Brisset, a member of the Royal Society of Canada, is a Professor of Translation Studies and Discourse Theory at the School of Translation and Interpretation of the University of Ottawa, Canada.

Freienfeld (Italian: Campo di Trens) is a municipality in South Tyrol, northern Italy, located in the Eisacktal valley. It encompasses several villages, including Trens, Stilfes, and Mauls, and has a population of 2,668 (as of 2010). Historically inhabited since prehistoric times, the area features significant archaeological finds and medieval castles, such as Reifenstein and Welfenstein. Freienfeld is also home to the Sanctuary of Maria Trens, a notable pilgrimage site. The municipality was formed in 1928 by merging three former municipalities and is predominantly German-speaking.

Ali Alipour Ghara (Lang-fa; born 11 November 1995) is an Iranian international footballer who plays as a forward for Gil Vicente.

At the age of 19 years and 185 days, Alipour was the youngest-ever player to score in the Tehran derby after scoring in Persepolis' 1–0 victory on 15 May 2015. He is the only player in Tehran derby history who has scored the winning goal in two derby matches.

WiQuest Communications was a private computer hardware company founded in 2003 in Allen, Texas. Specializing in Wireless USB technology, it held an 85% market share in early 2008, with customers including Toshiba, Dell, and Lenovo. The company ceased operations on October 31, 2008, due to challenges facing Wireless USB technology and economic conditions. Its Bangalore operations were acquired by Staccato Communications.

A debug port is an interface on microprocessors and microcontrollers that facilitates testing, debugging, and programming during development and manufacturing. Microprocessors, with their immense complexity, often include debug ports to manage their billions of transistors. Microcontrollers, which have evolved into system-on-a-chip (SoC) devices, also rely on debug ports for initial configuration, such as setting up clock trees or loading the first software program. These ports are critical for accessing internal flash memory and EEPROM-based configuration fuses.

Since around 2010, many microcontrollers have integrated USB peripherals with Device Firmware Upgrade (DFU) capabilities in factory ROM, appealing to hobbyists by eliminating the need for specialized hardware tools like JTAG. While DFU serves as a software-based debug interface, it lacks the robustness of dedicated hardware debug ports, as it depends on functional software to operate effectively.

East Rennell is the southern portion of Rennell Island in the Solomon Islands which is designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Rennell is the largest raised coral atoll in the world and the area in East Rennell surrounding Lake Tegano contains many endemic species.

In 2013, the World Heritage Committee added East Rennell to the List of World Heritage in Danger because of the threat of logging activities to the site's outstanding universal value.

Primitive Love is the ninth studio album and second English-language record by the Miami Sound Machine, released in August 1985, by Epic Records.

A 2CD remastered version of the album was scheduled for March 31, 2017 by Vibe on Records, but it was postponed because Estefan is working on her new album. To date there is no set date for the re-release; however, the track listing was revealed to the public: it will include five songs that were previously unreleased on CD, sixteen remixes, two soundtrack songs, one demo and the Spanish version of "Words Get in the Way". The album sold over 6 million copies worldwide.

The Slutsk uprising (Lang-be) or the Slutsk defence (Lang-be) was an unsuccessful armed attempt to establish an independent Belarus. It took place in late 1920, near the end of the Polish-Soviet War, in the region of the town of Slutsk. It involved a series of clashes between irregular Belarusian forces loyal to the Belarusian People's Republic and the Soviet Red Army, ending in a Soviet victory.
The Animal Welfare Act 2006 (c 45) is an Act of the Parliament of the United Kingdom.

The Mississippi Wing of the Civil Air Patrol (CAP) is the state-level organization of the CAP in Mississippi, supporting the United States Air Force. With over 500 members as of 2018, it fulfills CAP's three primary missions: emergency services, cadet programs for youth, and aerospace education for members and the public.
USS R-14 (SS-91) was an R-class coastal and harbor defense submarine of the United States Navy.

The Maas–Hoffman model is a mathematical model used to analyze crop tolerance to soil salinity. It represents the relationship between crop yield and salinity through two linear equations, with key parameters including maximum yield, salinity threshold, and the slope of the descending line. The model's parameters are determined using regression analysis to optimize data fit. An inverted version of the model is applied to growth factors, such as watertable depth, that negatively impact crop production at low values.

Patricia Murphy Derian (August 12, 1929 – May 20, 2016) was an American civil rights and human rights activist who served as the first Assistant Secretary of State for Human Rights and Humanitarian Affairs from 1977 to 1981 under President Jimmy Carter. A former nurse and supporter of the Civil Rights Movement, she played a key role in shaping U.S. human rights policy, opposing authoritarian regimes, and advocating for political dissidents worldwide. Derian's efforts significantly influenced global human rights advocacy during and beyond her tenure.

The Liberators' civil war (43–42 BC) was a conflict during the Crisis of the Roman Republic between the forces of the Second Triumvirate—Mark Antony, Octavian, and Lepidus—and the Liberatores, led by Brutus and Cassius, who had assassinated Julius Caesar. The war culminated in the two Battles of Philippi in Macedonia, where the triumvirs decisively defeated the Liberators. Both Cassius and Brutus committed suicide, marking the end of the Republican resistance. The victory solidified the triumvirs' control over Rome, though tensions with the Parthian Empire persisted.

Nnamdi Moweta (born March 27, 1958) is a Nigerian-American radio host, music promoter, producer, and boxing promoter, best known as the host of *Radio Afrodicia*, a long-running program on KPFK that highlights African and African-influenced music. Originally from Jos Plateau, Nigeria, Moweta moved to the United States in 1978 to study filmmaking and later established himself in Los Angeles as a prominent figure in promoting African culture and music. He has managed and produced for notable artists, including Chief Stephen Osita Osadebe, and co-founded Odogwu Entertainment to connect African musicians to global markets. Moweta has also worked as a music supervisor for award-winning films and as a boxing promoter and commentator.

Eucalyptus largiflorens, or black box or river box, is a tree that is endemic to Australia. It has rough, fibrous or flaky bark, dull greenish-grey, lance-shaped leaves, oval to club-shaped green to yellow flower buds, white flowers and hemispherical, cup-shaped or barrel-shaped fruit.

The Animal Welfare Act 2006 is a United Kingdom law that consolidated and updated animal welfare legislation, replacing the Protection of Animals Act 1911. It introduced a duty of care for animal owners, defined "protected animals," and established the "five welfare needs." The Act prohibits tail docking for cosmetic reasons (with exceptions for working dogs) and restricts the sale and gifting of animals to children under 16. It grants inspectors and constables powers to address animal suffering, enforce regulations, and prosecute offences, with penalties including fines and imprisonment.

John William Goode Jr. (March 10, 1923 – February 5, 1994) was an American lawyer, decorated World War II veteran, and Republican politician from San Antonio, Texas. He served as a Marine Corps officer in the South Pacific, earning the Silver Star and Bronze Star for his bravery. After the war, he pursued a legal career, becoming a prominent attorney and Republican leader. Goode was a delegate to the 1960 Republican National Convention and ran unsuccessfully for Congress in 1961. He was inducted into the Bexar County Republican Party Hall of Fame shortly before his death.
Marie Paule Woodson is an American politician serving as a member of the Florida House of Representatives from the 101st district. She assumed office on November 3, 2020.

Ida S. Baker High School is a public secondary school in Cape Coral, Florida, established in 2004. Part of the Lee County School District, it serves grades 9–12 with an enrollment of 1,809 students (2018–19). Named after Ida S. Baker, a pioneering educator, its mascot is Bruiser the Bulldog.

Brodie Smith is a former American Ultimate Disc League player, and current professional disc golf player and YouTube personality best known for his frisbee trick shot videos.

Brodie rose to prominence as a premier ultimate player at the University of Florida, where he won two USA Ultimate College Championships, in 2006 and 2010. He also won two National Club Championships, first in 2012 with Austin's Doublewide, and again in 2014 with Denver's Johnny Bravo. He has played professionally with the AUDL's Indianapolis AlleyCats, Chicago Wildfire, Dallas Roughnecks, and Jacksonville Cannons.

Brodie achieved internet fame in 2011 when his Frisbee Trick Shots video became popular on YouTube. As of July 2018, his channel has over 2 million subscribers and 300 million total video views. Clips of Brodie's trick shots have been featured on ESPN's SportsCenter "Top Plays" segment numerous times and other major media outlets, including the Huffington Post. He has a sponsored line of merchandise produced by DiscStore.com

He appeared in the 28th installment of The Amazing Race alongside Ultimate teammate and friend Kurt Gibson, finishing 5th.

As of 2020 he is a professional disc golf player on the DGPT (Disc Golf Pro Tour). He is currently signed to Discraft.

Gaylord Dewayne Shaw (July 22, 1942 – September 6, 2015) was an American journalist and Pulitzer Prize winner, known for investigative reporting and breaking major news stories, including President Richard Nixon's resignation.

*Rhyssa persuasoria* is a large ichneumon wasp species in the family Ichneumonidae, found across Europe, North Africa, the Near East, and parts of the Indomalayan and Australasian realms. It inhabits coniferous and mixed woodlands. Females use their long ovipositors to lay eggs on wood-boring larvae, which serve as food for their offspring. Adults, active from July to August, feed on carbohydrates like honeydew.

The Aeroprakt A-22 Foxbat is a Ukrainian two-seat, high-wing, tricycle landing gear ultralight aircraft that was designed by Yuri Yakovlev and is manufactured by Aeroprakt. In the United States the A-22 is referred to as the Valor, while in the UK and Australia it is called the Foxbat. It has also been marketed as the Vision. The Aeroprakt A-22 is supplied either as "ready-to-fly" factory built aircraft, or as a kit, consisting of 152 pieces. The kit can be built in about 500 man-hours.

The A-22 meets the definition of an FAI microlight.

Denshaw is a village in the civil parish of Saddleworth in the Metropolitan Borough of Oldham, in Greater Manchester, England. It lies by the source of the River Tame, high amongst the Pennines above the village of Delph, 4.6 miles (7.4 km) northeast of Oldham,3.3 miles (5.3 km) north-northwest of Uppermill and Shaw and Crompton. It has a population of around 500.

Historically a part of the West Riding of Yorkshire, Denshaw and its surroundings have provided archaeological evidence of Stone and Bronze Age activity in the area. The name Denshaw is of Old Norse derivation, and the oldest part of the village is an ancient hamlet.

Built up around the junction of five major roads, until the 20th century, Denshaw consisted mainly of smallholdings and a few public houses such as the Junction Inn, originally built as a coaching house for travellers. Denshaw is noted for its annual Whit Friday brass band contest.

New areas (Chinese: 新区) in the People's Republic of China are special economic development zones established to promote economic growth and attract investment. State-level new areas are supported by the central government and granted preferential policies by the State Council, while provincial- and prefectural-level new areas are managed by local governments.

North Hollywood High School (NHHS) is a public high school in North Hollywood, Los Angeles, California, established in 1927. Part of the Los Angeles Unified School District, it serves grades 9–12 and has an enrollment of 2,579 students as of 2019–20. The school offers three magnet programs—Highly Gifted Magnet, Zoo Magnet, and STEM Magnet—alongside Small Learning Communities. Known for its academic and extracurricular achievements, NHHS has received high rankings and offers extensive Advanced Placement courses and competitive academic teams.

Polad Israyil oghlu Hashimov (Lang-az; 2 January 1975 – 14 July 2020) was an Azerbaijani military officer, National Hero of Azerbaijan and a major general who served as the Deputy Commander and the Chief of Staff of the 3rd Army Corps of the Azerbaijani Armed Forces.

Hashimov was a popular military figure who took part in the First Nagorno-Karabakh War and the Four-Day War. He was awarded numerous honours during his military career, including jubilee and other medals. He commanded the Azerbaijani forces during the border clashes with Armenia in July 2020 and was killed on the morning of 14 July while fighting on the front-line in the Tovuz District of Azerbaijan. His death sparked violent protests throughout the country calling for war with Armenia, a prelude to the 2020 Nagorno-Karabakh war, which began in September of the same year. He was awarded the title of National Hero of Azerbaijan posthumously in December 2020.

Hashimov is the first and only general of the Azerbaijani Armed Forces to be killed during battle. Before him, Ismat Gayibov, the first Prosecutor General of Azerbaijan, and Mahammad Asadov, a major general in the Azerbaijani Ministry of Internal Affairs, were killed in November 1991 when the Mil Mi-8 helicopter they were in was shot down by Armenian forces.

Gabby La La (born Gabriel Lang on May 23, 1979) is a vocalist and multi-instrumentalist (including sitar, ukulele, accordion, theremin and toy piano), signed to Prawn Song Records. Her music is self described as "fun, unique, crazy (and) kooky". La La's debut album, Be Careful What You Wish For., is a collaboration with Les Claypool that though "peculiar. reveals gems of pop merriment."

Gabby La La is primarily a solo artist but, at times, she has been a member of Weapon of Choice, and she was a member of Les Claypool's Fancy Band.

*Brahmachari* is a 1992 Indian Tamil-language romantic comedy film directed by Muktha Srinivasan and starring Nizhalgal Ravi and Gautami. The film follows Ganesan, a bachelor eager to marry, as he navigates love, family opposition, and comedic misunderstandings. The music was composed by Deva.

Angela Jackson is a collegiate women's basketball coach. She coached the Chicago State University women's basketball team, the Cougars, for 15 years. Jackson led them to win the 2010-2011 Great West Conference Championships and to the Women's Basketball Invitational Final Four, for which she was named Coach of the Year. Among the coaches of Chicago State athletic teams, she has the strongest career record, 123 wins. She coached several 1,000 point scorers, including six players who went on to professional contracts.

Abubakar Umar Tutare (born 13 February 1965) is a Nigerian politician who served as the Senator for Taraba Central from 2011 to 2015. Previously, he held various positions in the Taraba State government, including commissioner roles and Secretary to the State Government. Tutare's political career included controversies, such as legal disputes over his senatorial candidacy and allegations of financial misconduct during his tenure as commissioner of finance. As senator, he focused on improving infrastructure, education, and healthcare in his district.

The Chalfont Viaduct (also known as the Misbourne Viaduct) is the first of two five-arch brick railway viaducts on the Chiltern Main Line in south-east England. It is located between Gerrards Cross and Denham Golf Club stations. The M25 motorway passes beneath it between junctions 16 and 17 at Gerrards Cross near Chalfont St Peter, from where the bridge gets its name. The bridge is known as Chalfont No. 1 Viaduct; the longer Chalfont No. 2 Viaduct is a short distance to the west and spans the A413.

The bridge is noted as a local landmark because for many years it bore a graffiti slogan, "give peas a chance" painted in large white letters on the south-facing parapet.
Gaylord Dewayne Shaw (July 22, 1942 – September 6, 2015) was an American journalist who won a Pulitzer Prize for National Reporting in 1978.

The Tres Amigas SuperStation is a proposed energy infrastructure project in the United States designed to connect the country's three major power grids: the Eastern Interconnection, Western Interconnection, and Texas Interconnection. Located near Clovis, New Mexico, the project aims to use superconductive high-voltage direct current (HVDC) transmission lines to enable controlled energy flow while isolating the grids' asynchronous frequencies. With a planned capacity of 30 GW, the SuperStation would facilitate renewable energy transmission and act as a hub for electricity trading.

Initially proposed in 2009, the project's estimated cost has fluctuated between $1 billion and $2 billion, with significant scaling back announced in 2017. Despite delays and challenges, the project remains under development, albeit with a reduced scope.
Gutter Phenomenon is the third studio album by American metalcore band Every Time I Die.

The Joint Assistant for Deployment and Execution (JADE) is a U.S. military planning system developed by BBN Technologies under DARPA's ARPA-Rome Planning Initiative. Initially a prototype in 1999, JADE was later integrated into the Global Command and Control System (GCCS) and the Adaptive Course of Action (ACOA) environment. Utilizing artificial intelligence, JADE supports rapid crisis action planning by generating Time Phased Force Deployment Data (TPFDD) for organizing combat-ready forces. It combines case-based and generative planning methods, enabling efficient plan adaptation and collaboration across command centers.
Andrew Keary (born 1987) is an Irish hurler who plays for Galway Championship club Killimor. He previously lined out with the Galway senior hurling team. Keary usually lines out as a forward.

Robin Tyler (born Arlene Chernick, April 8, 1942) is the first lesbian or gay comic to come out on national television, a feminist and pioneer in the grassroots struggle for LGBTQ civil rights and marriage equality in the U.S., and a producer. She emceed and produced the main stage at three marches on Washington for LGBTQ rights, including the historic first National March On Washington for Lesbian and Gay Rights in 1979. Tyler coined the phrase "We are everywhere" as a rallying cry for the LGBTQ community. This became the powerful signature chant of the 1979 march, appearing on banners and posters. The chant "We are everywhere" has continued as a popular rallying cry for LGBTQ equality.

Eclecticism is a philosophical and artistic approach that involves selecting and combining elements from various systems, styles, or ideas to create a cohesive whole. Originating in ancient Greek and Roman philosophy, it was practiced by figures such as Cicero, Antiochus of Ascalon, and the Stoics, who synthesized doctrines from multiple schools of thought. The term derives from the Greek word *eklektikos*, meaning "choosing the best." 

In architecture and art, eclecticism refers to the blending of historical styles, particularly prominent in 19th-century design. It also appears in fields like martial arts, philology, psychology, and modern philosophy, where it emphasizes adaptability and integration. Notably, eclecticism has been both praised for its inclusivity and criticized for lacking originality, reflecting its complex role across disciplines.

Dan Dotson (born November 23, 1962) is an American auctioneer who has been in the auctioneering business since 1974. Dan and his wife, Laura Dotson, run American Auctioneers, a full service auction company in Riverside, California. Dan is best known for being the auctioneer on A&E Network's Storage Wars. In 2018, he auctioned off a unit containing $7.5 million in cash in a safe.

Jean-Philippe Mateta (born 28 June 1997) is a French professional footballer who plays as a striker for Premier League club Crystal Palace. He has represented France internationally at U19 and U21 youth levels.
Sergio Garavini (18 May 1926 – 7 September 2001) was an Italian politician, writer and trade unionist.

Nathan's Famous, Inc. is an American company that operates a chain of fast food restaurants specializing in hot dogs. The original Nathan's restaurant stands at the corner of Surf and Stillwell Avenues in the Coney Island neighborhood of the Brooklyn borough of New York City, New York. The company's headquarters are at One Jericho Plaza in Jericho, New York, part of Oyster Bay, New York.

El Camino High School is one of two high schools of the Oceanside Unified School District, located at 400 Rancho Del Oro Drive in Oceanside, California. Declared a California Distinguished School in the early 2000s, it was originally named "Oceanside High School East", and officially became El Camino High School in 1976. El Camino's Truax Theatre was built in the early 1980s and houses a large performance venue, an adjacent classroom with stage and large music and drama rooms. It also recently built a new Science and Technology Building and is undergoing constant construction to clean up the campus. El Camino maintains a close athletic rivalry with Oceanside High School. The ECHS mascot is the "Wildcat" and the school colors are brown and gold.

The Northampton Red Sox were a minor league baseball team based in Cape Charles, Virginia, that played in the Class D Eastern Shore League from 1927 to 1928. The team, unaffiliated with the Boston Red Sox, played home games at Patton Field. After finishing last in their inaugural season, the Red Sox led the league in 1928 when it folded midseason. The franchise and the league ceased operations that year, and Cape Charles has not hosted another minor league team.

Software was a German electronic music duo active from the 1980s to 1999, known for releasing albums under the IC label. Founders Peter Mergener and Michael Weisser pursued separate careers post-dissolution.

The murder of Lieth Von Stein occurred on July 25, 1988, in Washington, North Carolina, when he and his wife, Bonnie, were attacked in their home. Lieth was killed, and Bonnie survived with serious injuries. The investigation revealed that Lieth's stepson, Chris Pritchard, had orchestrated the crime, enlisting his college friends Neal Henderson and James Upchurch to carry out the attack in hopes of inheriting part of Lieth's $2 million estate. All three were convicted, with Upchurch receiving a life sentence, Pritchard serving time before being paroled in 2007, and Henderson paroled in 2000.

The case gained national attention and was the subject of two books, *Cruel Doubt* and *Blood Games*, as well as television adaptations. These works controversially linked the crime to the perpetrators' interest in Dungeons & Dragons, a connection that critics have called tenuous.

Kentucky Route 8 is a 114.202-mile-long (183.790 km) east–west state highway divided into two distinct segments across northern Kentucky. The western terminus of the route is at KY 237 near Francisville. The eastern terminus is at U.S. Route 23 in South Portsmouth. The two distinct segments of this route were not meant to be connected together. KY 8 from its west end in Boone County to Augusta in Bracken County is designated as a part of an identified corridor for bikes named the Ramblin' River Tour by the American Association of State Highway and Transportation Officials (AASHTO).

KY 8 is named the Mary Ingles Highway for part of its length. It is rumored that she was the first white woman in Kentucky. Captured by Indians in Virginia in 1755 and taken to Ohio, she later escaped a salt-making party at Big Bone Lick and made her way across the Kentucky wilderness back home to Virginia.

Hanumasagara is a village in Karnataka, India, located in Koppal district's Kushtagi taluk. Known for its historical Venkateshwara Temple, ancient fort, and wind energy generation, it also produces pomegranates, hybrid seeds, and Ilkal sarees.

Sir Richard Vickers (born 21 August 1928) is a retired British Army lieutenant-general who served from 1948 to 1983, commanding key units and holding senior leadership roles, including Commandant of the Royal Military Academy Sandhurst.

The 297th Infantry Division was a German Army infantry division active during World War II from 1940 to 1945. It participated in Operation Barbarossa, the Battle of Stalingrad, and anti-partisan operations in Yugoslavia.

Boulos Fahmy is an Egyptian jurist and Chief Justice of the Supreme Constitutional Court, appointed on February 9, 2022. A graduate of Cairo University, he has held various judicial roles, including vice-president of the Supreme Constitutional Court and head of the Cairo Court of Appeal. Fahmy is the first Coptic Christian to lead the court in modern Egyptian history.

Braeden Matthew Lemasters (born January 27, 1996) is an American actor, musician, and singer. He began his acting career at age nine, appearing in television series such as *Six Feet Under*, *House*, and *Men of a Certain Age*, for which he received critical acclaim and award nominations. Lemasters has also appeared in films, including *Easy A* and *R.L. Stine's Monsterville: Cabinet of Souls*. 

In addition to acting, Lemasters is the lead guitarist and vocalist for the alternative rock band Wallows, alongside Dylan Minnette and Cole Preston. The band signed with Atlantic Records in 2018 and released their debut album, *Nothing Happens*, in 2019.

The E3 Tug Project is a joint R&D project between Smit International, Damen Shipyards Gorinchem and Alewijnse Marine Technology. The goal of the project is to create an energy efficient tugboat engine to be integrated into future tugboat designs. The purpose of which is to alleviate unnecessary ship pollution by studying the operational patterns of harbor tug use. The Port of Rotterdam is spearheading the project with a goal that by the 2020 it will have reduced its air pollution by ten percent.
Alan Brian Carter (born 1952, Lincolnshire, England) is Emeritus Professor of Moral Philosophy at the University of Glasgow.

*Year of the Gun* is a 1991 American political thriller film directed by John Frankenheimer and based on Michael Mewshaw's novel of the same name. Set in 1978 Rome during the violent activities of the Red Brigades, the film follows an American journalist whose fictional novel about a political kidnapping inadvertently entangles him in real-life events, including the abduction of Italian Prime Minister Aldo Moro. Starring Andrew McCarthy, Sharon Stone, and Valeria Golino, the film explores themes of political unrest and personal betrayal. Despite production challenges and mixed critical reception, it was noted for its atmospheric depiction of Rome and Frankenheimer's direction.

The "San Lorenzo March" is a military march composed by Cayetano Alberto Silva in 1901 and first performed on October 30, 1902, in Rosario, Argentina. Dedicated to General José de San Martín and the Battle of San Lorenzo, it later received lyrics by Carlos Javier Benielli in 1908. The march gained international recognition, being performed at events such as the coronations of King George V and Queen Elizabeth II and during the changing of the guard at Buckingham Palace. It is considered one of the finest military marches globally.

David John Lee (born 26 November 1969) is an English former professional footballer and coach. Primarily a central defender, he is best known for his decade-long tenure at Chelsea, where he made 196 appearances between 1988 and 1998. Despite struggling to secure a regular starting position, his time at Chelsea included notable moments, such as scoring on his debut and enduring a broken leg in an emotional match following the death of club chairman Matthew Harding. Lee also had loan spells at several clubs, including Reading and Portsmouth, before moving to Bristol Rovers in 1998. He later played for various teams, including Crystal Palace, Exeter City, and Parramatta Power, before retiring. 

Following his playing career, Lee transitioned into coaching and scouting. He served as chief scout for Bristol City and later became assistant manager at Northampton Town in 2011, briefly taking charge as caretaker manager.
