Airblue is a private Pakistani airline founded in 2003, with its headquarters in Islamabad. It commenced operations in 2004 and operates a fleet of 12 aircraft serving 26 destinations, including domestic and international routes. Known for innovations like e-ticketing and wireless check-in, Airblue also introduced a frequent flyer program, "Blue Miles." The airline initially expanded rapidly, competing with Pakistan International Airlines and launching international flights in 2005. Despite challenges, including the discontinuation of UK operations, Airblue remains a key player in Pakistan's aviation industry. Its subsidiary, Airbahn, operates in the United States.

Amanda Jaqueline DaCosta (born October 7, 1989) is a retired professional soccer player and coach who played as a midfielder. She had a successful collegiate career at Florida State University before playing for clubs in the United States and Europe, including Liverpool Ladies and multiple National Women's Soccer League teams. Internationally, she represented the United States at youth levels and later played for Portugal, earning 19 caps.

"Eu Quero Tchu, Eu Quero Tcha" is a 2012 single by Brazilian sertanejo duo João Lucas & Marcelo, written by Shylton Fernandes. The song gained widespread popularity with its soccer-themed music video featuring Brazilian footballer Neymar, who performs the signature "Tchu Tcha Tcha" dance. It was featured in the telenovela *Avenida Brasil* and referenced in Brazilian pop culture, including *The Simpsons*. The track inspired international adaptations and covers, including versions by Pitbull, Flavel & Neto, and others, further cementing its global appeal.

The Burrumbeet Cup is an annual thoroughbred horse race held at the Burrumbeet racecourse in Western Victoria, Australia. Inaugurated in 1891, the race is run over a distance of 1,800 metres on turf under handicap conditions and is open to horses meeting a Benchmark 78 qualification. Sponsored by Petrogas Regional, the event has a prize purse of $15,000 as of 2016. Traditionally held on New Year's Day, the Burrumbeet Cup is a popular local event, attracting large crowds and supporting charitable causes.

Notable recent winners include Asset Hound in 2015, trained by Glen and Barry James, and Heisman in 2014, trained by Darren Weir. The race has also featured significant community involvement, with funds raised for causes such as the National Jockeys Trust and cystic fibrosis. The event remains a highlight of the Burrumbeet Park & Windermere Jockey Club's calendar.

The 1975 Council of Ministers (Lang-pt, Lang-tet) was the Council of Ministers formed by the Fretilin political party in 1975 as the inaugural administration or cabinet of the Democratic Republic of East Timor proclaimed in November 1975.

The Murakumo-class destroyers (叢雲型駆逐艦, Murakumo-gata kuchikukan) ("Gathering Clouds") were a class of six torpedo boat destroyers (TBDs) of the Imperial Japanese Navy, built in Britain in 1897–99. The class is also sometimes referred to as the Shinonome-class destroyers (東雲駆逐艦, Shinonome-gata kuchikukan)("Daybreak"). All were named after celestial phenomena.

The 2020–21 UEFA Women's Champions League knockout phase took place from December 9, 2020, to May 16, 2021, featuring 32 teams from 21 associations. Matches were played over two legs, except for the final, which was held at Gamla Ullevi in Gothenburg, Sweden. Barcelona emerged as champions, defeating Chelsea in the final.

Janice Charette is a Canadian public servant serving as the clerk of the Privy Council and secretary to the Cabinet. She previously held the role from 2014 to 2016, before being appointed as the Canadian high commissioner to the United Kingdom in 2016.

She has been recognized as one of Canada’s Top 100 Most Powerful Women in 2013, 2014, and 2015.

The 1960 Louisiana gubernatorial election was held on April 19, 1960.

Primary elections were held in two rounds on December 5, 1959, and January 9, 1960. After defeating Chep Morrison in a Democratic primary which featured some of the most racially charged campaign rhetoric in Louisiana political history, Jimmie Davis was elected to his second nonconsecutive term as governor after defeating the Republican candidate, Francis Grevemberg, in the general election.

Eskişehirspor Kulübü is a Turkish professional football club located in Eskişehir. Eskisehirspor has a total of six trophies, making them the sixth most trophies holders in Turkey. Founded in 1965, Eskişehirspor competed in the top division of Turkish football for 16 years until they were relegated. After being out of the Süper Lig for 12 years the club won the TFF First League playoffs and were promoted in May 2008. Since being relegated from the Süper Lig in 2016 they have competed in lower leagues.

Force Majeure (IPA-fr; Lang-sv, "tourist") is a 2014 internationally co-produced black comedy film written and directed by Ruben Östlund. It follows the marital tension resulting from an apparent avalanche in the French Alps, during which the husband prioritizes his own escape over the safety of his wife and two children. The title used for the film in some English-speaking countries comes from force majeure, a contractual clause freeing both parties from liability in the event of unexpected disasters.

Force Majeure was acclaimed upon release, with critics praising its script and cinematography. It won the Best Film award at the 50th Guldbagge Awards, and was named one of the best films of 2014 by various publications.

Elizabeth Parr-Johnston, CM (born Elizabeth H. Parr in 1939 in New York, NY) is a Canadian business woman. She is the Managing Partner of Parr-Johnston Consultants, an economic policy consultancy based in Chester Basin, Nova Scotia. Parr-Johnston is a past president of two Canadian Universities, a recipient of the 125th Anniversary of the Confederation of Canada Medal in 1992, the Queen Elizabeth II Golden Jubilee Medal in 2002 and the Order of Canada in 2008.

Raised in Essex Fells, New Jersey, she attended Grover Cleveland High School (since renamed as James Caldwell High School).

Cabot Tower is a Gothic Revival structure located on Signal Hill in St. John's, Newfoundland and Labrador, Canada. Designed by architect William Howe Greene, it was constructed between 1898 and 1900 to commemorate Queen Victoria's Diamond Jubilee and the 400th anniversary of John Cabot's voyage to the New World. Built of red sandstone, the tower served as a flag signaling station until 1958 and later housed a Marconi station, which played a role in early wireless communication. Today, it is a prominent symbol of St. John's and a historic landmark.

Gracefield is a city in La Vallée-de-la-Gatineau Regional County Municipality in the Outaouais region of Quebec, Canada. It was reorganized on 13 March 2002 when three former municipalities (Gracefield, Northfield, and Wright) were merged into one.

Its name comes from Patrick Grace who opened there the first business and was mayor of Wright from 1885 to 1890.

Gracefield is located along the Gatineau River and Picanoc River. Its area is dotted with many lakes, most of which are lined with numerous summer camps and cottages.

"Wild Wild Life" is a song by American rock band Talking Heads, released as the lead single from their seventh studio album True Stories. It was the band's third and last top 40 hit on the U.S. Billboard Hot 100.
Dominic Michaelis (1938 – 22 December 2015) was an Anglo-French architect, inventor, and solar energy advocate.

Helmut Rahn (16 August 1929 – 14 August 2003) was a German footballer who played as a wing forward and earned legendary status for his decisive role in West Germany's victory at the 1954 FIFA World Cup, famously scoring the winning goal in the final, known as the "Miracle of Bern." Nicknamed "Der Boss," Rahn played for clubs including Rot-Weiss Essen and 1. FC Köln, and scored 21 goals in 40 international matches.

Bitter Tears: Ballads of the American Indian is a 1964 concept album, the twentieth album released by singer Johnny Cash on Columbia Records. It is one of several Americana records by Cash. This one focuses on the history of Native Americans in the United States and their problems. Cash believed that his ancestry included Cherokee, which partly inspired his work on this recording. The songs in this album address the harsh and unfair treatment of the indigenous peoples of North America by Europeans in the United States. Two deal with 20th-century issues affecting the Seneca and Pima peoples. It was considered controversial and rejected by some radio stations and fans.

In 2014 a tribute album, Look Again to the Wind: Johnny Cash's Bitter Tears Revisited, was released with contributions by Gillian Welch, Dave Rawlings, Emmylou Harris, Bill Miller, and others. This was also the name of a documentary film about the suppression of Cash's Native American-themed album in the 1960s. This aired on PBS in February and November 2016.

Stephen Langton (c. 1150 – 9 July 1228) was an English cardinal, theologian, and Archbishop of Canterbury, best known for his role in the creation of Magna Carta and his contributions to the division of the Bible into chapters. Appointed Archbishop in 1207, his tenure was marked by conflict with King John and significant political and ecclesiastical reforms. Langton also authored numerous theological works and presided over the Constitutions of Stephen Langton, which influenced English Church law.

Borough Green & Wrotham railway station is located in Borough Green, Tonbridge and Malling, England, and is managed by Southeastern. Opened in 1874 as Wrotham, it was renamed in 1962. The station has two platforms and serves the Maidstone Line. Historically, it included freight facilities, which were withdrawn in 1968. The station features a ticket office, a passenger-operated ticket machine, and a modern footbridge installed in 2008. Southeastern operates services to destinations including London Victoria and Ashford International.

Annihilation is a fundamental process in particle physics where a particle and its corresponding antiparticle collide and convert their mass into energy, often producing other particles. The simplest form of annihilation occurs between elementary particles, such as an electron and a positron, which typically results in the creation of photons. At low energies, this process conserves momentum and energy, producing two photons with a combined energy of 1.022 MeV. At higher energies, additional particles can be produced.

Proton–antiproton annihilation is more complex due to the composite nature of protons, consisting of quarks and gluons. This interaction produces a variety of mesons, which decay into stable particles like photons, electrons, and neutrinos. In certain cases, such as annihilation within a heavy atomic nucleus, the process can release significant energy, potentially disrupting the nucleus and triggering secondary reactions.

Annihilation also plays a role in high-energy collisions, such as those at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC), where quark and gluon interactions contribute to the production of the Higgs boson. In 2012, the discovery of the Higgs boson at CERN highlighted the importance of annihilation processes in understanding fundamental particles and forces. These reactions are central to both theoretical physics and practical applications, including potential uses in energy generation and propulsion systems.

Franciszek Piłsudski (1713–1791) was a Polish-Lithuanian nobleman, military officer, and politician. A Colonel in the Samogitian division of the Grand Ducal Lithuanian army, he served as Deputy Cup-Bearer and a member of the Sejm. He expanded his family's estates significantly and was awarded the Order of Saint Stanislaus.

*Song Bird* is the third studio album by American country artist Margo Smith, released in July 1976 by Warner Bros. Records. Produced by Norro Wilson, the album was recorded between January and March 1976 at Columbia Recording Studio in Nashville, Tennessee. It features ten tracks, including four written by Smith and covers of songs like "Save Your Kisses for Me." The album peaked at number 34 on the *Billboard* Top Country Albums chart, with its single "Save Your Kisses for Me" reaching the top ten on the *Billboard* Hot Country Singles chart.
Frank Schaffer Besson Jr., CBE (May 30, 1910 – July 15, 1985) was a United States Army general.

*Aussie Gold Hunters* is an Australian factual television series that premiered on the Discovery Channel on September 15, 2016. Produced by Electric Pictures, the series follows gold miners across Western Australia, Victoria, and Queensland as they use various techniques to meet their seasonal gold targets. The show has been a domestic and international ratings success, airing in 125 countries and reaching an estimated 40 million viewers. It has been renewed through its ninth season, with the eighth season introducing new teams.

Wafa Mustafa (b. 1989 or 1990 (age 34–35)) is a Syrian journalist and activist who campaigns for the release of Syrian detainees. As an activist and former member of Families for Freedom, Mustafa has extensively lobbied the United Nations Security Council to call for the release of the names and locations of all those that Syrian authorities have in captivity. She calls for all detainees in Syria to be freed, whether they are held by the Assad regime or by opposition groups, though she also supports the public demonstrations that began in 2011 against the government of Bashar al-Assad.

Mustafa also campaigns for international recognition of Syrian refugees and against normalized international relations with the Assad regime.

The American Federation of Aviculture, Inc. (AFA) is a national organization dedicated to aviculture, whose purpose is to educate the public and assist members regarding best practices for keeping and living with exotic birds. Local affiliate bird clubs throughout the United States, including Puerto Rico, along with national and international specialty organizations; comprise the Federation. The American Federation of Aviculture is registered as a non-profit 501(c)3 educational organization, with a business office located in Austin, Texas.

AFA members work to promote responsible breeding and care of birds. The volunteer organization provides assistance and resources to all people involved with companion birds including breeders, owners, researchers, vets and rescue organizations. AFA members actively support research projects designed to make life better for parrots in their native habitats.

James R. Jordan Sr. (July 31, 1936 – July 23, 1993) was an American mechanic and the father of basketball legend Michael Jordan. A lifelong basketball and baseball fan, he played a significant role in inspiring his son's athletic career. Jordan Sr. was tragically murdered in 1993 during a robbery while resting in his car. His death led to the conviction of two men, though questions about the case have persisted. He is remembered for his influence on Michael Jordan's life and career.

Auguste Marie Fabre (5 February 1839 – 26 December 1922) was a French industrialist. He had utopian ideas and was involved in various cooperative experiments. He was the author of the 1896 booklet Les Sky Scratchers in which he extolled modern technological developments.

Lezli Baskerville (born January 22, 1956) is an American lawyer and higher education advocate, known for her leadership as the first female president and CEO of the National Association for Equal Opportunity in Higher Education. A graduate of Howard University School of Law, she has held prominent roles in civil rights, government relations, and education policy, including serving as national legislative counsel for the NAACP and vice president for government relations at The College Board. Baskerville has been widely recognized for her contributions, receiving numerous honors such as being named one of Ebony Magazine's Top 100 Most Influential Association Leaders and earning an honorary doctorate from Benedict College.

*Dance of the Tiger* is a 1980 novel by Finnish paleontologist Björn Kurtén, set 35,000 years ago in Ice Age Scandinavia. The story follows a Cro-Magnon named Tiger as he confronts a tyrannical hybrid and explores interactions between Cro-Magnons and Neanderthals. The novel is noted for its scientific plausibility and depiction of interbreeding theories.

Jack Wong Sue OAM, DCM (12 September 1925 – 16 November 2009) was an Australian World War II veteran, author, and businessman. Serving in the Royal Australian Air Force from 1943 to 1946, he was a member of the Z Special Unit and participated in Operation Agas 3 in Borneo, earning the Distinguished Conduct Medal. After the war, he opened Western Australia's first diving equipment store and authored books, including *Blood on Borneo*. In 2006, he was awarded the Medal of the Order of Australia for his contributions to preserving military and maritime history. He passed away in Perth in 2009.

In mathematics and computer programming, exponentiating by squaring is a general method for fast computation of large positive integer powers of a number, or more generally of an element of a semigroup, like a polynomial or a square matrix. Some variants are commonly referred to as square-and-multiply algorithms or binary exponentiation. These can be of quite general use, for example in modular arithmetic or powering of matrices. For semigroups for which additive notation is commonly used, like elliptic curves used in cryptography, this method is also referred to as double-and-add.

St. Mary's High School is a co-educational school located in the Cantonment area in Belagavi, near the Head Post Office of Belagavi, India and is regulated and supervised by Karnataka Secondary Education Examination Board. It was founded in 1851 and is celebrating its 165th jubilee in 2016. Near by educational institutions are Islamiya College and B K Model High School.

Caserma Ederle (Camp Ederle) is a military complex in Vicenza, Italy, where the United States Army has troops stationed. It is under Italian military control and can be managed anytime by the Italian authorities. The Vicenza Military Community is composed of soldiers, family members, civilians and retirees with a small number of airmen and sailors also stationed there. The post serves as the headquarters of United States Army Africa and the 173rd Airborne Brigade. Caserma Ederle serves as the headquarters of U.S. Army Garrison Italy of the United States Army Installation Management Command, an umbrella for all U.S. military properties in Vicenza. The post is named after Major Carlo Ederle, an Italian hero of World War I and recipient of the French Croix de Guerre (French War Cross), among other military honors.
Martyrs Bus Service is a bus operator in Melbourne, Australia. It operates one route under contract to Public Transport Victoria. It is a subsidiary of the Dineen Group.

*The Technicolor Time Machine* is a 1967 science fiction novel by Harry Harrison. The story follows a struggling film director who uses a time machine to produce a historical movie about Viking settlers in Vinland, inadvertently becoming part of history himself. Combining humor and time travel, the novel explores paradoxes and the challenges of filmmaking. It was adapted into a BBC radio play in 1981.

The Historical Illuminatus Chronicles is a series of three novels by Robert Anton Wilson written after his highly successful The Illuminatus! Trilogy and his 1981 Masks of the Illuminati. His co-author from the first trilogy, Robert Shea, was not involved in this series, providing only a praising blurb.

It is composed of three books: The Earth Will Shake (1982) ISBN 1-56184-162-5, The Widow's Son (1985) ISBN 1-56184-163-3, and Nature's God (1991) ISBN 1-56184-164-1. A fourth book, The World Turned Upside Down, was promised at the end of Nature's God but was never written; Wilson also had stated he intended the Chronicles to be a pentalogy. His death in 2007 left the series as a trilogy, incomplete. There is an audiobook of the first novel read by Scott Crisp.

Mild androgen insensitivity syndrome (MAIS), also known as undervirilized male syndrome, is the least severe form of androgen insensitivity syndrome (AIS), a condition caused by mutations in the androgen receptor (AR) gene that impair the body's response to androgens. Individuals with MAIS have a 46,XY karyotype and are phenotypically male with fully masculinized genitalia, but may experience impaired virilization or spermatogenesis. Common features include infertility, mild gynecomastia, decreased secondary terminal hair, and occasionally minor genital abnormalities such as hypospadias. MAIS is estimated to account for 40% of male infertility cases, with 2–3% of infertile men carrying AR gene mutations.

A variant of MAIS, spinal and bulbar muscular atrophy (SBMA), also known as Kennedy's disease, is a neurodegenerative disorder caused by a specific AR mutation. Diagnosis of MAIS typically involves genetic testing, as its mild presentation often goes unnoticed. Management focuses on symptomatic treatment, including testosterone supplementation and surgical correction of gynecomastia or hypospadias. Lifespan is generally unaffected, though regular monitoring for associated conditions like breast and prostate cancer may be necessary.

The Arnot Art Museum, located in Elmira, New York, is a historic art museum housed in a Greek-Revival mansion built in 1833. Founded by banker Matthias Hallenback Arnot, it features a 19th-century private picture gallery and a 1982 addition. Its collection includes European, American, and Surrealist art, with works by renowned painters and sculptors.

Rodney Bullock (born June 3, 1994) is an American professional basketball player for KB Prishtina in the Kosovo Basketball Superleague. A 6 ft 8 in small forward, he played college basketball at Providence, where he earned Second-Team All-Big East honors in 2017. After going undrafted in the 2018 NBA draft, Bullock began his professional career in Europe, playing for teams in Hungary, Kosovo, and Saudi Arabia.

Jerry Collins (November 4, 1980 – June 5, 2015) was a Samoan-born New Zealand rugby union player, renowned for his physicality and leadership. Playing as a flanker or number eight, he earned 48 caps for the All Blacks between 2001 and 2008, captaining the team in several matches, including during the 2007 Rugby World Cup. Collins also had a successful club career, representing the Wellington Hurricanes, Toulon, Ospreys, Yamaha Júbilo, and RC Narbonne.

Off the field, Collins was known for his humility and community involvement. He tragically died in a car accident in France in 2015, saving his infant daughter, who survived. Porirua Park Stadium was renamed in his honor.

The Hồ dynasty (Vietnamese: Nhà Hồ, chữ Nôm: 茹胡;Sino-Vietnamese: Hồ triều, chữ Hán: 胡朝) was a short-lived Vietnamese dynasty consisting of the reigns of two monarchs, Hồ Quý Ly (胡季犛) in 1400–01 and his second son, Hồ Hán Thương (胡漢蒼), who reigned the kingdom of Đại Ngu from 1401 to 1406. The practice of bequeathing the throne to a designated son (not simply passing it on to the eldest) was similar to what had happened in the previous Trần dynasty and was meant to avoid sibling rivalry. Hồ Quý Ly's eldest son, Hồ Nguyên Trừng, played his part as the dynasty's military general. In 2011, UNESCO declared the Citadel of the Hồ Dynasty in Thanh Hóa Province a world heritage site.

Allobates femoralis is a small terrestrial frog species in the family Aromobatidae, native to the Amazon Basin and tropical eastern South America. Known for its complex reproductive behaviors, including prolonged courtship and tadpole transport, it inhabits lowland forests and is active during the day. Males are territorial and play a key role in guarding eggs and transporting tadpoles to aquatic sites. The species exhibits behavioral variation across its range, including differences in courtship signals. Listed as "Least Concern" by the IUCN, A. femoralis has a stable population and faces no significant threats.

BBC Radio Wiltshire is the BBC's local radio station serving the county of Wiltshire, England. Launched on 4 April 1989 as BBC Wiltshire Sound, it broadcasts local news, talk, and music from its studios in Swindon. The station operates on FM, DAB, Freeview, and online, with programming produced locally and shared regionally during off-peak hours.

The Republic of Lakotah or Lakotah is a proposed independent republic in North America for the Lakota people. Proposed in 2007 by activist Russell Means, the suggested territory would be enclaved by the borders of the United States, covering thousands of square miles in North Dakota, South Dakota, Nebraska, Wyoming, and Montana. The proposed borders are those of the 1851 Treaty of Fort Laramie between the United States government and the Lakota. These lands are now occupied by Indian reservations and non-Native settlements. None of the existing Lakota tribal governments support the proposed republic, and they were not consulted about the proposal.

Victor L. Shapiro (1924–2013) was an American mathematician known for his contributions to analysis. He earned his Ph.D. from the University of Chicago under Antoni Zygmund and held academic positions at Rutgers, Oregon, and UC Riverside. A prolific author, he published over 80 research articles and several books. Shapiro was elected a Fellow of the American Association for the Advancement of Science in 2003 and the American Mathematical Society in 2012.

Dacrydium cupressinum, commonly known as rimu, is a large evergreen coniferous tree endemic to the forests of New Zealand. It is a member of the southern conifer group, the podocarps. The former name "red pine" has fallen out of common use.
The Design Museum Den Bosch (previously: Stedelijk Museum 's-Hertogenbosch, SM's) is a museum for modern art in 's-Hertogenbosch, The Netherlands.

The *Lord Forbes* was a British merchant ship launched in 1803 and employed in various roles, including as an armed defense ship during the Napoleonic Wars and later as an East Indiaman for the British East India Company (EIC). She completed two voyages for the EIC between 1811 and 1814 before transitioning to private trade. In 1817, after sustaining damage en route to Bengal, she was condemned and sold for breaking up.

A customer data platform (CDP) is a collection of software which creates a persistent, unified customer database that is accessible to other systems. Data is pulled from multiple sources, cleaned and combined to create a single customer profile. This structured data is then made available to other marketing systems. According to Gartner, customer data platforms have evolved from a variety of mature markets, "including multichannel campaign management, tag management and data integration."

The Kettle War was a brief and largely bloodless conflict in 1784 between the Dutch Republic and the Austrian Netherlands, sparked by disputes over the Scheldt River's closure to shipping. The conflict ended with the Treaty of Fontainebleau, reaffirming the river's closure and compensating Austria.

The Committee of the Verkhovna Rada of Ukraine on Environmental Policy, Nature Resources Utilization, and Elimination of the Consequences of the Chornobyl Catastrophe is a parliamentary body established on June 28, 1990. It oversees Ukraine's environmental policies, natural resource management, and disaster response, including Chornobyl. The committee has played a key role in Ukraine's environmental legislation and international commitments, such as ratifying the UN Framework Convention on Climate Change in 1996.
Joseph Biddle Wilkinson Jr. (February 20, 1845 – October 23, 1915) was a Member of the Board of General Appraisers.
Robert "Robbie" Thorpe is an Aboriginal Australian activist and presenter of Fire First, a program on community radio station 3CR in Melbourne.

*Black Noise: Rap Music and Black Culture in Contemporary America* is a 1994 book by Tricia Rose that critically examines rap music, black culture, and their intersections with American society. Widely reviewed and praised, it received a 1995 American Book Award and is considered a foundational text in hip-hop studies.

*Zhenskii vestnik* was a Russian feminist magazine founded by Mariia Ivanovna Pokrovskaia in 1904 and published monthly in Saint Petersburg until 1917. It served as the official organ of the Union of Women until 1907, promoting women's independence and critiquing male-dominated political movements.

Building officials are professionals responsible for ensuring compliance with building codes, regulations, and safety standards. Their roles vary globally, encompassing project management, inspections, and approvals.

The Yawalapiti language is an Arawakan language spoken by the Yawalapiti people in the Xingu Indigenous Park, Mato Grosso, Brazil. As of 2006, it had only eight native speakers. The language features a complex phonemic inventory, agglutinative morphology with classifiers and derivational suffixes, and a subject-verb-object (SVO) word order.

Mijar is a village in the Manglore taluk in the city of Mangaluru of Dakshina Kannada district of the Indian state of Karnataka. Tenkamijaru Grama Panchayat which is 30 km from Mangalore, 12 km from the famous pilgrimage site Jain Kashi. The Grama Panchayat Contains 2 Revenue Villages, cultivation as the main source of income, the level of education and the quality of life of the people are in general. Gram Panchayat consists of Scheduled Castes, Scheduled Tribes, Backward Classes and Others.Hindu Muslim Christian, Jains lead lives in harmony.

The village has a total of 7 wards and has 19 members, Anganwadi Primary School, High School and PU College are located in the Gram Panchayat area. Includes 2 Engineering Colleges and MBA Courses. Ayurvedic herbal preparation parmesis, operating cashew factory, are useful in improving the quality of life of people.The Panchayath has its own fixed and variable assets and is always responsive to people's problems

There are mosques and temples in the Panchayath area, The temple of Lord Vishnu and Somanatheshwara the village headman The Dargah of Muslims in Thodar is religiously famous, People are living in communion with social politics and religion.

When Hope Calls is an American drama television series. The series is a spin-off of Hallmark Channel's When Calls the Heart, which is based on the Canadian West book series by Janette Oke. It premiered on August 30, 2019 as the inaugural original programming on Hallmark Movies Now, the Hallmark Channel's digital streaming service, with new episodes being released through October 25, 2019. The series was subsequently aired on Hallmark Channel in early 2020. The series was renewed for a second season and premiered on Great American Family on December 18, 2021.

*Kanyaka* is a leading Malayalam-language women's lifestyle magazine, known for its wide-ranging content, including cooking recipes, beauty and health tips, relationship advice, and a free family guide in every issue. Established over 33 years ago, it is the third-largest circulated women's fortnightly magazine in its segment. *Kanyaka* has been the official media partner for beauty pageants like Miss South India and Miss Asia, and has organized events such as the Kitchen Queen Cookery contest and a film award celebrating youth in Malayalam cinema.

Cape Newenham LRRS Airport (IATA: EHM, ICAO: PAEH) is a United States Air Force military airstrip located in Cape Newenham, Alaska. Built in 1951, it supports the Cape Newenham Long Range Radar Station, providing access for maintenance personnel and supplies. The gravel runway measures 3,950 by 150 feet.

Bathothauma lyromma, the lyre cranch squid, is a highly modified species of glass squid from the family Cranchiidae with a distribution which encompasses most of the world's oceans from the tropics to high latitudes.
Alimi Ballard (born October 17, 1977) is an American actor known for roles in *Loving*, *Numb3rs*, and *Dark Angel*. He has also appeared in *NCIS* and *CSI: Crime Scene Investigation*.

Serbs in Germany (Serbian: Srbi u Nemačkoj; German: Serben in Deutschland) are a significant ethnic group, with 304,741 people of Serbian ancestry as of 2014. Concentrated primarily in North Rhine-Westphalia, Baden-Württemberg, and Bavaria, they are numerous in cities like Munich, Stuttgart, and Düsseldorf. The community predominantly speaks German and Serbian and practices the Serbian Orthodox faith.

Fairhope Plantation is a historic Carpenter Gothic plantation house and historic district, located one mile east of Uniontown, Alabama, US. The 2+1⁄2-story wood-framed main house was built in the Gothic Revival style in the late 1850s. The plantation historic district includes six other contributing buildings, in addition to the main house. It was added to the Alabama Register of Landmarks and Heritage on December 19, 1991, and subsequently to the National Register of Historic Places on May 29, 1992, due to its architectural and historical significance.

Tobin Stokes (born 1966) is a Canadian composer from North Vancouver, British Columbia. He has composed several operas, including *Fallujah*, *Pauline*, *The Vinedressers*, and *Rattenbury*. Stokes has served as a resident composer for the Victoria Symphony and various international festivals. He holds a BMus in percussion from the University of Victoria and an MFA in creative writing from the University of British Columbia.
Radovan Pankov (Serbian Cyrillic: Радован Панков; born 5 August 1995) is a Serbian professional footballer who plays as a defender for Serbian club Red Star Belgrade.

Vijenac (English: The Wreath) is a biweekly magazine for literature, art and science, established in December 1993 and published by Matica hrvatska, the central national cultural institution in Croatia.

The Acheloos Painter, active around 525–500 BCE in Athens, was a vase painter of the black-figure style. His scenes were like those of the Leagros Group; however, unlike their work, his themes are comic episodes, like modern cartoons. Herakles was a favorite topic, as were banqueting scenes. His banqueters were portrayed satirically: overweight, aging, huge, jutting noses, and so on. The heroic is made anti-heroic by parody. His preferred vase forms are amphorae and hydriae.

Boris Chichkov is a physicist and professor at Leibniz University Hannover, specializing in laser physics, nanotechnology, and biophotonics. His research includes femtosecond laser processing, additive nanomanufacturing, and biomedical applications.

1939 Battle of Westerplatte, originally titled in Poland as Tajemnica Westerplatte ("The Secret of Westerplatte"), is a 2013 Polish-Lithuanian war film written and directed by Paweł Chochlew. It portrays the Battle of Westerplatte between the Polish Army and Nazi German forces at the start of World War II in 1939. The film focuses on the conflict between Westerplatte commander Henryk Sucharski (Michał Żebrowski) and his deputy Franciszek Dąbrowski (Robert Żołędziewski).
Callulops fojaensis is a species of frog in the family Microhylidae. It is only known from the Foja mountains in West Papua (Indonesia).

Brazil is a predominantly Christian country with Islam being a minority religion, first brought by African slaves and then by Lebanese and Syrian immigrants. Due to secular nature of the Brazil's constitution, Muslims are free to proselytize and build places of worship in the country. However, Islam isn't independently included in charts and graphics representing religions in Brazil due to its very small size, being grouped in "other religions", which generally represent about 1% of the country's population. The number of Muslims in Brazil, according to the 2010 census, was 35,207 out of a population of approximately 191 million people. This correspond to 0.018% of the Brazilian population.

Circumstance (Lang-fa; Lang-fr) is a 2011 French-Iranian-American dramatic film written and directed by Maryam Keshavarz starring Nikohl Boosheri, Sarah Kazemy, Reza Sixo Safai and Keon Alexander. It explores homosexuality in modern Iran, among other subjects.

NBA Street Vol. 2 is a basketball video game, published by Electronic Arts under the EA Sports BIG label and developed by NuFX and EA Canada. It is the sequel to NBA Street and the second game in the NBA Street series. It was released on April 29, 2003 for the PlayStation 2, GameCube, and, for the first time in the series, on the Xbox. Japan was only able to see a PlayStation 2 release of this game. The GameCube version was also planned to be released in that region, but it was canceled for unknown reasons.

The Beach 90th Street station (signed as Beach 90th Street–Holland station) is a station on the IND Rockaway Line of the New York City Subway. It is served by the Rockaway Park Shuttle at all times and ten daily rush-hour only A trains.
Wilkinson Heights is a census-designated place (CDP) in Orangeburg County, South Carolina, United States, with a population of 1,946 as of 2020.

The Council on Religion and the Homosexual (CRH), founded in 1964 in San Francisco, was the first U.S. organization to unite religious leaders and homosexual activists, advocating for LGBTQ+ rights and fostering dialogue between faith communities and the gay community.

Augustus Jones (c. 1757 – November 16, 1836) was an American-born Canadian surveyor and farmer, known for his significant contributions to the development of Upper Canada (now Ontario). Born in the Hudson River Valley, New York, Jones trained as a land surveyor before fleeing to Canada with his Loyalist family during the American Revolution. He became Deputy Surveyor General for the Nassau District in 1789, overseeing the surveying of numerous townships and major infrastructure projects, including Yonge Street and Dundas Street. His work helped establish the geographic and political boundaries of southern Ontario.

Jones developed strong relationships with Indigenous communities, becoming fluent in their languages and earning the trust of leaders like Joseph Brant. He married Sarah Tekarihogen, daughter of a Mohawk chief, and also had children with Tuhbenahneequay, a Mississauga woman. After retiring in 1800, Jones farmed in Saltfleet Township and later near Paris, Ontario. He died in 1836 and was buried in Brantford. A statue honoring his contributions was unveiled in Stoney Creek, Ontario, in 2005.

The Burrumbeet Cup is a thoroughbred horse race, held under handicap conditions over a distance of 1800 metres at the Burrumbeet racecourse, Burrumbeet, Victoria, Australia on New Year's Day every year.

The 2020 Petrogas Regional Burrumbeet Cup will be held on 1 January 2020 and will be the 130th running of the race.

Prize money for the 2020 Burrumbeet Cup is $25,000 and Petrogas Regional is race sponsor. Petrogas Regional became the Burrumbeet Cup sponsor from 2015 onwards after the decision by Scott Petroleum to end their Burrumbeet Cup sponsorship following the running of the 2014 Burrumbeet Cup.

The Burrumbeet Park & Windermere Racing Club (BP&WRC) administer racing at Burrumbeet under the jurisdiction of Racing Victoria and all 14 committee members are volunteers. President of the BP & WRC is Rod McKinnon and club secretary is Paul Brumby.

Celanese Corporation is a global chemical and advanced materials company headquartered in Irving, Texas. Founded in 1918 in New York City by Swiss chemist Camille Dreyfus, the company initially produced cellulose acetate fabrics and yarns. Over the years, Celanese expanded its operations, becoming a leading producer of acetyl intermediates, advanced engineered materials, and consumer specialties, including food preservatives and cellulose acetate for cigarette filters. The company is also known for its TCX Technology, a process for producing ethanol from natural gas. Publicly traded on the New York Stock Exchange under the symbol "CE," Celanese reported $8.54 billion in revenue and employed over 8,500 people as of 2021.

*L.A. Law: The Movie* (also known as *L.A. Law: Return to Justice*) is a 2002 American drama television film and reunion of the series *L.A. Law*. Directed by Michael Schultz and written by William M. Finkelstein, it aired on NBC on May 12, 2002. Set eight years after the series finale, the film follows the lives of the McKenzie Brackman law firm members as they navigate personal and professional challenges, including legal battles and relationship conflicts.

Deerfoot, also known as Lewis Bennett (1828–1897), was a Seneca long-distance runner renowned for his world-record performances and unique racing tactics. Competing under the nickname "Red Jacket," he gained international fame during an 1861–1863 tour of England, where he defeated many of the world's top runners and set records for 10 and 12 miles. Known for his unconventional pacing and colorful persona, Deerfoot captivated audiences and became a celebrated figure in both the United States and England.

Aristides Aquino Núñez (born April 22, 1994) is a Dominican professional baseball outfielder for the Chunichi Dragons of Nippon Professional Baseball (NPB). He has played for the Cincinnati Reds of Major League Baseball.
Nashipur is a village in the Murshidabad-Jiaganj CD block in the Lalbag subdivision of Murshidabad district in West Bengal, India.

Alison Mary Fiske (2 August 1943 – 26 July 2020) was an English actress, who won Actress of the Year in a New Play at the 1977 Laurence Olivier Awards for playing Fish in Dusa, Fish, Stats and Vi. She was also nominated in the 1979 Laurence Olivier Awards for Best Actress in a Supporting Role for playing Evie in For Services Rendered, and she won awards for her television performance in Helen: A Woman of Today.

"Al-Damad" Ahmad Nami or "Damat" Ahmet Nami (Lang-ar;‎ 1873 – 13 December 1962) was an Ottoman prince (damat), the fifth prime minister of Syria and second president of Syria (1926–28), and a lecturer of history and politics.

Kamaal the Abstract is the third studio album by American hip hop artist Q-Tip, released September 15, 2009, on Battery Records. Recorded in 2001, the album is a departure from his solo debut album Amplified (1999). Kamaal the Abstract is an eclectic album that features Q-Tip rapping, singing, and exploring his jazz influences. The album contains introspective and life-related lyrical themes.

The album was originally planned for release in 2002 as the follow-up to Amplified. However, it was shelved by Q-Tip's record label at the time, Arista, who doubted its commercial potential. It eventually leaked onto the internet, while the distribution of promotional copies led several publications to run reviews of the album.

After its official release in 2009, the album debuted at number 77 on the U.S. Billboard 200 chart, selling 6,000 copies in its first week. Upon its release, Kamaal the Abstract received positive reviews from most music critics.
